title: Phase 1 — scripts/refresh-gaia-nss.py + commit data/gaia_dr3_nss_two_body.tsv
description:
Part of the source-ID-anchored catalogue-pipeline rewrite (see parent epic for architecture).

# Scope

Gaia DR3 NSS (Non-Single-Star) two-body-orbit catalogue. Pulls the full `gaiadr3.nss_two_body_orbit` table and writes a thin TSV with upstream columns verbatim — no Thiele-Innes → Campbell conversion at this stage (that lives downstream in dch.31). Phase 2 Stage 4 (stellata-dch.31) prefers NSS over ORB6 inside Gaia's observability regime (P < ~10 yr, separation < ~1"). Depends on stellata-dch.21 (refresh_lib).

# Live-probe schema correction (2026-05-18)

Bead spec drifted from the actual `gaiadr3.nss_two_body_orbit` schema. Live 1-row probe surfaced 4 items; corrected below.

1. **Row count: ~443,205, NOT ~169k.** Original spec cited "~169k binaries" — wrong by 2.6×. The 169k figure may have referred to a subset (e.g. astrometric-only `Orbital` type = 134,598). Bounds re-pinned at [440k, 446k].

2. **Schema uses Thiele-Innes constants, NOT Campbell elements.** DR3 NSS encodes the orbit via:
   - `a_thiele_innes, b_thiele_innes, f_thiele_innes, g_thiele_innes` (mas)
   - `c_thiele_innes, h_thiele_innes` (AU) — non-null only for AstroSpectro* solution types
   - Plus errors on each
   The classical Campbell elements `semimajor_axis` and `ascending_node` from the original bead spec are NOT stored columns — they are DERIVED from Thiele-Innes via Heintz 1978 algebra in the downstream consumer (dch.31). `inclination` and `arg_periastron` ARE stored columns but are NULL for `Orbital`-type rows (inclination is encoded in Thiele-Innes constants); populated only for AstroSpectroSB1 / SB1+spectroscopic solutions.

3. **`nss_solution_type` is a categorical with 12 distinct values, not "two-body orbit".** Distribution (DR3 frozen, 2026-05-18 probe):
   - SB1: 181,327 (spectroscopic single-lined)
   - Orbital: 134,598 (pure astrometric)
   - EclipsingBinary: 86,918
   - AstroSpectroSB1: 33,467 (astrometric + spectroscopic combined)
   - SB2: 4,630
   - SB2C, OrbitalAlternative, OrbitalTargetedSearch, SB1C, OrbitalTargetedSearchValidated, EclipsingSpectro, OrbitalAlternativeValidated: < 1k each
   The TSV must surface `nss_solution_type` so downstream can filter / route conversion logic per type.

4. **70 Oph (HIP 88601) spot-check is impossible.** Two independent failures: (a) HIP 88601 = V 4.0, below the Gaia saturation limit — not present in `hipparcos2_best_neighbour`; (b) 70 Oph's P = 88 yr is far beyond NSS's max period (~9,936 d ≈ 27 yr). Replaced with a self-consistency spot-check against a bright HIP star confirmed in NSS by live probe (matches the Sirius PM pattern in refresh-hipparcos2.py).

# Deliverables

- `scripts/refresh-gaia-nss.py` (new) — ADQL pull from `gaiadr3.nss_two_body_orbit` (full table). Backend: ESA Gaia archive (refresh_lib default ESA → CDS fallback). Idempotent (`is_up_to_date`).
- `data/gaia_dr3_nss_two_body.tsv` (new, LFS) — columns:
  - `source_id` (int)
  - `nss_solution_type` (string)
  - `period`, `period_error` (float, days)
  - `t_periastron`, `t_periastron_error` (float, days)
  - `eccentricity`, `eccentricity_error` (float)
  - `a_thiele_innes`, `a_thiele_innes_error` (float, mas)
  - `b_thiele_innes`, `b_thiele_innes_error` (float, mas)
  - `f_thiele_innes`, `f_thiele_innes_error` (float, mas)
  - `g_thiele_innes`, `g_thiele_innes_error` (float, mas)
  - `c_thiele_innes`, `c_thiele_innes_error` (float, AU) — null except AstroSpectro
  - `h_thiele_innes`, `h_thiele_innes_error` (float, AU) — null except AstroSpectro
  - `inclination`, `inclination_error` (float, deg) — null for `Orbital` type
  - `arg_periastron`, `arg_periastron_error` (float, deg) — null for `Orbital` type
  - `mass_ratio`, `mass_ratio_error` (float) — non-null for SB2*
  - `goodness_of_fit` (float)
  - `significance` (float)
  - Total: 27 columns
- `package.json` — `refresh:gaia-nss` alias.

# Acceptance

- Row count in [440_000, 446_000] (DR3 frozen — observed 443,205 on 2026-05-18).
- Schema validated against EXPECTED_SCHEMA (subset assertion on the 27 columns we keep).
- Spot-check: bright HIP star confirmed in NSS via live probe — period and eccentricity self-consistent within tolerance (matches `refresh-hipparcos2.py`'s Sirius pattern).
- Deterministic regen with no source changes produces byte-identical TSV.

# Smoke

- `npm run refresh:gaia-nss` writes the TSV.
- `wc -l data/gaia_dr3_nss_two_body.tsv` shows ~443k + header.
- Re-run is a no-op (idempotency log line).
- Spot-check row passes self-consistency check.

# Commit

One commit. Standard PR.

LABELS: gaia, nss, phase-1, refresh-scripts

title: Phase 1 — scripts/refresh-hipparcos2.py + commit data/hip2_van_leeuwen.tsv
description:
Part of the source-ID-anchored catalogue-pipeline rewrite (see parent epic for architecture).

# Scope

Hipparcos van Leeuwen 2007 reduction — improved astrometric solution covering all 118k HIP stars. Critical for the bright-binary fallback (Phase 2 Stage 3 stellata-dch.30): when Gaia's 5-parameter PM is corrupted by orbital wobble in close binaries (Sirius, α Cen, Castor, Algol) or by saturation (Sirius A, Polaris, Vega), HIP2's longer-baseline PM averages over a different window on the orbit and is often the truer single-star PM. Depends on stellata-dch.21 (refresh_lib).

# Deliverables

- scripts/refresh-hipparcos2.py (new) — VizieR I/311/hip2 query via CDS TAP (whole-catalog dump; not source_id-restricted, so no batching).
- data/hip2_van_leeuwen.tsv (new, LFS ~10 MB) — schema and column naming per the live VizieR I/311/hip2 probe (column names in the published HIP2 paper differ from what the VizieR TAP table exposes; the script renames on write).
- package.json — "refresh:hip2" alias.

# Schema (live VizieR I/311/hip2 → TSV mapping)

VizieR exposes the table with these case-sensitive column names; the TSV uses Stellata-canonical lowercase names matching the rest of the catalogue pipeline:

  HIP    → hip                (int)
  RArad  → ra_icrs            (float, deg)   # name is a misnomer — values are in degrees, not radians
  DErad  → de_icrs            (float, deg)
  Plx    → plx                (float, mas)
  e_Plx  → e_plx              (float, mas)
  pmRA   → pm_ra              (float, mas/yr)
  pmDE   → pm_de              (float, mas/yr)
  e_pmRA → e_pm_ra            (float, mas/yr)
  e_pmDE → e_pm_de            (float, mas/yr)
  F2     → goodness_of_fit    (float, dimensionless — HIP2's chi² GoF statistic; ~N(0,1) for good solutions)
  Ntr    → n_transits         (int)          # # of HIP transits used in the astrometric solution

ref_epoch is NOT a column — it is a global constant J1991.25 for the whole catalogue, documented in the script docstring rather than written per-row.

# Acceptance

- Row count between 117_000 and 118_500 (HIP2 has 117,955 stars; small drift per Vizier mirror).
- Schema validated via refresh_lib.validate_schema against the actual VizieR column names.
- Spot-check Sirius A (HIP 32349): pm_ra ≈ -546.01 mas/yr, pm_de ≈ -1223.07 mas/yr (live VizieR I/311/hip2 values; the paper's nominal -546.05 / -1223.14 differs by ~0.05 mas/yr from the VizieR mirror). Compare against Gaia DR3's smaller pmRA values to confirm the saturation-cliff discrepancy that motivates the fallback.

# Smoke

- npm run refresh:hip2 writes the TSV
- wc -l matches the expected row count band
- Spot-check Sirius A row matches the live values within 0.1 mas/yr

# Commit

One commit. Standard PR.

title: Phase 1 — scripts/refresh-gaia-tyc-xmatch.py + commit data/gaia_dr3_tyc_xmatch.tsv
description:
Part of the source-ID-anchored catalogue-pipeline rewrite (see parent epic for architecture).

# Scope

Tycho-2 -> Gaia DR3 source_id cross-walk. Catches stars without HIP coverage (Tycho-2 reaches V~11.5 vs HIP's V~9-10). Depends on stellata-dch.21 (refresh_lib).

# Deliverables

- scripts/refresh-gaia-tyc-xmatch.py (new) — ADQL pull from gaiadr3.tycho2tdsc_merge_best_neighbour via refresh_lib.TapClient (~2.52M rows; single-query — no batching needed, the merge_best_neighbour table is small enough for one async TAP job).
- data/gaia_dr3_tyc_xmatch.tsv (new, LFS-tracked) — columns below, sorted by tyc (lexicographic; matches source).
- package.json — new "refresh:gaia-tyc" alias.

# ADQL query

SELECT
  original_ext_source_id AS tyc,
  source_id              AS gaia_source_id,
  angular_distance,
  number_of_neighbours,
  xm_flag
FROM gaiadr3.tycho2tdsc_merge_best_neighbour
WHERE source_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY original_ext_source_id

# TSV schema (5 columns)

- tyc (string, "NNNN-NNNN-N", no "TYC " prefix) — Tycho-2 / TDSC identifier
- gaia_source_id (int64) — Gaia DR3 source_id
- angular_distance (float, 6 decimals) — match separation in arcsec
- number_of_neighbours (int) — ambiguity flag (1 = unique Gaia neighbour)
- xm_flag (int) — Gaia cross-match flag

Mirrors the dch.22 (HIP) TSV's 5-column shape so Stage 1 indexers can ingest both with the same parser.

# Acceptance

- Row count between 2_510_000 and 2_530_000 (pinned around observed 2,518,330).
- Schema validated by refresh_lib.validate_schema.
- Deterministic regen: re-running produces a byte-identical TSV.

# Smoke

- npm run refresh:gaia-tyc writes the TSV deterministically.
- wc -l shows ~2,518,331 lines (rows + header).
- Spot-check: TYC 425-2502-1 (Barnard's Star) -> source_id 4472832130942575872, angular_distance ~= 0.201663 arcsec (cross-validates against the HIP xmatch — both refer to the same Gaia DR3 source).

# Commit

One commit. Standard PR with skip-version-bump label (pure infrastructure).

# Drift corrected from original bead spec (2026-05-15 author had three table-schema errors caught by live TAP probe)

- Table name was `tyco2tdsc_merge_best_neighbour` — actual is `tycho2tdsc_merge_best_neighbour` (missing 'h'). Both `gaiadr3.*` and `gaiaedr3.*` variants exist; we use the DR3 one.
- ADQL column `magnitude_difference` does NOT exist in this best-neighbour table — same shape of error as dch.22's HIP table. Replaced with `number_of_neighbours` + `xm_flag` (the only ambiguity flags published here), matching the HIP TSV's 5-column shape exactly.
- TYC format is `NNNN-NNNN-N` (no "TYC " prefix). Three components: GSC region (1-9999), GSC number (1-12119), component letter encoded as int (1-4). Stored as a single string column.
- Row count was specified as [2_400_000, 2_600_000] — actual is 2,518,330. Tightened to [2,510,000, 2,530,000] (DR3 is frozen).

LABELS: gaia, phase-1, refresh-scripts, tycho

title: Phase 1 — scripts/refresh-gaia-hip-xmatch.py + commit data/gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv
description:
Part of the source-ID-anchored catalogue-pipeline rewrite (see parent epic for architecture).

# Scope

Refresh script + committed TSV for the HIP -> Gaia DR3 source_id cross-walk. This is THE cornerstone of the new pipeline: every WDS component with a HIP id resolves to a Gaia source_id via this table without ever touching a position match. Depends on scripts/refresh_lib.py (stellata-dch.21).

Gaia DR3's `hipparcos2_best_neighbour` saturates on the brightest stars: Sirius (HIP 32349), Polaris (HIP 11767), Vega (HIP 91262) and other V <= 4 stars are ABSENT from this cross-walk. The Hipparcos-2-anchored fallback in dch.24 handles them.

# Deliverables

- scripts/refresh-gaia-hip-xmatch.py (new) — ADQL pull from gaiadr3.hipparcos2_best_neighbour. Idempotent (--force to override). Documents the full ADQL query in module docstring.
- data/gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv (new, LFS-tracked; ~3.7 MB) — columns below, sorted by hip.
- package.json — new "refresh:gaia-hip" npm alias.
- .gitattributes — add data/*.tsv LFS pattern.

# ADQL query

SELECT
  original_ext_source_id AS hip,
  source_id              AS gaia_source_id,
  angular_distance,
  number_of_neighbours,
  xm_flag
FROM gaiadr3.hipparcos2_best_neighbour
WHERE source_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY original_ext_source_id

# TSV schema (5 columns)

- hip (int) — Hipparcos identifier
- gaia_source_id (int64) — Gaia DR3 source_id
- angular_distance (float, 6 decimals) — match separation in arcsec
- number_of_neighbours (int) — ambiguity flag (1 = unique Gaia neighbour)
- xm_flag (int) — Gaia cross-match flag

# Acceptance

- Row count between 99_400 and 99_600. Empirical observation: 99,525 rows. Pinned in script as EXPECTED_ROW_COUNT_MIN / MAX; out-of-range fails the build.
- Schema validated by refresh_lib.validate_schema.
- Deterministic regen: re-running with the source unchanged produces a byte-identical TSV.

# Smoke

- npm run refresh:gaia-hip writes the TSV deterministically
- wc -l data/gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv shows ~99,526 lines (99,525 rows + header)
- Spot-check: HIP 87937 (Barnard's Star) -> source_id 4472832130942575872, angular_distance ~= 0.394425 arcsec
- Sirius (HIP 32349) is correctly ABSENT (Gaia saturation)

# Commit

Two topical commits on one PR (per commit-granularity):
1. fix(refresh_lib): numpy 2.x dtype + float matching — np.issubdtype(int32, int) is False in numpy 2.x, broke validate_schema; isinstance(np.float32(...), float) is False, broke write_tsv's round_floats. Both fixed in lib + tests.
2. feat: refresh-gaia-hip-xmatch.py + TSV (dch.22).

PR labelled skip-version-bump (no user-visible behavior; pure infrastructure).

# Drift corrected from original bead spec (2026-05-15 author had two table-schema errors)

- Row count was specified as [110_000, 118_000] — actual is 99,525 (~117k published HIP count includes stars Gaia could not observe).
- ADQL columns `hip` and `magnitude_difference` do not exist in gaiadr3.hipparcos2_best_neighbour. The HIP id column is `original_ext_source_id`; no magnitude difference is published in this best-neighbour table. Schema confirmed via TAP probe.
- Spot-check Sirius source_id 2947050466531873024 is not retrievable from this table (Sirius absent). Replaced with Barnard's Star.

title: Phase 1 — scripts/refresh_lib.py: shared TAP client + retry + batching + schema validation
description:
Part of the source-ID-anchored catalogue-pipeline rewrite (see parent epic for architecture).

# Scope

Shared infrastructure for all Phase 1 refresh scripts (HIP-Gaia, Tyc-Gaia, Hipparcos2, NSS, Astrometry, SIMBAD, Bailer-Jones, Apsis — 7+ scripts). Well past the two-call-site DRY threshold (memory stellata-named-constants-and-dry), so the shared lib lands first.

# Deliverables

- scripts/refresh_lib.py (new) — TAP client supporting both astroquery.gaia (ESA TAP) and CDS TAP (pyvo) with auto-fallback on transient (5xx / network) errors; exponential-backoff retry with injectable sleep/rand for deterministic tests; batching helper for chunked IN-list queries; output-schema-validation helper (column names + dtypes match expected); idempotency-check helper (mtime-based, mirrors scripts/build-clouds.py:is_up_to_date()); canonical TSV writer.
- scripts/requirements-refresh.txt (new) — pinned (lower-bound) versions of astropy, astroquery, numpy, pyvo, requests. Documented venv setup in module docstring.
- scripts/refresh_lib.test.py (new) — Python unittest covering retry behaviour, schema validation, idempotency, batching, TSV writer, and TapClient fallback. (Vitest not used because astroquery isn't Node-amenable.)
- .gitignore — add scripts/__pycache__/ (refresh_lib gets imported by the test, generating .pyc files the existing build-* scripts never produced).

# Acceptance

- importable from each Phase 1 refresh script (verified when those land)
- `python3 scripts/refresh_lib.test.py` green (the `.test.py` suffix matches the project's *.test.ts convention but conflicts with `python -m unittest`'s dotted-module loader — run via the __main__ block instead)
- module docstring documents the two TAP backends + the auto-fallback policy + the venv setup

# Smoke

- Activate venv: `python3 -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install -r scripts/requirements-refresh.txt`
- Run tests: `.venv/bin/python scripts/refresh_lib.test.py` (or just `python3 scripts/refresh_lib.test.py` — the suite uses only stdlib so the venv isn't strictly required for the tests themselves; the deps are for the live refresh scripts that will land in dch.22-25, dch.29, dch.46, dch.48).

# Files to touch

- scripts/refresh_lib.py (new)
- scripts/requirements-refresh.txt (new)
- scripts/refresh_lib.test.py (new)
- .gitignore (add scripts/__pycache__/)

# Commit

One commit. PR labelled skip-version-bump (no user-visible behavior; pure infrastructure).

title: Run Gaia DR3 ADQL upload + commit gaia_dr3_binaries.tsv to engage WDS optical-pair filter
description:
# Why

build-binaries.py's optical-pair filter cascade has three tiers:
1. WDS-Notes flag chars (S/U/X/Y → optical; T/V/Z → physical) — set on
   maybe 40% of pairs.
2. Gaia DR3 parallax 3σ overlap + ~5 mas/yr common-PM — the strong gate.
3. Mag-gap sanity heuristic — weak fallback.

Without `data/gaia_dr3_binaries.tsv` the cascade runs in degraded mode
(tiers 1 + 3 only). Optical WDS pairs without an explicit Notes flag
slip through as "unknown:kept", and build-binaries.py injects SYN-NNN
secondaries for them at the primary's heliocentric distance. Those are
genuine fake stars in the model — most visibly the ~4-5 spurious
companions around Sirius (WDS A-C, A-D, A-E, A-F, A-G entries that are
really kpc-scale background stars, not the 2.64 pc binary partner).

Sirius B (the real white dwarf, WDS A-B) is unaffected — its
parallax / PM agreement with A would survive either filter.

# Scope

User-driven manual data refresh — no Stellata code changes.

1. Run `npm run build:binaries` once to regenerate `data/wds_upload.csv`
   (component ra/dec list).
2. Upload `wds_upload.csv` to the Gaia archive
   (https://gea.esac.esa.int/archive/) as a user table.
3. ADQL JOIN against `gaiadr3.gaia_source` within 1″ (procedure
   detailed in SCIENCE.md "Multiple-star pipeline" section).
4. Download the JOIN result as `data/gaia_dr3_binaries.tsv` with at
   minimum columns: `wds_id, comp, parallax, parallax_error, pmra, pmdec`.
5. `git lfs track 'data/gaia_dr3_binaries.tsv'` if not already covered
   by the `data/*.tsv` LFS pattern.
6. Re-run `npm run build:catalog` and verify the build log shows
   `gaia:optical` / `gaia:physical` filter tags appearing in the
   per-pair source field.

# Smoke / acceptance

- `data/gaia_dr3_binaries.tsv` committed (LFS).
- Build log shows Gaia filter engaged (non-zero `gaia:optical` rejects).
- Spurious SYN-NNN count around Sirius drops to zero (visual smoke:
  focus Sirius A in the rendered scene, only A + B visible at the
  binary scale; no kpc-distance background-star injections).
- α Cen + Toliman + other famous physical pairs still pass through.
- No regression in `npm test` (renderer side is unchanged).

# Why this blocks dch.10

dch.10 lights up live orbital motion for every Regime 2/3 binary in
the catalog. If the catalog contains SYN-NNN injections from optical
pairs (no real orbit physics; their orbital elements are nonsense),
they'd visibly drift / oscillate alongside real binaries and amplify
the spurious-star problem under warp. Land this first so dch.10
operates on a clean catalog.
close_reason: superseded by Phase 1 refresh scripts (stellata-dch.22, 23, 24, 25). Position-anchored Gaia upload approach replaced with HIP/Tycho/source_id-anchored ADQL queries. The 43k-row TSV produced 2026-05-14 is discarded; refresh-gaia-binaries.py likewise.

title: Perf regression: idle frame.total 0.25→0.70ms after multiples catalog grows 9%
description:
# Symptom

After `stellata-dch.6` lands, idle-frame CPU cost more than doubles:

|                    | main (313k records) | dch.6 (341k records) |
|--------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| FPS, idle          | 1500-1800           | 800-1000             |
| `onFrame.total`    | ~0.25 ms            | ~0.70 ms             |
| `gpu.render`       | ~0.25 ms            | ~0.25 ms             |

Catalog grew 9% (`recordCount` 313,242 → 341,146, mostly SYN secondary
injections from `data/multiples.tsv`). GPU draw cost barely shifted —
the regression is entirely on the CPU side, in onFrame handler chain.

# Hypothesis

Two candidates worth pinning down before changing code:

1. **Cache-miss inflection.** The 14 MB catalog + 13 MB search-index
   working set may have crossed L2/L3 cache size on the test machine,
   making memory access on every star-position read more expensive
   even though no per-frame loop walked all 341k records.
2. **Per-frame walk that scales with N.** Some onFrame handler I'm
   not aware of iterates over `cat.count`. Per the docs/performance.md
   chart-mode optimisations, the sorted-distance core-mask window
   should be bounded — but worth confirming under the new catalog
   size.

# Procedure

1. Open `?perf` (or the keyboard shortcut that toggles the perf HUD)
   on both branches at the same focus / camera state.
2. Compare per-section breakdown (`controls.update`, `coreMask`,
   `pre-render`, `gpu.render`, individual chart-* if chart on).
3. Whichever section regressed by ~0.45 ms is the culprit.

# Likely fixes (gate on profile)

- If `coreMask` regressed: tighten the sorted-distance window or
  early-exit on `distFromCamera > threshold`.
- If a specific handler regressed: cache its work in a dirty-tracked
  way per `stellata-consistency-at-the-seam` (sentinel-init rules).
- If no single section accounts for it: cache-miss, more involved —
  consider splitting the catalog into hot (frequently-touched per
  frame) vs cold (looked up only on focus / search) typed-array views.

# Acceptance

- `onFrame.total` returns to within 10% of the main-branch value at
  the same focus / camera state.
- No correctness regressions in the existing test suite.
- A pinned vitest on whatever pure-helper changed (per
  `stellata-test-coverage-discipline`).
close_reason: No longer reproduces after stellata-9mm.190.1 (PR #68, merged 2026-05-16) made perf-hud tear down realMark/realMeasure/realFrame on debug-panel close. The original measurement was likely contaminated by the perf-hud install staying warm for the rest of the session once the panel had been opened — the ~12 perfMark/perfMeasure/perfFrame call sites per animate() frame kept doing real Map ops/ring writes even with the panel closed, which is exactly the kind of small-constant per-frame regression that scales with catalog growth. No perf issue observed in routine use since the 190.1 fix landed; re-file with a fresh capture if it reappears.

title: Debug panel: hidden triple-tap D shortcut + fold debug.arrows() into main panel
description:
Two small debug-panel UX changes for Alex's own testing convenience.

# 1. Hidden triple-tap 'D' to open the debug panel

Add a triple-tap on the 'D' key as a hidden affordance that opens the unified debug panel (same target as `window.debug.panel()`). Detection: three keydown events on KeyD within a short rolling window (e.g. ~500ms total). Wire into the existing global keydown dispatch in `keyboard-shortcuts.ts`.

Acceptance:
- Triple-tapping D anywhere (outside text inputs) opens the debug panel.
- Single/double tap does nothing (no spurious panel opens).
- Modifier keys (Cmd/Ctrl/Alt/Shift) suppress the trigger so it doesn't interfere with browser shortcuts.
- NOT documented in `keyboard-shortcuts.ts` `help-modal.ts`, `docs/ui-and-controls.md`, README, or the in-app `?` help overlay. Hidden affordance — keep it that way.

# 2. Fold debug.arrows() into the unified debug panel

`arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts` is currently its own floating panel hosted by `window.debug.arrows()` (see CLAUDE.md module roster). Move its UI into the main debug panel as a regular section (alongside `star-tuning`, `milkyway-tuning`, `perf-hud`, `pin-debug-hud`) and remove the standalone `debug.arrows()` entry point + its floating-panel chrome.

Acceptance:
- `debug.panel()` (and the new triple-tap) reveals an "Arrows" section showing the same Sol/GC arrow draw state + fade telemetry currently in `arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts`.
- `window.debug.arrows()` is removed; no callers remain (`grep -rn "debug.arrows" src/`).
- `arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts` either deleted or refactored to a section module matching the shape of the other tuning sections (see `debug-panel.ts` chrome + section helpers).
- CLAUDE.md module roster updated to reflect the consolidation.

# Smoke
- `npm run typecheck && npm test`
- Manual: open page, triple-tap D, confirm panel appears with the new Arrows section showing live draw state.
close_reason: merged via PR #65 (Triple-tap D + fold arrow HUD into debug panel)

title: Sol/GC arrows flash on HUD enable when close-zoomed; distance-vector fade gate likely mis-wired
description:
# Two related symptoms (likely one underlying defect)

## 1. Sol/GC pointer arrows flash on HUD enable (core bug)

When the user toggles the HUD on while already close-zoomed on a star, the Sol and GC reference arrows render briefly before the close-approach fade hides them — a visible flash.

Hypothesis: the toggle-on path in \`galactic-overlay.ts\` makes the arrows visible without first evaluating the fade alpha (\`coverage\` over \`refLenPx\`). The fade is computed on the next animation tick, so frame 0 after toggle-on paints at alpha=1 even though steady-state alpha is 0.

## 2. Distance-vector arrow doesn't appear to obey the fade gate at all

Independent of the flash: with the HUD enabled the distance-vector chevron is hidden, and with the HUD disabled it is shown — *regardless of star disc size*. That contradicts the design intent from [[stellata-a7d.2.2]] (closed, PR #22), which specified a single fade alpha computed from \`max(solLenPx, gcLenPx)\` and applied to **all three reference arrows: Sol, GC, and distance-vector**.

So the distance-vector either:
- never had the a7d.2.2 fade alpha wired in (peer-coverage miss at original implementation time), or
- had it wired and lost it in a later refactor (e.g. stellata-77m EventBus rewrite, stellata-akk SVG dirty-track perf pass, stellata-a7d.2.11 origin-recentre changes), or
- is hidden via a separate gate keyed to HUD-visibility instead of disc-coverage.

# Decision left open for the implementer

The user noted he isn't sure what the *correct* policy for the distance-vector arrow should be — it's a longer arrow than Sol/GC chevrons by design, so it can reasonably outlast them. Options to evaluate as part of the fix:

- **A. Match a7d.2.2's stated design**: distance-vector fades with Sol/GC on the same \`coverage\` from \`max(solLenPx, gcLenPx)\`. Pro: matches intent. Con: distance-vector can be much longer than the chevrons; fading it on chevron coverage may hide a still-readable arrow.
- **B. Per-arrow coverage**: each arrow uses its own \`projAlong / lenPx\` against the disc radius. Distance-vector fades only when the disc would actually start covering *it*.
- **C. Keep current asymmetry, but document it**: distance-vector visibility tied to HUD toggle, Sol/GC visibility tied to fade. Probably the least defensible — user is currently surprised by it.

Pick one, justify it in the PR body, and update [[stellata-a7d.2.2]]'s closure rationale prose if the chosen policy contradicts the original a7d.2.2 description (per [[stellata-rename-and-stale-prose-sweep]]).

# Files

- \`src/client/galactic-overlay.ts\` — Sol/GC arrow rendering and fade computation
- \`src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts\` — distance-vector chevron rendering; check whether the a7d.2.2 fade alpha reaches this overlay
- \`src/client/arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts\` — \`debug.arrows()\` HUD; useful for visual diagnosis of which alpha each arrow is computing at toggle-on

# Repro

1. Focus a nearby star (e.g. Sirius), zoom until its disc is visibly large at the focus-ring rim.
2. Symptom 1: toggle HUD off, toggle HUD on with keyboard shortcut — observe Sol/GC arrows flash briefly before fading out.
3. Symptom 2: with HUD enabled, observe distance-vector hidden; toggle HUD off — distance-vector now visible regardless of how close-zoomed you are.

# Acceptance

- Toggling HUD on while close-zoomed produces no flash on Sol or GC arrows — the fade alpha is evaluated synchronously at toggle-on (or the arrows start hidden and animate in only if alpha > 0).
- Distance-vector arrow visibility policy is consistent and documented — whichever of (A/B/C) is chosen, the behaviour is stable across HUD toggle, across close-zoom range, and across navigate↔observe transitions.
- \`debug.arrows()\` HUD reflects the chosen policy: any per-arrow alpha shown there matches what's rendered.
- [[stellata-a7d.2.2]] prose is updated if the chosen distance-vector policy diverges from "all three arrows share fade alpha".

# Test plan

Manual smoke (per [[alex-stellata-smoke-tests]]):
- Sirius close-zoom, HUD toggle off/on, no flash. With HUD on, also verify the distance-vector behaves per the chosen policy.
- Repeat with Vega + observer mode entry/exit to confirm no observe-mode regression.
- Open debug.arrows() and confirm alpha values match rendered state in both navigate and observe.

Vitest: if any pure helper is extracted from the fade-alpha computation (per [[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]]), unit-test it for the toggle-on initial-state edge case.
close_reason: merged via PR #70 (per-arrow fade alpha — HUD toggle-on flash fix + distance-vector policy); retrospective-review fallout in PR #71

title: Smooth focus-park behavior + generic mechanism, applied to stars
description:
# Problem

`focusStar()` at `src/client/stellata.ts:2169-2176` teleports the camera to `minDistForStar(idx)` unconditionally. Result:

- When already close to a star: jarring snap.
- When far away: no sense of 3D travel.

This is the user-visible UX fix for the parent epic `stellata-r9q`.

# Spec (per Alex's 2026-05-11 walk-through)

## New park distance formula

\`\`\`
parkDist = max(AU_PC + R_pc, dMinFloor [, extraFloor])
\`\`\`

- `AU_PC` imported from the shared astronomy-constants file (Child 1).
- `R_pc` for stars = `Reff = catalog.physicalRadius[idx] * R_SUN_PC * peakAmplitudeFactor(idx)`. **Use Reff** (handles variable stars correctly — Alex confirmed this is canonical, no debate).
- `dMinFloor` for stars = `distAtFillFraction(Reff, fovMinor, ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION=0.9)` — the current 90 %-fill floor exposed by `minOrbitDistForStar` at `src/client/stellata.ts:3136-3141`.
- `extraFloor` for stars = `binaryCompanionFloorPc(idx)`.

For Sol (R ≈ 0.0046 AU): parks at ~1.005 AU, just outside Earth's orbit.
For Betelgeuse (R ≈ 4.65 AU): `dMinFloor` clamps so star fills ~90 %, not 100 %.

## Branching in focusStar

Compute `parkDist`. Then:

- If `currentEyeDist <= parkDist`: **do not move the camera.** Update `controls.target` and `controls.minDistance`, broadcast focus state, but the camera stays put.
- If `currentEyeDist > parkDist`: animate position (and any needed reorient) over `CAMERA_LERP_MS = 2000` ms. Fast for very distant stars is intentional — conveys scale.

## One canonical 2 s constant

Today there are two `= 2000` literals for camera-move durations:

- `WARP_REORIENT_MS = 2000` at `src/client/stellata.ts:272`
- `AIM_T_MAX_MS = 2000` at `src/client/stellata.ts:283`

(`WARP_T_K_MS = 2000` at line 271 stays separate — it is a log-scale flight coefficient with different semantics.)

Consolidate to ONE definition that all three usages (warp reorient, aim, new focus-lerp) import. Per Alex: \"set that in one place and then alias it as you suggest so it's clear that it's a shared constant across the app.\" Pick a clear canonical name (e.g. `CAMERA_LERP_MS`) — implementation detail; the constraint is one literal, not the spelling.

## Generic-from-day-one mechanism

Per Alex: \"I want the same approach for future objects: clouds, planets, nebulae, etc. We MUST write code that is reusable.\"

Extract pure helpers in a new module (e.g. `src/client/focus-transition.ts`):

\`\`\`ts
// Pure, testable.
parkDistance(opts: { R_pc: number; fovMinorRad: number; dMinFloor: number; extraFloor?: number }): number;

// Animation primitive — small named helper that takes the target, current camera state,
// duration, and emits the lerp tween. focusStar / focusCloud / focusPlanet all call this
// with their own parkDistance(...) inputs.
focusLerpTo(target, parkDist, durationMs): void;
\`\`\`

Apply these to stars in this bead. Children stellata-r9q.3 and stellata-r9q.4 will route clouds and planets through the same primitives.

# Files

- `src/client/stellata.ts`
  - `minDistForStar()` at l. 3167-3174 — either rewrite or replace by a sibling `parkDistForStar(idx)` that delegates to the generic helper. Decide whether the existing manual-zoom floor (`minOrbitDistForStar`) stays untouched (it should — that's the user-visible 90 %-fill floor; the change is only to where the camera *automatically lands* on focus).
  - `focusStar()` at l. 2159-2186 — branch on currentEyeDist vs parkDist.
  - Constant definitions at l. 272 and l. 283.
- New: `src/client/focus-transition.ts` housing `parkDistance(...)` + focus-lerp animation primitive.
- Imports `AU_PC`, `R_SUN_PC` from astronomy-constants file (depends-on stellata-r9q.1 / Child 1).

# Open implementation questions (resolve at work-time)

- Naming for the canonical 2 s constant — `CAMERA_LERP_MS`, or keep `WARP_REORIENT_MS` as the canonical name and alias the others to it. Either is fine; the constraint is exactly one literal in the codebase.
- How the focus-lerp interacts with the warp state machine (likely fully separate: focus-lerp is the close-range nudge; warp is the log-scaled long-haul fly).
- Whether `binaryCompanionFloorPc(idx)` stays in the `max(...)` for stars (probably yes).

# Smoke (manual — per `alex-stellata-smoke-tests`, no dev server, no Playwright)

1. Park near Sol at ~0.5 AU via `?v=` URL; click-focus Sol → camera does NOT move; just the focus indicators appear.
2. Default Sol view (~5 AU); focus Sol → smooth ~2 s glide inward to ~1 AU.
3. From Sol, focus Betelgeuse → ~2 s glide; park clamps to `dMin`; Betelgeuse fills ~90 % of viewport, not 100 %.
4. From Sol, focus a faint distant star (via search) → ~2 s glide over many parsecs (intentionally fast — conveys distance).
5. Already-focused star, re-focus via click or search → no double-snap.
6. URL-restore focus from a saved view → arrives parked correctly (no animation on URL restore).
7. Existing long-haul warp behavior unaffected — `WARP_T_K_MS` and the log-scaled flight unchanged.

# Test coverage (per `stellata-test-coverage-discipline`)

- Unit tests for `parkDistance(...)` covering: Sol (~1.005 AU result, pinned with `toBe` not `toBeLessThanOrEqual`), a giant (dMin clamps), a star with binary companion floor, a variable (Reff > R).
- Read-back assertion that on focus-when-already-inside-park, camera position is unchanged (within float epsilon).
- Test that the canonical 2 s constant is exactly one literal in the codebase.

# Depends on

stellata-r9q.1 / Child 1 (constants consolidation).

# Blocks

stellata-r9q.3 / Child 3 (cloud migration) and stellata-r9q.4 / Child 4 (planet migration, deferred).

title: Smooth focus-park UX, generic across focusable objects
description:
# Problem

Focusing a star currently teleports the camera to `minDistForStar(idx)` (`src/client/stellata.ts:2169-2176`). When the camera is already close, this snaps backwards/forwards jarringly. When far away, the user loses the sense of moving *through* 3D space.

# Goal

Replace the teleport-on-focus snap with a lerp-or-noop, using a **generic mechanism** that applies uniformly to stars (now), molecular clouds, planets, and future focusable types (nebulae, etc.). Reusable from day one — don't reinvent the wheel for every object type.

# Behavior (per Alex's spec)

1. **New park distance formula:** `parkDist = max(1 AU + R_star, dMin)` where `dMin` is the existing 90 %-fill manual-zoom floor (`minOrbitDistForStar` = `distAtFillFraction(R, fov, ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION=0.9)`).
   - For Sol (R ≈ 0.0046 AU): parks at ~1.005 AU, just outside Earth's orbit.
   - For giants (e.g. Betelgeuse, R ≈ 4.65 AU): `dMin` clamps the formula so the star doesn't fill the viewport.
2. **On focus, branch on current eye-to-object distance:**
   - If `eyeDist <= parkDist`: leave camera in place. Update `controls.target` and `minDistance` but don't move the camera.
   - If `eyeDist > parkDist`: animate camera to the park position over **2 s**. Fast for very distant stars is intentional — it conveys the scale of interstellar distances.
3. **2 s constant must be the existing `WARP_REORIENT_MS` / `AIM_T_MAX_MS = 2000`** consolidated to one canonical name — no new `= 2000` literals. (`WARP_T_K_MS = 2000` stays separate; it's a log-scale flight coefficient, not a camera-move duration.)
4. **`Reff = R * peakAmplitudeFactor(idx)`** is the canonical radius for the star formula (handles variable stars correctly).

# Why P1

Per `stellata-prioritization-framework`: "Visible UX latency / jank that hits hot user paths." Star focus is the central interaction; the snap affects every user.

# Structure

- Child 1 — Consolidate astronomical constants into a single source file (blocks Child 2).
- Child 2 — Smooth focus-park + generic mechanism + apply to stars (the user-visible UX fix; blocks Children 3 & 4).
- Child 3 — Apply to molecular cloud focus.
- Child 4 — Apply to planet focus (filed under `stellata-3re`, deferred until planets become focusable; related-link back here).

Plus a separate sweep bead under `stellata-9mm` covering codebase-wide consolidation of astronomical constants (shaders, build scripts, tests), depending on Child 1.

title: Heliopause label visible on first-load (camera inside heliopause shell)
description:
# Symptom

On a bare-URL first load (no `?v=` fragment), the "Heliopause" SVG label is visible at the top of the screen even though the camera is parked inside the heliopause shell. From inside the bubble there is no projected silhouette to anchor against, so the label should not be shown.

# Why this happens

- First-load parks the camera at 5 AU from Sol (`src/client/first-load.ts` — `PARK_DIST_PC = 5 * AU_PC`).
- The heliopause shell extends to ~115 AU equatorial and ~122 AU upwind / 200 AU downwind (`src/client/heliopause.ts:46-49`).
- Camera at 5 AU is therefore deep inside the shell on first load.

The label-hide logic in `createHeliopauseLabel` (`src/client/heliopause.ts:274-283`) checks each sample and bails with `setVisible(false)` when any sample sits behind the near plane (the "camera straddles the egg" regime). That check fires correctly when inside the bubble — *but the resulting hide is a no-op on the very first frame*:

```ts
let visible = false;                   // initial state
...
const setVisible = (on: boolean): void => {
  if (on === visible) return;          // ← early-out
  text.style.display = on ? '' : 'none';
  ...
};
setVisible(false);                     // ← no-op, never writes display:none
```

The SVG element `<text id="heliopause-label">` in `src/client/index.html:67` has no inline `display: none` and no explicit `x`/`y`, so it renders at its SVG default position (x=0, y=0 with `text-anchor="start" dominant-baseline="hanging"`) until a real visibility transition writes `display: none`. On a session where the camera never crosses the bail threshold (i.e. starts and stays inside the egg), that transition never happens.

# Acceptance criteria

- Bare URL `/` load with no `?v=` fragment: "Heliopause" label is NOT visible at any point on first paint or on subsequent frames while the camera remains inside the shell.
- After warping to a star outside the shell and back to Sol focus, the label appears anchored to the silhouette as designed (no regression on the existing in-flight behaviour).
- Warping back inside the shell hides the label again.

# Suggested fix

Smallest change: hide the label in markup, either by adding `style="display: none"` to the `<text id="heliopause-label">` element in `index.html:67`, OR by writing `text.style.display = 'none'` unconditionally before the initial `setVisible(false)` call in `heliopause.ts:244`.

Either eliminates the "visible-by-default until first transition" gap. The same shape (sentinel-init that doesn't force the first write to land) is called out in the `stellata-consistency-at-the-seam` memory under rule 3 (sentinel-init for dirty-track) — same class of bug.

# Smoke step

- Hard-reload `/` with no `?v=` fragment. Confirm no "Heliopause" text anywhere.
- Focus Sol → ensure a planet is in view (label should now appear, anchored to the silhouette bottom-right).
- Zoom back inside the heliopause via free fly / `minDistance` relax → label hides again.
close_reason: merged via PR #64 (Fix heliopause label visible on first-load)

title: Multiple-star matching: BinaryOrbitField runtime — live per-frame orbital evolution (headline feature)
description:
Bead 7/7 of the stellata-dch decomposition (see parent for full spec).

# Goal

The headline feature. Per-frame walk of stars with `orbitIdx != NO_ORBIT`, evaluate Kepler offset against `Stellata.getT()`, write into the GPU star position buffer via `bufferSubData`. Algol's 2.87-day cycle, α Cen's 79.9-yr orbit, Castor Aa1↔Aa2's ~9-day cycle visibly orbit each other under warp.

# Scope

- `src/client/binary-orbit.ts` (NEW):
  - `BinaryOrbitField` class — analogue of `PlanetBodyField` (`src/client/planet-body-field.ts` is the reference architecture). Constructor takes the catalog's orbital-elements typed array + the list of star indices with `orbitIdx != NO_ORBIT`.
  - Per-frame `update(t)`: walk index list, call `evaluateBinaryOffset` from dch.9's pure module, compute final xyz = primary_base_xyz + offset (using FLAG_BINARY_SECONDARY to pick the right side via mass ratio), write perturbed (x,y,z) into the GPU star position buffer via `bufferSubData` (slice update — only the binary subset, ~6000 floats × 3)
  - Lifecycle: `dispose()` clears any cached buffers (per `stellata-consistency-at-the-seam` lifecycle pairing rule)
  - Reuses `Stellata.getT()` + `tToJDE` (`src/client/time.ts`) per time-consistency rule
- `src/client/stellata.ts`:
  - Construct `BinaryOrbitField` once at catalog load
  - Register `update()` in the per-frame loop before the GPU draw
  - Dispose on scene teardown (paired with `bus.on`-style lifecycle rules)

# Final stale-prose sweep

Before commit: grep entire repo for "static binary positions" / "J2000-only" / "build-time computation" prose introduced by earlier beads (dch.5–dch.8 documented their pieces against an in-progress story); update to reflect the now-live runtime. Per `stellata-rename-and-stale-prose-sweep`.

# Smoke

- `npm test` green (no new unit tests in this bead; logic is already covered by dch.9; this bead is glue + GPU plumbing)
- Manual (user-driven, no dev server by Claude):
  1. Focus Algol Aa1 → advance `t` under warp through ~3 days → Aa1 and Aa2 visibly orbit each other
  2. Focus α Cen A → advance `t` through several decades → A and B relative position drifts along published orbit
  3. Castor Aa1/Aa2 cycles through ~9 days at warp
  4. `debug.panel()` perf-hud shows `BinaryOrbitField.update` < 1 ms/frame at default mag cutoffs
  5. Static (non-orbital) stars unaffected — chart-mode wings glyph still one-per-system; non-binary star positions unchanged
- Verify `bufferSubData` writes do not interfere with the static star instanced-quad pass

# Doc updates

- `docs/solar-system.md` cross-ref to binary orbital math (shared Kepler pattern between ephemeris.ts and binary-orbit-pure.ts)
- Either new `docs/binary-orbits.md` or fold into `docs/architecture.md` / `docs/build-and-data.md` — depending on how large the BinaryOrbitField section grows
- `CLAUDE.md` — `binary-orbit.ts` line in the module roster under `src/client/`
- Final pass: re-read SCIENCE.md + all `docs/*.md` touched in earlier beads; correct any "static-only" / "build-time-only" prose
notes:
# Design contracts (filed at dch.8 close)

Two implementation invariants the runtime must enforce so the orbital elements stored in v5's elements section produce correct on-screen motion, plus one UX consideration triggered by dch.10's headline feature.

## Sky-plane → ICRS projection

ORB6's i, ω, Ω are sky-plane angles (i=0 face-on relative to the line of sight; Ω is the position angle of the ascending node, east of north). The Kepler eval produces (north, east) separation on the sky in arcsec. The runtime must:

1. Recover the system's (RA, Dec) from the stored xyz of either component (orbital motion << position, so either works).
2. Build the local tangent-plane basis at (α, δ):
   - east_hat = (-sin α, cos α, 0)
   - north_hat = (-sin δ cos α, -sin δ sin α, cos δ)
3. Convert (north_arcsec, east_arcsec) at distance d into a parsec Δxyz: `Δ = (n·north_hat + e·east_hat) · ARCSEC2RAD · d`.

`binary-orbit-pure.ts` (dch.9) is the natural home for this helper — same shape as `sky_offset_to_icrs_xyz` in build-binaries.py, but TypeScript and vitest-covered.

## ΔR(t) baseline, not R(t)

The stored xyz are the J2000 snapshot of each component's actual ICRS position. A sits at `X_com − q·R(J2000)`; B at `X_com + (1−q)·R(J2000)`. For a Kepler eval at time t, the per-frame offset is:

  ΔR(t) = R(t) − R(J2000)
  A_offset(t) = −q · ΔR(t)
  B_offset(t) = +(1−q) · ΔR(t)

Apply A_offset / B_offset to the stored xyz. Both R(t) and R(J2000) come from the same Kepler call — cache the per-orbit J2000 baseline at attach time so each frame is a single solve, not two.

## Why these contracts live here, not in dch.8

The binary's orbital-elements section stores runtime-ready elements but does NOT store the J2000 baseline or the (RA, Dec). Both are reconstructed at runtime from xyz. If dch.10 landed without these contracts, all 896 binaries would orbit on the wrong plane and drift unbounded from their published J2000 positions.

## Clock visibility (UX)

dch.10 makes wall-clock `t` (`Stellata.getT()`) load-bearing for any focused binary, not just Sol's planets. The current `time-readout.ts` gate ("planet-host focus OR warp active") was scoped to the solar-system layer per stellata-3re.11. With dch.10 evolving 896 binary systems against the same `t`, the readout needs to be visible whenever orbital motion is meaningfully on-screen.

Two options:

1. **Extend the gate.** Add a "any focused star has orbitIdx != NO_ORBIT" predicate to the existing visibility condition. Pros: minimal change, preserves the chrome-when-needed principle. Cons: another conditional that could miss cases.
2. **Always show the clock.** Now that `t` matters across multiple domains (solar system, binary orbits, future deep-space probes), the gating may be more confusion than help. Pros: simpler mental model, future-proofs further `t`-driven layers. Cons: chrome present even in pure-stellar exploration.

User leans toward (2). Worth a design decision at dch.10 implementation time; either way, the readout shouldn't disappear during binary-focused viewing.

If (2), the change is small and lands inside dch.10 — drop the gating predicate in `time-readout.ts`. If (1), it's still a one-liner in the same file.

title: Multiple-star matching: binary-orbit-pure.ts Kepler solver + projection math + tests
description:
Bead 6/7 of the stellata-dch decomposition (see parent for full spec).

# Goal

Pure functions for the Kepler-solve + sky-plane projection math. Runs in vitest; validated against literature fixtures. **No runtime hookup** at this bead — runtime integration lands in dch.10.

**Parallel to dch.7 and dch.8** — no dependency on the v5 format bump; can be picked up in any order.

# Scope

- `scripts/binary-orbit-pure.ts` (NEW):
  - `solveKepler(M, e): number` — Kepler's equation iteration (Newton's method or Markley 1995). Mirror the existing pattern in `src/client/ephemeris.ts`; consolidate into a shared utility if natural, otherwise sibling-symmetric implementations with the same convergence test pinned per `stellata-consistency-at-the-seam`.
  - `evaluateBinaryOffset(elements, t, isSecondary): { x, y, z }` — solve for eccentric anomaly E at time t, project (ρ, θ) on sky-plane, return xyz offset from system barycenter scaled to system distance in pc. Pure; no global state.
- `scripts/binary-orbit-pure.test.ts` (NEW):
  - Kepler convergence for e ∈ {0, 0.3, 0.9}: result satisfies `M = E - e·sin(E)` within 1e-9
  - Periodicity: `|offset(t) - offset(t + P)| < 1e-6` at multiple sampled t
  - Barycenter consistency: `primary_offset + secondary_offset ≈ 0` (within 1e-6) at multiple sampled t (note: this uses the mass-ratio q and signs along the orbit)
  - α Cen J2000 fixture: computed (ρ, θ) at J2000 matches Wiegert+1997 measurement within published uncertainties
  - Algol's 2.87-day period: offset magnitude cycles through full range within one P (sample at 10 phases, assert max-min spans the expected scale)

# Smoke

- `npm test` green; fixture tests pass
- No runtime / shader / build changes — pure-math module only
close_reason: Pure Kepler + Thiele-Innes math landed in src/client/binary-orbit-pure.ts; Newton solver extracted to shared src/client/kepler-solver.ts and consumed by ephemeris.ts. 675 tests pass.

title: Multiple-star matching: emit orbital elements into v5 binary
description:
Bead 5/7 of the stellata-dch decomposition (see parent for full spec).

# Goal

Populate the orbital-elements table introduced in dch.7. build-binaries.py emits per-system orbital elements; build-catalog.ts writes them into catalog.bin; catalog-loader.ts exposes them as a typed-array view. **Still no runtime evolution** — that lands in dch.10.

# Scope

- `scripts/build-binaries.py`:
  - For Regime 2+3 entries (rows already getting orbital data from ORB6 or spectroscopic derivation in dch.5), emit additional element columns in `multiples.tsv`: P (days), T (JD), e, a (AU), q (mass ratio m_B/(m_A+m_B)), i (rad), ω (rad), Ω (rad), distance (pc)
  - Mark primary as `orbit_role=primary`, secondary as `orbit_role=secondary` so build-catalog.ts knows which records get `FLAG_BINARY_SECONDARY`
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts`:
  - Parse element columns from `multiples.tsv`
  - Build per-system orbital-element table: 9 × float32 = 36 bytes per system entry; ~3000 systems total → ~108 KB
  - Write orbital-elements section to catalog.bin after name table (replace the empty section header from dch.7)
  - Per-record `orbitIdx` points to system entry; secondary records OR `FLAG_BINARY_SECONDARY` into flags byte
- `src/client/catalog-loader.ts`:
  - Parse orbital-elements section into Float32Array view
  - Expose `orbitalElements: Float32Array` + per-record `orbitIdx` mapping in the Catalog interface
- `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts` (new cases):
  - Element-table round-trip (write + read) preserves float32 values within epsilon
  - Primary + secondary records share the same `orbitIdx`; only secondary has FLAG_BINARY_SECONDARY set

# Smoke

- `npx tsx scripts/verify-catalog.ts` dumps orbital-elements section: α Cen has P≈79.91 yr, e≈0.5179, a≈17.57 AU (per Wiegert+1997); Algol shows P≈2.87 d
- `npm test` green
- Renderer in dev server unchanged (loader reads new section but doesn't act on it yet)

# Doc updates

- `docs/build-and-data.md` § "Orbital-elements section" — table schema (P, T, e, a, q, i, ω, Ω, distance), entry size, count, units
