- npm run typecheck clean (no .md changes affect TS, but defensively verify)
- npm test green
- One reviewer reads each touched doc cold to verify the prose still makes sense

# Commit

Single commit, PR labelled skip-version-bump (docs-only).

title: Decide HiPS sampling strategy for far-field cumulative glow
description:
Design-doc gate for stellata-36y. Output: written decisions (in notes/design fields of this bead) covering:

1. **Source HiPS map.** Default candidate: Gaia DR3 colour HiPS at CDS (alasky.u-strasbg.fr) — full-sky RGB from Gaia G/BP/RP. Alternatives to evaluate: DSS2 colour (older, well-sampled, includes nebulosity), 2MASS (NIR penetrates dust better), Gaia DR3 stellar density HiPS (counts not brightness).

2. **HEALPix order for the reduction.** Trade-off pixel scale vs tile count:
   - Order 5 ≈ 13.7 arcmin pixel, 12 288 tiles
   - Order 6 ≈ 6.9 arcmin pixel, 49 152 tiles
   - Order 7 ≈ 3.4 arcmin pixel, 196 608 tiles
   Pick the lowest order that gives a convincingly structured glow; smaller artifact, faster GPU sampling.

3. **Reduction artifact format.** Per-tile mean RGB → 2D HEALPix-projected texture, or per-tile scalar intensity, or both. Must subtract AT-HYG-resolved stars' flux contribution to avoid double-counting (each AT-HYG star already renders separately).

4. **Blending / handoff.** Always-on background glow vs magnitude-gated (user's magnitude slider could control: glow = everything fainter than the slider). How it composites with the volumetric Milky Way and with dust extinction.

5. **Render integration.** Three options: skybox sphere at far clip (cheapest, no parallax), sampled inside the MW raymarch (parallax-correct, integrates with dust), or both (skybox for sky-band-only contribution + MW raymarch for in-galaxy contribution).

# On completion

- Decisions live in this bead's notes/design.
- File implementation children under stellata-36y at P3:
  - Data pipeline child: one-shot HiPS reduction script + committed artifact under data/ per frozen-external-data.
  - Runtime child: shader/material integration.

# Out of scope for this bead

No code. Pure design output.

title: Far-field statistical augmentation from CDS / Gaia HiPS
description:
Stellata's near-field pipeline (AT-HYG + GCVS + Edenhofer dust) is per-object resolved. The far-field — the cumulative glow of the ~1.8B Gaia DR3 stars below AT-HYG's mag cutoff — is currently missing or approximated only by the analytic Milky Way disc/bulge.

# Goal

Add a statistical layer that represents the sky's true stellar density beyond the AT-HYG floor, sourced from CDS/Gaia HiPS data (or equivalent VizieR-hosted aggregate catalog), without competing with the per-star pipeline.

# Why now

Prompted 2026-05-12 by Alex's first encounter with the Gaia archive's Aladin HiPS visualisation — the visible stellar density is dramatically richer than AT-HYG suggests. The detail-gradient mission asks for highest-fidelity-near, statistical-far; far-field is currently the weakest link.

# CDS / VizieR ecosystem (orientation)

- **VizieR** — published catalog service. Already upstream of Stellata (AT-HYG, GCVS).
- **SIMBAD** — per-object database; cross-IDs, classifications, references.
- **Aladin** — interactive sky atlas + HiPS (Hierarchical Progressive Surveys) tiled all-sky maps including Gaia DR3 colour.
- **CDS portal** — umbrella access.

# In scope

- Cumulative glow layer below the AT-HYG catalog floor (sky-brightness reconstruction from HiPS or binned Gaia counts).
- Optional: refine the volumetric Milky Way disc/bulge profile against Gaia surface-brightness observations.

# Out of scope (file separately if/when desired)

- Direct Gaia DR3 NSS adoption (real binary orbits for AT-HYG-resolved stars).
- Direct Gaia DR3 Apsis adoption (Teff/log g replacing spectral-string parsing).
- Direct Gaia DR3 Variability adoption (supplement to GCVS).
- Gaia DR4 refresh (filed as separate deferred bead).

# Child structure

First child is the research / design-doc gate at P2. Implementation children (data pipeline, runtime layer) get filed after the research bead lands its decisions, at P3 per stellata-prioritization-framework.

# Acceptance

- All children closed and merged.
- Visible far-field glow at chart-mode and navigate modes.
- No live network fetch at build (frozen-external-data compliance).
- Documented in docs/milky-way.md or a new docs/far-field.md.

title: AT-HYG ↔ Gaia DR3 cross-match dedupe pass for native duplicate rows
description:
# Why

After stellata-dch.18 lands the Gaia DR3 WDS optical filter, the
remaining residual source of spurious stars is AT-HYG-native: rows
in `athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` that map to the same physical Gaia
source but appear as separate entries (different HIP / HD / catalog
origin, near-identical xyz).

stellata-dch.11 found exactly one such pathology at xyz=(-8.7310,
1.6110, 5.4470) — caught by `inferBinaries`'s sub-threshold safety
net. We don't know the catalog-wide rate; it could be 1, could be
100. A systematic cross-match is the only way to know — and to
remove any that exist.

The WDS optical filter doesn't help here because these duplicates
aren't WDS pairs — they're single rows that AT-HYG happens to
contain twice.

# Scope

Adds a new dedupe pass to the build pipeline (probably a
`scripts/dedupe-athyg.py` script invoked before `build-catalog.ts`).

1. Decide on Gaia DR3 sourcing — either:
   a. Pre-built local Gaia subset bounded by AT-HYG's distance and
      mag range (~30M sources, doable as a one-shot ADQL download +
      data/ commit), OR
   b. ADQL upload of all AT-HYG positions + JOIN against gaia_source
      (similar to dch.18's WDS upload procedure, but for the full
      catalog — uses a single user table).
2. Cross-match: for each AT-HYG row, find the nearest Gaia DR3 source
   within ~1-2″ tolerance with parallax overlap (3σ).
3. Group AT-HYG rows that share a Gaia DR3 source. For each group of
   size > 1: log all members, drop the fainter rows, keep the brightest
   (preferring HIP'd over un-HIP'd, then absmag tiebreak).
4. Emit `data/athyg_duplicates.tsv` (or similar) listing every dropped
   row + the kept master, for inspection.
5. `build-catalog.ts` reads the dedupe TSV and skips the listed rows.

# Acceptance

- Documented procedure for refreshing the Gaia-side data (similar to
  dch.18's manual upload step).
- Dedupe pass runs as part of `npm run build:catalog` (or as a
  prerequisite).
- Log line gives final count of AT-HYG rows dropped.
- All famous bright stars survive (Sirius A, α Cen A/B, Vega, Polaris,
  Betelgeuse, Rigel, Antares — spot-check at minimum).
- stellata-dch.11's xyz=(-8.7310, 1.6110, 5.4470) instance is one of
  the dropped duplicates (validates the pass against a known case).

# Why P2 (not P1)

Empirically the rate appears low — dch.11 surfaced one instance
across 313k rows, and the inferBinaries safety net catches the
collapsed case. The pipeline isn't broken; it has a residual gap.
Bump to P1 if post-dch.18 inspection reveals a non-trivial population
of catalogue-native duplicates that aren't caught by the safety net.

# Why this doesn't block dch.10

dch.10's runtime evolution operates on the orbital-elements section
of the binary, which only carries WDS-derived orbits. AT-HYG-native
duplicates that are NOT part of a WDS pair carry no orbital elements
(orbitIdx=NO_ORBIT) and won't move at runtime. The catalog still
contains spurious static rows, but they don't compound under live
orbital evolution.

# Related

- stellata-dch.11 — single-instance investigation that triggered this
  bead.
- stellata-dch.18 — Gaia upload (handles WDS-derived spurious; this
  bead handles AT-HYG-native spurious).
close_reason: folded into Phase 2 Stage 2 source-ID resolution chain (stellata-dch.28). AT-HYG-Gaia dedupe now an output of the resolution chain rather than a separate pass.

title: Multiple-star matching: orbit-derived eclipse photometry for EA/EB/EW eclipsing binaries
description:
dch.14 will suppress GCVS intrinsic pulsation for EA/EB/EW eclipsing binaries, on the basis that dch.10's orbital evolution will produce the photometric dip naturally. But dch.10's scoped surface (BinaryOrbitField) only evolves *positions* — it doesn't subtract luminosity during occultation. Without this bead, eclipsing-binary classes would render with no brightness modulation at all (pulsation suppressed by dch.14, eclipses not generated by dch.10) — losing exactly the famous Algol-style behavior that motivated suppressing the GCVS pulsation in the first place.

# What an eclipse looks like physically

When B passes in front of A (line-of-sight crosses each disc's projected outline):
- Geometric occultation: angular area of A covered by B → fractional luminosity removed.
- Limb darkening modifies the dip shape; skip for v1 (near-circular dip approximation reads correctly).
- Total eclipses → flat-bottomed dip (B's disc fully inside A's projected outline).
- Secondary eclipse (A in front of B) → smaller dip from B's lower luminosity.

# Implementation sketch

Per-frame, for each (A, B) pair whose orbit fits the eclipsing criterion (i close to 90°, near-equal physical radii, current orbital phase in the eclipse window):
- Compute B's center xy in A's local sky plane from R(t) (line-of-sight component → z; in-plane → xy).
- Compute B's angular radius and A's angular radius at the system distance.
- If |xy| < r_A + r_B, compute geometric overlap (analytical for two circles — closed-form, no integration).
- Subtract `(overlap_area / (π·r_A²)) * L_A` from A's effective luminosity.
- Adjust rendered photometric output downward by the same fraction.

# Touch points

- New `src/client/binary-eclipse-pure.ts` — circle-circle overlap helper (vitest-covered, analytical).
- BinaryOrbitField (dch.10) extended with `getEclipseAttenuation(starIdx, t): number` consumed by the renderer's brightness path.
- The renderer multiplies the per-star apparent-magnitude term by the attenuation factor.
- dch.16 (composite-spectrum split) must land first so B's physical radius is correct — otherwise the eclipse depth scales off the wrong disc area.

# Acceptance

- Algol shows a clear 2.87-day dip cycle when zoomed in enough to time it (warp speedup helps).
- β Lyrae (EB type) shows a longer-period dip with smoother shoulders.
- Non-eclipsing binaries (i far from 90°) show no dip — guard against false positives.
- α Cen still looks photometrically constant.
- The dip depth matches V-band amplitude from GCVS within ~30% (we can't model limb darkening + temperature contrast precisely; a rough match is the bar).

# Couples with

- stellata-dch.14 (pulsation suppression for EA/EB/EW): without dch.17, dch.14 leaves eclipsing stars flat. dch.17 fills in the dip; dch.14 stops double-counting.
- stellata-dch.16 (composite spectrum split): B's radius needs to be physically correct for eclipse-depth scaling.
- stellata-dch.10 (BinaryOrbitField runtime): supplies R(t) per frame; this bead extends BinaryOrbitField with the brightness pass.
close_reason: superseded by stellata-dch.41 (Phase 5 — Eclipse photometry for EA/EB/EW from orbital elements). Same scope but rephased to land AFTER Phase 2 (orbital elements) + Phase 5 (mass-ratio q) so the orbital + mass inputs are correct before photometry computation. --force used because dch.17 was blocked-by dch.10; the successor bead dch.41 carries the equivalent dep on dch.10's BinaryOrbitField runtime.

title: Multiple-star matching: split combined WDS spectral types per component
description:
build-binaries.py inherits the WDS combined-spectrum string verbatim onto SYN-NNN secondary records. WDS publishes the *system* spectral type (e.g. `A1Vm+DA2` for Sirius) which parseSpectral then truncates at the leading half — Sirius B's record carries `A1Vm+DA2`, parseSpectral returns the A1Vm half, and B is treated as A-class MS instead of a white dwarf.

# Visible impact

For every named WD secondary in WDS:
- Renderer assigns A's spectral class to B → wrong color, wrong T_eff.
- Stefan-Boltzmann radius uses A's class → wrong physicalRadius. Sirius B clamps to 0.08 R☉ (lower bound); real WD radius is ~0.013 R☉ — 6× too large.
- parseSpectral.isWhiteDwarf returns false on B → dch.15's WD mass branch never fires on Sirius B et al.

Affected pairs: Sirius B, Procyon B, 40 Eri B (WD secondaries); plus M-dwarf secondaries of hotter primaries.

# Fix

Split the WDS spectral field on `+` (most common) and `/` (rarer). When the field has exactly two halves, assign half[0] to A and half[1] to B; when ambiguous or unsplittable, fall back to current behaviour (whole string carried, log a debug warning so the affected pairs surface).

Touch points:
- scripts/build-binaries.py — in process_pair, when constructing b_spect for the SYN path, split pair.spectral on '+' or '/' and pick the trailing component. For composite primary specs (e.g. `O5III+OB`) the split is by sequence: A gets the first token, B the second.
- scripts/catalog-pure.ts — optional helper `splitCompositeSpectral(s: string): { a: string; b: string }` if the parsing complexity warrants a pure helper.

# Acceptance

- Sirius B parses as DA2 → isWhiteDwarf=true → renders as a tiny ~0.013 R☉ disc.
- Procyon B and 40 Eri B parse as WDs.
- M-dwarf secondaries of hot primaries inherit M class correctly.
- No regression on MS+MS pairs where both halves give the same letter (most α Cen, Castor entries).
- Add a vitest covering `A1Vm+DA2` → A, B halves; `K0V+DA4+M4V` → split on first `+` only or smarter? — decision in implementation.

# Couples with

- stellata-dch.15 (mass-ratio q): the WD mass branch needs this fix to fire at all on composite-spectrum WD secondaries. Together they form the full Sirius B fix.
close_reason: superseded by stellata-dch.36 (Phase 3 — parseSpectral splits composite WDS types per component). Scope is unchanged; execution gated on Phase 3 v6 binary format.

title: Multiple-star matching: spectral-class-aware mass-ratio q (WD/giant/subgiant fallbacks)
description:
build-binaries.py currently estimates q = m_B / (m_A + m_B) from each component's absmag via the main-sequence M-L relation M ∝ L^(1/3.5) (Cox 2000 §15.2). The relation is accurate to within ~20% on the bulk of the OBAFGKM main sequence but fails badly for non-MS components.

# Worked example — Sirius B

- M_V = 11.36 (real, WD luminosity)
- L/L☉ = 10^((4.74-11.36)/2.5) ≈ 0.0023
- MS-predicted M = L^(1/3.5) ≈ 0.18 M☉
- True mass (from orbit) ≈ 1.02 M☉
- q estimate at v5: 0.069 vs true ≈ 0.33 — factor of ~5 wrong

# Why MS M-L fails for WDs

White dwarfs aren't H-burning. Their luminosity is residual thermal cooling, decoupled from mass. The degenerate mass-radius relation R ∝ M^(-1/3) also inverts MS scaling: heavier WDs are *smaller*. The MS M-L is measuring the wrong physics entirely. Subgiants and giants are over-luminous for their mass (shell-burning), so the M-L over-estimates them — smaller error (≤2×) but still visualisation-relevant.

# Visual consequence

q controls the split of orbital motion between A and B about the system center of mass. With q wildly off, Sirius A barely wobbles and Sirius B swings far wider than reality. The relative orbit (A−B) is unaffected — P, e, a, i, ω, Ω all come from ORB6 — so the "they orbit each other on a 50-year ellipse" story stays right. The distortion only shows up zoomed in on one component watching it wobble against background.

dch.10's render will reveal whether this is visible. File first, discard if invisible.

# Proposed fix

1. Spectral-class-aware MS mass table — Cox (2000) §15.2 per-(spectClass, subclass) MS mass; lookup via parseSpectral().
2. WD branch — canonical 0.6 M☉ (mean of observed WD mass distribution) when parseSpectral returns isWhiteDwarf=true.
3. Giant/subgiant branch — luminosity-class-aware lookup keyed on (spectClass, lumClass).
4. Fold the new logic into a `componentMass(absmag, spectInfo): number` helper in catalog-pure.ts.

parseSpectral already returns spectClass + lumClass + isWhiteDwarf — the helper exists; build-binaries.py just needs to consult it instead of using absmag alone.

# Coupled concern — composite spectral inheritance

Currently SYN-NNN secondaries inherit the WDS combined-spectrum string (e.g. 'A1Vm+DA2' for Sirius B). parseSpectral takes the leading half ('A1Vm') and reports A-class MS. The WD branch in this bead only fires if the spect string starts with 'D' — so this bead either needs to come with a composite-split helper, or be co-filed with a separate 'split combined spectra per component' bead. See sibling follow-up.

# Acceptance

- Sirius B q ≈ 0.33 (currently 0.07).
- Procyon B q ≈ 0.4 (currently underestimated; Procyon B is a WD).
- 40 Eridani B q sensible.
- α Cen q stays at ~0.45 (MS+MS, no regression).
- SCIENCE.md 'Mass-ratio q' paragraph updated to describe the new fallback chain.

# Discard criterion

If dch.10's render lands and the q distortion is not visually perceptible at any zoom level we afford, close as 'no visible impact'. Stellata is ground-truthed against physical reality but doesn't need to model effects below the perceptual threshold.
close_reason: superseded by stellata-dch.40 (Phase 5 — Spectral-class-aware mass-ratio q with WD/giant/subgiant fallbacks). Scope is unchanged but execution is gated on Phase 2 stages 5-7 landing first.

title: Clarify TaskCreate vs beads split in CLAUDE.md
description:
Current CLAUDE.md rule ("Use bd for ALL task tracking — do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown TODO lists") overshoots: it conflates "ephemeral tracking is wasteful when work outlives the session" (true) with "ephemeral tracking is bad" (not true). TaskCreate has a legitimate job — surfacing a live ticking checklist for multi-step work in this session — that beads doesn't do well. A bead per micro-step is noise in bd ready and noise in the JSONL.

Update the Beads/Rules section in CLAUDE.md to:

- Keep beads as the canonical tracker for anything outliving the session (BEFORE-writing-code rule preserved).
- Permit TaskCreate / TodoWrite for in-session decomposition of an in-flight bead's sub-steps (too granular to file individually, no value next session).
- Tie-break "when in doubt, file a bead" — and include a promote-to-bead escape hatch when a sub-step turns out to be follow-up work.
- Keep "never use markdown TODO files".

No bd remember mirror — would just double up in session context.

Acceptance: CLAUDE.md Beads/Rules block reflects the split; PR labelled skip-version-bump (docs-only).
notes:
Pivoted from CLAUDE.md edit to bd remember key 'task-tracking-policy'.

The original approach (PR #62, now closed) placed the new wording inside CLAUDE.md's auto-managed <!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION --> block — flimsy, easily clobbered by a future bd integration regenerate. The memory survives.

See bd memories task-tracking-policy for the policy itself.

title: Suppress intrinsic pulsation for GCVS eclipsing-binary classes (EA/EB/EW); let dch.10 orbit produce the photometric dip
description:
# Problem

The build pipeline reads GCVS `period` + `(min − max)` magnitude
amplitude and feeds both into every variable star regardless of GCVS
variability *type*. Our shader then pulses every variable's disc on
its catalogued period via `R ∝ √L`, treating Algol-type eclipsing
binaries (EA) as if they were Mira-type radial pulsators.

This is the **right photometric signal** (the period and amplitude
are correct) but the **wrong physical mechanism**:

- EA / EB / EW types are eclipsing binaries — brightness drops happen
  because one component transits in front of the other along an
  edge-on orbit, not because the star physically swells.
- Algol Aa1 (HIP 14576, B8V) appears pulsing in our current build
  with `P = 2.87 d`. Physically Aa1 is a static B8V; the dip is Aa2
  passing in front. ζ Aur (long-period eclipser), β Lyr (EB), W UMa
  (EW contact) — same shape.

Discovered during smoke-testing of `stellata-dch.6`.

# Fix outline

1. Extend `parseGcvsMain` in `scripts/build-catalog.ts` to also read
   the GCVS variability-type column (currently we only pull period
   and max/min mag).
2. Persist the variability type — repurpose reserved byte 37 in
   `RECORD_LAYOUT` per `catalog-pure.ts`, with a small enum
   (0=non-variable, 1=intrinsic pulsator [M, SR, L, DCEP, RR, RV,
   δ Sct, …], 2=eclipsing [EA, EB, EW, ELL], …). Document the enum
   alongside `RECORD_LAYOUT`.
3. Renderer gates pulsation on `varType !== eclipsing`.
4. Once `stellata-dch.10` lands the live orbital evolution, the EA
   eclipse dip emerges naturally from one disc occluding the other
   — no rendering hack needed, the physics carries it.

# Important nuance

The gate is on **GCVS type**, not on "is this star in a binary." Some
intrinsic pulsators have binary companions (e.g. some Cepheids).
Those should still pulse — the binary status is orthogonal to whether
the pulsation mechanism is intrinsic or geometric.

# Order of operations

Best to land this AFTER `stellata-dch.10` (live orbital evolution),
so the eclipse dip is already in place before we remove the
fake-pulsation surrogate. Landing it first would leave EA stars
visually stable for the duration between this bead and dch.10,
which is worse than the current (physically-wrong but
photometrically-correct) signal.

# Smoke

- Algol: focus Algol Aa1, watch ~2.87-day cycle (engage warp). Should
  see Aa2 transit Aa1 producing a brightness dip, not Aa1's own disc
  pulsing.
- β Lyrae (Sheliak): EB type, ~12.94 d period — similar test.
- Mira (ο Cet): M type — should pulse intrinsically (radial
  pulsator), behaviour unchanged.
- δ Cephei: DCEP type — should pulse intrinsically, behaviour
  unchanged.

# Files likely touched

- `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` — extend `RECORD_LAYOUT` with `varType`
  byte; add the enum constant.
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts` — parse the type column; map GCVS
  abbreviations to enum.
- `src/client/catalog-loader.ts` — read new field into typed array.
- `src/client/shaders/star.frag.glsl` or vert — gate pulsation
  uniform on `varType`.
- Tests: `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts` (enum + layout), shader
  pulsation gating in `perceptual-magnitude.test.ts` (if mirrorable).
- `SCIENCE.md` — note GCVS-type vs renderer-mechanism distinction.

title: Re-evaluate focus / zoom minDistance rule for stars with close binary companions
description:
# Symptom

After `stellata-dch.6`, focusing α Cen A or Sirius A (or any newly
binary-flagged star) snaps minDistance out far enough to keep the
companion in frame. Sirius A↔B = 11.8 AU, α Cen A↔B = 18.7 AU; the
camera now refuses to zoom closer than that, which is right for
\"keep the companion visible\" but wrong for users who want to inspect
a single component up close.

Discovered during manual smoke-testing of dch.6 — Alex's first reaction
after seeing the pair render distinct was \"you can no longer zoom in
close to a star in a multi-star system due to the existing rule.\"

# Existing behaviour

`src/client/stellata.ts:252-267` (and adjacent) — when a focused star has
a binary companion, `controls.minDistance` is pushed out so the
companion remains in the viewport. Previously this fired on ~14 mutual
pairs (a rounding error); after dch.6 it fires on ~14k. Includes every
famous nearby binary.

# Design questions

1. **Relaxation policy.** Always allow zoom past the companion-fits
   bound? Allow only when the user explicitly opts in (double-click on
   the focused star to \"isolate\")?
2. **Companion-in-frame trigger.** Should the rule only kick in when
   the companion is bright enough to be visible at the new
   minDistance? Sirius B at absmag 11.36 may be too faint to render at
   the 12-AU range anyway.
3. **Companion-distance heuristic.** Maybe the rule should not kick in
   at all when companion separation is below some threshold (e.g.
   < 50 AU) — those are inspector-only views where the user knows what
   they're looking at, and the partner is always nearby on a small
   pan/orbit.

Recommend deciding (3) first since it's the cheapest fix and likely
covers the regression user-visibly.

# Smoke

- Focus Sirius A, zoom in close to inspect the disc — should be able
  to fly past Sirius B's location without the controls bouncing.
- Focus α Cen A, ditto.
- Focus a wide visual binary (e.g. 61 Cyg A, sep ~ thousands of AU) —
  prior \"keep partner in frame\" behaviour should still apply since
  the partner won't be on-screen otherwise.

# Files likely touched

- `src/client/stellata.ts` around the binary minDistance enforcement
  (lines ~252-267 + the setFocus path that calls it).
- `docs/camera-controls.md` if the rule semantics change.

title: Defer focus lerp until search modal close animation finishes
description:
# Problem

PR #61 (stellata-r9q) introduced a ~2 s glide-or-stay-put on focus
transitions. When the user opens the search typeahead via the `G`
keyboard shortcut and selects a star, the focus lerp starts
immediately on selection — but the search modal + its backdrop
darkening are still on screen (and fading out via CSS transition).
The first significant fraction of the camera animation is hidden
behind the dimming overlay, so the user only sees the tail end of the
glide. The "smooth focus-park" UX is effectively wasted on the
keyboard-driven path that PR #61's release notes explicitly
highlighted.

# Expected

After a star is chosen via the search picker, the camera lerp should
not begin until the modal + backdrop have fully cleared from view.
The user sees the full ~2 s glide against the live scene, the way
mouse-click focus already does.

# Scope

Likely applies to all search-driven focus paths, not just the `G`
shortcut — keyboard `Enter` selection and mouse-click selection
through the same picker hit the same close→focus sequence. Worth
confirming during fix; if only the keyboard path is affected, the
fix is narrower.

# Possible approaches

1. Chain `focusStar(...)` off the modal's CSS transition-end event
   (or a `transitionend` listener on the backdrop). Cleanest — ties
   the camera start to the real close animation.
2. `setTimeout(focusStar, MODAL_CLOSE_MS)` matching the modal's
   transition duration. Simple but couples two literals; risks drift
   if the CSS transition is later retuned.
3. Restructure the search-select pipeline so the picker emits a
   "selection committed" event after its close transition completes,
   and `search.ts` / wherever the focus call lives subscribes to
   that event rather than the immediate selection event.

Option 3 is the most architecturally clean and matches Stellata's
existing event-bus patterns; option 1 is the cheapest fix. The bead
implementer should pick after looking at the actual modal close
plumbing in `src/client/search.ts` / `typeahead.ts` /
`keyboard-shortcuts.ts`.

# Smoke

- Default Sol view → press `G` → type "Betelgeuse" → Enter →
  full ~2 s glide is visible against the live scene; no overlap
  with the dimming overlay.
- Same with mouse click on a search result.
- Same with the constellation typeahead (`C` shortcut → DOM-relocated
  picker), if applicable to focus there.

# Notes

- The keyboard-shortcut + DOM-relocate plumbing for the `G` / `C`
  pickers is documented in `docs/ui-and-controls.md`.
- The lerp primitives live in `src/client/focus-transition.ts`
  (added in PR #61); no change there expected — the fix is in the
  caller that decides *when* to invoke `focusStar`.

title: Apply proper motion + radial velocity to star positions for current epoch
description:
# Problem

AT-HYG positions are at a fixed reference epoch (likely J2000.0); stars
with high proper motion are visibly displaced from their true present-day
positions. Barnard's star moves ~10.3 arcsec/yr — over the 26 years since
J2000.0 that's ~4.5 arcmin of angular drift, which is easily visible when
the user focuses on it in NAVIGATE mode. Other high-PM nearby stars
(Kapteyn's, Groombridge 1830, 61 Cyg, Proxima, etc.) have similar issues.

The solar-system layer (stellata-3re) already updates planet positions
to a wall-clock `t` via `tToJDE` / `isLive` (see `src/client/time.ts`,
`src/client/ephemeris.ts`). The star catalog does not — so the rest of
the scene is anchored to "now" while stars are frozen at the catalog
epoch.

# Scope of this bead (research / design)

Produce a written design before any code lands. Cover:

1. **AT-HYG reference epoch.** Confirm the epoch for ra/dec/distance in
   the `athyg_33_classic_ids` source. Document in SCIENCE.md.
2. **Available velocity columns.** What proper-motion columns
   (pm_ra*cosdec, pm_dec, in mas/yr or arcsec/yr) and what radial-
   velocity column does the source CSV carry? What fraction of the
   ~313k stars have full pm + RV, pm only, or neither? Spot-check via
   `scripts/verify-catalog.ts` / a Python pass over the source CSV.
3. **Correction math.** For stars with pm + RV, the rigorous correction
   is a 3D linear extrapolation in barycentric Cartesian coordinates
   from the catalog epoch to the target epoch (years × 3-vector
   velocity). Without RV, only the tangential component is known; the
   radial component must be zero-assumed (distance held fixed at
   catalog epoch). Cite the standard references (e.g. ESA SP-1200 §1.5
   for Hipparcos, or Butkevich & Lindegren 2014 for Gaia).
4. **Stars with no velocity information.** Options to evaluate:
   - Leave at J2000.0 position (simplest; visible error on high-PM
     stars only, but those are precisely the ones the user notices).
   - Use a spectral-class / luminosity-class mean velocity as a prior
     (galactic-rotation correction). Risk: false precision.
   - Mark them in a per-star flag and visually deprioritise (e.g. a
     subtle uncertainty halo). Probably over-engineering for v1.
   - Just leave them at J2000.0 — the no-velocity subset is dominated
     by faint distant stars where angular drift is sub-arcsecond per
     century anyway. Likely the right call; confirm via the data
     distribution.
5. **Build-time vs runtime correction.** Two options:
   - **Build-time:** `scripts/build-catalog.ts` reads pm + RV, advances
     positions to a fixed epoch (e.g. 2026.0) at preprocess time,
     writes the corrected position into `public/catalog.bin`. Simple;
     no shader / loader change. Rebuild required to advance the epoch.
   - **Runtime:** Catalog binary carries J2000.0 position + velocity
     columns. Per-frame (or per-second; positions move slowly) the
     client applies `pos_now = pos_J2000 + v · (t - t0)` either on the
     CPU (instance buffer update on the seconds timescale) or on the
     GPU (uniform `t` + per-instance velocity attributes). Matches the
     solar-system layer's wall-clock semantics; lets the user warp
     time via the same `t` knob and see star drift; but adds memory
     (3 extra float32 per star, ~3.5 MB on top of catalog.bin),
     shader changes, and per-instance velocity in the GLSL pipeline.
6. **Time reference baseline.** If build-time: pick the fixed target
   epoch (2026.0 is the obvious choice — current observed year, aligns
   with the time-readout convention). If runtime: route through
   `Stellata.getT()` per stellata-consistency-at-the-seam rule 4
   (single source of truth for time).
7. **Test plan.** Pin Barnard's star (HIP 87937), Kapteyn's (HIP 24186),
   61 Cyg A/B, Proxima, Groombridge 1830 — known motion vectors are in
   the literature; the corrected positions should match published
   2026 positions to ~arcsecond. vitest in `scripts/catalog-pure.ts`
   for the pure math.

# Output of this bead

A design doc (likely `docs/proper-motion.md`) capturing the answers to
1–6 above, plus follow-up implementation beads filed against the
chosen approach (build-time vs runtime).

# Why this matters

Cat 2 from stellata-prioritization-framework — physical reality
fixes. The model claims to be a snapshot of the sky; an out-of-date
snapshot of the *nearby* (i.e. high-PM, low-distance, high-importance)
stars contradicts that claim. The error is concentrated exactly in the
stars users are most likely to recognise and focus on.

# Related work

- stellata-3re (solar system epic) — established the `t` / `tToJDE` /
  `isLive` infrastructure; runtime approach would reuse it.
- `src/client/time.ts`, `src/client/ephemeris.ts` — wall-clock plumbing
  already in place.
- SCIENCE.md — must be updated to document the chosen epoch and
  correction model.

title: Log-distance arrival profile in camera-motion.ts
description:
## Why this exists

The visible UX change for stellata-2br. Once the extraction (sibling child 2) is in, this swaps the easing inside the shared helper. The three park-arrival sites (focus-park, warp Fly, unfocus) get the new feel for free.

Warp Phase 3 is explicitly out of scope — it stays inline as the OBSERVE-mode handover. See `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Inventory + § Implementation plan.

## What needs to be done

Inside `src/client/camera-motion.ts`, swap the legacy piecewise smoothstep for the cubic-Hermite log-distance profile from `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Profile:

```
u_eased = 3·u² − 2·u³
d(u)    = d0 · (d_end / d0) ^ u_eased
pos     = target.center + (pStart − target.center).normalize() · d(u)
```

Camera distance from `target.center` evolves as a cubic-Hermite smoothstep over `log d`, giving equal time per decade and uniform angular-size growth. The orientation track (quaternion slerp) keeps its current ease — only the distance track changes shape. The same formula handles outbound (unfocus): when `d0 < d_end`, `log(d_end/d0) > 0` and the camera carries outward over the same `u_eased`.

The cubic-Hermite form (one polynomial, C¹-continuous, identical to GLSL's `smoothstep`) replaces the piecewise quadratic. Shape difference is invisible (~3% at quartiles) but collapses the implementation to a single line.

Cache `dir = (pStart − target.center).normalize()` at construction so tickArrival doesn't allocate or re-normalize per-tick.

## Tests at `src/client/camera-motion.test.ts` (extend the file from child 2)

- **Monotonicity**: `d` strictly decreases for inbound (`d0 > d_end`), strictly increases for outbound (`d0 < d_end`).
- **Curve pin**: synthetic d0=5, dEnd=1 — pin `d(u)` at `u ∈ {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1}` bit-exact via `toBe(d0 · (dEnd/d0)^f)`. Catches drift in the formula or the helper.
- **Worked-example pinning**: pin the doc's Sol-from-1pc and Betelgeuse-from-200pc tables. Use `toBe` against the formula (bit-exact, locks helper-implements-formula) AND `toBeCloseTo` against the doc table's rounded values (3-sig-fig, locks doc-table-to-helper). Per `stellata-test-coverage-discipline` (b).
- **Endpoint pinning**: `d(0) = d0`, `d(1) = d_end` exactly — implemented via explicit short-circuit in tickArrival so `camera.position.equals(pEnd)` stays bit-exact at u≥1.
- **Zero-velocity endpoints**: numerical derivative at `u → 0` and `u → 1` must be at least an order of magnitude smaller than the slope at `u = 0.5` (where f'(0.5) = 1.5, the curve's peak rate). Absolute thresholds like "within 1e-6 of zero" are unachievable — the natural endpoint-slope scale is `O(d0 · ε · |log(dEnd/d0)|)`, e.g. ~2.76 for d0=100, dEnd=1, ε=0.001. The relative comparison vs midpoint slope is the right test.
- **Degenerate `d0 == d_end`**: log ratio = 0, formula reduces to `d(u) = d0` for all u — graceful no-op, camera stays put. (Verify by sampling at u ∈ {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1}.)

The "already-at-park" / `d0 ≤ d_end` short-circuit framing the previous bead version carried referenced a `tickFocusLerp` short-circuit that doesn't exist. The actual contract is: the **caller** guards (e.g. `stellata.ts:focusStar` checks `eyeDist > parkDist` before calling `newFocusLerpFrom`). The helper itself runs the formula in all cases — necessary because outbound unfocus has `d0 < d_end` and that's a valid input.

## Acceptance

- `npm run typecheck`, `npm test` pass.
- Pinned-curve test from child 2 is now expected to **diverge** (no longer a no-op vs piecewise legacy). Replace those expectations with the new cubic-Hermite log-d values pinned bit-exact via `toBe`.

## Manual smoke (per alex-stellata-smoke-tests)

1. **Sol from 1 pc** — disc grows smoothly across the last ~3 decades, not in a final flash.
2. **Betelgeuse from 200 pc** — slowdown visible well outside the giant disc; perceptual kick-in around d ≈ 0.01 pc.
3. **Cloud focus** — ellipsoid arrival doesn't slam.
4. **Warp Sol → Betelgeuse** (navigate) — Fly phase matches focus-park feel.
5. **Unfocus from inside parkDist(Sol)** — outbound mirror does not snap.
6. **Warp in observe mode** — Phase 3 (1200 ms) parallax slerp should be **unchanged** (it stays inline); only the Fly phase before it should feel decelerated.

## Out of scope

- Per-object-type calibration knobs. Single profile, no `dWindow`, no `WINDOW_FACTOR` — see `docs/camera-arrival.md` § What about a two-region split at `dWindow`?
- Angular-rate-cap variant (`effectiveRadius` path is reserved for a future bead if wanted).
- Warp Phase 3 (out of scope; stays inline).

title: Extract camera-motion.ts (behaviour-preserving refactor)
description:
## Why this exists

Implementation step 1 of stellata-2br. Establish the shared module without changing any visible behaviour. Once this lands, the log-distance change (next bead) is a small, isolated diff inside one file with provable scope.

Depends on the design doc (sibling child 1). See `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Helper API sketch + § Implementation plan.

## Scope: three park-arrival sites (warp Phase 3 explicitly out)

The design doc concluded that warp Phase 3's position lerp (`pEnd → (0,0,0)`) is the OBSERVE-mode handover that absorbs the parkDist-sized gap between Fly's endpoint and OBSERVE's origin invariant — it is NOT a park-arrival in the angular-slam sense. It stays inline in `updateWarp` and is not routed through `tickArrival`. See `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Inventory.

## What needs to be done

1. **Create `src/client/camera-motion.ts`** with the API agreed in the design doc:
   ```ts
   export interface ArrivalTarget { center: Vec3; parkDist: number; effectiveRadius?: number; }
   export interface ArrivalState { pStart, pEnd, qStart?, qEnd?, target, startMs, durationMs, d0, dEnd }
   export function newArrival(...): ArrivalState;
   export function tickArrival(s, nowMs, camera): { done: boolean };
   ```
   Initial implementation is the **current piecewise smoothstep** (`2t² / 1−2(1−t)²`) — no log-distance or cubic-Hermite swap yet (both land in 2br.3).

2. **Route three arrival sites through it**:
   - `src/client/focus-transition.ts:tickFocusLerp` becomes a thin wrapper that constructs an `ArrivalState` and delegates. Keep the existing exported signature so `stellata.ts` callers don't change.
   - `src/client/stellata.ts:updateWarp` Fly phase — replace inline smoothstep with `tickArrival`.
   - `src/client/stellata.ts:unfocus` outbound transition — replace inline lerp with `tickArrival`.

3. **Warp Phase 3 stays inline.** Don't touch `updateWarp`'s post-arrival position+quaternion smoothstep block.

4. **`parkDistance` stays in `focus-transition.ts`** — per-object property, not a motion concern.

5. **Tests** at `src/client/camera-motion.test.ts`:
   - **Curve-pinning**: at `u = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0`, asserted distances match the legacy piecewise smoothstep within 1e-9. Proves the refactor is a no-op.
   - Endpoints match `pStart`/`pEnd` exactly.
   - `done` flips at `u ≥ 1`.

## Acceptance

- `npm run typecheck`, `npm test` pass.
- No file outside the touched ones changes behaviour.
- `grep -n '2 \* t \* t' src/client/*.ts` returns ONE remaining match (warp Phase 3, explicitly excluded). The three park-arrival smoothsteps are gone.

## Smoke (manual, per Alex's manual-smoke convention)

1. Focus Sol from search — arrival looks identical to before.
2. Warp Sol → Betelgeuse navigate — Fly phase looks identical.
3. Inside parkDist of Sol, unfocus — outward lerp looks identical.
4. Warp into Sol in observe mode — Phase 3 parallax looks identical (it should — it wasn't touched).

## Out of scope

- Log-distance profile and cubic-Hermite swap (next bead 2br.3).
- Cross-host audit (2br.4).
- Warp Phase 3 position lerp (stays inline by design — see § Scope).
- Cloud `parkDist` refactor (clouds already have their own; leave alone).

title: Camera-arrival design doc — log-distance profile + calibration
description:
## Why this exists

Design gate for stellata-2br. Pin down the math + parameter choices before any code lands, so the implementation children have a doc to point at and tests have a target curve.

## What needs to be done

Write `docs/camera-arrival.md` covering:

1. **The angular-arrival problem**: `θ = 2·atan(R/d)` blow-up at small `d`; why time-parameterised smoothstep doesn't fix it.
2. **Log-distance profile**:
   ```
   d(u) = parkDist · (dWindow / parkDist) ^ (1 − u_eased)
   ```
   - Derivation showing this gives equal time per decade of distance.
   - Choice of `u_eased`: identity, smoothstep, or a tanh-edge for the in-window region.
3. **`dWindow` formula**: default `512 × parkDist` (geometric midpoint of user's empirical 400–1000 range). Justify the factor. Note that for cloud arrivals where `parkDist` is centroid-based, the same multiplier applies.
4. **Seam continuity at `d = dWindow`**: match position **and** velocity. Document the closed-form derivation that determines the outside-window time spent (so the smoothstep blends smoothly into the log-distance segment).
5. **Edge cases**:
   - `d0 ≤ dWindow` (already inside the window at start) — entire arrival is log-distance.
   - `d0 ≤ parkDist` (already inside park) — no-op or instant settle, as today.
   - Target without an effective radius (clouds) — `parkDist` is the only knob; log-distance still works.
   - Outbound (unfocus) — time-reversed inbound; document the mirror property explicitly.
6. **Add an entry to CLAUDE.md's doc index** pointing to the new file.

## Acceptance

- `docs/camera-arrival.md` exists, ~150–250 lines, with derivations and a worked example (Sol from 1 pc; Betelgeuse from 200 pc).
- CLAUDE.md doc-index updated.
- All math chosen here is what stellata-2br child (2) will lift into the helper.

## Smoke / verification

Doc-only commit. Verify by reading the worked examples and confirming the curve hits `parkDist` exactly at `u = 1` and `dWindow` exactly at the seam.

## Out of scope

No code changes in this bead. Pure design.

title: Unified camera-arrival profile (log-distance deceleration)
description:
## Why this exists

Arriving at a focused star feels like getting slammed into it. Angular subtense scales as `θ = 2·atan(R/d)`, so the last decade of distance delivers nearly all of the perceptual size change. Every existing park-arrival interpolates position by **time** (smoothstep `2t² / 1 − 2(1−t)²` re-implemented inline at each site), which damps the time profile but does nothing about the `1/d` blow-up in angular space. The disc stays a pinprick for most of the arrival, then explodes into the frame.

Empirical user thresholds for where deceleration should kick in:
- Sol: ~1000 AU remaining → `d / parkDist ≈ 1000`
- Betelgeuse: ~0.01 pc remaining → `d / parkDist ≈ 295`

i.e. somewhere in **10²–10³ × parkDist**.

## Goal

**Unification is the headline.** Every park-arrival that ends at a focusable object today re-implements its own easing inline. As future object layers land (planets, exoplanets, Local Bubble, nebulae, Radcliffe Wave, etc.) the arrival behaviour should be inherited from a single helper, not re-decided per object type. The deliverable is the helper as much as the deceleration itself.

## Design output: `docs/camera-arrival.md` (stellata-2br.1)

The design doc concluded:

1. **Single-region log-distance smoothstep**, no `dWindow` split. Velocity-continuity at a linear-d/log-d seam forces ~97 % of total time outside the window, which collapses the perceptual goal. The single-region log-d profile delivers "decel kicks in over the last 3 decades" property naturally from the smoothstep shape.
2. **Cubic-Hermite easing** (`u_eased = 3u² − 2u³`) over `log d`. One polynomial, C¹-continuous, identical to GLSL's `smoothstep`. Replaces the codebase's legacy piecewise quadratic.
3. **Warp Phase 3 stays inline** — it lerps `pEnd → (0,0,0)`, the OBSERVE-mode handover, not a park-arrival. Routing it through the helper would require a `d_end = 0` exception for zero perceptual gain.

## Inventory of park-arrival sites (helper-routed)

| Path | Location | Duration | Endpoint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Focus-park lerp (star, cloud) | `focus-transition.ts:tickFocusLerp` | `FOCUS_LERP_MS = 2000` | `parkDist` |
| Warp Fly phase | `stellata.ts:updateWarp` | log-scaled `WARP_T_MIN_MS … WARP_T_MAX_MS` | `endOffset` (≈ destination `parkDist`) |
| Navigate-mode unfocus | `stellata.ts:unfocus` | `OBSERVE_TRANSITION_MS = 1200` | `parkDist` (outbound) |

## Out of scope (intentional)

- **Warp Phase 3 (observe→observe arrivals).** Stays inline as the observe-mode handover. See `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Inventory.
- **Observe-mode double-click aim slerp** (`aimAt`, `updateObserveAim`). Rotation-only, no angular-size problem.
- **Pure quaternion swings in warp Reorient (Phase 1).** Rotation-only.

## Shared module

New `src/client/camera-motion.ts`:

```ts
export interface ArrivalTarget {
  center: Vec3;
  parkDist: number;
  effectiveRadius?: number; // reserved for future angular-rate variants
}

export interface ArrivalState {
  pStart, pEnd, qStart?, qEnd?,
  target,
  startMs, durationMs,
  d0, dEnd
}

export function newArrival(...): ArrivalState;
export function tickArrival(s, nowMs, camera): { done };
```

`focus-transition.ts:tickFocusLerp`, the warp Fly branch of `updateWarp`, and the unfocus lerp delegate to it. `parkDistance` stays in `focus-transition.ts` — per-object property, not a motion concern. Future hosts supply `{center, parkDist}` and inherit the profile.

## Children

1. **stellata-2br.1** — Design doc at `docs/camera-arrival.md`. Doc-only commit. Blocks 2br.2.
2. **stellata-2br.2** — Extract `camera-motion.ts` with legacy piecewise smoothstep preserved. Route the three park-arrival sites through it. Behaviour-preserving; vitest pins the legacy curve. Blocks 2br.3.
3. **stellata-2br.3** — Swap to cubic-Hermite log-distance profile. Visible UX change. Blocks 2br.4.
4. **stellata-2br.4** — Cross-host coverage audit for park-arrivals. Phase 3 explicitly out of scope.

## Acceptance

- All three park-arrival paths (focus-park, warp Fly, unfocus) route through a single helper.
- Sol arrival from ≥1 pc and Betelgeuse arrival from ≥200 pc feel decelerated across the last ~3 decades of distance.
- Cloud arrival inherits the new profile.
- Cross-host audit confirms zero remaining inline smoothstep at park-arrival sites.
- Warp Phase 3 untouched.

title: Apply smooth focus-park to molecular cloud focus
description:
# Problem

Cloud focus (in `src/client/molecular-clouds.ts` and the orchestrator in `stellata.ts`) currently has its own park / minDistance logic. After Child 2 (stellata-r9q.2) lands the generic `parkDistance(...)` + focus-lerp primitives, clouds should route through them instead of duplicating the snap behavior.

Per Alex (2026-05-11): \"I want the same approach for future objects: clouds, planets, nebulae, etc.\"

# Scope

1. Identify the cloud-focus entry point (likely the click-handler in `molecular-clouds.ts` and the focus-set call in `stellata.ts`).
2. Compute cloud `parkDistance(...)`:
   - `R_pc` = cloud's bounding-ellipsoid radius (use the largest semi-axis, or the geometric mean — decide at work-time based on what reads best for the user).
   - `dMinFloor` = whatever the current cloud manual-zoom floor is (or its analogue).
   - No `extraFloor` for clouds (no binary-companion analogue).
3. Replace the cloud-focus camera move with the shared `focusLerpTo(...)` primitive — same lerp-or-noop branching as stars.

# Note on visibility

Per CLAUDE.md, the molecular-cloud overlay is shelved in v1.0 — visibility off by default. The code path is intact (chart-mode still drives the invisible layer). This bead can still land; it just won't be user-visible until clouds re-enable. The point is to avoid divergence: when clouds light back up, the focus UX should already match stars.

# Files

- `src/client/molecular-clouds.ts` — cloud picking + focus path.
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — wherever cloud focus is dispatched.

# Smoke

Manual (when clouds are re-enabled, or via debug flag during the work):
1. Toggle clouds on; click a distant cloud → ~2 s glide; parks at sensible distance.
2. Toggle clouds on; navigate close to a cloud, then click-focus it → camera stays put.
3. Re-focusing an already-focused cloud → no double-snap.

`npm run typecheck` and `npm test` green.

# Depends on

stellata-r9q.2 / Child 2 (generic mechanism must exist).

title: Consolidate astronomical constants into single source
description:
# Problem

Astronomical constants are defined inline in multiple TS files:

- `AU_PC = 1 / 206264.80624709636` at `src/client/ephemeris.ts:23` — file-local, not exported.
- `R_SUN_PC = 2.2543e-8` at `src/client/stellata.ts:250`.

Per Alex (2026-05-11): \"AU constant will only ever be one value, absolutely import it. Create an astronomical constants file if it helps to consolidate all of these constants.\"

The follow-up bead `stellata-r9q` (smooth focus-park) needs to use `AU_PC` from a shared location rather than redefine it.

# Scope

1. Create `src/client/astronomy-constants.ts` (or merge into an existing pure-helper file if a clear home exists) exporting at minimum:
   - `AU_PC`
   - `R_SUN_PC`
2. Replace `src/client/ephemeris.ts:23` and `src/client/stellata.ts:250` with imports from the new file.
3. Add a tiny unit test asserting the numeric values (guards against silent typo regression in future).

**Zero behavior change.** Anything else discovered (shader / Python / test stragglers) is OUT of scope for this bead — those are handled by the companion 9mm sweep bead.

# Files

- New: `src/client/astronomy-constants.ts` (or chosen home)
- Modified: `src/client/ephemeris.ts:23`, `src/client/stellata.ts:250`
- New test: alongside the constants file

# Smoke

- `npm run typecheck` — green.
- `npm test` — vitest green; includes the new constants-values test.
- Build artifacts (catalog.bin, etc.) byte-identical before and after.

# Blocks

`stellata-r9q` Child 2 (smooth focus-park applied to stars) and the 9mm sweep bead.

title: Automate build:catalog smoke check — assert record counts against committed expected values
description:
Recurring smoke-step pattern across PRs that touch build-catalog.ts: 'npm run build:catalog reports the same record counts (313242 records, 11316 Hip + 13926 HD GCVS xrefs, 3677 variables matched) as pre-PR.' Alex flagged 2026-05-11 that this is hard to eyeball — automate it programmatically.

Approach: build-catalog.ts writes a structured summary of every headline count it logs (records, GCVS xrefs, variables, binaries, CCDM doubles, search-index entries, name-table size) and compares against scripts/build-catalog-expected.json. Mismatch exits non-zero with a clear diff. UPDATE_BUILD_COUNTS=1 env var refreshes the expected file when counts legitimately change.

Acceptance:
- scripts/build-catalog-expected.json committed with current shipped counts.
- npm run build:catalog passes when counts match, fails clearly when they don't.
- npm run build:catalog UPDATE_BUILD_COUNTS=1 rewrites the expected file.
- PR-template smoke step for build-catalog.ts changes drops to 'build:catalog passes (count assertion is automated)' or similar.
close_reason: Closed via PR #59 (Automate build:catalog count smoke check) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Split camera-modes.md and extract url-state.md
description:
Per-session context efficiency. Two doc restructures:

1. Split docs/camera-modes.md (464 lines) into 3 files:
   - docs/camera-warp.md — warp animation state machine
   - docs/camera-observe.md — OBSERVE mode + look-around controller + POI dispatch
   - docs/camera-controls.md — near-plane vs minDistance, TrackballControls tuning, two-finger roll

2. Extract URL state from docs/architecture.md into docs/url-state.md (~85 lines).

Update CLAUDE.md doc index and all inbound cross-doc references.

Pure-docs change: attach skip-version-bump label on PR.

title: togglePoi silently fails on three reject paths — needs visible feedback
description:
**Problem**

`togglePoi` (`src/client/stellata.ts:1227-1248`) has three silent-reject paths: Sol exclusion, missing HIP, and the POI hard-cap. Each only emits a `console.info` line — the user sees nothing on screen. Click feels broken.

Reported by Alex (2026-05-10) after a click on what appeared to be a normal star did nothing; only checking the console revealed `[POI] cannot pin a star without a Hipparcos ID.`.

**Locations**

- `src/client/stellata.ts:1229-1232` (Sol exclusion)
- `src/client/stellata.ts:1233-1236` (no HIP)
- `src/client/stellata.ts:1243-1246` (POI_HARD_CAP reached)

**Suggested approach**

Surface each rejection visibly without leaving observe-mode. Options:

- (a) Toast / chip at top-right of the canvas, fades in ~2s. Existing styles in styles.css cover `.toast` shape; nothing similar wired today.
- (b) Brief pulse on the cursor pointer + tooltip text override ("Sol is shown via the bottom arrow" / "This star has no HIP — POIs require one" / "POI cap reached (16) — unpin one first").
- (c) Status line in the existing brand/info area.

(a) is probably cleanest and matches the prevailing UI pattern. Wire from `togglePoi` via a callback or a fresh event-bus channel (`pois:rejected` carrying reason).

Sister-layer extension: `setPois` (`src/client/stellata.ts:1255-1270`) silently drops the same three reject paths during URL restore. That's by design (URL was already validated) but a one-line `console.info` could help future debugging — separate from this bead.

**Smoke**

After fix:
1. Try to pin Sol in observe → reason surfaces.
2. Try to pin a star with no HIP → reason surfaces.
3. Pin 16 stars, click a 17th → cap message surfaces.

Cross-link to stellata-02d — if (a) of that bead lands (in-memory POIs for non-HIP stars), the no-HIP reject here goes away, leaving Sol + cap.

title: POIs silently reject stars without a HIP ID — decide non-HIP fallback strategy
description:
**Problem**

`togglePoi` at `src/client/stellata.ts:1227-1248` rejects any catalog row where `catalog.hip[idx] === 0`. The gate exists because the POI URL-state encoding (`src/client/url-state.ts` ~line 640 and ~line 924) packs each POI as a 3-byte HIP ID, so a non-HIP star couldn't survive a URL reload anyway.

This produces a silent UX failure: stars in the AT-HYG classic-IDs subset that have a non-empty display label (Bayer, Flamsteed, HD, HR, or Gl) but no Hipparcos ID look perfectly pin-able to the user. Hover surfaces the data card; clicking does nothing visible. Only the console `console.info('[POI] cannot pin a star without a Hipparcos ID.')` reveals why. Surfacing the rejection is the sister bead stellata-ace; **this** bead is the architectural question of whether non-HIP stars should be pin-able at all.

**Why this exists**

AT-HYG v3.3 cross-matches stars across HIP / HD / GLI / TYC / etc. Many rows have null `hip` but a valid display label assembled from one of the other ID columns. The PR #53 picker resolves these correctly under the cursor, so the gate is the only thing preventing pinning.

(Audit on 2026-05-10 confirmed AT-HYG's `hip` column is bounded to the real Hipparcos range 1–120404 — no synthetic / out-of-range HIP IDs are ever displayed.)

**Decision needed**

- (a) **In-memory-only fallback** — allow non-HIP POIs in the live session; they don't get serialised to the URL. Warn the user on share (or just silently drop them on URL save). Lightest-touch architectural change.
- (b) **Expand URL encoding to row indices** — switch POI encoding from 3-byte HIP to 4-byte row index (or hybrid: HIP when available, fall back to row index). Adds ~25% URL bytes per POI but every star becomes pin-able and shareable. Requires a URL state version bump (currently v3 — see stellata-p69).
- (c) **Keep the gate; surface visibly only** — defer the architectural question. Handled entirely by stellata-ace. User still can't pin non-HIP stars; just gets a clear reason.

**Locations**

- `src/client/stellata.ts:1227-1248` (togglePoi)
- `src/client/stellata.ts:1255-1270` (setPois — URL-restore path; mirrors the gate)
- `src/client/url-state.ts` POI encode/decode (search 'POI' and '3-byte HIP')

**Smoke**

After fix, attempt to pin a known non-HIP star (search the catalog for rows with `hip=0` and a non-empty display label — e.g. a Gliese-only or HD-only star). Behaviour must match the chosen option.

title: [PR #54] startUrlSync change-detector EPS too coarse at solar-system scales
description:
The per-frame URL change detector in startUrlSync uses a fixed absolute EPS=1e-3 pc for cam/tgt component-delta comparison. 1e-3 pc ≈ 206 AU, so when the camera is at solar-system scales (e.g. first-load's 5 AU park) a zoom-out doesn't trip any axis until it reaches ~258 AU on the dominant component. Pre-existing bug exposed by stellata-3re; surfaced during PR #54 smoke testing.

Fix: scale the per-axis threshold to 1% of vector magnitude (clamped to [1e-9, 1e-3] pc), so AU-scale zoom triggers fire at ~0.05 AU resolution while scene-scale (>= 0.1 pc) keeps the existing 1e-3 absolute floor (where float32 ULP can approach 5e-4 pc and would otherwise spam writes from steady-state noise).

Affects URL write throttling, not encoding correctness — applies to both v2 and v3.

title: Add Share affordance: button/icon that copies current URL to clipboard
description:
The current page URL already encodes the full view (focus, mode, time, filters via url-state.ts), so users can already share what they see by copying the address bar. But that affordance is invisible — users don't realise the URL is shareable.

Add a small Share control somewhere on the page (likely a share icon, e.g. in the top-right card alongside the navigate/observe pill, or in the brand box area). Clicking it copies window.location.href to the clipboard via navigator.clipboard.writeText and shows a brief 'Link copied' confirmation.

The point is purely advertising the affordance: we want users to share what they're looking at, and a visible Share button signals 'this URL captures my view, go ahead and send it'.

# Acceptance criteria

- Visible share icon on the page in a sensible location (placement decision noted in PR).
- Click copies current URL to clipboard (navigator.clipboard.writeText).
- Brief visual confirmation (toast / inline label flip / icon swap) so the user knows it worked.
- Clipboard fallback or graceful degradation if navigator.clipboard is unavailable (e.g. old Safari, insecure context) — at minimum, don't throw.
- Does not appear in chart mode if it visually clashes (judgement call at impl time).
- Works on desktop; mobile is best-effort (mobile epic still deferred).

# Smoke step

Open app, focus a star, click Share, paste into a new tab — should restore the same view.

title: Lambertian phase function phi(α) lives only in vert shader — pure math, no CPU mirror, no test
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The Lambertian phase function `φ(α) = (sin α + (π − α)·cos α)/π` is computed at planet.vert.glsl:90:

```glsl
float phi = (sin(alpha) + (PI_CONST - alpha) * cos(alpha)) / PI_CONST;
```

This is a closed-form pure scalar function — ideal for unit testing on the CPU — but no TS mirror exists, and no vitest covers it. The PR description explicitly tags this as "Medium" honesty: "The Lambertian phase function … is the standard formula; not vitest-tested."

This is **distinct from 9mm.96** (which covers the integrated `m_planet = M_host + 5·log10(d/10) − 2.5·log10(...)` formula). 9mm.96 names the wider integration as the gap; phi(α) is the *factor inside* that integration that has its own boundary conditions worth pinning:

- φ(0) = 1 (full illumination at opposition — used as the cull-distance proxy maximum).
- φ(π/2) = 1/π ≈ 0.318 (quadrature — the half-lit Earth-from-Moon case).
- φ(π) = 0 (back-illuminated — new Moon).
- Monotonically decreasing on [0, π].

The PR even calls out a sister concept: future work via Mallama 2018 (PR #56) replaces phi() for Sol bodies. With phi() un-pinned, swapping in the Mallama polynomial wont have a pre-existing baseline to compare against — the polynomial→Lambertian boundary issue 9mm.171 raises is exactly the kind of regression a phi-unit-test would have caught.

**Location**
`src/client/shaders/planet.vert.glsl:84-90` (phi computation in shader); `src/client/perceptual-magnitude.ts` (where the mirror should land — alongside `apparentMagnitude`).

**Suggested approach**
Add `lambertianPhaseFactor(alphaRad: number): number` to `perceptual-magnitude.ts`. Mirror it identical to the GLSL line. Pin tests against the four boundary conditions above plus a monotonicity sweep. When PR #56s `peakPhaseFactor` lands, it can call this same helper for the c0=0 default case instead of redefining phi inline.

**Why**
phi(α) is a small enough function that the cost is one TS function + ~5 unit tests, and it pays for itself the moment a CPU consumer wants to ask "would this planet at this phase angle be visible?" (e.g. focus-distance heuristics, search-rank ordering). Filing this as separate from 9mm.96 because the suggested fix shape differs (a tiny pure helper, not an integrated mirror), and because it lights up the Mallama integration in PR #56.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` includes the four-boundary tests + monotonicity sweep. Mutation-test by negating one term in the formula and confirm a test fails.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: PlanetBodyField.update() pipeline has zero test coverage beyond the cull-distance gate
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`PlanetBodyField` has 9 lifecycle tests (`planet-body-field.test.ts:97-202`) but **none of them call `update()`** — the per-frame method is the load-bearing part of the class. The 9mm.102 bead covers the cull-distance gate specifically; the rest of `update()`s contract is unguarded:

1. `update()` calls `host.positionsAt(t, scratch)`, then `rotateInto(scratch, bufLocalRel, base, host.orientation)` (lines 516-525). No test asserts the buffer actually receives the values.
2. `recenter(newWorldOffset)` mutates each hosts `hostLocalPos = hostAbsPos − worldOffset` and writes it into `bufHostLocalPos` (line 268-275). The "recenter shifts hostLocalPos" test (test L136) only re-attaches and asserts `group.visible` — never actually peeks at `bufHostLocalPos` to confirm the shift landed.
3. `rotateInto()` rotates the placeholder/ephemeris XYZ triples through `host.orientation`. Untested. A regression that flipped the quaternion or mis-applied the rotation would not be caught.
4. The `host.positionsScratch` allocation gate (`positionsAt ? new Float32Array(n*3) : null`) is untested — both branches of `writeHostPositions` (positionsAt-driven vs placeholderEccentricAnomaly fallback).

Combined with 9mm.102 (cull gate), this is the integration cluster the prior reviewer flagged with `alex-large-pr-honesty`: the math is unit-tested, the lifecycle is unit-tested, the *integration* of math + lifecycle into a per-frame buffer write is not.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts:296-313` (`update`); `src/client/planet-body-field.ts:265-275` (`recenter`); `src/client/planet-body-field.ts:512-535` (`writeHostPositions`); `src/client/planet-body-field.ts:539-556` (`rotateInto`); `src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts` (where the tests belong).

**Suggested approach**
Three cheap test additions (one per gap):

1. `update()` calls a stub `positionsAt` that writes `[1, 2, 3]` for one planet. After update, assert `getHostLocalPositions(idx).slice(0, 3)` matches the rotated value (identity orientation for Sol → ecliptic-pole rotation). Mutation-test by changing the rotation axis and confirming the test fails.
2. After `recenter(newOffset)`, peek `getHostLocalPositions` is irrelevant (its iLocalRel) — instead expose `getHostLocalPosForTesting(idx)` that returns the cached `host.hostLocalPos`, or peek `bufHostLocalPos[base..base+3]` directly. Assert `hostAbsPos − newOffset = hostLocalPos`.
3. Placeholder branch: `attachHost` with `positionsAt: undefined`, then `update()`. Assert the buffer holds the placeholder Keplerian layout (one entry at perihelion +x for n=1).

**Why**
This is exactly the integration-point gap the user called out: "unit-test coverage probably has gaps." 9mm.96 covers the math-formula CPU mirror; 9mm.102 covers one branch of `update()` (cull). The other three branches are untested and form the real-world per-frame pipeline.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` passes the new tests; mutation-test by reverting the `applyQuaternion` line in `rotateInto` and confirming the rotation test fails.

title: [PR #56] Polynomial→Lambertian boundary creates visible brightness step (especially Saturn)
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
At α = alphaMaxDeg the renderer hard-switches from the empirical polynomial to Lambert with no smoothing, producing a brightness step in the worst cases. Quick numbers: Mars at α=50° polynomial gives φ ≈ 0.474, Lambert gives 0.708 — a ~50% jump (≈0.4 mag). Saturn at α=6.5°+ε is the more dramatic case: polynomial gives `10^((0.55 − 6.5·0.026)/2.5) = 10^((0.55 − 0.169)/2.5) ≈ 1.42`; Lambert at α=6.5° gives ~0.97 — a ~30% drop (≈0.4 mag). The PR test plan flags Mars ("There's a known small brightness step at the polynomial→Lambertian handover — flag if jarring") but doesn't mention Saturn. Saturn's case is mostly camera-irrelevant in v1 because typical Stellata viewing angles for Saturn from outside its orbit stay near α=0, but a viewer flying from Sol toward Saturn will cross the 6.5° boundary visibly.

**Location**
`src/client/phase-function.ts` line 82 (`if (aDeg > coefs.alphaMaxDeg) return lambertianPhaseFactor(alphaRad);`) and the parallel branch in `src/client/shaders/planet.vert.glsl` line 104.

**Suggested approach**
Three options worth Alex's call. (a) Accept and document — note in the source comment that the discontinuity is a known v1 artefact; defer smoothing to 3re.x. (b) Smoothstep blend over a ~2° band on either side of `alphaMaxDeg` — cheap in shader, mirror in TS, mask the step. (c) Anchor the polynomial's α=alphaMaxDeg value as a one-shot constant brightness offset folded into Lambert beyond the boundary — preserves continuity, costs a single `mix`. Option (a) plus a smoke test plan note for Saturn at α≈6° is the lowest-cost path; option (b) is a small shader change.

**Why**
Empirical phase curves with hard cutoffs are a classic source of "why does the planet flicker" bug reports. Saturn's c0 boost makes the discontinuity especially material because the polynomial sits well above 1 just inside the boundary and Lambert sits well below 1 just outside. Worth being explicit about whether v1 lives with it or smooths it.

**Smoke step**
Manual: position camera so Saturn's α slowly crosses 6.5° (e.g. fly from outside Saturn's orbit through the inner solar system); look for a brightness step. If shipping option (a), add a note to the `docs/solar-system.md` phase-function section. If shipping (b)/(c), add a unit test pinning `mallamaPhaseFactor(SATURN_PHASE, 6.5° ± ε)` continuity.

title: [PR #56] No test that iPhaseCoefsA/iPhaseCoefsB buffer values are written correctly
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The PR adds two per-instance vec4 attribute buffers (`bufPhaseA`, `bufPhaseB`) plumbed through allocate, grow, write-static, flush, and shift-down code paths. The PR description rates this "Medium confidence — exercised by 14 existing field tests... but the new buffer values themselves aren't directly read back." The lifecycle tests check attach/detach/grow/recenter mechanics but don't assert buffer contents — so a bug like swapping `pc.c4` and `pc.c5` in `writeHostStaticAttributes`, or forgetting to copy phase buffers in `growCapacity`, or shifting the wrong stride in `shiftInstancesDown`, would not be caught by the existing suite. The Sol-only v1 happens to land all 9 planets in slots 0..8, which means the grow path (capacity 16 → 32) and the shift-down path (compaction after detach) both execute on currently-unattested data.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts` lines 521–531 (writeHostStaticAttributes phase block), 388–397 (growCapacity copies), 636–637 (shiftInstancesDown phase shifts). Test file `src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts` (gap).

**Suggested approach**
Add a small read-back test that attaches a host with a planet carrying a non-trivial `phaseCoefficients` (Saturn is the natural choice — non-zero c0, c1, alphaMaxDeg=6.5), then reads back the InstancedBufferAttribute arrays via `(geometry.attributes.iPhaseCoefsA as THREE.InstancedBufferAttribute).array` and asserts the Saturn slot's 8 floats match. One assertion across the static path catches the single-planet write bug; the existing grow/detach lifecycle tests will then exercise the copy/shift paths over the same data.

**Why**
This is the single-largest piece of new mechanical plumbing in the PR (~40 lines split across allocate/grow/write/flush/shift), and it currently has zero direct coverage. A regression here is silent — the rendered Saturn just looks slightly off, no test fails — and the GPU side is the only consumer.

**Smoke step**
Run the new test against current code (passes). Swap `pc.c4` ↔ `pc.c5` in `writeHostStaticAttributes` and re-run (fails). Restore.

title: No integration test pins the eleven-event surface against StellataEventMap
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The PR's headline contract is 'eleven typed events, named in StellataEventMap, emitted from stellata.ts at exactly the points the prior fire*Change methods used to fire.' `event-bus.test.ts` covers the `EventBus` core in isolation. There is no test that asserts the eleven events are actually emitted from stellata.ts (e.g. setting focus emits exactly `'focus'` then `'state'`; setVectorTo with a previous cloud destination emits exactly `'vectorCloud'(null)`, `'vector'(idx)`, `'state'` in that order). Per Alex's large-PR-honesty framing, integration points like this are exactly where the unit tests + a smoke step won't catch a missed wiring.

**Location**
`src/client/stellata.ts` — every `this.bus.emit('…', …)` site (~16 emits across 11 names)
Test suite — gap

**Suggested approach**
Add a Stellata-level test fixture that boots a minimal `Stellata` (or stubs the parts that need WebGL) and asserts emission sequences for the high-fanout entry points: `setFocus`, `setFocusedCloud`, `setVectorTo`/`setVectorToCloud`, `setFilter`, `enterObserve`/`exitObserve`, `startWarp`/`finishWarp`. If a full Stellata boot is too heavy for vitest, extract the pub/sub-fire portions into pure helpers and test those.

Alternatively: a static checker that walks `stellata.ts` AST and confirms every keyof `StellataEventMap` has at least one `bus.emit` call site — catches the 'event added to map but never fired' regression cheaply.

**Why**
The integration surface is what the smoke checklist in the PR description is leaning on, and a smoke checklist is the weakest layer in a regression-prevention strategy. Adding even one structural test would catch a future emit being deleted while the map still advertises the event.

**Smoke step**
New test runs in `npm test`; mutating a single `bus.emit` call site (e.g. delete the focus emit) makes the test fail.

title: [PR #56] No test that peakPhaseFactor widens cull distance for Saturn-style hosts
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The new code path in `PlanetBodyField.attachHost` folds `peakPhaseFactor(planet.phaseCoefficients)` into the per-planet reflectance proxy that drives `cullDistancePc`. Saturn's c0=−0.55 boost raises Saturn's reflectance by ~1.66× and the cull distance by ~√1.66 ≈ 1.29×. The PR description acknowledges this widening but no test asserts it: the `cullDistancePc` block in `planet-body-field.test.ts` is a pure-function suite that doesn't exercise `attachHost`, and the lifecycle tests that do call `attachHost` use a synthetic `makePlanet` factory that never sets `phaseCoefficients`. The Saturn ring-boost path in production code is therefore untested at the integration level.

**Location**
Production code: `src/client/planet-body-field.ts` lines 213–224 (the `for (const planet of ps.planets)` block computing `brightestReflectance`). Test file: `src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts` (gap — no test references `SATURN_PHASE` or the `AttachedHost.cullDistance` field after attach).

**Suggested approach**
Add a focused test in the `PlanetBodyField lifecycle` describe block: attach a host whose `PlanetSystem` contains a single planet with `phaseCoefficients = SATURN_PHASE` and another identical planet with `phaseCoefficients` undefined; assert that the resulting `cullDistance` (read off the host map via a small accessor or via re-derived `cullDistancePc`) widens by the expected √(peakPhaseFactor(SATURN_PHASE)) factor. Three lines + an expected ratio. Related: `stellata-9mm.102` covers the broader cull-distance optimization gap; this is a separable per-planet test of the new ring-boost path.

**Why**
The whole point of the `peakPhaseFactor` integration is to keep Saturn visible past the prior cull threshold. Without a test, a future refactor that drops the multiplication (e.g. "the phase factor cancels out at α=0") silently re-narrows Saturn's cull and the bug shows up as Saturn vanishing at distances where it should still render.

**Smoke step**
The new test passes against current code; mutate the multiplication out (delete `* peakBrightness`) and watch the assertion fail.

title: onFocusHandlers fire 1.2 s before warp lands on observe→observe
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
Pulling the destination recentre forward to phase-3 start changes `focusedStar` and fans out `onFocusHandlers` immediately at the start of the 1.2 s parallax slerp, instead of at `finishWarp` (the prior behaviour for observe→observe). Listeners include the focus-search box (renames the 'Location' / 'Focus' row to the destination's name during the slerp), the mode-toggle pill (re-enables OBSERVE based on `hasFocus` — already enabled here, so a no-op), and the focus-ring overlay (visibility flips to show; hidden during warp via `body.warping` CSS so user-invisible). The most user-noticeable is the search row — it pops to the destination's name about a second before the camera arrives.

**Location**
src/client/stellata.ts:2473 (recenterFocusToStar's `for (const h of this.onFocusHandlers) h(newIdx)` called from updateWarp:3674)

**Suggested approach**
Two options. (1) Defer the focus-event fan-out until `finishWarp` for the observe→observe path: have `recenterFocusToStar` accept a `fireFocusEvent: boolean` (or split out the geometry from the event fan-out), and let `finishWarp`'s `swapObserveAnchor` call fan out the event. (2) Accept the early event because phase 3 is the user's first frame at the new vantage and labels updating early reads as 'arrived' rather than 'arriving'. Option (1) preserves the prior contract; option (2) is what the PR ships. Worth Alex's call — this is a UX-visibility judgment, not a correctness bug.

**Why**
The PR comments lean on 'mirrors the navigate-mode path (setFocus(destIdx) recentre at finishWarp)' for the rationale, but navigate fires onFocusChange at finishWarp, not at phase-3 start. So the observe path's event timing is now AHEAD of navigate's by `postArrivalMs` (1.2 s). The asymmetry is undocumented and is a behavioural shift even if benign.

**Smoke step**
1. Long observe→observe warp Sol → Betelgeuse with the search panel open: confirm whether the 'Location' field switching to 'Betelgeuse' partway through phase 3 reads as bug or as feature.
2. Mode-toggle pill stays steady; focus-ring stays hidden until warp ends.

title: getComputedTextLength cache keyed only on text — misses font-load + CSS-class-change invalidations
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`distance-vector-overlay.ts` introduces a `getComputedTextLength()` cache keyed solely on the rendered string (`labelWidthCacheText`). The cache invalidates on text change. It does not invalidate on:

1. **Web-font load (FOUT/FOIT)**: if the page's typeface loads asynchronously after the first measurement (common on first cold load), the measured pixel width may be from a fallback font. Subsequent ticks with the same string will keep returning the stale fallback width, mis-anchoring the warp affordance and right-edge clamp.
2. **CSS class change on `#dist-label`**: chart-mode toggles a body class that re-paints the label colour. If a future style rule on `.chart-mode .dist-label` changes weight/letter-spacing/font-size, the cache won't invalidate.
3. **Window resize w/ media-query font-size scaling**: less likely but plausible.

Today (a) is the realistic concern. The cache hit rate is high in steady-state, so this isn't a hot-path correctness problem; it's a one-frame-at-page-load mis-anchoring that may or may not be visible.

**Location**
src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:62-67 + :184-191.

**Suggested approach**
Cheapest robust fix: invalidate the cache on `document.fonts.ready` (one-shot, settles within a few hundred ms of page load). If the fonts API isn't available, fall back to a frame-N measurement (re-measure once at frame ≥30 even if the cached text matches).

Heavier-weight fix: hook `MutationObserver` on the label element's `class` attribute and invalidate on changes — overkill for the current threat model.

Or just bump the cache key to include the body's chart-mode class — single line change.

**Why**
The optimisation (cache the layout flush) is sound; the cache key is just under-specified for the actual surface area of "things that change rendered text width." One reasonable answer is "do nothing — FOUT is one frame of mis-anchoring nobody notices." Worth a deliberate decision rather than the implicit assume-text-only that the current code encodes.

**Smoke step**
Reload the page on a slow connection (devtools network throttle, "Slow 3G"), focus a star, set a destination, and check whether the warp affordance lands at the correct x-offset on first paint vs. after the font finishes loading. If the text shifts but the warp doesn't, the cache is stale.
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (getComputedTextLength cache invalidates on document.fonts.ready)

title: SCIENCE.md Mercury angular-rate sanity-check rounds the wrong way
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
SCIENCE.md (PR #38 add, "Solar system" → "Time `t`" subsection) justifies the 60 s ephemeris-cache granularity with:
> Mercury moves ~3e-5 rad seen from Earth in 60s ≈ 8″, well below pixel resolution at any zoom.

3e-5 rad = **6.18″**, not 8″. The cache-granularity argument still holds at 6″, but the new science doc carries an off-by-30% rounding in a quoted angular-motion sanity check.

**Location**
SCIENCE.md "Time `t`" subsection (PR #38 add, near the end of "## Solar system")

**Suggested approach**
Either: (a) replace "≈ 8″" with "≈ 6″" and recompute if a different conjunction-config was intended; (b) replace the round-trip arc with a different self-consistent illustration ("a 60 s motion of ~3e-5 rad falls below ~6″, well below sub-pixel"). Same one-line edit either way.

**Why**
SCIENCE.md is positioned in this PR as the citation-grade source for the project's claims. A factual rounding error in a quoted arcseconds figure undermines the document's authority — exactly the surface where it's least defensible.
