**Smoke step**
1 rad = 206264.806″ → 3e-5 rad = 6.188″. Confirm the arithmetic, pick the corrected value, edit one line.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: [PR #56] SCIENCE.md 'same DOI' cross-reference is incorrect
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The new `Planet phase functions` paragraph in `SCIENCE.md` says "Mallama, Krobusek, Pavlov 2018 (Icarus 282; same DOI)", referring back to the immediately-prior `Planet geometric albedos` paragraph which cites `https://doi.org/10.1016/j.icarus.2017.05.018`. The Mallama 2018 phase-curve paper (Icarus 282) is actually DOI `10.1016/j.icarus.2016.09.023` — and that DOI is what `phase-function.ts` and the new `index.html` credit entry both correctly cite. So either (a) the prior albedo block has an unrelated DOI that the phase block should not equate to, or (b) the albedo block's DOI was already wrong. Either way "same DOI" misleads readers chasing the citation.

**Location**
`SCIENCE.md` line ~277 ("Mallama, Krobusek, Pavlov 2018 (Icarus 282; same DOI)") versus the prior block at line ~270 (`https://doi.org/10.1016/j.icarus.2017.05.018`).

**Suggested approach**
Either replace "same DOI" with the correct phase-paper DOI (`10.1016/j.icarus.2016.09.023`), or restructure so the albedo + phase paragraphs both cite the same Mallama 2018 paper once with the right DOI (the comprehensive 2018 Icarus 282 paper publishes both). The existing `2017.05.018` reference is a different Mallama paper and may need its own audit — likely Mallama & Hilton 2018 photometry — but that's separate from this fix.

**Why**
Citation hygiene. SCIENCE.md is the index of "every external data source (catalogues, papers, DOIs, licences)" per CLAUDE.md, so a wrong DOI cross-reference undermines the file's purpose. Reader chasing the phase polynomials gets sent to a different paper than the one the code actually used.

**Smoke step**
Resolve both DOIs in a browser; confirm the phase paragraph points to "Comprehensive wide-band magnitudes and albedos for the planets" (Mallama, Krobusek, Pavlov, Icarus 282, 2018) — the paper whose Tables A-1.2 through A-6.2 the coefficients quote.

title: no test coverage for warp arrival pipeline (recenterFocusToStar / swapObserveAnchor / phase-3 pEnd shift)
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #50 fixes a destination-star jitter caused by float32 precision loss in the projection chain during the 1.2 s observe→observe parallax slerp. The fix relies on three correctness invariants: (1) `recenterFocusToStar` is idempotent w.r.t. `focusedStar` (so `swapObserveAnchor`'s late-recentre path is a no-op when phase-3 already ran), (2) the `state.pEnd` shift exactly matches the `(dx, dy, dz)` applied by `recenterOrigin` (so the lerp `pEnd → B` continues to read the same physical waypoint), (3) skip mid-phase-3 vs skip mid-fly-phase land at the same destination pose. None of these are exercised by the test suite — `grep -rn 'swapObserveAnchor|recenterFocusToStar|finishWarp|updateWarp' src/client/*.test.ts` returns nothing.

**Location**
No file. The gap is in src/client/*.test.ts. Adjacent: src/client/stellata.ts:2466-2494 (recenterFocusToStar + swapObserveAnchor), src/client/stellata.ts:3644-3700 (updateWarp phase 3).

**Suggested approach**
Add a thin testing seam over the warp pipeline. The cleanest cut is to extract the pure-math waypoints (pStart/pEnd computation given A, B, sourceOffset, endOffset; the dx/dy/dz delta application; the first-frame-of-phase-3 invariant) into stand-alone helpers — same shape as catalog-pure.ts / perceptual-magnitude.ts. Vitest covers the math in isolation: idempotency under repeated recentres, pEnd-frame consistency after a synthetic recentre, the phase-3 lerp endpoint = (0,0,0) within 1e-6. The full Stellata class doesn't need to boot in jsdom for this — the pure helpers are enough to lock the invariants.

**Why**
The fix is a precision-pressure mitigation; a regression that flips the sign of the pEnd shift, drops the re-bind of B, or breaks idempotency would still pass typecheck and would only manifest as a re-introduction of jitter on long observe→observe warps — exactly the symptom this PR was filed to fix. Without test coverage that invariant is one careful refactor away from breaking again.

**Smoke step**
1. New tests pass.
2. Mutation check: temporarily remove the `state.pEnd.x -= dx` shift — at least one new test fails.
3. Mutation check: remove the `if (this.focusedStar !== newIdx)` guard in swapObserveAnchor — at least one new idempotency test fails.

title: EventBus.emit varargs trade allocations for ergonomics on the per-frame 'frame' event
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`EventBus.emit` uses `(...args: EmitArgs<M, K>)` and reads `args[0]` / `args[1]` for the name and payload. Each call site allocates the rest-args array. For the `'frame'` event, which fires from `animate()` once per render (60–120 Hz), the allocation is on the steady-state hot path that every overlay walks today. The old code (`for (const h of this.onFrameHandlers) h();`) made zero allocations.

`docs/performance.md` documents the perf-hud's section budgets; `onFrame.total` (now arguably `'frame'`) is the one section the budget guidance specifically calls out as 'the full handler loop … overlays + chart labels.' Adding a per-tick allocation under that umbrella is the kind of regression the rest of the file warns against (see e.g. the URL-state cam-snapshot rewrite in PR #39, which removed string allocations from the same idle path).

**Location**
`src/client/event-bus.ts:30-36`

**Suggested approach**
Two-overload signature: `emit<K extends NoPayloadKeys<M>>(name: K)` + `emit<K extends WithPayloadKeys<M>>(name: K, payload: M[K])`. Single concrete implementation reads `name` and `payload` directly without spread. Keeps the same caller ergonomics while dropping the per-emit array allocation. Or accept the trade-off and add a comment in `event-bus.ts` explaining the choice ('the V8 inline cache erases the spread cost; benchmarked at …') so the next reader doesn't second-guess.

**Why**
Hot paths in this codebase are explicitly defended (perf-hud, idle-state URL writes, overlay self-gating). Trading a tiny ergonomic win in `emit` syntax for an extra allocation per frame is the wrong direction by the project's own conventions.

**Smoke step**
Pre/post benchmark: `debug.panel()`, watch the `onFrame.total` ring-buffer with the panel open at 60 fps for 30 s. Bonus: a microbenchmark in `event-bus.test.ts` (skipped by default) capturing 1M `emit('tick')` calls.

title: createTimeReadout teardown does not unsubscribe its three new bus listeners
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`createTimeReadout` subscribes to `'planetSystem'`, `'filter'`, and `'warp'` and returns a teardown — but the teardown only clears the per-second `setInterval`. The three bus subscriptions stay attached forever, capturing `el` and the `updateVisibility` closure. Because `stellata.on()` now returns a real unsubscribe (the headline win of this PR), the fix is mechanical and the migration is a natural moment to make this teardown actually complete.

This was a pre-existing leak (the old API also didn't return an unsubscribe for these three), but the PR header explicitly calls out 'generalises the unsubscribe return — previously only onFrame had it … to all events,' so leaving the most prominent teardown in the codebase that *should* have used the new affordance still wired to the old no-op pattern is a missed integration point.

**Location**
- `src/client/time-readout.ts:74-76` — three `stellata.on(...)` calls, none of their unsubscribes are stored
- `src/client/time-readout.ts:81-86` — teardown only clears `timer`

**Suggested approach**
Capture each `stellata.on(...)` return and call all three from the teardown alongside the existing `clearInterval`. Pattern matches `pin-debug-hud.ts` and `arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts` (which the PR already updated). One commit, one file.

**Why**
PR description explicitly motivates the migration on lifecycle: 'generalises the unsubscribe return … to all events.' The migration is incomplete if subscribers that already expose a teardown contract don't adopt the new affordance. Concretely: `time-readout` is created/torn-down by main.ts on its module boundary, so this affects HMR cycles in the dev loop today.

**Smoke step**
Add a vitest that calls `createTimeReadout(...)()` then asserts the three subscribers were removed (e.g. via a mock Stellata that records sub/unsub counts). Manual: HMR-edit the module a few times in dev and confirm the bus internal handler set for `'planetSystem'` doesn't grow.

title: [PR #54] No direct unit test for varintLen / writeVarint / readVarint helpers
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The LEB128 helpers (`writeVarint`, `readVarint`, `varintLen` — lines 343-379) are tested only indirectly through end-to-end round-trips and one truncation-rejection test (url-state.test.ts:872-874). Specifically:
- No test asserts `varintLen(v) === bytes` returned by `writeVarint` for representative masks (so the duplication risk in 9mm.106 has no guardrail).
- No test that `readVarint` rejects a varint > 5 bytes (the `shift >= 32` guard at line 367) — only the `runs past blob end` path is tested.
- No test that `readVarint` correctly decodes a 4-byte varint produced by `writeVarint` (bit-21 `t` field would emit one once stellata-nmu lands).
- No test that the empty mask varint is exactly 1 byte (`varintLen(0) === 1`).

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:343-379 (helpers); src/client/url-state.test.ts:824-875 (integration tests but no helper-level tests).

**Suggested approach**
Add a focused `describe('LEB128 helpers')` block. Export the three helpers (or test through a small integration). Cover masks {0, 1, 0x7f, 0x80, 0x3fff, 0x4000, 0x1fffff, 0x200000, 0xfffffff, 0x10000000, 0xffffffff} for write-then-read symmetry and for varintLen consistency. Also add an explicit test for the `Varint mask too long` error path.

**Why**
The varint is the only piece of v3 that doesn't have a fixed-size analogue in v1 or v2 — it is genuinely new code, with two error paths and a length-determining loop. Helper-level tests are cheap and catch regressions in code paths that the integration tests would only exercise once stellata-nmu enables emission of bit 21 `t`.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` runs the new helper tests.

title: No test coverage for new dirty-track sentinels — first-write + idle-skip behaviour untested
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The PR adds new dirty-track state across five overlays (chart-labels.ts already had it). The PR description correctly identifies sentinel-init bugs as "the most likely culprits" for regressions, and routes them to manual smoke checks. But none of the new state is covered by automated tests:

- No assertion that identical inputs frame-to-frame skip the setAttribute write.
- No assertion that the very first frame after a state change writes (sentinel-init).
- No assertion that hide()/show() cycles correctly reset the dirty state so a stale value across visibility transitions doesn't survive.
- `projectWithNearClip` has 18 unit tests but they predate the scratch-vector swap, and none assert "caller's input vectors are not mutated" — the implementation does `projVa.copy(worldA)` (correct) but a future refactor that drops the `.copy()` would not be caught.

The PR cites "485/485 passing" tests but the count is unchanged from baseline — no new tests were added for the new behaviour. The "unit tests catch projection math; visual checks catch DOM-state regressions" framing is honest, but it leaves the regression class the PR description itself flags (sentinel-init) as the one without an automated catch.

**Location**
src/client/distance-vector-overlay.test.ts (existing tests for projection only). No `hud-overlay.test.ts`, `poi-overlay.test.ts`, `disc-mask.test.ts`, `constellation-overlay.test.ts`, or `focus-ring-overlay.test.ts` exists.

**Suggested approach**
Add lightweight unit tests around the dirty-track contract. Two practical shapes:

1. **Per-overlay smoke**: a JSDOM-based test that creates the overlay, drives `onFrame` twice with identical inputs, and asserts `setAttribute` was called exactly once for each tracked attribute (use a spy on the SVG element). Test `hud-overlay.ts` (most state) and `poi-overlay.ts` (per-Entry state) at minimum.

2. **`projectWithNearClip` mutation assertion**: extend the existing test file to assert that the `worldA` and `worldB` inputs are unchanged after the call returns (catches a regression where the module-scope scratch is replaced with direct mutation).

(2) alone is one new `expect` per test, ~15 lines total. (1) is the meatier addition but is the only way to lock in the dirty-skip behaviour.

**Why**
The PR adds a new failure mode (stale dirty-track state) and routes verification to manual eyeballs in seven distinct browser scenarios. The unit-test gap is exactly the area the PR description flags as highest-risk. Adding tests in a follow-up bead is appropriate; gating the merge on them is the EM's call.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` should grow by N tests; tests should fail if the dirty-track guards are removed or the sentinels are mis-initialised.

title: EventBus has no clear() — Stellata.dispose() leaves subscribers attached
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`EventBus` exposes only `on()` / `emit()`. There is no `clear()` or `off(name)` method, and `Stellata.dispose()` does not touch the bus. This matches the pre-PR behaviour (the old eleven `on*Handlers` arrays were also never cleared on dispose), so it isn't a regression — but the migration would have been the natural moment to introduce `bus.clear()` since the new central abstraction can clean up all eleven event types in one call.

In the current single-page architecture this is moot (`Stellata.dispose()` is only called by HMR teardown / tests), but the closures captured by every subscriber pin the entire `Stellata` instance through the bus's internal Set, defeating any GC of the disposed scene graph until the page is reloaded. Long-running dev sessions that HMR-recreate Stellata accumulate stale subscribers from prior generations.

**Location**
- `src/client/event-bus.ts:11–34` — bus class, no clear/off
- `src/client/stellata.ts:3730–3769` — dispose() releases every subsystem but never touches `this.bus`

**Suggested approach**
Add `clear()` to `EventBus` (`this.handlers.clear()`) and call `this.bus.clear()` from `Stellata.dispose()`. Also call subscriber-side cleanup in modules that already return a teardown (e.g. `createTimeReadout`, `pin-debug-hud`) so the bus side empties before the listener-owned timers do. Optional second method `off(name)` for symmetry but probably not needed today.

**Why**
The bus is now the single chokepoint for stellata's pub/sub — adding a one-line lifecycle method makes the abstraction complete and lets future-Claude reason about teardown without scanning eleven handler arrays. Test coverage already pins `on/emit/unsubscribe`; `clear` is a natural sibling.

**Smoke step**
Unit test in `event-bus.test.ts`: register handlers, call `clear()`, emit — handlers don't fire. Manual: in dev tools call `stellata.dispose()` and confirm `bus.handlers.size === 0`.

title: HTML title and PR-body smoke step disagree (case + missing "An")
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
PR #38's test plan says:
> Browser tab title reads "Stellata — An Observational 3D Model of Our Galaxy".

The actual `<title>` element in `src/client/index.html` reads:
> "Stellata — Observational 3D Model of our Galaxy"

Differences: missing "An" prefix and "our" lowercased. The same string lives only in one place, so whichever spelling was intended, the literal smoke-test step doesn't match.

**Location**
src/client/index.html line 6

**Suggested approach**
Pick one spelling and align both. Title-case style ("An Observational 3D Model of Our Galaxy") matches typical document titles and the PR-body checklist; the shipped lowercase-"our" form reads more colloquial and might be what Alex actually preferred. Either is defensible — flagging the inconsistency between the smoke step and the artifact, not picking a winner.

**Why**
The PR's own checklist contained a literal-string assertion that the merged code does not satisfy. Future "did the title regress?" smoke checks will diverge from the PR record.

**Smoke step**
View `src/client/index.html` head; compare `<title>` to the PR body's smoke-step phrasing.
close_reason: shipped title 'Stellata — Observational 3D Model of our Galaxy' is canonical; PR #38's smoke-step phrasing was wrong in the PR body but is historical record. No code change needed.

title: [PR #54] Test gap: worldOffset sub-mask elision (per-component) only covered for the all-zero case
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The v3 vec3 sub-mask is the headline optimisation, but the test file only directly verifies sub-mask elision for **cam** (lines 134-178) and the all-zero default case for tgt/up/worldOffset (lines 121-132, 180-183). There is no test that worldOffset with one or two components diverging from the default emits the right per-component sub-mask byte count — e.g. `worldOffset=[5,0,0]` should produce sub=1 + 4 bytes payload (5 byte field), not 12 bytes.

Same gap for tgt and up sub-mask elision: tests that check `up=[0.7071, 0.7071, 0]` round-trip exist, but none assert the byte count of the resulting blob is smaller than v2's flat 12-byte vec3.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.test.ts:36-205 (vec3 fields block).

**Suggested approach**
Extend the existing `blobBytes(blob)` helper from the LEB128 block to assert byte counts on partial-divergence cases for each of tgt, up, worldOffset:
- `tgt=[5,0,0]` → 1 ver + 1 mask + 1 sub + 4 x = 7 bytes
- `up=[0,0,1]` → 1 ver + 1 mask + 1 sub + 4 z = 7 bytes (1+0=elide y, but 0+1=non-default x and z)
- `worldOffset=[100,0,0]` → 1 ver + 3 mask + 1 sub + 4 x = 9 bytes

**Why**
The encoder's per-key `componentDefaults` is the single most regression-prone piece of v3 — a future change that flips a default constant or breaks the strict-equality predicate would silently revert the bytes-saved gain to zero, with all current tests still passing. Byte-count assertions catch this immediately.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` runs the new assertions.

title: disc-mask.ts: tryPlace closure rebuilt every frame; siblings (clearCircle/placeCircle) hoisted out of the tick
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`tryPlace` is declared inside the per-frame `onFrame` callback, which means a fresh closure is allocated every animation frame. The other two helpers in the same file — `clearCircle` and `placeCircle` — sit at factory scope (allocated once per `createDiscMask` call) so they don't churn per frame. The new code mixes the two patterns.

**Location**
src/client/disc-mask.ts:94-99 (the `const tryPlace = (idx: number) => { … }` inside the onFrame callback)
Compare: src/client/disc-mask.ts:46 (factory-scope `clearCircle`) and src/client/disc-mask.ts:54 (factory-scope `placeCircle`).

**Suggested approach**
Hoist `tryPlace` to factory scope and have it read the `used` counter and `seen` Set from a small mutable `state` object captured by closure once per factory call (or pass them in as args). The factory-scope helpers already follow this pattern — `placeCircle` takes `c` and `idx` as args, with `v` and `stellata` captured once. Mirror that.

**Why**
Architectural fit. The file's other helpers were deliberately lifted out of the tick (likely for the same v8 hidden-class / closure-allocation reason this matters). Mixing the two patterns is mildly confusing — the next reader has to wonder whether `tryPlace`'s in-tick definition is load-bearing (it isn't — it just reads `used`/`seen`/`circles`/`ensureCircles`/`placeCircle`, all stable references after factory init).

**Smoke step**
Diff is mechanical (move + capture); `npm run typecheck` clean; manual PR smoke unchanged. Optional: `debug.perf()` can confirm the per-frame timing of the disc-mask tick before/after — should drop slightly in long-lived sessions.

title: Welcome-modal copy is a sentence fragment after #info-star-count substitution
description: **Confidence: Medium** **Finding** The new welcome-modal first paragraph reads: > Stellata is a physics-based, empirical 3D model of our galaxy that simulates what it would be like to actually be there. \—\ real stars, the planets of our Solar System, the Sun's heliopause, ... `info-modal.ts` does `countEl.textContent = starCount.toLocaleString()`, so at runtime the prose becomes "...to actually be there. 313,000 real stars, the planets of our Solar System, ..." — a verbless sentence fragment. The previous wording wove the count into a complete clause: "Stellata is an interactive 3D map of \ real stars within ~1250 parsecs of the Sun." The PR loses the grammatical hook. **Location** - src/client/index.html lines 119-126 (welcome-modal first paragraph) - src/client/info-modal.ts lines 9-11 (textContent replacement) **Suggested approach** Rewrite the first paragraph so the span sits inside a complete clause, e.g. "...to actually be there. The model includes \—\ real stars, the planets of our Solar System, the Sun's heliopause, local interstellar dust, and the Milky Way backdrop — every object comes from a published observational catalogue or direct measurement." Or split into two sentences: keep the framing sentence intact, then start a new sentence "It includes \… real stars, the planets…". **Why** Welcome modal is the first surface a fresh user sees. The substitution-driven fragment reads as a typo. Caught only by reading the rendered result, not the source string — easy to overlook on review. **Smoke step** Local dev run, clear localStorage `stellata.info-dismissed`, reload, read the first paragraph aloud. Should parse as a grammatical sentence with the star count substituted.

title: [PR #54] No test for v2->v3 auto-upgrade rewrite path (applyFromUrl setTimeout debounce)
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`applyFromUrl` (lines 1138-1159) detects `decoded.version !== SCHEMA_VERSION` and schedules a 300ms-debounced `writeUrl` to silently upgrade the URL to v3. This is the central piece of the PR's `no bookmarks break, address bar shrinks` claim, and the test plan's manual smoke step calls it out (`Confirm it renders correctly and the address bar silently rewrites to ?v=AwEA... after the 300 ms debounce`).

The test file has `v1 backward compatibility` and `v2 backward compatibility` blocks that verify the *decoder* path, but neither asserts that a v2/v1 blob, after being decoded and applied, re-encodes as a *v3* blob with byte 0 = 3. Behavioural drift here is silent: a future schema v4 that re-uses bit 0 differently would need this test to catch the upgrade rewrite producing the wrong shape.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:1138-1159 (applyFromUrl auto-upgrade); src/client/url-state.test.ts has no test of this code path.

**Suggested approach**
Add a test that (a) builds a v2 blob via the existing buildV2Blob helper, (b) round-trips it through `decodeBlob` to a view, (c) re-encodes the view via `encodeBlob`, and (d) asserts the result starts with version byte 3 (decode the new blob, assert version === 3) and that the view decodes back to the same DecodedView shape. This validates the upgrade-rewrite contract directly without needing applyFromUrl's location/setTimeout side effects.

**Why**
The test plan calls this out as a manual smoke; it is a 5-line vitest. Manual smokes regress between releases more readily than automated ones.

**Smoke step**
The test itself is the smoke. `npm test` should run an additional check.

title: disc-mask.ts reads stellata.getFilter() per frame instead of caching highlightCon via onFilterChange
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The new tick body reads `stellata.getFilter().highlightCon` every animation frame. The sibling overlay that consumes the same field — `constellation-overlay.ts` — caches it via `onFilterChange` (`current = f.highlightCon` inside an `update` registered with `stellata.onFilterChange(update)`) and reads only the cached number in its tick. Disc-mask should follow the same pattern.

**Location**
src/client/disc-mask.ts:101-109 (the `const conIdx = stellata.getFilter().highlightCon;` block inside `stellata.onFrame`)
Compare: src/client/constellation-overlay.ts:19-29 (`update`/`tick` split with cached `current`)

**Suggested approach**
Lift a module-scope `let highlightCon = -1` and register `stellata.onFilterChange((f) => { highlightCon = f.highlightCon })` alongside the initial DOM setup. Read `highlightCon` directly in the tick. Same shape as constellation-overlay.ts. The `getFilter()` accessor likely returns the live FilterState object so the cost is structural-property-access only, but the architectural fit win is the bigger lever — both overlays now react to filter mutations through one mechanism instead of one polling and one event-driven.

**Why**
DRY + architectural fit. Two SVG overlays consuming the same field should consume it the same way; mixed patterns make it harder for the next reader to know whether a filter mutation will reach the overlay synchronously or via callback. Caching also makes the tick's null-equivalent fast-path (no constellation highlighted, no focus) a couple of branches with no method dispatch.

**Smoke step**
After the refactor: highlight a constellation via the typeahead, confirm cutouts appear; clear it, confirm cutouts disappear within one frame; `npm run typecheck` clean; manual smoke from PR test plan still passes.

title: SCIENCE.md/solar-system.md: Voyager wording is factually wrong
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The newly-authored Solar-system science section labels Voyager 1 2012-08-25 as a "termination-shock crossing" — that date is the *heliopause* crossing; the termination shock was crossed 2004-12-16. Similarly Voyager 2 2018-11-05 is described as a "flank" crossing, but V2 also crossed the heliopause along the spacecraft trajectory (the 115 AU figure is its heliocentric distance at that crossing, not a measured "flank" radius). The doc reads as if these two probes measured two different boundary surfaces; in reality both crossed the same surface at different points.

**Location**
- SCIENCE.md (PR #38 add) lines under "Heliopause boundary": "Upwind boundary at 122 AU — Voyager 1 termination-shock crossing, 2012-08-25" and "Flank boundaries at 115 AU — Voyager 2 crossing, 2018-11-05"
- docs/solar-system.md lines 148-149 carry the same two bullets

**Suggested approach**
Reword to: "Upwind boundary at 122 AU — Voyager 1 heliopause crossing, 2012-08-25" and "Flank inferred at ~115 AU from Voyager 2 heliopause crossing 2018-11-05, combined with the apex-aligned ellipsoid model". Or, if the "flank vs upwind" distinction is load-bearing for the 115 AU number, cite the asymmetric-bubble model that derives it (e.g. Opher et al.) explicitly. Both occurrences must move together because docs/solar-system.md mirrors SCIENCE.md.

**Why**
Stellata is positioned in this very PR around the empirical-model principle ("every object comes from a published observational catalogue or direct in-situ measurement"). A factual mislabelling of the headline observation in the heliopause section directly undermines that framing. SCIENCE.md is the citation source the README and Credits modal point readers at.

**Smoke step**
Cross-check Voyager 1 / Voyager 2 boundary-crossing dates against NASA mission summaries; confirm the 115 AU figure source. Build does not need re-running — text-only edit. `npm test` should remain 427/427.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: focus-ring-overlay.ts skipped — sixth SVG overlay still writes cx/cy/r every frame
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #55's release notes claim coverage of "all five remaining overlay modules" but `focus-ring-overlay.ts` is a sixth per-frame SVG overlay (listed in CLAUDE.md and docs/overlays.md as a peer of the others) that is NOT touched. It unconditionally writes `cx`, `cy`, `r` and toggles `style.display` every animate tick, with the same `.toFixed(1)` precision floor and the same stationary-camera idle profile that motivated the rest of the pass. The exact pattern the PR mirrors from chart-labels.ts is missing on the one overlay that should have been mechanical to convert.

**Location**
src/client/focus-ring-overlay.ts:87-90 (per-frame setAttribute writes), :11-12 / :32 / :43 / :53 / :64 / :79 (display toggles via show/hide).

**Suggested approach**
Add `lastCx / lastCy / lastR` (sentinel `-Infinity`) and a `lastDisplay` string to the closure, gate `ring.setAttribute('cx'|'cy'|'r', ...)` on `Math.abs(value - last*) >= ATTR_DIRTY_PX`, and gate the `show()`/`hide()` writes on the cached display string the same way `hud-overlay.ts` does for the OBSERVE ring. One commit, mechanical mirror of the disc-mask Slot pattern.

**Why**
This is the most-instantiated focus-ring overlay (visible whenever a star is focused in NAVIGATE — the primary mode) and it bypasses the very pattern the PR is selling. Leaves the per-frame DOM cost reduction inconsistent across the overlay layer that the PR title ("perf(overlays): dirty-track + scratch-vector pass") claims to cover.

**Smoke step**
Focus a star (Polaris) in NAVIGATE, sit idle, watch the focus-ring `cx/cy/r` setAttribute calls in DevTools' SVG element inspector — should not change while the camera is steady. Then orbit the camera; values should update smoothly.
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (dirty-track pass + sentinel hygiene under 9mm.188)

title: PlanetBodyField cull-distance optimisation has no test coverage
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The architectural payoff of the global PlanetBodyField is the per-host `cullDistancePc` — bk5 will scale to "potentially hundreds of attached hosts" and the per-frame `if (dToHost > host.cullDistance) continue;` gate at `update():L320` is what makes that tractable. `cullDistancePc` itself has 5 unit tests; the *gate* it drives in `update()` has zero. There is no test that verifies "a host past its cull distance does NOT have its positions buffer rewritten this frame" — the entire optimisation could silently regress (e.g. someone changes `>` to `>=`, or the comparison runs against `host.hostAbsPos` instead of `host.hostLocalPos`) and 479 tests still pass.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts:311-327` (`update()` method); `src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts` (where the test belongs).

**Suggested approach**
Add a test that attaches a host with `positionsAt` (e.g. a stub that increments a counter on each call) and a known small cullDistance, places the camera inside cullDistance + asserts the counter increments per `update()` call, then moves the camera past cullDistance + asserts the counter stays frozen. Two assertions, one bug class permanently nailed.

**Why**
The integration point is exactly what the user flagged: "unit-test coverage probably has gaps." The cull is the architectural promise; not testing it leaves the bk5 scaling claim un-pinned. This is the single most leverage-bearing test gap in the PR.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` passes the new test; mutation-test by flipping the comparison to `<` in `update()` and confirming the test catches it.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: No unit tests for the new Typeahead<T> class (claim/release, blur-restore, X-clear sequence)
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #43 introduced `Typeahead<T>` as the consolidated DOM-glue abstraction (focus/blur lifecycle, deferred hide, arrow-nav, mousedown picking, displayName reverse-sync, X-clear pre-clearing input.value, TypeaheadGroup ownership across shared resultsEl). The class has zero direct unit-test coverage. The existing suite covers only `applyHoverClass` (typeahead-util.test.ts, ex-9mm.6) and pure helpers `filterConstellations` / `splitBayer` — none of the actual glue the PR consolidates.

The PR description's 'Test plan' is entirely manual smoke. The class encodes several non-obvious invariants: (a) blur-deferred restore writes `displayName` back even when group ownership has flipped to a sibling typeahead, (b) X-clear must clear `input.value` BEFORE `onClear?.()` so the synthetic `input.focus()` doesn't re-render against an orphan query (comment at typeahead.ts:84-95 cites a real bug this guards), (c) `setName(name)` preserves user typing iff `group.active === this`, (d) ESC bails without preventDefault so global ESC handlers still see the key, (e) cross-input group claim/release prevents blur from hiding the dropdown when focus moves between focus+to inputs.

None of these have a regression net.

**Location**
src/client/typeahead.ts (entire file, no corresponding test file)

**Suggested approach**
Add `src/client/typeahead.test.ts` using jsdom (vitest `environment: 'jsdom'` per-file via `// @vitest-environment jsdom` comment, or add jsdom dep). Cover at minimum:
- setName updates input.value when not focused; preserves input.value when focused (group.active === this).
- setName updates clearBtn.hidden iff clearBtn provided; no-op otherwise.
- TypeaheadGroup claim on focus, release on blur; sibling typeahead in same group prevents blur-hide.
- X-clear sequence: clears input.value, calls onClear, refocuses, render shows empty results (depends on runQuery).
- ESC key: hides results, blurs input, does NOT call preventDefault.
- pick() invokes onSelect, hides resultsEl, blurs input.
- handleKey ArrowDown/Up wraparound, Enter on hoverIdx<0 no-op.

The harness pattern is similar to how observe-controls.test.ts (closed 9mm.12) tests DOM event lifecycles — drives synthetic events on a stub input + ul.

**Why**
PR #43 is a straight refactor consolidating three parallel implementations. The unit suite is the project's documented regression-prevention layer (see CLAUDE.md). Without class-level tests, any future tweak to the focus/blur/group/X-clear interaction risks silently regressing one of the call sites the PR's manual smoke happened to cover. The closed bead 9mm.6 added tests for the `applyHoverClass` / `escapeHtml` helpers but explicitly did not extend to the binders' state machinery — that gap moves wholesale into typeahead.ts under PR #43.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` shows new typeahead.test.ts file with at least 6 cases covering the bullets above; existing tests still pass.
notes: Reverting to open: claimed 2026-05-11 but no work landed — src/client/typeahead.test.ts does not exist in main. Stale in_progress state from an abandoned session.

title: No CPU mirror of the planet apparent-mag formula — shader is the only test
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`perceptual-magnitude.ts` ports the disc-radius math (`perceptualDmEff`, `perceptualAppSizePx`, `apparentMagnitude`) for vitest coverage, but the integration that actually defines a planet — `m_planet = m_host + 5·log10(d_vh/10) − 2.5·log10(p · (R/d_vp)² · (d_vh/d_hp)² · φ(α))` — lives only in `planet.vert.glsl:127-142`. The PR description acknowledges this gap explicitly ("Medium" honesty bucket): the formula was hand-verified against three reference points, no GLSL test harness. There is also no CPU helper a future debug-panel readout / search-rank consumer could call.

**Location**
`src/client/shaders/planet.vert.glsl:127-142` (sole production location); `src/client/perceptual-magnitude.ts` (where the mirror should land).

**Suggested approach**
Add `planetApparentMagnitude(hostAbsmag, d_vh, d_vp, d_hp, albedo, radiusPc, phaseFactor)` to `perceptual-magnitude.ts` returning the same value the shader computes. Pin tests against the three reference points the bead description already uses (Jupiter from Earth opposition −2.7, Jupiter outside heliopause +5.2, Jupiter from α Cen +21). Bonus: pin against Lambert vs Mallama to lock the φ(α) branch behaviour.

**Why**
This formula is the load-bearing physics contract of stellata-3re.16. Drift (e.g. a future refactor swapping the squared term order, or changing the d_vh denominator) currently has no automated guard — the only way to notice is to run the smoke checklist and eyeball Jupiter brightness ratios. A 5-line CPU port + 3 reference-point tests closes that.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` includes the new helper exercises; cross-check by tweaking one term in the mirror, observing the test fail, then verifying the shader still has the original term.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: PlanetMaterialUniforms duplicates 17 star-pipeline uniforms by hand
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`PlanetMaterialUniforms` in `planet-body-field.ts` re-declares 17 uniform fields (uMaxAppMag through uFovYRad) — every single one a pointer to the same `{ value }` object the star pipeline already created in `stellata.ts` (the `sharedUniforms` literal at L751). The interface and the pickoff in `buildMaterials` (L437-455) duplicate that list. If a new star-pipeline uniform is added (or removed) the planet field silently desynchronises until somebody hand-syncs both call sites.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts:58-75` (interface) and `:438-455` (sharedPlanetUniforms pickoff). Source of truth at `src/client/stellata.ts:751-842`.

**Suggested approach**
Extract a `PerceptualDiscUniforms` type (e.g. in `perceptual-magnitude.ts` next to the math chunk it shadows) listing only the fields the planet pipeline actually consumes, and have stellata.ts construct `sharedUniforms` to include that subtype. Then planet-body-field can `Pick<>` from a single canonical definition. Or simpler: `keyof typeof sharedUniforms` extraction at the call site — drop the bespoke interface entirely. Either way the manual list-of-17 disappears.

**Why**
The whole architectural point of the perceptual-disc chunk is that stars and planets share one source of truth for the math + uniforms. Duplicating the uniform shape contradicts that and is the most likely place a future tuning change drifts (e.g. someone adds uVisibleSomethingNew to the chunk + star side and forgets the planet side).

**Smoke step**
After refactor: `npm run typecheck && npm test` clean; manually verify in the browser that adjusting any star-disc tuning slider (visibleThreshold, sizeKnee, etc.) still propagates to planet bodies live.

title: Row colour dirty-check compares hex string against serialized style.color (always mismatches)
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The per-row dirty-track for colour is `if (slot.line.style.color !== colour) slot.line.style.color = colour;` where `colour` is a 3-digit hex (`'#cfe' / '#fc8' / '#f88'` from `colourForAvg`). Per CSSOM, the inline `style.color` getter returns the **serialized** form — modern browsers serialize `#cfe` to `rgb(204, 238, 255)`. So `slot.line.style.color` after the first write is `rgb(204, 238, 255)`, which never equals `'#cfe'`. The comparison **always fails**, so the colour is rewritten every tick — defeating the dirty-track. Same pattern is correctly avoided for histograms (which cache the input hex in `histoLastColour[]` and compare against that, not against `style.background`).

**Location**
src/client/perf-hud.ts:308-309 (`const colour = colourForAvg(r.avg); if (slot.line.style.color !== colour) slot.line.style.color = colour;`)

**Suggested approach**
Mirror the histogram pattern: maintain a parallel `rowLastColour: string[]` cache (or extend each row pool slot with a `lastColour` field), and compare against the cached hex rather than the serialized DOM value. Same fix shape as `histoLastColour`. Cost: one extra string per row × 8 rows.

**Why**
Correctness — the whole point of the row's dirty-tracking is to skip identical-value writes for stable workloads (the bead's stated motivation). Without the fix, the row colour writes 8 times per tick at 5 Hz forever; small absolute cost but the abstraction lies. Also: the same hex-vs-rgb gotcha probably needs a comment so future dirty-tracks don't repeat it.

**Smoke step**
`npm run typecheck && npm test`. Add a vitest that builds the section, calls render twice with stable input, and asserts the second tick doesn't reassign `style.color` (stub via a Proxy / spy on the setter, or assert the cached colour matches one of the three known hex values).

title: Calibration: debug-panel calibration section + cross-layer knob spotlight
description:
**Goal:** debug surface that supports the calibration procedure from sibling **stellata-dbl.1** by spotlighting the cross-layer-affecting knobs in one place. Spec context in parent **stellata-dbl**.

### Scope (decide during design — extend existing per-layer panels OR add a unified calibration panel)
- Spotlight the knobs that affect calibration across stars / MW / planets:
  - magnitude cutoff
  - MW brightness multiplier
  - planet-disc floor
  - dynamic-range exponent
  - any preset-driving constants
- Live readout of the current preset's numeric targets next to the sliders.
- Optional side-by-side compare HUD: scene render vs reference image cropped to the same FOV.

### Files
- `src/client/star-tuning.ts`, `src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts`, `src/client/debug-panel.ts` — existing tuning surfaces.
- New section module if going the unified-panel route.

### Acceptance
- The calibration procedure in `docs/calibration.md` can be executed without leaving the debug panel.
- Each knob's current value + the active preset's target are readable side-by-side.
- No regression on existing tuning sections.

### Depends on
**stellata-dbl.1** (procedure tells us which knobs need spotlighting).

title: Calibration: write docs/calibration.md procedure
description:
**Goal:** a documented, repeatable calibration procedure that ensures stars, Milky Way background, and planets are correctly sized + brightness-matched at each magnitude preset. Spec context in parent **stellata-dbl**.

### Visual ground-truth benchmark
ESO eso0932a panorama: https://cdn.eso.org/images/screen/eso0932a.jpg

Use this as "what does the real sky look like at perceptually-good calibration?" Compare Stellata's render at each magnitude preset against an image-stretching of this panorama matched to the same magnitude cutoff.

### Deliverable: `docs/calibration.md`
For each magnitude preset (mag 6 / 5 / 4 / 3 etc.):
- Scene to set up: camera position, FOV, observe vs navigate, mode selection.
- Target star(s) and MW region to reference.
- Ground-truth comparison image / known-star pair to match against.
- Knobs to adjust and the order — likely stars first, then MW, then planets (or whichever ordering settles).
- Expected visual outcome — quantified where possible (e.g. "star X should be brighter than MW patch Y by N×").

### Acceptance
- `docs/calibration.md` committed.
- A reviewer can follow the doc end-to-end on any of the magnitude presets and produce the documented expected outcome.
- Coordinated with `stellata-f9b` / `stellata-v3j` (preset-driven UX): the calibration procedure feeds back into what each preset's defaults should be.

### Out of scope
- Building per-preset auto-tuned coefficients.
- Debug-panel updates — sibling **stellata-dbl.2**.

### Coupling
- **stellata-3re.16** (apparent-magnitude planet visibility) — planet calibration steps depend on this landing or co-evolves.
- **stellata-zsr** (perceptual polish) — specific spectral-correction / dynamic-range work feeds into individual calibration steps.

title: Search index: V-number variable-star designations (audit + ingest)
description:
**Goal:** make GCVS V-numbers searchable so a user typing "V645" finds Proxima Centauri (V645 Centauri). Highest-priority designation class per parent **stellata-nxb** — GCVS data already flows through the build pipeline.

### Scope
1. **Audit** what alternate-name fields exist in AT-HYG v3.3 (`data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv`) and what currently lands in `search-index.json` from `scripts/build-catalog.ts`. Document which classical designations are present today.
2. **Greek-letter abbreviations** (Alf Cen C alongside Alpha Centauri C) — flag whether these are partly handled already or part of this scope.
3. **Ingest V-numbers** from GCVS via the existing GCVS cross-match path. Add `V*` keys to search index entries for matched stars.
4. **Verify** `src/client/search.ts` dual-input typeahead handles new keys (likely no change needed; just more entries).

### Acceptance
- `bd memories pickStar`-style verification: search for "V645" returns Proxima Centauri at top.
- A handful of representative V-numbers (V1500 Cyg, V1280 Sco, V404 Cyg) resolve to their canonical stars.
- `npm test` covers the V-number search-index entries.
- No regression on existing typeahead behaviour.

### Files
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts` / `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` — search-index entry generation; add V-number keys.
- Tests in `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts`.

### Out of scope
GJ / Gliese, CCDM, esoteric proper-motion catalogues — sibling **stellata-nxb.2**.

title: Galactic arm dust: bake arm contributions into voxel grid (build-dust.py)
description:
**Goal:** apply the data-source + scope decisions from sibling **stellata-ju3.1** to extend the dust voxel volume with arm-overdensity contributions. Spec context in parent **stellata-ju3**.

### Approach
- Modify the existing chunked 3D-texture pipeline rather than adding a parallel pass — same principle that **stellata-c7u.2** uses for Zucker MCs. Single source of truth for absorption in `star.vert.glsl`.
- If grid extents need to grow (per ju3.1 decision), bump them in `manifest.json`; the chunking already supports larger grids.

### Files
- `scripts/build-dust.py` — overlay arm contributions into the voxel volume.
- `data/dust/manifest.json` — bump version; record arm-source provenance.
- `data/<arm-source-files>` — committed per `frozen-external-data`.

### Acceptance
- Distant sightlines toward galactic-disc-plane targets show physically-plausible reddening/dimming relative to off-plane targets.
- Comparable to the ESO Milky Way panorama benchmark (eso0932a) used by **stellata-dbl** calibration.
- No regression in near-Sol fidelity (Edenhofer high-res unchanged inside its cube).

### Out of scope
- Per-arm visualisation overlays (UI showing where arms are) — file under **stellata-io3** if wanted.
- Time-dependent or stochastic dust structure.

### Depends on
**stellata-ju3.1** (data source + scope must be settled).
### Related
**stellata-c7u.2** (same pipeline, Zucker MC overlay), **stellata-74e** (analytical-slab fallback at boundary), **stellata-cns** (more sightlines through arms once the bigger catalog lands).

title: Galactic arm dust: scoping + sightline-coverage analysis (research)
description:
**Goal:** produce the scoping note that drives implementation in sibling **stellata-ju3.2**. Spec context in parent **stellata-ju3**.

### Decisions to settle
- **Whole-galaxy vs near-Sol scope.** The likely answer is near-Sol for v1 (rendered-object sightlines mostly stay within ~1–2 kpc of Sol). Confirm with the sightline-coverage analysis below.
- **Data source choice.** Evaluate at minimum:
  - **Drimmel & Spergel (2001)** — analytical 3D dust with explicit arm components, COBE-DIRBE based.
  - **Marshall et al. (2006)** — 3D dust extinction from 2MASS, galactic plane to ~10 kpc, lower spatial resolution.
  - **Green et al. (2019) Bayestar19** — 3D extinction from Pan-STARRS + 2MASS, ~3 kpc.
  - **Lallement et al. (2022)** — high-res 3D dust beyond Edenhofer at ~3 kpc.
- **Voxel grid extent.** Whether Edenhofer's existing 1.25 kpc cube needs to grow; the chunking + manifest already supports a larger grid.
- **Frozen-data convention** applies — all candidates committed under `data/`, no live fetch at build.

### Sightline-coverage analysis
- For currently rendered objects (catalog stars + Zucker clouds + nebulae once 9hp lands), what fraction of sightlines passes through arm-overdense regions today missed by both Edenhofer (inside cube only) and the analytical slab (smooth axisymmetric)?
- Quantify the visual gap so the scope decision (near-Sol vs whole-galaxy) is data-backed.

### Deliverable
Short scoping note attached to the issue (or written into `docs/build-and-data.md`) covering:
1. Data source choice + retrieval steps.
2. Scope decision (near-Sol vs whole-galaxy) with sightline-coverage rationale.
3. Whether the voxel grid extents need to grow.

No code in this phase.

title: Hide-chrome toggle (body[data-chrome-hidden] + show-UI affordance)
description:
**Goal:** independent toggle that hides UI chrome (panel, top bar, overlays except canvas + maybe scale bar) for clean screenshots / presentation. Works regardless of fullscreen state. Spec context in parent **stellata-kip**.

### Scope
- Single `body[data-chrome-hidden]` selector hook is the cleanest pattern; verify existing layout containers cooperate.
- Reversible by same shortcut/control.
- Small unobtrusive "show UI" affordance (corner button or bottom hint) so users aren't trapped after hiding.
- Independent of fullscreen — composable in either order.

### Files
- `src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts` — shortcut.
- `src/client/help-modal.ts` — list the shortcut.
- `src/client/index.html`, `src/client/styles.css` — chrome targets (panel container, brand box, top-bar, overlays); add the "show UI" affordance.
- `src/client/url-state.ts` — natural home if state persists (single bit). Skip persistence if first-load with no UI is confusing.
- `src/client/brand-modal.ts` (or `controls.ts`) — visible toggle button.

### Acceptance
- Hide chrome via shortcut — UI disappears; canvas + scale bar remain.
- Restore chrome via same shortcut and via the visible "show UI" affordance.
- Help modal lists the shortcut.
- `npm run typecheck` and `npm test` pass.

### Out of scope
Browser fullscreen (sibling **stellata-kip.1**); kiosk auto-hide-on-idle; mobile-specific chrome rules.

title: Browser fullscreen toggle (Fullscreen API + Esc progression)
description:
**Goal:** Fullscreen API on the canvas container so panel + overlays stay visible inside fullscreen by default. Spec context in parent **stellata-kip**.

### Scope
- Wire `requestFullscreen()` / `exitFullscreen()` to a button (brand box or settings panel — decide during implementation) and a keyboard shortcut.
- Esc progression: pressing Esc exits fullscreen first (if active), then falls through to the existing observe→navigate fallback in `keyboard-shortcuts.ts:184`.
- On exit, layout returns exactly as it was — no layout drift.

### Files
- `src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts` — new shortcut + Esc precedence.
- `src/client/help-modal.ts` — list the shortcut.
- `src/client/brand-modal.ts` (or `controls.ts`) — visible toggle button.

### Acceptance
- Toggle works in Chrome, Safari, Firefox.
- On exit, layout matches pre-toggle.
- Help modal lists the shortcut.
- `npm run typecheck` and `npm test` pass.

### Out of scope
Hide-chrome (sibling **stellata-kip.2**); kiosk / auto-hide-on-idle; mobile-specific chrome rules.

title: Probes: runtime loader + rendering layer
description:
**Goal:** render the 5 probes in the scene at their position for the current `t`. Spec in parent **stellata-ywn**.

### Scope
- New `src/client/probe-loader.ts`:
  - Fetch all 5 JSONs in parallel (small enough — fire-and-forget at startup or lazy on first solar-system-layer activation).
  - For a given `t`, find bracketing entries and **linearly interpolate** x/y/z. Probes move ~3–4 AU/year — 30-day samples are visually indistinguishable from the true trajectory.
  - Hide probe entirely if `t < launchUnix`.
  - Visual flag for "extrapolated past last-contact": Pioneer 10 after 2003-01-23, Pioneer 11 after 1995-09-30. Render dimmer, append " (signal lost)" to label.
- Render layer (likely extends solar-system module or new `probe-system.ts`):
  - Same instanced-quad pattern as planets, sized small.
  - Always-labelled when visible (probes are a discovery feature).
  - Optional: thin fading trail showing trajectory between launch and current `t` (no future projection).
- Visibility gate: solar-system layer active (Sol-focused or unfocused at Sol).

### Acceptance
- All 5 probes render at plausible AU positions when Sol focused.
- Pioneer 10/11 dim and pick up "(signal lost)" label suffix when `t > lastContactUnix`.
- Hidden when `t < launchUnix` per probe.
- No probe at `t = 0` (1970-01-01) extrapolating backward from first trajectory entry.

### Comments to include in code
- Linear interpolation between monthly samples is fine for visualisation but is NOT a precision ephemeris. Prevent future-self from mistaking it for one.
- HORIZONS forward-projections past `now` are HORIZONS' projections, not certainties.

### Files
- New: `src/client/probe-loader.ts`, render module.
- Touch: `src/client/stellata.ts` (visibility gate + register layer), `package.json`, `.gitignore`.
- Post-landing: `CLAUDE.md` solar-system section gains probe data pipeline + last-contact handling.

### Depends on
**stellata-ywn.1** (data files must exist).

title: Probes: HORIZONS fetch + sync to public/
description:
**Goal:** static probe trajectory data committed under `data/probes/` and mirrored to `public/probes/` at build time. Spec in parent **stellata-ywn**.

### Scope
- New `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts` — manual `npm run fetch:probes`, **not** wired into default build chain (matches GCVS convention; `frozen-external-data` rule applies).
- Per probe (Pioneer 10 / -23, Pioneer 11 / -24, Voyager 1 / -31, Voyager 2 / -32, New Horizons / -98) query HORIZONS:
  ```
  https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/api/horizons.api?format=json
    &COMMAND=<id>&CENTER=500@10
    &MAKE_EPHEM=YES&EPHEM_TYPE=VECTORS
    &VEC_TABLE=2&REF_PLANE=FRAME
    &START_TIME=<launch>&STOP_TIME=2050-01-01&STEP_SIZE=30d
  ```
  `REF_PLANE=FRAME` returns ICRS equatorial directly — same frame as ATHYG. No ecliptic rotation.
- Parse `\$\$SOE…\$\$EOE` block, extract `JDTDB` and `X, Y, Z` (AU). Defensive on schema drift; clear error if `\$\$SOE` block missing or columns unexpected.
- Output `data/probes/{id}.json` per parent's schema (id, label, launchDate, launchUnix, lastContactUnix, trajectory[{jd,x,y,z}]).
  - `lastContactUnix`: `2003-01-23` for Pioneer 10, `1995-09-30` for Pioneer 11, null for active probes.
- ~225 KB total across 5 probes. Plain-text commit, no LFS.
- New `scripts/sync-probes.ts` mirrors `data/probes/` → `public/probes/` (matches `sync-dust.ts` pattern). Add to dev/build chains.
- Add `/public/probes/` to `.gitignore`.

### Acceptance
- `npm run fetch:probes` populates `data/probes/{voyager1,voyager2,pioneer10,pioneer11,new-horizons}.json` with valid trajectory arrays.
- `npm run dev` / `npm run build` propagates them to `public/probes/`.
- Schema spot-check: each JSON has `id`, `label`, `launchUnix`, `trajectory[]` with monotonic `jd` and finite `x,y,z`.

### Out of scope
Runtime probe rendering; that's the sibling **stellata-ywn.2**.

title: Calibration workflow: stars vs Milky Way vs planets across magnitude presets
description:
**Goal:** repeatable, documented calibration procedure that ensures stars, Milky Way background, and planets are correctly sized + brightness-matched relative to one another at each magnitude preset.

# Concern motivating the work

It's not clear that the volumetric Milky Way background's apparent brightness is correctly calibrated against the star field as the magnitude slider moves. A user dimming the magnitude cutoff to mag ~4 (suburban-sky preset) should see the MW band at a correspondingly dimmer integrated brightness — not just fewer stars on the same MW. Same concern for planets at host-focus when the slider moves.

# Visual ground-truth benchmark

ESO eso0932a panorama: https://cdn.eso.org/images/screen/eso0932a.jpg

Use this as the benchmark image for "what does the real sky look like at perceptually-good calibration?" Compare Stellata's render at each magnitude preset against an image-stretching of this panorama matched to the same magnitude cutoff.

# Deliverables

## 1. Documented calibration procedure (step-by-step)

A playbook (probably `docs/calibration.md`). For each magnitude preset (mag 6 / 5 / 4 / 3 etc):
- What scene to set up (camera position, FOV, observe vs navigate, mode selection).
- What target star(s) and MW region to reference.
- What ground-truth comparison image / known-star pair to match against.
- What knobs to adjust (in which order: stars first, then MW, then planets — or whichever ordering the doc settles on).
- Expected visual outcome — quantified where possible (e.g. star X should be brighter than MW patch Y by N×).

## 2. Debug-panel updates (likely)

The existing tuning surface lives in `src/client/star-tuning.ts`, `src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts`, `src/client/debug-panel.ts`. Calibration may need:
- A new "calibration" debug section that spotlights the cross-layer-affecting knobs (mag-cutoff, MW brightness multiplier, planet-disc floor, dynamic-range exponent) in one place rather than buried per-layer.
- Live readout of the current preset's numeric targets next to the sliders.
- A side-by-side compare HUD: scene render vs a reference image cropped to the same FOV.

Decide during design whether to extend the existing per-layer panels or add a unified calibration panel.

## 3. Per-layer relative-magnitude formula fixes (if revealed)

If calibration uncovers that the MW or planet apparent-magnitude formulas are off, file the fixes as children of this bead.

# Coupling

- stellata-f9b / stellata-v3j (magnitude presets as primary UX): the calibration workflow should feed back into what each preset's defaults should be.
- stellata-3re.16 (apparent-magnitude visibility cutoff for planets): planet calibration depends on this landing first or co-evolves with it.
- stellata-zsr (perceptual polish epic): calibration is the umbrella process; specific spectral-correction / dynamic-range work under zsr feeds into individual calibration steps.

# Out of scope

- Building per-preset auto-tuned coefficients. The output is a documented manual procedure Alex follows; automation can come later.

title: Design: classify renderable layers (Physical-visible vs Representational); define 3-mode UI surface
description:
Design gate for the three-mode framework epic. Until this lands, no implementation children should ship — the layer classification and UI surface need approval first.

# Deliverables

## 1. Layer classification table

For every renderable layer in Stellata today, classify as one of:

- **Physical-visible** — would be seen with human eyes from the camera's location.
- **Representational** — informational/inferential, not visible.
- **Style overlay** — chart-mode-only paper aesthetics. Orthogonal to the 3 modes.

Initial pass (verify in design):

| Layer | Category |
|---|---|
| Stars (point glow + disc) | Physical |
| Planet bodies (reflected light) | Physical |
| Galactic-MW volumetric band (integrated unresolved star light) | Physical |
| Effect of dust extinction on starlight | Physical |
| Heliopause shell | Representational |
| Planet orbit rings | Representational |
| Planet labels + apex labels | Representational |
| Time-readout, scale-bar, distance-vector, focus-ring | Representational |
| Constellation stick-figures | Representational |
| Molecular-cloud ellipsoids | Representational |
| Sol/GC SVG arrows, HUD ring, mode-toggle pill | Representational |
| Dust voxel debug visualisations | Representational |
| Chart-mode glyphs / isobars / hard-edged discs | Style overlay |

## 2. Per-layer mode behaviour matrix

For each layer, specify visibility in (Pure Visual, Standard default, Advanced default-on, Advanced user-controlled). Standard is Alex's curated set; Advanced exposes per-layer toggles.

## 3. Unfocused-host behaviour

For representational layers tied to a focused host (heliopause shell, orbit rings, planet labels, time-readout): hide when host unfocused. For physical layers (planet bodies): keep rendering, gated only by their own physics (apparent magnitude, etc.). This rule is implemented concretely by the unfocus-split bead.

## 4. UI surface design

Where the 3-mode selector lives in the panel. How Advanced exposes per-layer toggles. Couples tightly with stellata-f9b / stellata-1yu (settings reorg) — coordinate so the panel doesn't end up half-redesigned.

# Coupling

- stellata-f9b / stellata-1yu / stellata-v3j / stellata-iuh: settings panel rework. The UI surface for this lives there.
- stellata-zsr (perceptual polish): refinements like spectral correction / BC_photopic / limb darkening belong to Pure Visual / Standard *visual fidelity*, not to Advanced layer toggles. Clarify in the design doc.
- stellata-3re: solar-system layer hosts the first concrete applications.
- stellata-m40 (chart mode): orthogonal axis. Chart × any of 3 modes is a valid combination.

# Out of scope here

Implementation. This bead is the design doc — implementation children get filed once classification + UI is approved.

title: Apparent-magnitude visibility cutoff for planets (Sol planets + Pluto; generic to any host)
description:
**Goal:** treat planets like stars for visibility. Compute each planet's apparent magnitude as reflected light from its host star, then apply the existing magnitude slider as the cutoff. Drop the unconditional pixel-floor that keeps planets visible at any distance.

# Why

Planets don't emit light — they reflect their host star's. At parsec distances, even Jupiter (the brightest planet from Earth) drops below naked-eye visibility:

| View | Jupiter apparent mag (≈) |
|---|---|
| Earth at opposition | −2.7 |
| Just outside Sol's heliopause (~150 AU) | +5.2 |
| Alpha Cen (1.34 pc) | +21 |

Stellata already gates star visibility on apparent magnitude via the slider. Planets should obey the same rule.

# Apparent-magnitude formula

For a planet of geometric albedo `p` and equatorial radius `R`, with the viewer at distance `d_vp` from the planet, `d_vh` from the host, and `d_hp` from the host to the planet:

```
m_host_at_viewer = M_host + 5·log10(d_vh / 10pc)
m_planet         = m_host_at_viewer
                 − 2.5·log10( p · (R/d_vp)² · (d_vh/d_hp)² · φ(α) )
```

where `α = ∠(viewer–planet–host)` is the phase angle and `φ(α) = (sin α + (π − α)·cos α)/π` is the Lambertian phase function (3re.17).

# Treatment

- Reuse the perceptual-disc abstraction (`shaders/perceptual-disc.glsl`) — same brightness-PSF saturation physics for any point of light.
- Reuse the existing magnitude slider — no new control.
- Drop the unconditional pixel-floor on planet bodies; sub-cutoff planets fade naturally.

# Pluto

Already wired (PLANET_ORDER, ELEMENTS via Standish & Williams 2010, SOL_PLANETS). Albedo 0.49 added in this PR.

# Geometric albedos (Mallama 2018 / NASA fact sheets)

Mercury 0.142 · Venus 0.689 · Earth 0.434 · Mars 0.170 · Jupiter 0.538 · Saturn 0.499 · Uranus 0.488 · Neptune 0.442 · Pluto 0.49

# Generic to all hosts

Same code path renders planets for any star with a `PlanetSystem`. Forward-compatible with stellata-bk5 (exoplanets).

# Feeds chart mode

Output of this bead (per-planet apparent magnitude) is consumed by stellata-m40.3 (chart-mode planets at current `t`).

# Files actually touched

- `src/client/perceptual-magnitude.ts` (NEW), `shaders/perceptual-disc.glsl` (NEW): shared math chunk for the brightness → disc-pixel mapping.
- `src/client/planet-system.ts`: Planet gains an `albedo` field; populated for all 9 entries.
- `src/client/shaders/planet.vert.glsl` + `planet.frag.glsl`: full rewrite for apparent-mag math, three-pass parity (depth-mask + disc + glow), Lambertian phase.
- `src/client/star-system.ts` → renamed to `orbit-rings-layer.ts` with body code stripped.
- `src/client/planet-body-field.ts` (NEW): global instanced body field with per-host distance cull.
- `src/client/stellata.ts`: drop warpDestStarSystem; thread shared uniforms; wire recenter/maxAppMag to the body field.
design:
# Formula correction (REPLACES the formula in this bead's description)

The description's formula
    m_planet = m_host − 2.5·log10(p · (R/d_hp)² · (R/d_vp)²)
is dimensionally wrong (R appears twice). The correct Lambertian-
sphere formula has one R plus the viewer↔host leg ratio:

  m_host_at_viewer = M_host + 5·log10(d_vh / 10pc)
  m_planet = m_host_at_viewer
           − 2.5·log10( p · (R/d_vp)² · (d_vh/d_hp)² · φ(α) )

Verified against Jupiter (radius 69,911 km, p=0.538):
- Earth at opposition (d_vh=1 AU, d_hp=5.2 AU, d_vp=4.2 AU): −2.7 ✓
- ~150 AU outside heliopause (d_vh=150 AU, d_vp≈155 AU): +5.2 ✓
- α Cen (1.34 pc): +21 — the bead's stated +28 is incorrect.
The bead's heliopause-range estimate (+5 to +6) is correct. The
bead's α-Cen estimate (+28) is wrong; correct value is +21.

# Albedo data to add to SOL_PLANETS (Mallama 2018 / NASA fact sheets)

Mercury 0.142 · Venus 0.689 · Earth 0.434 · Mars 0.170
Jupiter 0.538 · Saturn 0.499 · Uranus 0.488 · Neptune 0.442
Pluto 0.49

Add `albedo: number` field to the `Planet` interface; populate.

# Pluto already wired

The bead's "Two options for v1" for Pluto's ephemeris is moot.
Pluto is already in PLANET_ORDER, ELEMENTS (Standish & Williams 2010
mean elements), and SOL_PLANETS (radius 1188 km, type 'rocky',
representative tan-pink colour). The only data missing for Pluto is
geometric albedo (0.49 above).

# Glow halo on planets (not atmospheric twinkle)

Drop the unconditional PLANET_DISC_MIN_PX=2.0 floor. Apply the same
brightness-driven sizing stars use (pxSize = max(physSize, appSize),
where appSize ∝ √(Δm / sizeSpan)). Add a glow halo pass mirroring
the star pipeline. The halo simulates ocular-PSF + display-saturation
spreading, NOT atmospheric scintillation. Same physics for any point
of light.

# Cross-cutting architecture

See parent stellata-3re --design for the full plan: PlanetBodyField
+ OrbitRingsLayer rename + drop warpDestStarSystem + shared
perceptual-disc abstraction + per-host distance cull + three-commit
ordering. This bead lands across commits 2 and 3.

# Files actually touched (revised)

- src/client/perceptual-magnitude.ts (NEW) — TS helpers.
- src/client/shaders/perceptual-disc.glsl (NEW) — GLSL chunk.
- src/client/planet-system.ts — add `albedo` to Planet, populate.
- src/client/shaders/planet.vert.glsl + planet.frag.glsl — apparent-
  mag math, three-pass parity (depth-mask + disc + glow).
- src/client/star-system.ts → OrbitRingsLayer (body-render code
  removed; that moves to PlanetBodyField).
- src/client/planet-body-field.ts (NEW) — global instanced layer.
- src/client/stellata.ts — wire attachHost / per-host cull.
close_reason: Landed in PR #45 (v1.4.0): planet apparent-magnitude pipeline + Lambertian phase + global PlanetBodyField architecture.

title: Unfocus host: keep physical objects rendered, hide representational layers
description:
**Goal:** when the focused host changes away from Sol (or any future planet host), the *physical* objects of that system stay rendered. The *representational* layers used to explain the system disappear.

First concrete application of the physical-vs-representational rule from the three-mode framework epic.

# Concrete behaviour

When a planet host (Sol now; any host with a `PlanetSystem` later via stellata-bk5) is no longer the focused star:

| Layer | Category | Behaviour when host unfocused |
|---|---|---|
| Planet bodies (`star-system.ts`) | Physical | Keep rendering, subject to per-planet apparent-magnitude cutoff (separate bead) |
| Heliopause shell (`heliopause.ts`) | Representational | Hide (Standard+ shows it only when host focused) |
| Orbit rings (`star-system.ts`) | Representational | Hide |
| Planet labels (`planet-labels.ts`) | Representational | Hide |
| Apex label (`heliopause.ts`) | Representational | Hide |
| Time readout (`time-readout.ts`) | Representational | Already host-gated, no change |

# Why

Planets exist whether you're looking at them or not. Current behaviour drops them when host is unfocused, which is physically wrong — and forward-incompatible with exoplanets (when looking at Alpha Cen with planets from Sol, you'd want to see those planets).

# Generic to all planet hosts

Implementation must not hard-code Sol. Use the `PlanetSystem` data model (stellata-3re.6) so any future host with planets gets the same treatment automatically.

# Files likely touched

- `src/client/stellata.ts` — focus state machine; no longer flips solar-system layer entirely off on unfocus.
- `src/client/star-system.ts` — split orbit-ring rendering (representational, host-gated) from planet-body rendering (physical, magnitude-gated).
- `src/client/heliopause.ts` — gate render on host-focused.
- `src/client/planet-labels.ts` — gate render on host-focused (probably already).
- `src/client/time-readout.ts` — already host-gated, no change.

# Watch out for

- The "Solar-system layer is only meaningful when focused star has planets … at all other focuses, the layer must not render" line in stellata-3re's description is no longer accurate. Update there as part of this work.
- Apparent-magnitude cutoff for planet bodies is a separate bead (its own implementation effort) — this bead is the gating split. **Land them together** if practical, otherwise apparent-mag must land first or planets will pop visible at unconditional pixel-floor from far hosts.

# Depends on

The apparent-magnitude planet visibility bead.
design:
# Implementation context

When focus changes away from any planet host, the representational
layers (rings, heliopause shell, apex label, planet labels) disappear;
bodies keep rendering via the new global PlanetBodyField, gated only
by apparent-magnitude visibility (3re.16) which in practice means the
per-host distance cull collapses them to invisible from any non-Sol
focus at default presets.

# Labels follow rings, not body apparent-mag cutoff

If the orbit ring for planet i is visible, the planet label for i is
visible — EVEN IF the body's apparent magnitude is below the slider
cutoff and the body itself isn't being drawn. Labels answer "what
would I be seeing here," not "what am I rendering." planet-labels.ts
:99-108 already gates on isOrbitRingVisible(i); preserve this. Do
NOT add a body-app-mag gate on labels.

# Cross-cutting architecture

See parent stellata-3re --design for the full plan: PlanetBodyField
+ OrbitRingsLayer rename + drop warpDestStarSystem + perceptual-disc
abstraction + per-host distance cull + three-commit ordering. This
bead lands as part of commit 3 (architecture refactor).

# Files actually touched (revised)

- src/client/planet-body-field.ts (NEW) — global instanced layer.
- src/client/star-system.ts → renamed/restructured to OrbitRingsLayer
  with body code stripped.
- src/client/heliopause.ts — visibility wiring already correct;
  verify it fires on focus-away-from-Sol.
- src/client/planet-labels.ts — verify (and preserve) the
  isOrbitRingVisible gate.
- src/client/stellata.ts — replace per-focus starSystem.setPlanetSystem
  with PlanetBodyField.attachHost(...) at startup; drop
  warpDestStarSystem entirely.
close_reason: Landed in PR #45 (v1.4.0): planet apparent-magnitude pipeline + Lambertian phase + global PlanetBodyField architecture.

title: Physical vs representational rendering — three-mode framework (Pure Visual / Standard / Advanced)
description:
**Status:** design framework. Captures a structural distinction Stellata needs to draw between *what you would see with human eyes* (stars, planets, the *effects* of dust extinction on starlight, etc.) and *structures we are aware of but cannot see* — too diffuse, too small, non-luminous, or outside the visible spectrum (heliopause / astrospheres, molecular-cloud bounding ellipsoids, planet orbit rings, labels, HUD).

**Goal:** every renderable layer is classified Physical-visible vs Representational, and the user controls them via a 3-mode selector that is independent of camera mode (nav/observe) and rendering style (chart/realistic).

# Three modes

1. **Pure Visual** — exactly what a human eye would see at the camera's location. No labels, no HUD, no orbit rings, no heliopause shell, no cloud ellipsoids, no constellation lines. Photons only. Intent: VFX-quality reference; screenshots that could pass for real astrophotography.
2. **Standard** — Alex's curated mix. Adds the representational layers most users find helpful: heliopause shell (when host focused), planet orbit rings, focus ring, Sol/GC arrows, scale bar, distance vector, time readout, constellation stick-figures, molecular-cloud ellipsoids. Interstellar dust voxel structure is *not* shown directly — only its extinction effect on starlight, which is physically visible.
3. **Advanced** — user picks layers individually. Per-layer toggles for everything in the representational set (orbit rings, heliopause, planet labels, MW volumetric band, cloud ellipsoids, constellation lines, dust voxel debug visualisations, etc.).

# Visual-perspective controls (orthogonal axis)

Independent of mode: apparent-magnitude cutoff, dynamic range, FOV, theme. These shape *how* visible things are rendered — not *which* things. They apply equally in all 3 modes.

# Analogy

Anatomy book. Pure Visual = a photograph. Standard = the artist's chosen labelled / cutaway figure. Advanced = layer-by-layer transparent overlays for studying structure in detail.

# Why this epic exists

Today the app mixes representational and physical rendering ad-hoc:

- The heliopause shell looks beautiful but isn't visible to human eyes (Voyager 1 detected it via plasma instruments — no photons emitted).
- The whole solar-system layer disappears when Sol is unfocused, which drops the *physical* planets along with the *representational* orbit rings.
- There's no path to a "no annotations" view for users who want a pure-photographic experience.

# Children (initial)

- Design gate: classify every layer, define the 3-mode UI surface, define unfocused-host behaviour. Filed as a child of this epic.
- Unfocus split (parented in stellata-3re, related to this epic): first concrete application of the rule.
- Apparent-magnitude planet visibility (parented in stellata-3re, related to this epic): falls out of "physical objects render per physics, not per pixel-floor".

# Coexists with chart mode

Chart mode (`stellata-m40`) is a *rendering style* — paper-atlas glyphs, isobars, hard-edged discs. Orthogonal to this framework. Chart mode should be combinable with any of the 3 modes (e.g. Pure Visual + chart = a Sky-Atlas-style realistic-magnitude rendering with no labels).

# Settings UX integration

Mode selection is the new top-level grouping for the settings panel. Couples with stellata-f9b's settings rework (mag presets primary, everything else Advanced) and stellata-1yu's contextual-by-camera-mode design — settings shown depend on (mode × camera mode). Coordinate the two designs so the panel doesn't end up half-redesigned.

title: Solar-system follow-ups: 4 AU park + about/credits + SCIENCE/README/docs
description:
Follow-up tasks landing in a single PR after the solar-system layer (PR #36 / stellata-3re) merged.

# What

1. Default first-load park distance for Sol drops to 4 AU (was TARGET_PARK_FRACTION-derived, ~0.11 AU). 4 AU sits just inside Jupiter's orbit so the inner planets frame the scene with Jupiter on the rim — the empirical "you are here" anchor.
2. About + Credits modal updated for the new layer: planets, heliopause, JPL Standish, NASA fact sheets, Voyager/IBEX, Frisch & Slavin 2013.
3. Welcome modal + HTML title rewritten around the empirical principle ("Stellata — An Observational 3D Model of Our Galaxy") — every object comes from observation; theoretical-only structures (Oort cloud, beyond-Gaia conjectures) are out by design.
4. README intro paragraph rewritten to match. Features list gains a solar-system bullet. Data-sources table gains JPL/NASA/Voyager rows.
5. SCIENCE.md gets a new "Solar system" section covering Standish ephemerides (and the VSOP87 rejection), planet physical data sources, orbital-plane orientation rule (Sol ecliptic vs other-host galactic plane), `t = now` model, and the heliopause asymmetric ellipsoid + apex direction.
6. New `docs/solar-system.md` topic doc following the per-feature doc pattern (milky-way.md, molecular-clouds.md). CLAUDE.md docs index points at it.

# Why

Solar-system layer (3re) shipped without doc / front-page / modal updates per the `defer-doc-updates` rule. This PR catches up: documents the new science in SCIENCE.md, surfaces it in the user-facing modals + README, and adds the per-feature topic doc.

The 4 AU first-load change is a UX adjustment driven by the same logic: now that planets exist, the prior auto-park (which sat the camera *inside* Mercury's orbit at 0.11 AU) hides the scene's headline. 4 AU surfaces it.

# Relates to

- `stellata-vjm` — broader "default first-load view" bead (Sol + Orion + HUD on + constellations on). This PR addresses only the distance piece. Whether the rest is still wanted is a separate decision.
- `stellata-3re` — solar-system layer epic, now merged.
notes: PR #38: https://github.com/alexmensch/stellata/pull/38

title: Kuiper belt visualisation layer — design + data sources
description:
Add a Kuiper belt layer to Stellata as the next outward feature after the heliopause (stellata-3re.5) and the bridge between solar-system scale and the interstellar layers (Local Bubble stellata-dk3, etc.). The KB sits at 30–50 AU classical, with scattered/detached/sednoid populations extending to ~1000 AU.

# Scope tension to resolve at design-gate

stellata-3re's description deliberately excludes "minor planets". KBOs are minor planets. Two reasonable resolutions:
1. Keep under 3re and amend the parent's deliberately-excluded list to read "asteroids and inner-system minor planets" (KBOs are an outer-system structural layer, not asteroid-belt content).
2. Split into its own layer epic (sibling of c7u, dk3, 9hp).

Recommendation: option 1 — the KB is part of the solar-system layer at the user's perception level (a ring of objects around the Sun), and splitting forces an epic for what is really one renderable structure. But this is a judgment call for Alex.

# What this bead captures

The data-source survey + structural design that any future implementation builds from. Research already done as of 2026-05-08 — see Design and Notes sections for the substance. Implementation-children to be filed once design-gate is approved.
design:
# Structural design — what to render

## Four overlays, not one

The "Kuiper belt" is four dynamical populations with different geometries. Render each as its own particle layer with separate uniforms:

1. **Cold classical disk** — thin annulus 42–47 AU, scale-height H/r ≈ tan(2°) ≈ 0.035, sharp outer truncation at the 2:1 resonance ("Kuiper cliff" at 47.7 AU). Near-circular orbits (e ≲ 0.1).
2. **Hot classical / scattering torus** — 30–80 AU radial extent (with tail to several hundred AU), H/r ≈ tan(15°) ≈ 0.27. Fat torus, almost a thick disk. Long sparse radial tail from high-e orbits.
3. **Detached / extended scattered** — a ≈ 50–250 AU, q > 40 AU. Sparse halo, not a disk.
4. **Sednoids** — q > 50, a > 200 to 1000+ AU. 4 known objects (Sedna, 2012 VP113, Leleākūhonua, 2023 KQ14). Render as individual orbits, not a continuum.

## Inclination distribution (Brown 2001 functional form)

`sin(i) × [f·N(σ_cold) + (1−f)·N(σ_hot)]` — two Gaussians. Best modern values (Gulbis et al. 2010, DES): σ_cold = 2.0° ± 0.5, σ_hot = 8.1° (+2.6/−2.1). Brown's original σ_hot ≈ 17° is contested; literature spans 6–17° depending on whether the high-i tail is Gaussian or power-law.
