Per-knob tweaking should be reserved for power users in an Advanced section.

Acceptance criteria:

1. Three named magnitude presets surface as the primary settings-panel control, replacing the current preset selector + exposed star-knob layout:
   - naked-eye (mag 6.5)
   - binoculars (mag 10.5)
   - telescope (replaces 'all', mag ~15 — adjust if perceptually better)
   Pick a clear segmented-control or large-button affordance so the choice is obvious; this is the basic-mode entry point.

2. Each preset drives the full bundle of viewing settings: maxAppMag, sizeMin, sizeMax, sizeSpan, starExaggerationK. PRESET_BASE + starExaggerationK[preset] already do this data-model wise (stellata.ts:96-147); the change is presentation — the per-knob sliders no longer surface in basic mode.

3. Advanced section (collapsed by default, sessionStorage-persisted open/closed state) exposes:
   - All star-rendering knobs already on the panel today (sizeMin/Max/sizeSpan, exaggeration K)
   - FOV slider (currently main panel — debatable; revisit during design)
   - Anything else that's a calibration knob rather than a viewing decision
   Reverse-sync (controls.ts:218-221, 259) must keep working through both basic preset selection and Advanced-section overrides.

4. Reset/override semantics still work: tweaking a value in Advanced sets the override flag (sizeMinOverridden etc.) — the basic preset chip should subtly indicate 'modified' if any override is set, with a one-click 'reset to preset' affordance.

5. New 'telescope' preset replaces 'all'. Tune sizeSpan / starExaggerationK separately; defaults today are { naked-eye: 12, binoculars: 9, all: 5 }. URL state encoding (url-state.ts) needs the rename handled with a back-compat decode for any 'all' tokens already in the wild.

Files:
- src/client/index.html (preset chips + panel structure around line 398)
- src/client/controls.ts (slider wiring + reverse-sync)
- src/client/stellata.ts (PRESET_BASE rename, MagPresetName type)
- src/client/url-state.ts (preset encode/decode + back-compat for 'all')
- docs/ui-and-controls.md (panel layout + magnitude-preset doc)

Related: stellata-iuh (Advanced-section reorg of star-size knobs — this bead supersedes it; close iuh once design is locked in here). stellata-1yu (nav-vs-observe contextual rework — orthogonal, can land before or after).

Out of scope: nav/observe contextual presentation (1yu); new telescope-only data fields beyond the preset itself (visual tweaks only).

title: Move star-size knobs into an Advanced section of the settings panel
description:
Today the right-side settings panel exposes power-user knobs at the top level: star size exaggeration K, sizeMin, sizeMax, sizeSpan (controls.ts:46-48, 178; index.html:398). These are calibration knobs — Alex sets them once to be right in most circumstances, and only someone who really wants to play with the rendering should tweak them. They don't belong alongside everyday controls like the magnitude preset and theme.

Deliverable: collapse star size exaggeration + sizeMin/sizeMax/sizeSpan (and any future star-rendering calibration knobs) behind an 'Advanced' section that is collapsed by default. Reverse-sync (controls.ts:218-221, 259) must continue to work whether the section is open or not. The reset buttons stay in their current places.

The new uSizeKnee uniform from stellata-a7d.2.7 is debug-panel-only (star-tuning.ts) and does NOT belong in the main panel at all — it's a rendering tuning knob, not user-facing.

Out of scope: the broader nav-vs-observe contextual rework (stellata-1yu). This bead is specifically the basic/advanced split for calibration knobs and can ship independently.

title: Default-load focus on Sol is non-functional (claims focused but can't zoom past unfocused floor)
description:
When the app loads with no URL params, the UI says Sol is focused, but the user cannot zoom in any closer than GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC (the unfocused-floor distance). A truly focused star should allow close-approach down to its per-star physical orbit floor (minOrbitDistForStar — 90% disc fill).

So either (a) Sol isn't actually focused on default load and the UI label lies, or (b) Sol is focused but some piece of state (TrackballControls.minDistance, the focusedStar guard, etc.) hasn't been updated to reflect the focus and is still applying the unfocused floor.

# Investigation entry points

- src/client/stellata.ts init / boot path — what does the UI use to render "focused on Sol"? Is focusedStar actually set to Sol's index on boot?
- TrackballControls.minDistance — when a star is focused, this should be set to minOrbitDistForStar(focusedStar). On default load, is it being set, or is it stuck at GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC?
- setFocus() vs the boot flow — boot may be skipping setFocus(solIdx) and instead just labelling the UI.
- url-state.ts:692 — how URL restore handles focus might be inverted from no-URL-params boot.

# Likely fix shape

Either (a) actually call setFocus(solIdx) on no-URL-params boot (which also fixes stellata-a7d.2.4's startup-distance unification — would naturally inherit minDistForStar(solIdx) and minOrbitDistForStar(solIdx)), or (b) wire the UI label to read from focusedStar so it stops claiming focus when there isn't one.

Probably (a) — "focused on Sol" is the desired default UX, just needs to actually engage focus state.

# Acceptance

- On clean-slate load: focused on Sol, can zoom in to minOrbitDistForStar(Sol) (~physical orbit floor for a Sol-class star).
- The pin (uPinFocusToCenter) engages and Sol stays at NDC (0,0) on close approach.
- UI label and behavior agree.

# Related

- stellata-a7d.2.4 (auto-park unification) — the startup-distance bullet there overlaps with this. Closing this issue likely involves the same code path; coordinate.
- stellata-a7d.2.1 (centering follow-up) — Sol-on-default-load centering was claimed fixed by 5035edf, but if focus wasn't actually engaging the pin couldn't have been the reason.

# Files

- src/client/stellata.ts (boot path, setFocus, TrackballControls config)
- src/client/url-state.ts (compare URL-restore vs no-URL boot)
close_reason:
Fixed in commit 0e018af. Two parts:

1. Constructor change: focusedStar = catalog.solIndex (raw assignment) → setFocus(catalog.solIndex). This wires up controls.minDistance to Sol's per-star floor; previously the unfocused GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC clamp stayed in place even though the UI claimed focus.

2. setFocus snap (deeper invariant fix): after recenterOrigin runs in setFocus(idx), snap controls.target to (0,0,0) and shift camera by the same delta. Preserves camera-to-target relationship (user-visible pose unchanged) while eliminating any residual on target. This was the actual root cause of the visible drift — Sol's catalog position is (5e-6, 0, 0) pc, not exactly origin, so recenterOrigin's bulk subtract left target at (-5e-6, 0, 0) and the pin guard (target.lengthSq < 1e-12) failed on the first frame. The same pattern protects every other setFocus(idx) call site (focusStar, finishWarp, setOrbitTarget) from analogous Float32-residual breakage.

Verified via temporary debug.pin() HUD: target.x lands at exact 0 on first load and pin engages immediately. User confirmed Sol stays centred under zoom-in.

Side effects of fix:
- Stellata-a7d.2.4 startup-distance bullet: still NOT fully addressed — camera still parks at (0,0,30) at boot, not minDistForStar(Sol). Focus engagement is now correct, just the boot distance is unchanged. Continue addressing under a7d.2.4.
- stellata-a7d.2.1 finishWarp residual fix (commit d6c97da) is now redundant given the setFocus snap; left in place as defensive code.

Also added in commit c9daa88: debug.pin() — a permanent diagnostic HUD with latched extremes for target/camera position. Kept in the codebase for future pin-related reports.

title: Smooth unfocus zoom-out transition (currently teleports to GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC)
description:
When close-zoomed on a focused star (e.g. Sol filling ~90% of viewport), unfocusing causes an instant teleport-out to GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC (5e-3 pc — see src/client/stellata.ts:170 and the unfocused-distance clamp). Other camera transitions in the app (warp, aim, observe entry/exit) animate over ~2s with easing; the unfocus jump is the outlier and reads as jarring against the rest of the UX.

# Expected

Unfocus from a close-zoomed state should animate the camera distance from its current value to GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC over ~2s with the same easing/feel as warp/aim/observe transitions.

# Investigation entry points

- src/client/stellata.ts:170 — GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC = 5e-3
- The unfocus path: search for setFocus(null) and any direct camera/controls writes that snap distance.
- Existing animated camera transitions to mirror style:
  - finishWarp / startWarp at stellata.ts:~1735, ~1841 (3-phase warp state machine)
  - aimAt at stellata.ts:~2004 (animated rotation)
  - observe-transition (yaw/pitch lerp into observe mode)
- docs/camera-modes.md describes the warp animation phases — reuse the easing curve / duration constants if defined there.

# Acceptance

- Unfocus from any close-zoomed state animates over ~2s (or matches whatever shared duration constant the other transitions use).
- Easing matches the warp / aim feel (no linear ramp).
- No regression: unfocus from already-far distances should be a no-op (no animation if already at floor).
- Cancellable: starting a new focus / warp during the animation interrupts cleanly without leaving the camera in a partial-transition state.

# Out of scope

- Tuning the floor itself (5e-3 pc) — separate concern.
- Changing what 'unfocus' means semantically (still: drop focusedStar, return controls to free-fly).

# Files likely touched

- src/client/stellata.ts (unfocus handler + new animation state)
- maybe docs/camera-modes.md if a new phase/state is added

title: Scale-bar UX revisit + camera-to-focus distance indicator
description:
User feedback during a7d.2 validation: "How do I know how far I am from a star? Does the scale at bottom left show angular distance or the distance the camera is from the focused object? It's actually really confusing." Needs design decision + implementation.

# Current state (src/client/scale-bar.ts)

- NAVIGATE mode (lines 31-44): shows physical width-of-space at the camera-target depth (e.g. "100 px = 0.5 pc at the focused star's depth"). It's the size of what you're looking at, NOT camera-to-target distance.
- OBSERVE mode (lines 21-30): shows angular FOV in degrees of sky.

So "neither pure angular nor camera-to-object distance" — it's "how big is what I'm seeing." Useful for "is this constellation 100 pc or 1000 pc across" questions but doesn't answer "how far am I from the focused star."

# Options (ask user before implementing)

A) Add separate "distance to focused {star/cloud}" text indicator. Keep current scale bar.
B) Replace navigate-mode scale-bar with camera-to-target distance. Loses "scale of what I'm seeing" info.
C) Both: keep scale bar, add a separate camera-to-focus distance line. Most info-dense; my recommendation in the prior conversation.

User did not commit to one yet. Default proposal C unless they redirect.

# Implementation hints (option C)

- Could repurpose distance-vector-overlay's label, which already shows distance to a vector destination — extend to show camera-to-focused-star when no vector is set but a focus is.
- Or add a small text element next to scale-bar in the bottom-left — fmtDist(camera.position.distanceTo(controls.target)) updated each frame.
- Use existing fmtDist (src/client/distance-util.ts) for unit-consistent formatting (pc/ly toggle).

# Acceptance

- User can read camera-to-focus distance at a glance in navigate mode.
- Scale bar still useful for "size of what I'm seeing."
- No regression in observe (degrees-of-sky reading).
- Updates each frame; debounced for stability if numbers flicker.

# Branch

worktree-a7d.2-physical-disc-geometry, PR #22.

title: Auto-deploy to Cloudflare on version-bump merges to main
description: Add GitHub Actions workflow that, on push to main, detects whether package.json#version changed vs HEAD~1 and if so: builds (catalog + clouds + dust-sync + client), deploys via wrangler, tags v<version>, and creates a GitHub release with auto-generated notes. Symmetric with version-guard.yml on PRs and folds in the manual RELEASING.md steps. Skip-version-bump label applied to this PR since CI workflow files don't ship runtime code.

title: Rename version-guard job key to match workflow name
description: Branch protection's required-check rule `version-guard` never matched because the reported check name was `check` (the job key under `jobs:`), displayed as `version-guard / check` in the merge box. BP matches the job name, not the slash-display string. Renaming the job key to `version-guard` (mirroring `test.yml`'s self-named pattern) makes the reported check just `version-guard`, restoring the user's original BP rule.

title: 61 Cyg: disc-edge falloff blends unnaturally where two star discs overlap
description:
Visual artifact observed in 61 Cyg A/B (and likely all close binaries with similar-sized discs and tight on-screen separation, e.g. α Cen) when the two discs are close enough on screen that their soft super-Gaussian edges intersect.

# What you see

User-reported in 61 Cyg with two screenshots:
- View 1: the smaller (B, K7V, absmag ≈ 8.33) and larger (A, K5V, absmag ≈ 7.49) discs partially overlap. Where their edges meet, the boundary forms a sharp lens-shaped intersection that reads as a seam rather than a natural blend.
- View 2: the discs are nearly tangent. Where their fuzzy outer falloffs touch, the *junction* brightens unnaturally — the additive contribution of two falloffs in the same screen pixel exceeds either source individually, producing a visible bright cusp at the contact point.

# What's happening (working theory)

The disc pass uses the unified super-Gaussian profile (see docs/rendering.md). Two overlapping disc instances both write to the same screen pixel, and the blend mode for the disc pass is *additive* on the falloff edge — the depth-mask core is opaque, but the outer ring contributes additively, so two falloffs sum.

For widely-separated stars this is invisible (no shared pixels). For tight binaries imaged near minimum-distance the falloffs overlap and sum.

# Fix directions to consider (not for this ticket)

- Switch the disc-edge blend from additive to a max() / replace, so two overlapping falloffs don't sum.
- Per-system depth ordering: opaque disc of the nearer star occludes the falloff of the farther star.
- Re-tune the super-Gaussian outer falloff so the blend region is sharper (less overlap zone in screen pixels).

# Repro

Focus 61 Cyg A in navigate mode, zoom toward minimum distance until both discs are visible side-by-side. Compare to a single isolated star (e.g. Sol) at the same fill-fraction — the edge does not look this way.
close_reason: Fixed in PR #25 — switched disc-pass blend equation to MaxEquation so overlapping fragments resolve per-channel max instead of summing premul-alpha. Eliminates the core depth-test seam, the halo cusp at near-tangency, and the same additive lift over the Milky Way.

title: FilterState patch type-safety hole at url-state.ts:659
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/url-state.ts:659 — the `patch` object passed to `setFilter` is typed as `Record<string, number | boolean>`. This defeats `FilterState`'s field-name type checking: typos in keys like `'minDistSol'` or `'sizeMinOverridden'` would compile silently.

Fix: Declare `const patch: Partial<FilterState> = {};` directly. All keys assigned are already `keyof FilterState`, so the Partial form will catch typos at compile time.

Related (smaller): src/client/main.ts:74 casts `(window as unknown as { stellata: Stellata }).stellata = stellata;` — better to declare the global in src/client/globals.d.ts and assign without the cast.

Severity: HIGH (real type-safety hole, single-file fix).

title: url-state: drop per-frame toFixed×9 string hash for change detection
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/url-state.ts:781-810 — `startUrlSync` `onFrame` callback constructs a 9-component string-formatted hash every frame via `toFixed(3) × 9` plus template literal. That is 10+ string allocations per frame just to detect change in the steady idle state.

Fix: Replace with component-wise epsilon comparison against scratch Float64s for cam/tgt/up. Schedule the URL update only on first non-equal check. No allocations on the no-change path.

Severity: HIGH (steady-state per-frame allocation, easy fix).
close_reason: Landed in PR #35 (v1.1.4): per-frame toFixed×9 hash replaced with Float64Array scratch + component-wise epsilon comparison. EPS=1e-3 matches prior toFixed(3) precision so threshold unchanged. Smoke pass per Alex 2026-05-07.

title: pickStar 313k-star linear scan: prefilter window + hoist constants
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/stellata.ts:2133-2178 — `pickStar` linearly scans all 313k stars on every hover (after 280ms throttle) and every click. For each star, it calls `renderedSizePx` (line 2237-2297) which re-reads ~10 uniforms and recomputes log/sqrt/pow per star.

Two improvements:

1. **Bound the scan** — the existing `sortedDistFromSol` prefilter used by the core-mask gate can window the scan: binary-search the slice `[f.minDistSol, f.maxDistSol]` and iterate only stars in that range.

2. **Hoist constants out of the loop** — `renderedSizePx` reads uniforms inside; in `pickStar`'s loop these are constant per call. Inline the disc-size formula with hoisted uniform reads, or pass an "ambient state" struct in.

Also flagged: `renderedSizePx` and `minDistForStar` (lines 2329-2350) duplicate the `physMinPx + logRatio*(physMaxPx-physMinPx)` math. The companion-separation block at lines 2306-2316 and 2342-2350 is also duplicated. Consider a private `_physSizeAtRef(idx)` helper.

Severity: HIGH (hover throttle masks the cost but click latency is user-visible).
close_reason: Landed in PR #35 (v1.1.4): pickStar windows the 313k scan via binary-search into sortedDistFromSol, dropping the per-star distSol sqrt + bounds check. Stale parts of the bead (physMin/physMax DRY, renderedSizePx uniform hoist) noted in commit message. Smoke pass per Alex 2026-05-07.

title: stellata.ts hot-path Vector3 allocations: adopt xxxInto(idx, out) pattern
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

stellata.ts: several helpers return a fresh `THREE.Vector3` and are called from per-frame paths:

- `starLocalPosition(idx)` (line 2093-2096) — called by overlays per frame, by HUD, by `pickStar`'s 313k loop, by `updateGalacticLayers` (line 2722), by `updateWarp` (lines 2784, 2802)
- `cloudLocalPosition(idx)` — same problem in cloud-warp paths
- `currentFocusLocalPos()` (line 1689-1696) — uses `.clone()`

Each call allocates a new Vector3. In steady-state this is 5-10 GC-eligible allocations per frame.

Fix: Add `xxxInto(idx, out: THREE.Vector3): THREE.Vector3` variants that write into a caller-provided scratch vector. Migrate hot-path callers (`animate()`, `updateGalacticLayers`, `updateWarp`, `pickStar`'s loop, HUD update). Either remove the allocating shims or keep them only for cold paths (initial focus selection, click handlers).

Severity: HIGH (steady-state GC pressure on the per-frame path).
close_reason: Landed in PR #35 (v1.1.4): added starLocalPositionInto / cloudLocalPositionInto non-allocating helpers; per-frame internal callers (animate HUD, updateWarp fly + post-arrival, getWarpInfo, renderedCloudSizePx) and external per-frame callers (chart-labels per-cloud loop, distance-vector-overlay, scale-bar) migrated onto scratch APIs. Cold paths keep the allocating shims, which now delegate to the *Into variants. Smoke pass per Alex 2026-05-07 (focus/HUD, warp end-to-end, chart labels, distance-vector cloud endpoints with Aquila Rift + Coalsack).

title: unit-toggle: hidden module-level singleton + redundant applyUnit shim
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/unit-toggle.ts has two issues:

1. **Hidden module-level singleton**: line 3 caches `buttons` at module scope. `bindUnitToggle` populates it. A second call to `bindUnitToggle` would silently overwrite the cache and abandon the previously-attached listeners — causing them to fire on the wrong button references. Currently called only once, so the bug is latent, but the API doesn't enforce single-call.

Fix: Capture `buttons` in a closure inside `bindUnitToggle`; pass it explicitly to `syncButtons`.

2. **`applyUnit` shim** (line 15): one-line wrapper around `setUnit`. It IS used by `url-state.ts:655`, so it cannot be deleted, but it's a redundant indirection.

Fix: Inline `applyUnit` callers to call `setUnit` directly, then delete `applyUnit`.

Severity: HIGH (hidden singleton is a real bug-in-waiting; wrapper is style).

title: observe-controls: missing pointercancel handler causes stuck drag state
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/observe-controls.ts handles `pointerdown`/`pointermove`/`pointerup` for look-around drag. It is missing a `pointercancel` handler. If the OS preempts the pointer mid-drag (touch interrupted by a phone call, system gesture stealing the pointer, browser cancelling the touch), `dragging` stays `true` and the next `pointermove` continues a phantom drag — sometimes with momentum applied.

Fix: Add a `pointercancel` listener that mirrors `onPointerUp` (probably without the momentum-promotion path — a cancelled drag shouldn't feel like the user released).

Severity: HIGH (real UX bug, hits mobile users specifically).

title: MolecularClouds.setIsobar: uniform reference mutation + missing blending restore
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

molecular-clouds.ts:117-129 (`setIsobar`):

1. `mat.uniforms.uMaxAppMag = magnitudeUniform` — replaces the `{value:...}` reference every call. If a caller passes a different shared uniform on a subsequent invocation, prior bindings are silently abandoned. Three.js convention is to mutate `.value`, not the wrapper object.
2. When `on === false`, `mat.blending` isn't restored. Clouds remain in their isobar blending mode after the toggle off, until `setMonochrome` is called again (which today only happens on theme change).

Note: the molecular-cloud overlay is shelved for v1 (CLAUDE.md), so this is latent — it will bite when the layer is re-enabled.

Fix:
- Set the uniform reference once at construction (or first call) and afterwards only mutate `.value`.
- Restore `mat.blending` to the appropriate non-isobar mode when `on === false`.

Severity: HIGH (latent bug — would manifest on chart-mode toggle once clouds are re-enabled).

title: dust-loader: validate buffer size + handle WebGL context loss
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

**dust-loader.ts:73-81** — When parsing a `particles.bin` file, the count check at line 73 only warns; a length mismatch causes either a Float32Array constructor throw ("byte length should be a multiple of 4") or an out-of-bounds view. The header `count` is taken from the `manifest.json` but the actual file size isn't validated.

Fix: After parsing the header `count`, assert `ab.byteLength === 16 + count * 16` and bail out (return null) on mismatch, instead of using `meta.count` as the source of truth for the allocation size.

**dust-loader.ts:226-258** — `uploadChunk` reaches into `renderer.properties.get(tex).__webglTexture` (undocumented Three.js private API). There is no fallback on context loss; the code logs and silently drops the chunk, leaving permanent holes in the dust grid.

Fix: Add `webglcontextlost` / `webglcontextrestored` listeners to the canvas; on restore, re-upload all already-loaded chunks from cached source data. Or at minimum surface the failure prominently.

Severity: HIGH (correctness on edge cases — bad files, context loss).

title: observe→navigate exit: camera roll snap proportional to look-around in observe
description:
When exiting OBSERVE → navigate after rotating the camera (yaw/pitch via drag) inside observe, there is a visible camera jump at the end of the exit lerp. The magnitude of the jump scales with how much the camera was rotated in observe mode.

# Likely root cause (from a quick code read — confirm during fix)

`stellata.ts:startObserveExit` (~line 990) translates the camera backward along its current forward vector by `minDist` — preserving the eye→target axis (target = star, at fromPos). `finishObserveTransition` then runs `this.controls.target.copy(state.fromPos); this.controls.update();`.

The translation is correct (camera and target are collinear with the rotated look direction, so lookAt's rotation about the eye→target axis would be a no-op). What jumps is **roll**: TrackballControls.update() runs lookAt(target), which uses `camera.up` to resolve the roll axis. The observe look-around controller pitches the camera quaternion without updating `camera.up`, so when lookAt re-aligns to `camera.up = (0,1,0)` it removes the accumulated pitch's tilt as a roll snap.

Reproduces on the deployed build — pre-exists the a7d.1 log-depth-buffer change.

# Suspected fix surfaces

- `src/client/observe-controls.ts` — keep `camera.up` consistent with the look quaternion as the user pitches; or
- `src/client/stellata.ts:startObserveExit` / `finishObserveTransition` — set `camera.up` from the current quaternion before re-enabling controls so lookAt is roll-stable.

# Verification

- Focus a star, enter observe, drag yaw + pitch noticeably, exit observe via the mode toggle. The end-of-lerp frame should match the previous frame's roll exactly — no snap.
- Repeat with the search-X clear path (`clearFocusOnExit=true`) and the no-animate hard-switch path (no focus).
- `npm test` + `npm run typecheck` clean.

title: Heliopause shell + label (drop scale rings — orbit rings cover the spatial-scale role)
description:
**Goal:** render the heliopause as an asymmetric wireframe shell with a single text label at the upwind apex. Provides spatial context for the inner solar-system view.

### Scope reduction (2026-05-07)

The original scope included faint reference rings at 1 AU and 50 AU. **Dropped per Alex's UX call** — the per-planet orbit rings from stellata-3re.7 already give the user a clear sense of solar-system scale (Earth's orbit is the 1 AU reference; Neptune's is at 30 AU). Adding more rings + labels would clutter the view without informational gain.

### Heliopause (kept)

Asymmetric ellipsoid, best-known shape per IBEX observations + Voyager 1/2 crossing data:

- **Apex (upwind, toward solar apex):** ~122 AU. Voyager 1 crossed at ~121 AU on 2012-08-25.
- **Flanks (perpendicular to apex direction):** ~115 AU. Voyager 2 crossed at ~119 AU on 2018-11-05 at ~67° from apex.
- **Heliotail (downwind):** ~200 AU. Recent IBEX/Cassini ENA data + global heliospheric models suggest 200–350 AU; the tail is the least-constrained direction. 200 AU is a defensible mid-estimate.

Apex direction: solar apex of motion through the local interstellar medium. ICRS approx **RA 17h53m, Dec +27.4°** (Frisch & Slavin 2013 LISM inflow direction).

### Implementation as a clean ellipsoid + offset

- Total length along apex–tail axis = 122 + 200 = 322 AU.
- Major semi-axis (along apex–tail) = 161 AU.
- Equatorial semi-axes (in flank plane) = 115 AU.
- Centre of ellipsoid offset from Sol by (200 − 122)/2 = 39 AU toward the heliotail (anti-apex).

Construction: `SphereGeometry(1, ...)` → wireframe → non-uniform scale `(115, 115, 161)` along its local axes → orient so local +Z is the anti-apex direction → translate by 39 AU along that direction. Convert AU → pc.

### Render style: wireframe mesh

Sparse latitude/longitude lines via `WireframeGeometry(SphereGeometry(...))` with low segment counts (~16x8). Very dim colour (matches existing dim chrome). Reads as 'boundary' rather than 'medium'. No backface culling concerns; both inside-out and outside-in viewing should look clean.

### Label

Text label `Heliopause` anchored to the upwind apex point, projected to screen via the existing SVG-overlay pattern (`planet-labels.ts` style). Same dim styling as other static labels.

### Visibility

Active only when solar-system layer is active (focused star = Sol per stellata-3re.6). Hidden in chart mode (matches the rest of the layer's contract).

### Files

- New: `src/client/heliopause.ts` (mesh + label).
- Touches: `stellata.ts` (instantiate + visibility gate), `index.html` + `styles.css` (label container + styling).

### Independent of `t`

Heliopause is static geometry — no time dependence on human timescales.

title: Sol planet bodies: correct diameters, type-aware disc edge, representative colour
description:
**Goal:** render Sol's 8 planet bodies as physically-sized discs with correct diameters, type-appropriate appearance (gas giant vs solid, Earth's atmosphere), and representative colour.

**Important:** orbital positions are placeholders in this task. Real VSOP87 positions backfill via stellata-3re.3 — the two are decoupled. This means a planet might initially render at any reasonable position around its orbit (e.g. at +x AU on its semi-major axis); 3re.3 lands later and replaces the placeholder with `getPlanetPositions(t)`.

### Disc sizing

Use `θ = 2·atan(R/d)` from stellata-a7d.2 (already merged). Each planet has a known equatorial radius in AU; convert to parsecs:

| Planet | R (km) | R (pc) |
|---|---|---|
| Mercury | 2,440 | 7.91e-11 |
| Venus | 6,052 | 1.96e-10 |
| Earth | 6,371 | 2.06e-10 |
| Mars | 3,390 | 1.10e-10 |
| Jupiter | 69,911 | 2.27e-9 |
| Saturn | 58,232 | 1.89e-9 |
| Uranus | 25,362 | 8.22e-10 |
| Neptune | 24,622 | 7.98e-10 |

Same `max(appSize, physSize)` floor as stars so planets stay visible at typical zoom levels even when truly sub-pixel.

### Type and appearance

Each planet record (in the `PlanetSystem` from stellata-3re.6) carries:
- `type`: `"rocky"` | `"gas_giant"` | `"ice_giant"` (Sol-internal categorisation; bk5 collapses ice/gas in its own enum)
- `colour`: representative RGB sourced from observed average tones (Mars rust, Jupiter banded yellow, Neptune blue, etc.). Single colour per planet — no banding / atmospheric scattering yet.
- `hasAtmosphere`: boolean. When true, the disc gets a faint additive halo (Earth, Venus, the giants).
- `solidity`: subtle shader difference between solid bodies and gas giants — gas giants get softer disc edges (super-Gaussian profile with lower exponent), solid bodies get hard-edged discs.

### Render gate

Only when focused star has planets per stellata-3re.6. Visible only when solar-system layer is active.

### Implementation

- New rendering module `src/client/solar-system.ts` (or extend if 3re.7 lands first).
- New shader pair, or extend the existing star shaders if instance attributes can encode the difference cleanly.
- Reads `PlanetSystem` from 3re.6.
- Reads positions from `getPlanetPositions(t)` once 3re.3 lands; until then, hardcoded placeholder positions per planet.

### Out of scope

- Lighting. v1 uses unlit / self-illuminated discs — planets are bright enough at the framing distances that real shadowing is not the visual story. Defer until needed.
- Surface texturing. Decided in stellata-3re.10 (texture research). v1 ships representative-colour discs unless 3re.10 says otherwise.
- Ring systems (Saturn, faint rings of the other giants). Defer.
- Moons.

### Depends on

- `stellata-3re.6` (the `PlanetSystem` data contract).
- `stellata-a7d.2` ✓ (true angular geometry — already merged).

### No longer depends on

- `stellata-3re.3` (positions). Decoupled — 3re.3 backfills real positions later.
notes:
Per stellata-3re.9 design (2026-05-07): planets render with always-on body-anchored labels. Name only. No hover, no click action in v1. Hidden in chart mode (m40.3 handles chart-mode planet labels separately). Per-planet labels also hide when their associated orbit ring is suppressed by the pixel-gap heuristic — keeps labels in lockstep with rings; bodies stay rendered at the pixel-size floor regardless.

Atmosphere halo + Earth-specific shading DEFERRED to stellata-2f6 (planet-as-object: select / focus / zoom) on 2026-05-07. Reason: at any reasonable host-relative viewing distance, planet bodies project below the pixel-size floor and clamp to a 2 px disc — Jupiter and Mercury are visually indistinguishable. Atmospheric haloes / banding / scattering / texture maps all depend on a close-zoom affordance the renderer doesn't currently support, so the v1 scope is the disc + colour + type-aware edge only. The full Planet schema dropped its hasAtmosphere field as part of this scope cut; restore it (and add per-planet texture / banding / scattering fields) when 2f6's design gate clears.

title: JPL Standish planet positions (ecliptic, per-frame caching)
description:
**Goal:** compute heliocentric ICRS positions for the 8 planets at any `t`. No network calls; fully offline; no new npm dependencies.

### Implementation: JPL Standish 1992 mean-elements + Kepler

Per-planet J2000 mean orbital elements (a, e, i, Ω, ω, M0) plus per-century linear secular rates, with cubic Jupiter–Neptune correction terms (b, c, s, f). Designed validity ±3000 years from J2000 to ~arcminute accuracy — well below the angular size of a billboarded planet disc at any zoom we render at.

Beyond ±3000 years the model degrades; see stellata-1gh for the deep-time research follow-up.

### Per-frame (when solar-system layer is visible)

1. `T = (tToJDE(t) - 2451545.0) / 36525` — centuries since J2000.
2. For each planet, compute current elements: `a(T) = a0 + ȧ·T`, etc.
3. Mean anomaly `M = L − ϖ` (modulo 2π); for Jupiter–Neptune apply the cubic Standish correction.
4. Solve Kepler's equation `M = E − e·sin(E)` iteratively for eccentric anomaly E (Newton's method, 5 iterations is plenty).
5. In-orbit cartesian: `x = a·(cos E − e)`, `y = a·√(1 − e²)·sin E`, `z = 0`.
6. Rotate by ω (in-plane), i (inclination), Ω (longitude of node) → heliocentric ecliptic cartesian.
7. AU → parsecs (`/ 206264.806`).
8. Caller (orbital plane orientation in star-system.ts) rotates ecliptic → ICRS by applying the J2000 obliquity around +x.

### Caching

Per-frame results cached, recomputed only when `t` changes meaningfully (e.g. rounded to 60-second buckets). At billboarded-disc scale, sub-minute time resolution is invisible. Time scrubber (stellata-nmu) updates `t` in user-facing increments that always invalidate the cache.

### Output

API `getPlanetPositions(t): { mercury: Vec3, venus: Vec3, …, neptune: Vec3 }` in **heliocentric ecliptic** parsecs (not ICRS — the orbital-plane-orientation quaternion in star-system.ts handles the ecliptic→ICRS rotation downstream, mirroring the per-host plane dispatch from stellata-3re.8).

Consumed by stellata-3re.4 (planet body rendering) — backfills the placeholder eccentric-anomaly positions currently shipped.

### Position of this task in the epic order

Lands **after** stellata-3re.4 (planet bodies) per the order Alex set on 2026-05-07. The rendering ships first with placeholder positions; this task backfills real ephemerides.

### Depends on

- `stellata-3re.1` for `t` + JDE conversion.

### Why JPL Standish over VSOP87 / astronomia

VSOP87 (sub-arcsec accuracy ±4000 years) is overkill for billboarded planet discs. The astronomia npm package would add ~500 KB gzipped to the bundle for accuracy that's invisible at the rendering scale. JPL Standish 1992 is ~50 lines + an 8-row element table, no dep, accuracy floor ~arcminute over ±3000 years — already sub-pixel for any zoom. See stellata-1gh for the deep-time follow-up if scrubbing past ±3000 years is ever in scope.

title: The 't' time variable: state, URL semantics, JD conversion
description:
**Goal:** introduce the wall-clock time variable `t` (Unix-seconds, double precision) that drives planet positions. Foundational for the rest of the solar-system layer.

### State

- Lives on the stellata filter state alongside other filter fields.
- Default at startup: `Date.now() / 1000`.
- v1 keeps `t` pinned to "now" (re-evaluated each frame, or once per second) — no scrubbing yet.
- Public setter exists from day one so the time-scrubber epic (stellata-nmu) can plug in without API changes.

### URL semantics

v1: `t` is never written to the URL (always "now"). The URL writer code path in `url-state.ts` should be wired but pass-through, so the time-scrubber epic just enables it.

Eventual contract (deferred to stellata-nmu):
- URL state writes `t=<unix>` only when the user has scrubbed away from "now".
- Absence in URL = "live, defaults to current wall-clock at load time."
- Shared link with `t=` preserves the sender's chosen date; without `t=` resolves to receiver's local-now.

### Conversion utilities

- `tToJDE(t: number): number` → `(t / 86400) + 2440587.5` (Unix-seconds → Julian Date in the TDB scale, accurate enough for VSOP87).
- "Live" detection helper: `isLive(t)` returns true when `|t - Date.now() / 1000|` is below a small tolerance — used by stellata-3re.11's readout to label "Live" vs an explicit timestamp.

### Out of scope

- The scrubber UI (`stellata-3re.2`, now in stellata-nmu).
- The static readout (`stellata-3re.11`).
- Variable-star animation continues using its independent `uTime` cosmetic clock — `t` and `uTime` are orthogonal.

title: Unified nebulae data ingest pipeline (PNe + RNe + H II + SNR)
description:
**Goal:** produce `public/nebulae.json` from the four catalogues, filtered to within ~3 kpc to match the dust volume, with consistent per-nebula schema.

**Sources** (full details in parent `stellata-9hp`):
- Strasbourg-ESO PNe (Acker et al., VizieR V/84) — ~1,500 PNe with multiple distance estimates
- Magakian RNe (J/A+A/399/141) — 913 reflection nebulae, illuminator cross-match needed
- Sharpless H II (VII/20) — 313 entries, pair with Avedisova (V/115) for distances
- SIMBAD TAP query as the unified fallback for object types PN/RNe/HII/SNR

**Per-nebula schema:** ID, common name, type (PN|RNe|HII|SNR), RA/Dec, distance, → xyz, angular size → physical radius, illuminating star ref (RNe only).

**Output:** new `scripts/build-nebulae.py` (mirroring `build-clouds.py` pattern) emitting `public/nebulae.json`. Add to `.gitignore`. Wire into `dev` and `build` scripts.

**Watch out for:**
- Drop entries with no parsed distance
- Filter to ATHYG-relevant volume; >5 kpc clutters search without rendering usefully
- RNe illuminators: parse HD/HIP from source, leave the cross-match to `stellata-9hp.4`

title: A.1 Research + design doc: physics model for molecular cloud rendering
description:
**Goal:** produce the implementation design before any rendering code is written. Output is a written model in `docs/molecular-clouds.md` (replacing the current shelved-state blurb) that drives phases A.2–A.7.

**Research scope:**
- **Extinction physics**: τ = ∫κ·ρ dl. Map Zucker bbox + assumed density to optical depth at V band. Calibrate κ × ρ_total so a typical dense cloud (e.g. Taurus core) produces realistic Av (~5–25 mag). Sanity-check against published Av values for known clouds.
- **Selective extinction (reddening)**: confirm RV ≈ 3.1 for diffuse ISM; note RV ≈ 5.5 for dense cloud cores. Cardelli-Clayton-Mathis (1989) parameterisation. Decide which RV to use (single value, or per-cloud-type, or per-cloud).
- **Density structure**: log-normal PDF (Vazquez-Semadeni 1994; Padoan, Nordlund & Jones 1997) + power-law tail at high densities (Federrath & Klessen 2013). Mach-number dependence. Filamentary substructure (Hi-GAL, Herschel filament catalogues).
- **Cloud taxonomy**: dark cloud vs GMC vs SF region. Which Zucker entries fall in each class? Use known classifications (Taurus dark-cloud, Orion A active SF, etc.) where available; default behaviour for unclassified.
- **Embedded young stars + cavities**: Strömgren-sphere radius for an O/B star in a cold MC, scaling with stellar luminosity and ambient density. Stellar-wind bubbles. How sharp is the cavity edge?
- **HII regions / reflection nebulosity**: when does an embedded star produce visible emission/scattering? Threshold spectral type / luminosity.
- **Inside-cloud experience**: what does a real observer at the centre of Taurus see? Reddened/dimmed background, very dark ambient — confirm this is the correct mental model.

**Design output (written into `docs/molecular-clouds.md`):**
- The per-cloud parameter set we'll bake at build time (centroid, semi-axes, peak density, density-profile-shape, embedded-star list, cavity radii).
- The shader-side model for the substructure noise (octaves, frequency, amplitude).
- The reddening model (B vs R extinction ratio).
- The integration strategy with the Edenhofer voxel field (replace, blend, or augment).
- Decision on whether the shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` warm-glow becomes A.6 or is replaced.
- Concrete equations, constants, and source citations.

**No code in this phase** — but should produce enough specificity that A.2–A.7 implementers can work without ambiguity.

title: Physical molecular cloud rendering — extinction, reddening, density, embedded-star cavities
description:
**Goal:** render the ~96 Zucker MCs with physically-grounded extinction, reddening, density structure, and cloud-type variation — the experience of actually flying through these clouds, to the best of current scientific knowledge. Plus a *very* subtle stylised "presence" so an empty-sky background still hints at cloud shape.

**Current state:** `cloud.frag.glsl` is decorative — `pow(|n·v|, 1.5)` × warm colour, additive blend. No extinction, no reddening, no real density. The layer is currently shelved (`FilterState.showMolecularClouds = false`). Re-enables once this epic lands.

**Decisions captured (from scoping discussion):**

1. **Realism-inspired.** Physically-correct extinction + reddening of background light is the primary visual. A *very subtle* stylised hint of cloud shape rides on top so users can perceive clouds against starless backgrounds. Stylisation must read as decorative, never compete with the physical signal.

2. **Procedural substructure** acceptable for v1. We won't search for higher-resolution per-cloud data products as part of this epic. (Separate future issue is filed for that as a follow-up.)

3. **Inside-the-cloud experience** should follow physics: stars whose line of sight traverses the cloud dim and redden, peripheral stars are unaffected, ambient gets very dark. Deep-research piece: surface ideas like *young massive stars carving low-density cavities via stellar winds and ionising radiation* — i.e., cloud density isn't a static envelope but is sculpted by embedded stars (Strömgren spheres, HII regions). Investigate whether modelling embedded O/B stars as low-density cavity centres with a radial density drop-off produces a believable inside-cloud experience.

4. **Cloud-type / age variation.** Model differently based on whether the cloud hosts young stars. Spectral type → age proxy gives a rough cloud-region age. HII regions, reflection nebulosity, and cavity-carving should be type-dependent (e.g., a quiescent dark cloud renders differently from an Orion-Nebula-class active SF region).

5. **Architecture: Zucker replaces Edenhofer in overlap regions.** In practice this likely means modifying the dust voxel volume at build time (`scripts/build-dust.py` + `manifest.json`) so the per-star extinction raymarch in `star.vert.glsl` becomes the single source of truth for absorption — Zucker supplies the higher-fidelity local density structure. Avoids double-counting.

**Phases (filed as child issues):**

- **A.1** Deep research + design doc — produce the physics model we'll implement and the equations/constants/references.
- **A.2** Build pipeline — bake Zucker density into the Edenhofer voxel grid at build time.
- **A.3** Wavelength-selective extinction in `star.vert.glsl` (B vs R, RV ≈ 3.1) — current path appears single-channel; confirm and add reddening.
- **A.4** Procedural density substructure (3D fBm/Worley noise shaped by the ellipsoid envelope, modulating density inside the bbox).
- **A.5** Embedded young-star cavities + cloud-type variations (cross-match O/B + YSO catalogues with cloud bboxes; reduced density near hot hosts; optional HII emission for the hottest).
- **A.6** Subtle stylised "presence" pass — re-frame or supersede the shelved warm-glow renderer; very subtle by design.
- **A.7** Inside-the-cloud immersion + ambient response tuning — confirm flying into Taurus reads as physics.

**Scope notes:**
- The currently shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` may be re-purposed as A.6's stylised presence or replaced — design-doc decision in A.1.
- Re-enables `FilterState.showMolecularClouds` (and possibly the URL flag) once the epic lands.
- No hard dependencies on other open issues.

**References to seed research:**
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_cloud
- Cardelli, Clayton, Mathis (1989) — selective-extinction law, RV ≈ 3.1
- Edenhofer et al. (2024) — current dust voxel field that this epic augments
- Zucker 2020 / 2021 — the cloud catalogue already in use (`data/molecular-clouds/`)
- Standard MC astrophysics: log-normal density PDF + power-law tail, Strömgren-sphere geometry, GMC vs dark-cloud taxonomy

title: Default first-load view: park near Sol with planets, Orion, and HUD on
description:
Replace the current "no URL state → drop the user into a generic starfield at `(0, 0, 30)`" default load behaviour with a deliberately framed first-impression view.

# Why

Stellata's first three seconds need to do emotional work. Today when someone loads the bare URL — no `cam=`, no `to=`, no view state — they see a vast unlabelled starfield with no anchor: no obvious "you are here," no implicit prompt to explore. Pretty, but inert.

A first-load that parks the camera near Sol with the Sun's disc clearly visible — at a distance that also frames the inner-solar-system planet orbits and a recognisable constellation in the background (Orion is the obvious pick — everyone in the northern hemisphere recognises the belt; equatorial latitude makes it visible from most of Earth) — gives the visitor an immediate story:

> *That's our Sun. Those are our planets. Orion is over there. Now look around.*

That single framing converts "what is this?" into "where do I want to go?" — and the rest of the app's exploration affordances (warp, focus, observe mode) follow naturally.

# What changes

When the URL has no view state on initial load:

1. **Camera focus = Sol** with disc clearly resolved.
2. **Camera distance** in the regime where the inner-solar-system planet orbits (Mercury through Mars at minimum, Jupiter ideally) are simultaneously visible. Probably tens of AU.
3. **Camera direction** chosen so Orion sits prominently in the background — likely centred, but a slightly off-centre framing might feel less staged. Defer the exact aim choice to implementation.
4. **HUD on** (`hud-ring` + coord sphere) so the first-time visitor immediately gets a sense of the celestial reference frame.
5. **Constellation overlay on** so Orion's stick figure is visible without the user having to discover the toggle.
6. **URL stays empty** until the user interacts. The default doesn't write itself to the URL — the share-link-equals-current-pose contract still holds; first interaction is the first URL write.

When the URL *does* carry view state, behaviour is unchanged.

# Why this is blocked by the solar-system layer (and transitively by the rewrite)

- Sol must be a focusable target with a sensible close-approach floor. Today `controls.minDistance = 0.005 pc` (≈1031 AU) means we cannot park anywhere near tens-of-AU range from Sol. **`stellata-3re`** introduces the Sol-focus minDistance relaxation that makes this regime reachable.
- The Sun's disc must render at correct angular size at close range. **the physical-scale rewrite (`stellata-a7d`)** — which the solar-system layer already blocks on — replaces today's log-mapped pixel formula with `θ = 2·atan(R/d)`. Without it, the Sun's on-screen disc at the chosen distance won't reflect physical reality.
- Planet orbits to draw rings around come from **the solar-system layer**.

This issue is the user-visible payoff of the physics + solar-system stack: the moment those two land, this becomes the natural default.

# Implementation sketch

- A new module (or a small block in `stellata.ts` bootstrap) decides the initial camera state when `url-state.ts` reports no view fragment present.
- Fixed values, hard-coded for the default — not a slider or preset:
  - Focus: Sol (catalog index TBD; the solar-system layer will have established how Sol is referenced).
  - Distance: a single tuned constant (e.g. `~30 AU = ~1.45e-4 pc`) chosen so:
    - Sun's disc reads as a clear orb (R_Sol ≈ 4.65e-8 pc → θ ≈ 6.4e-4 rad ≈ 2.2 arcmin → at 50° FOV in a 1080-tall viewport that's ~24 px diameter — comfortably resolved).
    - Mars's orbit ring (1.52 AU) fits well inside the framing.
    - Jupiter's orbit ring (5.2 AU) likely also visible toward the edge.
  - Aim: a unit vector toward Orion's centroid (RA ≈ 5h 30m, Dec ≈ +0°). Compute once at startup or hard-code as an ICRS vector constant.
- HUD: ensure the existing HUD-on default isn't overridden by the new bootstrap path.
- Constellation overlay: same — turn on for Orion specifically, or all constellations (probably all, with Orion happening to be the focus of the framing).

# Open questions / deferred to implementation

- Whether to nudge the camera into a slow drift on first load (an autonomous "look, the world is alive" gesture) or leave it static. Lean static — drift is a separate Wander-mode-adjacent feature (`stellata-8r1`), and a static frame respects the user's first input as the start of *their* experience.
- Exact distance + aim — needs a screenshot pass once the solar-system layer lands so we can pick numbers that feel right rather than just compute correctly.
- Whether the chart-mode default should differ. Probably no — chart mode is opt-in and inherits the same view.
- Mobile framing — viewport aspect changes the Orion centring story. `stellata-47c` (mobile UX audit) may need to feed back into this.

# Acceptance

1. Loading `stellata.app/` with no query string parks the camera at the framed Sol view.
2. Sol's disc is clearly visible (resolved, not a point glow).
3. At least the inner planet orbits (Mercury → Mars) are visible as orbit rings.
4. Orion's belt is visible and recognisable in the background, with the constellation stick-figure overlay drawn.
5. The HUD ring + coord sphere are on.
6. The URL bar shows no view-state parameters until the user interacts (drags, clicks a star, opens a modal that writes state, etc.).
7. Loading with view-state in the URL bypasses the framed default entirely — existing behaviour unchanged.

# Related work

- Blocked by `stellata-3re` — provides Sol-as-focus + planet orbits.
- Transitively depends on the physical-scale rewrite (`stellata-a7d`) via 12a — provides Sol's disc at correct angular size.
- Mobile layout caveat — `stellata-47c` may want to revisit framing on small viewports.
- Wander mode (`stellata-8r1`) is a separate idea; this issue deliberately stays static on first load.
close_reason: Shipped in PR #39 (merge daaeffc). First-load module parks camera at 5 AU aimed at the galactic centre, HUD on. Replaces the stellata.ts constructor 4 AU baseline.

title: Enable log depth buffer + add <logdepthbuf> chunks to all shaders
description:
Prerequisite for the disc-formula rewrite. With per-star physical sizing, `d_min` for Sol-class stars drops to ~5×10⁻⁸ pc. Standard z-buffer precision collapses long before that. Without log depth buffer, the rewrite will z-fight on close approach.

# Scope

- `stellata.ts:468` — set `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` on the `WebGLRenderer` constructor.
- `stellata.ts:483-488` — drop `camera.near` to `1e-10` (log depth handles the dynamic range).
- Add the standard log-depth chunks to every custom GLSL3 ShaderMaterial:
  - `src/client/shaders/star.{vert,frag}.glsl`
  - `src/client/shaders/cloud.{vert,frag}.glsl`
  - `src/client/shaders/dust-particle.{vert,frag}.glsl`
  - `src/client/shaders/milkyway.{vert,frag}.glsl`

  Each pair needs:
  - **vert pars**: `#include <common>` + `#include <logdepthbuf_pars_vertex>`
  - **vert main** (after `gl_Position` is set): `#include <logdepthbuf_vertex>`
  - **frag pars**: `#include <common>` + `#include <logdepthbuf_pars_fragment>`
  - **frag main** (early): `#include <logdepthbuf_fragment>`

  All four pairs already use `THREE.ShaderMaterial({ glslVersion: THREE.GLSL3, … })` — three.js will inject the chunks correctly under GLSL3 (verified in `stellata.ts` and `milkyway.ts`).

# Verification

No visible change is expected. Run `npm run dev` and confirm:
1. Default view looks identical to `main`. Diff a screenshot if in doubt.
2. Warp / observe / chart mode still render correctly.
3. Molecular clouds, dust particles, milky way all draw in the right depth order with no new banding.
4. `npm run typecheck` clean; `npm test` green.

# Acceptance

- `WebGLRenderer` constructed with `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true`.
- All 8 shader files include the log-depth chunks.
- `camera.near` lowered to `1e-10`.
- No visual regressions vs. `main`.

# Reference

Three.js docs: https://threejs.org/examples/#webgl_camera_logarithmicdepthbuffer
Plan file: `~/.claude/plans/resume-planning-from-users-alexm-claude-reflective-wombat.md`
close_reason: Landed in PR #7 (commit e6868e2). Verified: typecheck clean, 244 tests pass, runtime shader compile + render confirmed by user across navigate/observe/chart modes.

title: Fullscreen mode + optional 'hide chrome' toggle
description:
Two related affordances filed in one issue because they both serve the same end (immersive / presentation / screenshot use):

1. Browser fullscreen — wire the Fullscreen API to a button in the brand box (or settings panel — decide during implementation) and a keyboard shortcut. Toggling enters/exits fullscreen on the canvas container so the panel and overlays are still visible inside fullscreen by default.

2. Hide chrome — a separate toggle that hides UI chrome (panel, top bar, overlays except the canvas itself + maybe scale bar) so the user can grab a clean shot or present without the UI. This works independently of fullscreen — you can hide chrome without going fullscreen, and vice versa.

Critical files:
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts — Esc progression at line 184 (must precede the existing observe→navigate fallback when fullscreen is active); add the new shortcut(s) here.
- src/client/help-modal.ts — must list both shortcuts.
- src/client/index.html, src/client/styles.css — chrome targets (panel container, brand box, top-bar, overlays). A single body[data-chrome-hidden] toggle is the cleanest selector hook; confirm during implementation that the existing layout containers cooperate.
- src/client/url-state.ts — natural home for state persistence (single bit each in the URL flag set). Skip persistence if it causes a confusing first-load (user lands on a page with no UI).
- src/client/brand-modal.ts (or controls.ts) — wherever the user-visible toggle button lands.

UX:
- Pressing Esc exits fullscreen first (if active), then falls through to the existing Esc progression in keyboard-shortcuts.ts:184.
- "Hide chrome" should be reversible by the same shortcut/control; show a small unobtrusive "show UI" affordance (corner button or bottom hint) so users aren't trapped.

Verify before close:
- Fullscreen toggle works in Chrome, Safari, Firefox.
- On exit, layout returns exactly as it was.
- Hide-chrome restores cleanly via shortcut and via the visible "show UI" affordance.
- Help modal lists both shortcuts.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.

Out of scope: any kiosk/auto-hide-on-idle behaviour, mobile-specific chrome rules (those land under the mobile epic).

title: 'F' shortcut: find a star and point camera at it (no warp)
description:
Add a keyboard shortcut F that opens the same search modal used by G, but instead of warping the camera to the chosen star, only orients the camera at it.

Behaviour by mode:
- Observe: rotate the camera (yaw + pitch) to point at the selected star. Animate the rotation rather than snap. FOV unchanged. Camera position unchanged.
- Navigate: orient the orbit gaze toward the star without travelling — i.e. rotate the trackball target so the star sits at the centre of the view, but do NOT warp the camera position to it (that's G's job) and do NOT mark it as focused (no focus-ring, no disc-mask). The animation should match observe-mode feel.

Critical files & reuses:
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts — existing G binding at line 89; add the F case adjacent. Modal-open + editable-target guards already in place around line 80.
- goModal (instantiated in keyboard-shortcuts.ts) — confirm during implementation that it can be parameterised by intent ("warp" vs "look") rather than duplicated.
- src/client/search.ts — the search/typeahead behind the modal.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — yaw/pitch source for the observe rotation animation.
- src/client/stellata.ts — trackball target / orbit gaze; the navigate-mode "swing target without travel" lives here.
- src/client/help-modal.ts — must be updated to list F.

UX:
- Pressing F while a modal is already open is a no-op (existing modal-open guard handles this).
- Esc cancels the search modal as today.

Verify before close:
- In observe, F → pick a star → camera smoothly rotates to it.
- In navigate, F → pick a star → orbit gaze swings to it; camera position unchanged; no focus indicators activate.
- npm run typecheck and npm test pass.
- Help modal shows F.

Out of scope: changing what G does. Adding F to the panel as a button (keyboard-only is fine for v1).

title: Design: rework settings panel to be contextual to camera mode (nav vs observe)
description:
The right-side settings panel today shows a single static control set regardless of which mode the user is in. Navigate and observe expose different affordances and want different things at the user's fingertips, so the panel ends up cluttered for both. This is a usability/UX issue: the user wants ready access to mode-relevant controls without scanning the whole panel.

Deliverable: a written design spec (in docs/ or attached to this issue) covering, at minimum:
- For each mode, which controls are primary vs secondary vs hidden.
- Layout: are mode-specific sections inserted/removed, or shown via tab/segmented control, or via collapse-by-default rules?
- Behaviour on mode-switch — animation, scroll position, collapse memory.
- Are there observe-mode controls that don't yet exist and should be added (e.g. FOV preset buttons, label-density slider) — flag them for separate feature issues, do not add scope here.
- Reverse-sync implications: today the panel reads back from Stellata.getFilter()/state; the design must keep that contract intact.

Critical files to read before designing:
- src/client/controls.ts — every existing widget + reverse-sync.
- src/client/panel-layout.ts — top-level + per-group collapse behaviour the redesign must respect or replace.
- src/client/mode-toggle.ts — the navigate↔observe pill that triggers the mode change the panel needs to react to.
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — affordances that exist only in observe (drag, FOV wheel) and should inform what observe surfaces in the panel.
- docs/ui-and-controls.md — documents existing layout containers, magnitude presets/override flags, CSS gotchas ([hidden] specificity, backdrop-filter stacking contexts).

Out of scope: implementation. Once the design is approved, file an implementation issue and close this one.

title: Deep-space probes
description:
**Status:** Depends on the solar-system layer's time-scrubber framework.

**Goal:** render the five interstellar probes — Pioneer 10, Pioneer 11, Voyager 1, Voyager 2, New Horizons — at their actual positions for the current `t`. Visible only when solar-system layer is active. A small but emotionally significant addition: "you can see how far they've gotten" against the scale of the solar system and the nearest stars.

### Probe data via NASA HORIZONS

| Probe        | Launch     | HORIZONS ID | Status                |
|--------------|------------|-------------|-----------------------|
| Pioneer 10   | 1972-03-03 | `-23`       | Silent since 2003-01-23 |
| Pioneer 11   | 1973-04-06 | `-24`       | Silent since 1995-09-30 |
| Voyager 2    | 1977-08-20 | `-32`       | Active                |
| Voyager 1    | 1977-09-05 | `-31`       | Active                |
| New Horizons | 2006-01-19 | `-98`       | Active                |

### Build script: `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts`

**Manual** — `npm run fetch:probes` only, not part of the default `build` chain. Same convention as the GCVS files (committed once, refreshed when upstream updates).

Per probe, query HORIZONS:
```
https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/api/horizons.api?format=json
  &COMMAND=<id>
  &CENTER=500@10
  &MAKE_EPHEM=YES
  &EPHEM_TYPE=VECTORS
  &VEC_TABLE=2
  &REF_PLANE=FRAME
  &START_TIME=<launch date>
  &STOP_TIME=2050-01-01
  &STEP_SIZE=30d
```

`REF_PLANE=FRAME` returns ICRS equatorial directly — same frame as ATHYG and the rest of the app. No ecliptic rotation needed.

Parse the `$$SOE…$$EOE` block, extract `JDTDB` and `X, Y, Z` (in AU) per step. Output `data/probes/{id}.json`:
```json
{
  "id": "voyager1",
  "label": "Voyager 1",
  "launchDate": "1977-09-05",
  "launchUnix": 242265600,
  "lastContactUnix": null,
  "trajectory": [
    { "jd": 2443413.5, "x": 0.12, "y": -0.03, "z": 0.01 },
    ...
  ]
}
```

`lastContactUnix` is null for active probes, populated for Pioneer 10 (`2003-01-23`) and Pioneer 11 (`1995-09-30`).

30-day step from launch to 2050: ~880 entries × ~50 bytes = ~45 KB per probe, ~225 KB total for 5 probes. Plain-text commit, no LFS.

### Sync to runtime

Mirror `data/probes/*.json` → `public/probes/*.json` via a small `scripts/sync-probes.ts` (same pattern as `sync-dust.ts`). Add to `build` and `dev` script chains. `public/probes/` gitignored.

### Runtime: `src/client/probe-loader.ts`

- Fetch all 5 JSONs in parallel (small enough — fire-and-forget at startup or lazy-load on first solar-system-layer activation).
- For a given `t`, find bracketing entries and **linearly interpolate** x/y/z. Probes move ~3–4 AU/year — 30-day samples are visually indistinguishable from the true smooth trajectory.
- Hide probe entirely if `t < launchUnix`.
- Visual flag for "extrapolated past last-contact": Pioneer 10 after 2003-01-23, Pioneer 11 after 1995-09-30. Render dimmer, with a "(signal lost)" label suffix.

### Probe rendering

Same instanced-quad pattern as planets. Tiny — a few px. Always-labelled when visible (probes are a discovery feature, the labels are the point). Optional: a thin fading trail showing the trajectory between launch and current `t`.

### Files

- New:
  - `scripts/fetch-probe-trajectories.ts` — manual HORIZONS fetcher.
  - `scripts/sync-probes.ts` — `data/probes/` → `public/probes/`.
  - `src/client/probe-loader.ts` — JSON load + interpolation.
  - `data/probes/{voyager1,voyager2,pioneer10,pioneer11,new-horizons}.json` — committed (no LFS).
- Touch:
  - `package.json` — `fetch:probes` script + `sync-probes` in chain.
  - `solar-system.ts` (from 12a) — register probe layer alongside planets, share label/render plumbing where sensible.
  - `.gitignore` — `/public/probes/`.
- After landing, extend the `CLAUDE.md` "Solar-system layer" section with the probe data pipeline and last-contact handling.

### Watch out for

- HORIZONS rate-limits and occasional schema drift. Fetch script should be defensive — clear error if `$$SOE` block is missing or columns are unexpected.
- Pre-launch hiding is per-probe based on `launchUnix`. Don't accidentally render a probe at `t = 0` (1970-01-01) where its trajectory array's first entry would extrapolate backward.
- Linear interpolation between widely-spaced 30-day samples is fine for visual purposes but should not be billed as "true ephemeris." Comment in code so future-you doesn't mistake it for a precision ephemeris API.
- Active probes' projected trajectories past *now* are HORIZONS forward-projections, not certainties. The data is correct as far as projections go but the wording in any tooltips should be careful.
- Annual-ish refresh: re-run `npm run fetch:probes`, commit the refreshed JSONs. Document the cadence in CLAUDE.md.

### Estimate

Half a day to a day. Fetch script is the bulk; runtime interpolation + rendering is small once 12a's planet rendering is in place.

### Deliberately excluded

- Other interstellar probes / spacecraft (Parker Solar Probe, JUICE, etc.) — not yet at solar-system-edge scale where they'd be visually meaningful in this layer.
- True ephemeris precision (sub-AU). Linear interpolation between monthly samples is good enough for visualisation.
- Trajectory-line rendering past current `t` for active probes. Trail is launch-to-now only; future projection isn't shown (would need clearer "this is a projection" framing in UI).

title: Solar-system layer (time + planets + heliopause + scale rings)
description:
**Status:** v1 solar-system layer. Builds on the physical-scale rewrite (stellata-a7d).

**Goal:** when focused on Sol (or unfocused at Sol), render the local solar-system context — the 8 planets in their positions for the current wall-clock time `t`, faint per-planet orbital rings, the heliopause boundary as an asymmetric shell, and reference scale rings at 1 AU and 50 AU. The framework that lands here is generic enough to be reused for exoplanet host stars (stellata-bk5).

### Order of work (per Alex's 2026-05-07 walk-through)

The children of this epic are sequenced to ship value early and defer complexity:

1. **stellata-3re.6** — generic per-star "has planets" data model + render gating (groundwork; reused by bk5).
2. **stellata-3re.7** — per-planet orbit rings with angular-separation visibility heuristic (Sol initially; reused for any host).
3. **stellata-3re.8** — design rule: Sol's rings tilt to the ecliptic; all other host stars' rings lie in the galactic plane.
4. **stellata-3re.9** — UX: how to indicate stars-with-planets at distance, how to label planets up close. Same UX feeds bk5.
5. **stellata-3re.4** — Sol planet bodies: correct diameters, gas/solid distinction, Earth atmosphere, representative colour. **Placeholder orbital positions OK** — orbital location not yet driven by VSOP87.
6. **stellata-3re.10** — research: do we ship texture maps for Sol's planets, or stay schematic?
7. **stellata-3re.3** — VSOP87 ephemerides via `astronomia` populate real positions for `t = now`. Backfills the rendering from step 5.
8. **stellata-3re.11** — discreet `t` readout in `.ui-bottom`. Display only — no scrubbing in v1. Format: UTC Zulu (default recommendation).
9. **stellata-3re.5** — heliopause shell + 1 AU / 50 AU scale rings.

### What's deferred to other epics

- **Time scrubber UI** → `stellata-nmu` (its own epic). v1 sets `t = now` and shows the readout but does not let the user move time.
- **Deep-space probes** → `stellata-ywn` (depends on `t` from 3re.1 + per-star data plumbing from 3re.6).
- **Exoplanets** → `stellata-bk5` (reuses 3re.6 data model, 3re.7 ring renderer, 3re.8 plane rule, 3re.9 UX).
- **Default first-load view framing the inner solar system** → `stellata-vjm` (depends on this epic landing).
- **Chart-mode planets** → `stellata-m40.3` (depends on 3re.4).
- **Physical-vs-representational split** → the three-mode framework epic. Concrete applications under this epic are the unfocus-split and apparent-magnitude planet visibility beads (filed as P2 children).

### Sol-focus minDistance relaxation

When focused on Sol, drop `controls.minDistance` from `0.005 pc` (≈1031 AU) down to `~0.5 AU` (≈ `2.4e-6 pc`) so the user can fly into the inner solar system and resolve individual planets. Other focal stars retain the existing `0.005 pc` floor.

This is safe specifically for Sol because Sol sits at the world origin — the float32 jitter that bites at small distances *from non-origin focal stars* (CLAUDE.md "Camera near plane vs controls minDistance") doesn't apply when the focal frame is also the world frame.

`camera.near` already at `0.001 pc` is fine; for Sol-focus consider dropping to `1e-7 pc` (≈0.02 AU) so very close planet inspection isn't culled. Keep the strict-less-than invariant (`camera.near < minDistance`).

### Planet positions via VSOP87 (`astronomia` npm)

Detail in stellata-3re.3. Adds the `astronomia` dependency (~500 KB; offline; no network). Computes heliocentric ICRS positions at any `t`.

### Heliopause asymmetric ellipsoid

Detail in stellata-3re.5. ~122 AU upwind (Galactic apex direction RA 17h53m, Dec +27.4°), ~115 AU flank, ~200 AU heliotail. Modelled as an ellipsoid offset 39 AU toward the heliotail. Static — no `t` dependence on human timescales. The shell is a *representational* layer (the heliopause itself is a particle-density boundary, not optically visible) — gated on host-focused per the unfocus-split bead.

### Watch out for

- **Sol-focus `minDistance` is a per-focus override.** When focus switches from Sol to another star, the floor must snap back to `0.005 pc` *before* the new focus's recenter pulls the camera in. `setFocus` is the right hook.
- **`t` vs `uTime`.** `uTime` keeps ticking at `uSecondsPerDay = 0.2` regardless of where the scrubber is. Variable-star pulsation is cosmetic — don't accidentally couple `t` and `uTime`.
- **Ecliptic ↔ equatorial obliquity.** Use J2000 ε = 23.4392911° consistently. Don't reach for the time-varying obliquity term.
- **Physical objects render everywhere; representational layers gate on host-focused.** Planet bodies (physical) render whether or not their host is focused, gated only by their own apparent-magnitude visibility. Heliopause shell, orbit rings, planet labels, apex label, time readout (representational) gate on host-focused. See the unfocus-split bead for the concrete split.
- **`camera.near` invariant.** Anything that drops `minDistance` must keep `camera.near < minDistance`.

### Deliberately excluded

- Moons (Earth's Moon, Galilean satellites, Titan, etc.). VSOP87 doesn't cover these; satellite ephemerides are a separate effort.
- Asteroids and minor planets.
- Time-series proper motion of stars. Stars remain snapshot-only; `t` is solar-system-only in v1.
- Earth-rotation / time-of-day effects. Heliocentric scene; daily rotation has no meaning.
- Auto-advancing `t`. Scrubber + animation are the time-scrubber epic.
design:
# Architecture for the 3re.15 + 3re.16 + 3re.17 PR (planet visibility refactor)

This PR refactors the solar-system planet pipeline so it scales to
thousands of planet-bearing hosts (stellata-bk5).

## Layer split

- **PlanetBodyField** (NEW) — global InstancedMesh of every attached
  host's planet bodies. v1 attaches Sol; bk5 attaches each exoplanet
  host as its `PlanetSystem` shard lands. Bodies are PHYSICAL — render
  whenever attached, subject only to apparent-magnitude visibility.
  Per-instance attributes: iHostLocalPos, iLocalRel, iRadiusPc,
  iAlbedoP, iColour, iSolidity, iHostAbsmag. Vert shader derives
  m_planet from these + camera, then maps to disc size via the shared
  perceptual-disc abstraction.
  API: attachHost(idx, ps, hostAbsPos), detachHost(idx),
  recenter(deltaWorldOffset) on focus change, update(t) per-frame.

- **OrbitRingsLayer** — RENAME of current StarSystem with body-rendering
  stripped out. Single-host, focus-driven. Builds/tears down on
  onPlanetSystemChange. Rings stay focused-host-only forever
  (showing every host's rings simultaneously is visual noise).

- **Heliopause** — Sol-only by data availability (no astropause data
  for exoplanets in the foreseeable future). Stays as-is. Visibility
  gates on focusedHost === solIndex.

- **PlanetLabels** — focused-host-only via getFocusedPlanetSystem().
  Unchanged.

- **DROP warpDestStarSystem.** Existed only to preview destination's
  bodies/rings during warp under the focused-host-owns-the-instance
  model. Under PlanetBodyField, all hosts' bodies render whenever
  attached. Rings just turn on at warp completion.

## Per-host distance cull (critical for bk5 perf)

For each attached host, precompute cullDistancePc — the d_vh at
which its brightest planet would just cross the current maxAppMag
slider. Solved closed-form from host absmag + brightest-planet
sqrt(p)·R/d_hp term. Recomputed when maxAppMag changes.

Per frame: skip the host's positionsAt + orientation-rotate + buffer
upload entirely if camera-to-host > cullDistancePc. The cull is the
primary line of defense; per-fragment discard in the vert shader is
the backup. For Sol with naked-eye preset (m=6.5), Jupiter's d_vh
ceiling is sub-parsec — so any non-Sol focus already culls Sol's
bodies, exactly as desired.

## Shared perceptual abstraction (DRY)

The apparent-magnitude → perceptual disc/glow mapping is the SAME
PHYSICS for any point of light: ocular PSF + display luminance
saturation. (NOT atmospheric scintillation; that's twinkle and
isn't modelled.) Same treatment applies to stars, planets, future
moons/asteroids/etc.

- TS helper — src/client/perceptual-magnitude.ts (NEW). Pure
  functions for any CPU-side mapping (search ranking, label gating,
  app-mag-driven sizing). Vitest-covered.
- GLSL chunk — included in star.vert.glsl and planet.vert.glsl.
  Same numerical output as today's star pipeline (star rendering
  bit-for-bit unchanged).
- Three-pass parity for planets — depth-mask + disc + glow,
  mirroring stars. Disc and glow use the chunk; depth-mask is
  trivial.

## Three-commit ordering (independently revertable)

1. Extract perceptual-disc chunk — pure refactor; star pipeline as
   sole consumer. NO behavior change. Smoke against named stars
   (Sirius, Sol from 1pc, faint near-cutoff binoculars-preset stars,
   Milky Way diffuse-glow proportionality).

2. Apparent-magnitude pipeline + glow + Lambert phase + albedo
   applied to planets, on the EXISTING per-host StarSystem (still
   focus-only). Drops the unconditional 2-px floor. Adds glow pass
   + depth-mask pass. Forward-compat vert shader (iHostLocalPos
   attribute always (0,0,0) in this commit; populated for real in
   commit 3).

3. Architecture refactor: PlanetBodyField + OrbitRingsLayer rename
   + drop warpDestStarSystem. Bodies become global, always-on. From
   this commit on, Sol's planet bodies render even when Sol unfocused
   (subject to apparent-mag cutoff via per-host distance cull).

## Formula correction (re-stated for cross-reference)

The 3re.16 description has a typo — apparent-mag formula written as
p · (R/d_hp)² · (R/d_vp)² (R appears twice) is dimensionally wrong.
Correct Lambertian-sphere formula has one R plus the viewer↔host
leg ratio:

  m_host_at_viewer = M_host + 5·log10(d_vh / 10pc)
  m_planet = m_host_at_viewer
           − 2.5·log10( p · (R/d_vp)² · (d_vh/d_hp)² · φ(α) )

Verified against Jupiter: −2.7 from Earth at opposition, +5.2 from
~150 AU outside heliopause, +21 from α Cen (1.34 pc). The bead's
own α-Cen estimate of +28 is wrong; +21 is correct. Heliopause-range
estimate (+5 to +6) is correct.

## Glow halo intent (also a comment to add at the call site)

Glow exists because a sufficiently bright point of light appears
larger on the retina (and on a non-HDR display) than its physical
disc — diffraction + ocular aberrations + display saturation PSF.
NOT atmospheric twinkle. Treatment is identical for stars and
planets; Venus at -4 from Earth gets a perceptible halo for the
same reason Sirius does.

## Labels follow rings, not bodies

Orbit-ring visibility gates planet labels (already the case in
planet-labels.ts:99-108). Body apparent-magnitude cutoff does NOT
gate labels — if the ring is up, the label is up, even if the body
itself is below the slider cutoff. Labels answer "what would I be
seeing here," not "what am I currently rendering." Don't accidentally
couple the two.
notes: Now blocked by stellata-a7d (physical-scale rewrite). The Sol-focus close-approach floor described here (drop minDistance to ~0.5 AU) is a special case of the per-star d_min(R) = R/tan(fov_minor·0.9/2) that the rewrite introduces for every focusable star, and the suggested camera.near drop to 1e-7 pc requires the log depth buffer that the rewrite enables. Re-evaluate the Sol-specific values in this issue once stellata-a7d lands — they may collapse to 'just use the default per-star floor'.

title: Per-star dust slab fallback for sightlines exiting the Edenhofer voxel volume
description:
**Goal:** correct per-star extinction along sightline segments outside the high-fidelity 3D dust volume(s). One layer of the multi-source dust stack under parent **stellata-ty4**.

`star.vert.glsl` raymarches camera→star through the Edenhofer voxel cube (~1.25 kpc AABB). Lines of sight that exit the AABB get zero attenuation beyond — wrong at the edge. A star sitting just outside the cube renders unattenuated despite intervening dust along the sightline. From extragalactic vantages (e.g. M31, parent ty4 trigger case) the entire ~780 kpc sightline is unattenuated.

**Fix:** lift the analytical disc profile already in `milkyway.frag.glsl` (`analyticalDustDensity` = `exp(-(R-R₀)/L_R) × exp(-|z|/h_z)` with h_z ≈ 125 pc, evaluated in Galactic frame) into a shared shader include, and integrate it over sightline segments outside the cube. Per-star extinction then has a sensible value everywhere — high fidelity inside the cube, smooth analytical fallback outside.

Once **stellata-ty4.1** (Bayestar shell) lands, this layer becomes the outermost fallback after both 3D maps are exhausted. The shader logic is the same; the AABB test gates whether to fall through to slab vs Bayestar.

**Files:**
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — call the shared slab function beyond the AABB(s).
- New `src/client/shaders/dust-extinction.glsl` shared include (or extend `perceptual-disc.glsl`-style chunk).

**Watch out for:**
- Slab evaluation requires ICRS→Galactic rotation matching the MW volumetric layer. Keep it as one shared shader function so both phases use identical maths.
- Boundary continuity — the analytical profile and Edenhofer voxel values should be roughly continuous at the AABB boundary (no sudden brightness jump as a star crosses the edge). Verify visually.
- Long-sightline raymarch density — DUST_STEPS = 48 is fine at kpc scales but inefficient at Mpc scales. The analytical disc has a closed-form line integral over the segment intersecting the slab; consider using it instead of an even-step raymarch where the segment is much longer than the slab thickness. See **stellata-ty4.2** for the Mpc-scale discussion.

**Estimate:** ~2 hours.

**Note:** this issue used to bundle a UI status chip + dataset-boundary mesh under the same scope. Those have been extracted into the artificial-visualisation cluster (`stellata-io3`) since they're a different conceptual category — opt-in visualisation of dataset metadata, not a 'you're in degraded mode' indicator.

title: Local Bubble shell mesh
description:
**Goal:** translucent surface mesh of the Local Bubble (low-density ISM cavity surrounding the Sun, ~200 pc radius). Subtle by default, toggleable for emphasis. Gives the user immediate context that the Sun sits *inside* a bubble.

**Data sources:**
- **O'Neill, Zucker, Goodman & Edenhofer 2024** ("The Local Bubble Is a Local Chimney", ApJ 973, 136 / arXiv:2407.18238). The paper should have a data availability section; if not, check Catherine Zucker's GitHub: https://github.com/catherinezucker
