- **Zucker et al. 2022** (Nature, "Star formation near the Sun is driven by the expansion of the Local Bubble") — earlier 3D mesh. Also linked from her interactive 3D gallery at https://catherinezucker.github.io/

**Implementation sketch:**
- Source format likely OBJ/PLY/HDF5 mesh. Convert at preprocessor time to a tiny binary (vertex positions + face indices). Few thousand vertices typically.
- Three.js: `THREE.Mesh` with `THREE.MeshBasicMaterial({ transparent: true, opacity: 0.05, side: DoubleSide, blending: AdditiveBlending })`.
- Subtle inner/outer gradient via vertex colours (front-facing vs back-facing) so users can perceive 3D shape.
- Label "Local Bubble" at the centroid, similar to constellation labels.
- UI toggle.

**Watch out for:**
- Coordinate frame — likely Galactic; rotate to ICRS on ingest.
- Mesh might be in different units (kpc vs pc); verify scale.
- Front-vs-back face ordering matters for the gradient effect.

**Estimate:** 3–5 hours.

title: Nebulae catalogs (PN, RNe, H II, SNR)
description:
**Goal:** classical named nebulae (Orion Nebula, Ring Nebula, Helix, Veil, Horsehead, etc.) as discrete blob objects with type-based colour coding and search-bar entries.

**Data sources** (filter to within ~3 kpc to match the dust volume):
- **Strasbourg-ESO Catalogue of Galactic Planetary Nebulae** (Acker et al. 1992, updated): VizieR `V/84` or HEASARC `PLNEBULAE`. ~1,500 PNe with up to four independent distance estimates each.
- **Merged Catalog of Reflection Nebulae** (Magakian 2003): VizieR `J/A+A/399/141` or HEASARC `REFNEBULAE`. 913 reflection nebulae; cross-match the illuminating star to ATHYG via HD/HIP for distance.
- **Sharpless H II catalog**: VizieR `VII/20`. 313 entries; pair with Avedisova's "Catalogue of Star-Forming Regions" (VizieR `V/115`) for derived distances since the original catalog has none.
- **SIMBAD TAP query** for unified all-types fetch: object types `PN`, `RNe`, `HII`, `SNR` with a distance cut, via `astroquery.simbad`.

**Per-nebula data:** ID, common name, type, RA/Dec/distance → xyz, angular size → physical radius, illuminating star (for RNe).

**Rendering — this is where raymarching IS appropriate.** Each nebula occupies a small AABB (typically 1–50 pc), so per-nebula raymarching with 16–32 samples is dense in voxel-space and produces smooth volumetrics without the aliasing that killed the all-sky fog. See `memory/feedback_raymarch_for_nebulae.md` for context. Recover the fog shader pattern from git history at commit `5ad5444` if useful — it's a clip-space fullscreen triangle, ray-AABB slab intersection, fixed-step march with log-encoded density decode.

**Type-based visuals:**
- H II regions — warm reddish-pink, soft sphere with density falloff
- Planetary nebulae — greenish-teal, often shell-like; small central point + thin shell
- Reflection nebulae — bluish, irregular blob biased toward the illuminating star's colour (look up via ATHYG cross-ref)
- Supernova remnants — filamentary; pink→blue gradient by age

**No photographs.** All visuals procedural, matching the rest of the app's aesthetic.

**Watch out for:**
- Many PNe have only rough distance estimates; drop entries with no parsed distance.
- Filter to ATHYG-relevant volume — most distant PNe (>5 kpc) won't render usefully and clutter search.
- Procedural shape variation per type — without it, every nebula is a blob and the type encoding gets lost.

**Estimate:** 1–2 days for catalogs + ingest + rendering. The volumetric raymarching for the per-nebula effect is ~half the work.

title: Override PRs should enumerate row-population partitions in PR description + per-partition build-counts assertions
description:
# Finding

PR #98 (stellata-dch.47, Bailer-Jones distance override) shipped without a `dist_src` eligibility filter — it overrode every AT-HYG row carrying a Gaia DR3 source_id, including rows whose AT-HYG `dist_src` was HIP / GJ / N / OTHER. For ~11+ stars with low-S/N Gaia parallaxes but a canonical non-Gaia distance, this silently regressed their position onto Bailer-Jones's smooth Galactic-density prior (~10–40 kpc at mid-latitudes), placing them outside the disc.

PR #99 (stellata-dch.8l0) added `BJ_ELIGIBLE_DIST_SRCS = {'G_R3', 'G_R2'}` + `isBailerJonesEligible` and re-gated the override. Bug gone, but the **process gap** that let it land in the first place is real and worth a small write-time control.

The shape — applying a transform that fires on a column-keyed population (here: rows with `gaia` non-empty) without enumerating how the population partitions under *other* columns that should gate the transform (here: `dist_src`) — is exactly [[stellata-pattern-coverage-across-peers]].

# Why this matters going forward

The dch epic has three more override layers queued — `stellata-dch.55` (SMC kinematic), `stellata-dch.56` (LMC structural-disc fallback), `stellata-dch.58` (OGLE Cepheid cross-match). Each fires on a different population partition. Without an explicit row-population enumeration discipline, each is a fresh opportunity for the same shape of regression: a population we forgot to think about gets silently rerouted onto a prior whose semantics we never validated for that population.

`stellata-dch.57` (Distance-regression QA: SIMBAD + self-consistency build gate, P2) is the **after-the-fact** detector. This bead is the **before-the-fact** preventer.

# Scope (one commit/PR — small)

Two complementary deliverables, both small:

1. **Authoring discipline — add to the override-PR template.** A short paragraph in `docs/build-and-data.md` (in the section that documents the distance override stack) listing the row-population partitions any new override layer must enumerate in its PR description: AT-HYG `dist_src` × `gaia` × override-source-availability. Each partition must have a stated outcome (overridden / preserved / dropped). The PR description must include the per-partition count from a dry run, **and** the matching assertion must land in `scripts/build-counts.test.ts`.

2. **Per-`dist_src` partition assertion in `build-counts.test.ts`.** Extend `BuildCounts` (and `build-catalog-expected.json`) with at minimum:
   - `bjOverriddenByDistSrc`: a `Record<'G_R3' | 'G_R2' | 'HIP' | 'GJ' | 'N' | 'OTHER', number>` (zeros for the non-eligible sources).
   - A vitest assertion that pins each entry. The HIP / GJ / N / OTHER entries must be 0 — this is the assertion that would have caught the dch.47 bug at write time.
   The same partition shape applies forward to LMC / SMC / Cepheid override layers; pick a structure that generalises.

# Acceptance

- `docs/build-and-data.md` carries the override-PR authoring-discipline paragraph.
- `BuildCounts` extended; `build-catalog-expected.json` snapshot extended; vitest passes with the HIP/GJ/N/OTHER entries pinned to 0.
- A retroactive sanity-check: confirm the assertions would have failed against pre-PR-99 catalog state (i.e. the assertion has teeth).
- [[stellata-pattern-coverage-across-peers]] memory updated with the dch.47/99 case as a representative example (memory grooming, one paragraph).

# Smoke

- `npm test` + `npm run typecheck` + `npm run build:catalog`. No user-visible scene change.

# Connects

- `stellata-dch.57` — related (after-the-fact regression detector; this bead is the write-time complement). Link via `bd dep add` `--type=related`.
- [[stellata-pattern-coverage-across-peers]] — the underlying authoring rule.
- [[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]] — extends `build-counts.test.ts` with per-partition headline assertions, the canonical pattern.
- `stellata-dch.55`, `stellata-dch.56`, `stellata-dch.58` — the imminent override layers that should use the new authoring discipline.

title: Phase 2 follow-up: WDS notes 'primary/secondary' synonym anchoring in tier 2
description:
Part of the source-ID-anchored catalogue-pipeline rewrite (see parent epic for architecture). Follow-up to stellata-dch.28.

# Why

Stage 2 tier-2 (``wds_notes_hip``) currently honours only explicit component-letter context for HIP tokens in WDS Notes prose — patterns ``Component X is HIP NNN`` / ``X is HIP NNN`` / ``HIP NNN = X``. Many real WDS Notes use the synonyms ``primary`` / ``secondary`` / ``companion`` instead — e.g. α Cen's note reads ``Difficult for Hipparcos with secondary (= HIP 71681)``. Today these tokens are dropped (the bare ``HIP 71681`` without an explicit letter anchor is ambiguous).

The synonym CAN be disambiguated against the WDS pair's ``components`` field: ``AB`` → primary=A, secondary=B; ``AC`` → primary=A, secondary=C; ``Aa,Ab`` → primary=Aa, secondary=Ab. The parser doesn't carry the pair context at note-load time, so the binding has to happen at resolve time (parser returns a synonym → HIP map, resolver picks the right letter from the pair).

# Scope

Extend ``parse_wds_notes`` to ALSO emit a synonym dict alongside the existing letter dict: ``{wds_id: {"primary": HIP, "secondary": HIP, "companion": HIP}}`` for prose like ``primary is HIP NNN``, ``secondary (= HIP NNN)``, ``HIP NNN is the secondary``. Then in ``resolve_component``, after the explicit-letter lookup misses, consult the synonym dict using the pair's ``components`` field to resolve ``primary`` and ``secondary`` to actual letters.

Conservative scope:
- Only handle ``primary``/``secondary`` synonyms (skip ``companion`` — it doesn't disambiguate from secondary in many pairs).
- Only when the pair has exactly 2 components (binary, not triple+).
- Match against pairs where ``components`` splits cleanly via ``split_components``.

# Expected coverage uplift

Manual sample (α Cen, Sirius, Procyon, Castor) suggests low-hundreds of systems will benefit. Bigger systems are already covered by tier 4 (PM-propagated position match, ``stellata-dch.29``/``.30``) so this isn't critical-path — file as P3.

# Files to touch

- ``scripts/build-binaries.py`` — ``parse_wds_notes`` returns ``(letter_dict, synonym_dict)``; ``resolve_component`` consults synonyms after letter miss.
- ``scripts/build-binaries.test.py`` — fixture covering primary/secondary synonym patterns and the disambiguation against ``components``.

# Smoke

- ``python3 scripts/build-binaries.test.py``
- ``npm run build:binaries -- --force`` — wds_notes_hip count should rise by 100+ from current baseline.
- Spot-check α Cen (WDS 14396-6050): notes synonym ``secondary (= HIP 71681)`` should now resolve component B (in pair AB) and component C (in pair AC) to HIP 71681's gaia source_id.

title: PR 98/99 cleanup: remove unfulfilled DIST_SRC_* scaffolding + dead branch + test tautology in catalog-pure.ts
description:
# Why

PR 98 introduced `DIST_SRC_BAILER_JONES = 'BJ'` (and PR 99 added `DIST_SRC_LMC_KIN = 'LMC_KIN'`) as part of a distance-provenance plan that was never followed through: the override block recomputes `dist/x/y/z/absmag` but never tags which source the new value came from. The Star struct has no `dist_src` field; v4 RECORD_LAYOUT has no slot for it; v6 (dch.33) plans only `gaia_source_id` surfacing; v6→v7 Apsis (dch.49) plans Teff/logg/[M/H]/A0 only. **Distance provenance is not on the dch roadmap.**

The result is misleading scaffolding:
- `DIST_SRC_BAILER_JONES` / `DIST_SRC_LMC_KIN` are exported but appear only in build-time `console.log` strings and one self-referential test that asserts `DIST_SRC_BAILER_JONES === 'BJ'`.
- `applyBailerJonesOverride`'s JSDoc says "The caller swaps these into the star record and tags `dist_src = 'BJ'`." — no such tagging happens.
- A future reader hunting for where overridden stars are flagged will spend time deriving "the tagging never landed."

Two additional small cleanups in the same area, in the same commit since they're all one-touch:

- **Dead branch in `parseBailerJonesTsv`**: `if (lines.length === 0) return out;` is unreachable. `''.split(/\r?\n/)` returns `['']` (length 1) in Node — confirmed empirically. An empty TSV takes the missing-columns throw path.
- **Test tautology in `catalog-pure.test.ts`**: the tier-A fixture suite's "pins the BJ distance and recomputes x/y/z + absmag consistently" case asserts `expect(out!.absmag).toBeCloseTo(f.mag - 5 * Math.log10(f.bjDist / 10), 10);` — this is the function's defining formula asserted against itself. Catches a mag↔dist swap, doesn't catch the formula being wrong. The adjacent `apparentToAbsoluteMagnitude` test already pins known values (e.g. m=15, d=100 → M=10).

# Scope (one commit/PR)

1. Remove `DIST_SRC_BAILER_JONES` export from `scripts/catalog-pure.ts`.
2. Remove `DIST_SRC_LMC_KIN` export from `scripts/catalog-pure.ts`.
3. Update `scripts/build-catalog.ts` log lines (around lines 794 / 801) to inline the literal strings or drop the `dist_src='…'` framing entirely — the log line's job is just human-readable progress.
4. Remove the JSDoc clause on `applyBailerJonesOverride` claiming the caller tags `dist_src = "BJ"`. Same for `applyLmcKinematicOverride`.
5. Remove the self-referential `expect(DIST_SRC_BAILER_JONES).toBe('BJ')` test in `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts`.
6. Remove the dead `if (lines.length === 0) return out;` branch in `parseBailerJonesTsv`.
7. Remove the tautological `expect(out!.absmag).toBeCloseTo(f.mag - 5 * Math.log10(f.bjDist / 10), 10);` line in the tier-A fixture suite. Leave the `out!.dist` and `sqrt(x²+y²+z²)` assertions as-is (those pin external numbers).

# Acceptance

- `npm test` green (1087+ tests).
- `npm run typecheck` clean.
- `npm run build:catalog` runs end-to-end; the build-time log lines still report override coverage in human-readable form.
- `grep -nE "DIST_SRC_(BAILER_JONES|LMC_KIN)" scripts/ src/` returns zero hits.
- `grep -n "tags \`dist_src" scripts/` returns zero hits.

# Why this isn't deferred

This is cleanup of misleading code that already shipped. The longer it sits, the higher the chance a future override PR (dch.55 SMC, dch.56 LMC structural-disc, dch.58 OGLE Cepheid) copies the same pattern and adds a third / fourth dead constant. Catching it now is one commit; catching it after three more layers ships is a sweep.

If distance provenance ever needs to land in the runtime binary, the bead that lands it can re-introduce the constants alongside the binary-format change. Speculative scaffolding before that decision is taken is review-blocking under [[stellata-named-constants-and-dry]].

# Smoke

- `npm test` + `npm run typecheck` + `npm run build:catalog`. No user-visible scene change expected. Pure cleanup.

# Connects

- [[stellata-named-constants-and-dry]] — write-time discipline against unfulfilled scaffolding.
- [[stellata-rename-and-stale-prose-sweep]] — the unfulfilled docstring is the same shape of drift.
- [[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]] — the tautological assertion is the "test that doesn't test anything" anti-pattern.
- Sibling DRY refactor bead (filed in same review pass) — shared DistanceOverride interface; lands first, this cleanup follows.

title: Small consistency fixes: WDS_NOTES_HIP regex constraint + Bin.mod_K assertion + ORB6 grade fallback log
description:
Three small fixes bundled into one bead (each is ~3 lines).

**D6 — `_WDS_NOTES_HIP_RE` is broad enough to false-match.** `build-binaries.py:224` matches `HIP\s+\d+` anywhere in the line. A note like "HIP catalog version 2" or "HIP 1986" (year-like number, unrelated) gets harvested as a HIP identifier. The dedup helps, not perfectly.

Fix: constrain to common appearances (`(?:=|as|companion)\s*HIP\s+(\d+)`) OR document in the docstring that the result is best-effort and downstream must validate.

**D8 — `Bin.mod_K > 0` not asserted.** `refresh-simbad-sample.py:101` — a typo `mod_K=0` in BINS would divide-by-zero in ADQL or hang silently. 

Fix: add `assert b.mod_K >= 1` in `Bin.__post_init__` or at the top of `fetch_bin_candidates`.

**D9 — ORB6 grade fallback silently swallows malformed grades.** `build-binaries.py:326`: `grade=int(grade_str) if grade_str.isdigit() else 5`. A garbage grade (e.g. `"X"` or `"?"`) gets the worst grade silently. Downstream Stage 4 will deprioritise this row but won't know it was malformed.

Fix: log a count of fallback-grade rows after parsing (`log(f"ORB6: {n_fallback} rows had non-numeric grade — coerced to 5")`).

title: refresh-bailer-jones: add richer per-batch / coverage report (parity with refresh-gaia-apsis)
description:
`refresh-bailer-jones.py` prints a one-line summary (`matched X/Y = Z%`). `refresh-gaia-apsis.py:report_coverage` prints a 4-line breakdown (rows / phot-only / spec-only / union).

Asymmetric observability across the suite makes triage harder ("which script tells me what again?"). A future "why did this script return only 91% coverage?" debug session has to learn each script's reporting layer separately.

## Acceptance

Either (a) lift a `report_coverage` helper to `refresh_lib` and have both scripts call it (the only differing axis is which columns count as "populated"), or (b) duplicate Apsis's pattern locally in bailer-jones (`{r_med_geo} non-null, {r_med_photogeo} non-null, union`).

Option (a) preferred if bead 9mm.201's refactor lands first; option (b) acceptable as a standalone.

title: Refresh scripts: row-count slack consistency + coverage-floor rationale comments
description:
Two consistency issues across the refresh-script suite.

**M8 — coverage floors are aspirational, not justified.** `refresh-bailer-jones.py:74` pins `EXPECTED_COVERAGE_MIN = 0.90` (empirical 98.7%). `refresh-simbad-sample.py:123` pins `GAIA_COVERAGE_MIN = 0.95` (empirical 96.7%). `refresh-gaia-apsis.py:111` pins `EXPECTED_UNION_COVERAGE_MIN = 0.80` (empirical 84.8%). The slack is reasonable, but the rationale isn't pinned anywhere — if coverage falls to 91%, is that "still fine" or "investigate"?

**M9 — row-count slack is inconsistent across "frozen catalog" scripts.** `refresh-gaia-hip-xmatch` allows ±0.2% (tightest). Tycho-xmatch ±0.4%. NSS ±0.7%. HIP2 ±1.3%. DR3 is frozen, but VizieR/ESA mirror re-indexes have historically caused 100-row-class drift. The HIP xmatch is 3-7× tighter than its peers without a documented reason.

## Acceptance

1. **For M8:** one-line comment per coverage floor stating the historical low and why the floor sits N% below it. E.g. `EXPECTED_COVERAGE_MIN = 0.90  # empirical 98.7%; floor at 90% absorbs VizieR mirror drift up to 8.7%`.
2. **For M9:** review all `EXPECTED_ROW_COUNT_{MIN,MAX}` constants across the suite. Adopt a consistent ~1% slack as default unless there's a specific documented reason to go tighter. Each tight bound carries a one-line comment explaining the rationale.

Pure-comment change; no behaviour shift.

title: refresh_lib: _dtype_matches pandas branch + coerce_masked broader coverage
description:
Two latent gaps in `refresh_lib` discovered in the retrospective on PRs 90-97.

**M6 — `_dtype_matches` doesn't handle pandas Extension dtypes.** The docstring (`refresh_lib.py:168`) claims pandas DataFrame support, but the type-matching logic only branches on numpy `np.dtype` and Python `type`. A pandas `StringDtype()` or `Int64Dtype()` would fall through to `dtype in wants` and likely fail. None of the current callers use pandas, so this is latent.

**M7 — `coerce_masked` coverage is narrow.** `refresh_lib.py:326-344` checks `value is np.ma.masked` and `isinstance(value, np.ma.core.MaskedConstant)`. Astropy can also produce `MaskedArray` scalars with `.mask == True` (depending on column dtype and access pattern). The existing tests pin the cases that currently surface, but a future astropy upgrade might return masked values via a different code path and `coerce_masked` would pass them through as-is.

## Acceptance

1. For M6: either add the pandas Extension-dtype branch to `_dtype_matches` (handling StringDtype / Int64Dtype / Float64Dtype) OR remove the pandas mention from the docstring. Choose based on whether we expect a pandas-DataFrame consumer in the next 6 months.
2. For M7: add a fallback in `coerce_masked` for "anything with a `.mask` attribute whose `bool(.mask) == True`" (best-effort). Add a test exercising a `MaskedArray`-element scalar (not just the `np.ma.masked` constant).

title: build-binaries: per-parser cardinality bounds (currently log-only)
description:
The refresh scripts all have row-count bounds. `build-binaries.py`'s parsers do not — each just `log(f"loaded N rows")` and continues. If AT-HYG is corrupted with 10M rows (or 100 rows), the script slurps it all into RAM and reports the cardinality without flagging.

## Acceptance

Add an expected-cardinality bound to each parser in `build-binaries.py`:

- `parse_athyg`: ~310k-320k rows (AT-HYG v3.3 classic-IDs subset)
- `parse_wds_summ`: ~155k rows
- `parse_orb6`: ~3,500 rows
- `parse_gcvs`: ~58k rows
- `parse_ccdm`: ~5,000 rows (overlap with bead M1)
- `parse_hip2`: ~118k rows (already row-count-bounded upstream in `refresh-hipparcos2`, but worth pinning here too as defence-in-depth)

Raise `SystemExit` on out-of-band; widths ~10% per side to absorb minor catalogue maintenance.

Coordinate with bead 9mm.201 — `assert_row_count` helper applies equally well here once lifted.

title: refresh-simbad-sample: validate schema for every bin, not just the first
description:
`refresh-simbad-sample.py:381-382` only schema-validates the first bin's candidates table. The other three bins (each with a different WHERE — including the MOD-K subsample) skip validation.

If SIMBAD's query planner returns a different schema for the MOD-K branch (unlikely but possible), no check catches it. Pattern matches the bailer-jones one-time check, but the risk is higher here because the bins have **different WHERE clauses**.

## Acceptance

Call `rl.validate_schema(table, BASIC_SCHEMA, label=f"SIMBAD basic+allfluxes bin {i+1}")` for every bin, not just `i == 0`. Cost is negligible vs. the multi-minute TAP query per bin.

title: Batched refresh scripts: lose all progress on mid-run network failure
description:
`refresh-bailer-jones.py` and `refresh-gaia-apsis.py` run ~60-70 batches each (5000 IDs per batch). Apsis takes tens of minutes per the bead notes. A network drop on batch 65 of 70 throws away every preceding batch — back to batch 1.

## Acceptance

A simple incremental TSV write: batch N appended to a `.tmp.batch-N` file, with a final merge step. On re-run, detect existing `.tmp.batch-*` files and resume from the next batch.

Alternative shape: write directly to the final `.tmp` file with one row per batch flushed; the existing atomic-write swap (bead 9mm.196) then takes care of the final commit.

## Notes

Lower priority — operationally manageable today via re-run. But: DR4 may double the dataset size and re-running a 1-hour query because batch 65 timed out is a real footgun.

Coordinate with bead 9mm.196 (atomic write) and 9mm.201 (lift `run_in_batches`) so this lands cleanly inside the helper.

title: refresh-simbad-sample: build_output_row assumes v_mag is never null
description:
`refresh-simbad-sample.py:326` does `float(rl.coerce_masked(row["v_mag"]))` with no guard. The `f."V" IS NOT NULL` WHERE clause is the only guarantee.

If SIMBAD's TAP ever returns a NULL for a row that passed the filter (cold cache, planner quirk, mid-query upstream change), this crashes with `TypeError: float() argument must be a string or...`. The exception would surface (so not silent corruption), but the script would die mid-write after writing the header — at which point sibling bead 9mm.196 (atomic write) is the safety net.

## Acceptance

Belt-and-braces: `if v_mag is None: continue` (or raise an explicit `SystemExit` with the offending oid) before any per-row math.

Test: fixture where one selected row has `v_mag=None` → either the row is skipped with a logged warning OR the script raises with a clear message naming the oid.

title: build-binaries: CCDM parser sentinel detection is fragile + no row-count bound
description:
`parse_ccdm` (`build-binaries.py:421-424`) waits for a line starting with `------` to flip `in_data`. If VizieR changes the separator to `======` or removes the dashes entirely, `in_data` stays False and the function returns `[]` — no error, no warning.

Combined with no row-count bound (CCDM has ~5,000 rows expected), this could go undetected.

## Acceptance

1. Either (a) detect the data block by a more robust heuristic — e.g. "first row whose first tab-separated field parses as int" — or (b) keep the dash sentinel but assert `len(rows) > 0` post-parse with a "CCDM sentinel detection failed; check VizieR file format" diagnostic.
2. Add an expected-row-count bound (~4500-5500 based on current ~5k) and raise on out-of-band.
3. Test: fixture with a `======` separator → either parses correctly (option a) or raises with the diagnostic (option b).

title: Data-pipeline refresh-script robustness follow-ups
description:
Themed sub-epic of `stellata-9mm` collecting medium / low-severity findings from the retrospective on PRs 90-97 (data-pipeline refresh scripts + `build-binaries.py`).

The high-severity findings (silent-data-corruption bugs + the lift-into-`refresh_lib` DRY refactor) are filed as standalone siblings under `stellata-9mm`. This sub-epic gathers the residual robustness / observability / consistency items so they sit on the backlog as a coherent grooming queue rather than scattering across 9mm at large.

## Children themes (see individual beads for detail)

- CCDM sentinel detection + row-count bound (M1)
- SIMBAD `v_mag` null-guard (M2)
- Incremental batched-query checkpointing for long runs (M3)
- Validate schema for every SIMBAD bin, not just the first (M4)
- Parser cardinality bounds in `build-binaries` (M5)
- `refresh_lib` internals: `_dtype_matches` pandas branch + `coerce_masked` broader coverage (M6+M7)
- Row-count slack consistency + coverage-floor rationale comments (M8+M9)
- Richer per-pipeline coverage report for bailer-jones (D5)
- Small consistency fixes: WDS_NOTES_HIP regex, `Bin.mod_K` assertion, ORB6 grade fallback log (D6+D8+D9)
- Pin Python 3.10+ requirement in `requirements-refresh.txt` (D10)

## Priority

P3 default — code-quality / observability / consistency, not user-visible. Individual children can bump to P2 if one becomes a blocker for in-flight `stellata-dch` work.

title: refresh_lib: lift 5 helpers (read_athyg_source_ids, run_in_batches, validate_spot_rows, assert_row_count, write_tsv atomic)
description:
DRY refactor of the refresh-script suite per the retrospective architectural finding (PRs 90-97). Lifts the substantive duplicates into `refresh_lib` so each refresh script becomes a thin orchestration shell with no boilerplate. Sibling to the bugfix beads filed alongside — some helpers land via those (see Cross-references).

## Why helpers, not a framework

In the retrospective we considered a full framework refactor (config-driven driver / class hierarchy). Rejected because the variation across refresh scripts is **semantic, not parametric** — each script has script-specific quirks (SIMBAD's divergent ADQL dialect; HIP2's misnamed `RArad`/`DErad`; Apsis's union-of-two-pipelines coverage; NSS's Thiele-Innes vs Campbell discriminant; SIMBAD's stratified MOD-then-local-random subsample). The right level of abstraction is helper-functions, not framework. After this lift, each script reads top-to-bottom as: "here's the ADQL, here's the schema, here's the bounds, here's the spot pin, run it." That's the right amount of visible logic.

## Five helpers (acceptance criteria)

**1. `read_athyg_source_ids(path) -> list[int]`** — verbatim duplicate between `refresh-bailer-jones` and `refresh-gaia-apsis`. Lifted to `refresh_lib` with the "skip empty AND skip '0'" convention baked in. Both callers replaced.

  → **Lands via sibling bead H4+H5+D1+D7 (AT-HYG ingest consolidation).** Verify here that the helper sits in `refresh_lib` and both scripts import it.

**2. `run_in_batches(client, query_template, ids, batch_size, *, log_label, schema_validate=None) -> list[row]`** — combines:

  - Batched IN-clause splitting (current `refresh-bailer-jones.py:167-181` + `refresh-gaia-apsis.py:307-323`)
  - Per-batch timing + progress logging (currently copy-pasted as ~10-line blocks across 3 scripts incl. SIMBAD ident batching)
  - First-batch schema validation hook
  
  Returns concatenated row list. Callers replace ~25 lines of orchestration with one helper call.

**3. `validate_spot_rows(rows_by_id, specs, script_name)`** — unifies the spot-check pattern in `refresh-gaia-nss.py:204-244` and `refresh-gaia-apsis.py:197-232`. Per-field dispatch:

  - `(expected, tol)` tuple → numeric ±tolerance match
  - `None` → cell must be null/masked
  - `str` → exact-string match
  
  Raises `SystemExit` with a delta-list (all mismatches in one failure message) per the current pattern. NSS's string-match branch is the parameterised difference.

**4. `assert_row_count(n, low, high, label)`** — extracts the row-count guard currently copy-pasted across 6+ scripts. Makes the guard unit-testable in `refresh_lib.test.py` (currently a typo like `< low` vs `<= low` would slip through). Each refresh script's `if not (MIN <= n <= MAX): raise SystemExit(...)` collapses to one call.

**5. `write_tsv` atomic + `is_up_to_date` auto-includes `refresh_lib.__file__`**

  → **Lands via sibling P1 bead H1+H2.** Verify here that the behavioural change is in place and tests pass.

## Net effect

Estimated ~120 LoC removed from the refresh scripts. Each script reduces to ~70-90 lines from current ~120-430. The remaining lines are exactly the substance worth reading (ADQL, schema, bounds, spot pins, format quirks).

## Cross-references

Sibling bugfix beads under `stellata-9mm`:

- H1+H2 — atomic write + lib mtime tracking (lands helper #5)
- H3 — xmatch angular_distance default (independent of this refactor)
- H4+H5+D1+D7 — AT-HYG ingest consolidation (lands helper #1)
- H6 — spot-checks for 3 scripts (consumes helper #3 after this lands; can land first with inline pattern)
- H7 — WDS/ORB6 sanity nets (independent of this refactor)

Recommended order: bugfix beads land first; this refactor consolidates afterwards. Helper #3 (`validate_spot_rows`) ideally lands before H6 so the new spot-checks consume the lifted helper.

## Acceptance

- All 5 helpers exist in `refresh_lib` with full docstrings + tests in `refresh_lib.test.py`.
- All 7 refresh scripts updated to call helpers; line counts approximately as projected above.
- Re-run `--force` on every refresh script; all TSVs byte-identical to pre-refactor.

## Smoke

Force-rebuild one TSV per script; diff against pre-refactor committed version → byte-identical.

title: [epic] Magellanic Cloud stellar-population overlay — LMC + SMC dedicated catalog ingest
description:
Long-term goal: render the LMC + SMC's actual stellar populations from dedicated extragalactic catalogues, replacing the sparse ~60-star AT-HYG approximation inside the Magellanic Cloud volumes.

# Why this epic exists

The Stellata catalog is built from AT-HYG, which is a Milky-Way-centric stellar catalog with a hard brightness cut at G ~ 10-12.5. The LMC has billions of stars; AT-HYG sees ~60. The SMC has hundreds of millions; AT-HYG sees ~1-5.

The result: even with stellata-dch.8l0 (LMC kinematic distance refinement) + stellata-dch.55 (SMC kinematic) + stellata-dch.58 (OGLE Cepheid per-star) + stellata-dch.56 (LMC structural-disc fallback), the rendered LMC is a sparse cluster of ~57 supergiants on (or near) a 2D shell. The actual LMC has a structured disc + bar + 30 Doradus + halo, none of which AT-HYG can ever resolve.

For the camera-anywhere viewing model (see `stellata-camera-anywhere-perception` memory), this matters: a user warping to the LMC sees ~60 stars where they should see millions.

# Approach (two-stage)

## Stage A: Variable-star population overlay (OGLE-IV)

- **Data**: OGLE-IV LMC + SMC Cepheids, RR Lyrae, LPVs / Miras.
- **Stars**: ~4.7k LMC + 4.9k SMC Cepheids; 39k LMC + 6k SMC RR Lyrae; 91k LMC + 22k SMC LPVs. Total ~170k Magellanic variables.
- **Distance**: per-star, ±1-5% via PL/PLZ relation (Cepheids best, RR Lyrae good, LPVs ~10%).
- **Rendering**: instanced quads sharing the existing star pipeline, separate vertex stream loaded from a new binary (e.g. `public/magellanic-variables.bin`).
- **Pros**: Real per-star distances, real positions, well-calibrated.
- **Cons**: Variable-star sub-population only — misses the bulk of LMC/SMC stars (main sequence, RGB).

## Stage B: Full stellar-population overlay (SMASH DR2)

- **Data**: SMASH DR2 (Nidever et al. 2021, ApJ) — ugriz photometry for ~360M sources across LMC + SMC + Bridge + foreground.
- **Stars**: ~10s of millions in the LMC volume after extracting LMC members via photometric / positional cuts.
- **Distance**: photometric distance modulus per star via isochrone fitting / TRGB / MS-fitting. Precision ~10-20% per star (limited by reddening + age + metallicity degeneracies).
- **Rendering**: similar instanced approach but scaled up to millions of stars. May need a tiled / LOD strategy.
- **Pros**: Full LMC/SMC stellar populations including disc, bar, 30 Dor, tidal arm.
- **Cons**: Distance per star is mediocre (~5 kpc uncertainty at 50 kpc). May produce a thick smear rather than thin structure for sub-populations. Massive data ingest (10s-100s of millions of rows).

## Interaction with existing AT-HYG LMC subset

Two options:
1. **Suppress AT-HYG LMC stars inside the overlay volume** — avoids double-counting. AT-HYG's ~57 supergiants are already in the overlay's catalog under different IDs.
2. **Keep AT-HYG as the "labeled bright members" on top** — search, hover, focus, constellation cross-references all keep working for the named LMC supergiants (HD 268749, HDE numbers, named Cepheids). Overlay is the background population only.

Lean toward option 2: keep AT-HYG's labelled stars, render overlay as the diffuse background, give the overlay no search / focus interaction at first.

# Children

To be filed when this epic is picked up:

- Research / design-gate child: evaluate OGLE-IV vs SMASH vs VMC; pick a Stage A data source; design the per-star binary format; figure out the render-pipeline integration (instanced quads share with stars? separate pass? LOD?).
- Stage A data ingest: refresh script + binary build for the chosen catalog.
- Stage A renderer: per-star draws in the LMC/SMC volume.
- Stage B research / design-gate: when Stage A is solid and the appetite for more depth is real.
- Stage B data + renderer.

# Why P3 (and an epic, not a task)

- P3: refinement, not regression. The LMC currently renders as ~57 stars on a flat shell, which is a known limitation rather than a bug. dch.55 + dch.56 + dch.58 address per-star precision within that sparse population; this epic addresses the population SIZE.
- Epic: 4-5 child beads, multi-PR, depends on data ingest + renderer changes + binary format extensions. Won't land in one commit.

# Prerequisites

- dch.8l0 (LMC kinematic) merged — gives us the LMC volume to overlay.
- dch.55 (SMC kinematic) merged — gives us the SMC volume.
- dch.58 (OGLE Cepheid cross-match) merged — proves the OGLE ingest plumbing.
- v6+ binary format (dch.33) merged — needed if we want gaia_source_id on the overlay sources too.

Realistically a 2027+ epic; filing now so the structure exists in the bd graph.

# References

- Soszyński et al. 2015 (OGLE-IV LMC Cepheids), 2016 (RR Lyrae), 2009 (LPVs)
- Nidever et al. 2017 + 2021 (SMASH, SMASH DR2)
- Cioni et al. 2011 (VMC)
- Zaritsky et al. 2004 (MCPS)
- Bonanos et al. 2009 (LMC luminous + variable stars)

title: Phase 3: LMC structural-disc fallback distance (where OGLE cross-match misses)
description:
Geometric per-star distance fallback for AT-HYG LMC members not caught by the OGLE Cepheid cross-match (filed separately). Lower-priority than OGLE because the structural disc model is geometric (~±2-3 kpc precision) where OGLE PL distances are physical (~±1 kpc per star).

# Goal

After dch.8l0 (LMC kinematic shell at flat 49.594 kpc) and OGLE Cepheid cross-match (per-star Cepheid distances for the ~5-15 Cepheids in AT-HYG's LMC subset), the remaining ~40-50 LMC members are B-supergiants with no per-star distance from any survey. Give those a per-star structural distance derived from the LMC's inclined-disc geometry, so the catalog renders the LMC's tilted shell instead of a flat sphere.

# Method (inclined disc model)

van der Marel & Cioni 2001 / van der Marel 2002 / Choi et al. 2018 give:

- Inclination: i ≈ 32° (range 24-35° depending on tracer)
- Line-of-nodes PA: ≈ 122° (range 100-150°)
- Distance to dynamical centre: d_0 = 49.594 kpc (Pietrzyński 2019 anchor)

For each (RA, Dec), project into the LMC disc plane, compute the line-of-sight depth offset Δd, set per-star distance = d_0 + Δd.

Existing implementation lives in stellata-38m's LMC wireframe (`src/client/local-group/`). Build-catalog.ts side can either:
- Duplicate the disc parameters in `catalog-pure.ts` (or `lmc-pure.ts` if we factor out).
- Or share the local-group source if reasonable (cross-folder import already present elsewhere).

# Priority

P3 because:
- OGLE cross-match (filed separately) is the precision-preferred approach and lands first.
- Structural-disc precision (~3 kpc at 50 kpc) is meaningful only when the camera is close to the LMC; not visible from Sol viewpoint.
- The 40-50 stars without OGLE coverage currently render at the flat anchor — that's a known limitation, not a regression vs pre-dch.8l0 (pre-dch.8l0 they were smeared 5-200 kpc).

Bump to P2 if the long-term LMC stellar-population overlay epic gets deferred and the structural-disc fallback becomes the visible LMC shape for an extended period.

# Acceptance

- Each LMC_KIN star not also caught by OGLE ends up at a per-star distance within ±3 kpc of 49.594 kpc, derived from the disc model.
- The 54-57 LMC stars now form a tilted disc, not a flat shell, when viewed from a position near the LMC.
- Disc orientation matches the stellata-38m LMC wireframe layer (visual consistency).
- Tests pin per-star distance at SE/NW/centre fixtures.

# Out of scope

- OGLE Cepheid cross-match (filed separately).
- Full LMC stellar-population overlay (filed as separate epic).
- SMC structural disc — SMC has more complex 3D structure; defer until SMC kinematic layer (dch.55) lands.

# References

- van der Marel & Cioni 2001, AJ 122, 1807 — original inclined-disc model
- van der Marel 2002, AJ 124, 2639 — refined parameters
- Choi et al. 2018, ApJ 866, 90 — modern SMASH disc model
- Pietrzyński et al. 2019, Nature 567, 200 — distance anchor

title: End-of-epic writeup: dch catalogue pipeline — community share + before/after visualisations under research/dch-pipeline/
description:
# Why this exists

The dch epic is a top-to-bottom rewrite of Stellata's star-catalogue ingest pipeline: source-ID-anchored cross-matching across AT-HYG / GCVS / CCDM / WDS+ORB6+Gaia, plus Bayesian distance posteriors, kinematic refinements for the LMC, NSS-based binaries, hand-verified known-stars corpus, and so on. By the time the epic completes, every catalogue star will trace its position, distance, magnitude, multiplicity, and (where applicable) orbital elements through a documented chain of authoritative sources rather than a single merge artefact.

That's worth sharing with the community: someone evaluating browser-based star catalogues, someone hitting the same naive-1/π distance bug in their own pipeline, or someone curious how a single-developer hobby project built up "best-possible-model-from-current-data" without compromising on principles. The work itself is also load-bearing context for understanding choices baked into the rest of Stellata's render stack.

Alex's framing: not a journal paper — colloquial "research paper" means a public-facing community share. Could be a blog post, a documentation article on stellata.xyz, or a long-form writeup checked into `research/dch-pipeline/` and linked from README. Decide format at writing time.

# Precedent: `research/star-spectral-rendition/`

The repo's existing `research/` folder follows a stable pattern for end-of-epic writeups, established for `stellata-zsr.1` (star-colour calibration):

- `RECOMMENDATION.md` — long-form prose findings, structured as TL;DR → evidence summary → implementation guidance.
- Python scripts that produced the evidence (`coverage.py`, `blackbody_color.py`, `compare_sample_stars.py`, `scene_swatches.py`, etc.).
- TSV input snapshots (`apsis_sample.tsv`) and TXT result tables (`coverage.txt`, `sample_comparison.txt`).
- PNG visualisations (`sample_swatches.png`, `hr_panels.png`, `scene_swatches.png`, `p3_swatches.png`).
- `requirements.txt` for repro.

This bead's deliverable mirrors that shape under `research/dch-pipeline/` (or similar — pick a slug at write time).

# Scope (when this bead runs)

End-of-dch-epic, after all phases have shipped:

- Phase 0 prose sweep
- Phase 1 refresh-script infrastructure (dch.20–26, dch.46, dch.48, dch.50)
- Phase 2 build-binaries.py (dch.27 shipped Stage 1; dch.28 onward for the remaining stages)
- Phase 3 build-catalog.ts overrides (dch.47 shipped B-J; dch.8l0, dch.33, dch.34, dch.35 outstanding)
- Phase 4 validation harness (dch.37 known-stars corpus, dch.51 BA-supergiant validation)
- Layer 5 doc sweep (dch.42)

The writeup surveys what was actually done — not what was planned — so the bead deliberately sits at the tail. Trying to write it mid-epic invites scope drift and rework when later phases change the story.

# Deliverable shape

`research/dch-pipeline/RECOMMENDATION.md` (or `STORY.md` / `WRITEUP.md` — pick at write time) with:

1. **TL;DR** — the problem class (position-based cross-matching fails for famous close binaries; naive 1/π distances are catastrophic for low-S/N parallaxes; the LMC doesn't fit a Galactic-density prior; etc.) and the architectural answer (source-ID anchoring + layered distance refinement + kinematic membership rules).
2. **Evidence per phase** — for each layer, the actual numbers shipped. Example for dch.47: 99.5% B-J override coverage, +15 stars rescued from prior 50-kpc cutoff, HIP 25733 14.3 → 5.5 kpc, HIP 23785 stays within 0.3%.
3. **Before/after visualisations** — Alex called out at least these explicitly:
   - Total star volume before vs after B-J correction (a "big shift" visible at scene scale).
   - LMC region before B-J / after B-J alone / after B-J + LMC_KIN (the three-frame story showing why the lockstep ordering matters).
   - HR-diagram-style scatter of catalog absmag distributions before/after, showing how the catastrophic-inversion tail (the "−9 mag supergiants at 14 kpc") collapses.
   - Possibly: a single-star case study (HIP 25733) showing its position move from "intergalactic void" to "comfortably inside disc".
4. **Architectural decisions explained** — why source-ID over position-based; why B-J runs before LMC_KIN; why the 50-kpc cutoff stays in place post-refinement; why we kept naive AT-HYG distances for the 0.5% B-J miss tail (see dch.52).
5. **What didn't make it** — scoped-out items worth flagging: SMC / Sgr supergiants don't enter the brightness regime yet, Magellanic Stream stars too faint, hand-curated per-star overrides rejected per `stellata-data-fidelity-principle`.
6. **What's next when Gaia DR4 lands** — broader B-J coverage, SMC kinematics enter scope, NSS catalogue depth improves.

Supporting Python scripts produce the visualisations from the committed binary + the data/ snapshots. Goal: a curious reader can `python3 research/dch-pipeline/foo.py` and reproduce every figure.

# Acceptance criteria

- `research/dch-pipeline/` folder exists with structure mirroring `research/star-spectral-rendition/`.
- `RECOMMENDATION.md` (or chosen filename) covers all six bullets above.
- At least 3 PNG visualisations, including the total-star-volume before/after (Alex's specific request).
- All Python scripts have a `requirements.txt` and run end-to-end against committed `data/` + `public/catalog.bin`.
- README.md gains a one-line pointer to the writeup. (`docs/` index optional.)
- Considered: whether any of this material wants to graduate to a stellata.xyz article (or blog post) once the in-repo writeup is settled.

# Connects

[[stellata-data-fidelity-principle]] — the architectural bar the writeup explains.
[[bead-authoring-scope]] — this is a deliberate single-bead deliverable; the prose IS the artefact. Not splittable.

Parent: stellata-dch (epic).
Runs after: every dch phase. Deferred via implicit dependency — at write time, file `bd dep add stellata-<new-bead> stellata-<last-dch-phase>` for whichever bead actually closes the epic.

Author: Alex Marshall. AI assistance for evidence compilation + visualisation scripts welcome; the prose voice and architectural narrative are his.

title: Document B-J override ceiling + optional HIP→DR3 source_id enrichment for ~191 empty-gaia AT-HYG rows
description:
# Finding (filed 2026-05-19 from dch.47 follow-up probing)

The Bailer-Jones DR3 distance override shipped in dch.47 fires for 310,379 / 312,024 AT-HYG rows with a Gaia source_id (99.5%). The remaining ~1,645 stars + ~1,233 stars without any gaia source_id together account for ~0.9% of the binary's 313,257 records. Probing whether the existing HIP / TYC cross-match tables (`data/gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv`, `data/gaia_dr3_tyc_xmatch.tsv`) can recover more B-J overrides shows the override is at ceiling for the current Gaia release — but a small source_id enrichment is still worth doing for downstream pipeline value.

# Breakdown of the 0.9% miss

**Population A — AT-HYG rows with empty `gaia` column (2,310 rows in source CSV, ~0.7% of binary).**

Of those:
- 1,394 have a HIP — looking up `gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv` recovers a DR3 source_id for **191** of them
- 2,298 have a Tycho-2 ID — looking up `gaia_dr3_tyc_xmatch.tsv` recovers **0** (the existing TYC xmatch table doesn't carry rows for our specific empty-gaia population; hand-checked, those Tycho-2 IDs simply aren't in the xmatch)
- **None of the 191 recovered DR3 source_ids are in Bailer-Jones** — so this enrichment unlocks zero new distance overrides

**Population B — AT-HYG rows with a `gaia` source_id but missing from Bailer-Jones (3,913 rows).**

Of those:
- 2,472 have no HIP, can't probe
- 1,378 have HIP and AT-HYG's `gaia` agrees with `gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv` — they ARE DR3 source_ids, B-J just didn't publish a posterior. Almost certainly 2-parameter (position-only) astrometric solutions, or sources excluded by B-J's quality filter
- 51 have HIP and AT-HYG's `gaia` DIFFERS from the HIP xmatch DR3 source_id (AT-HYG was carrying a DR2 source_id, or the HIP xmatch is wrong, or both candidates are real and AT-HYG picked one) — **0 of the alternative DR3 source_ids are in Bailer-Jones either**

# Conclusion — B-J override is at ceiling

No HIP-/TYC-backfill chain unlocks any new Bailer-Jones overrides. The 1,645 missing rows lack usable Gaia DR3 parallaxes, full stop. Bailer-Jones 2021 only published posteriors for sources with 5- or 6-parameter astrometric solutions. Gaia DR4 (target launch ~2026) will improve coverage; until then we're at the principled limit.

This means: **don't re-derive this when the next session wonders.** The finding is the finding. Any further uplift in override coverage waits on Gaia DR4 + a Bailer-Jones DR4 republication.

# Why the enrichment might still be worth doing — beyond B-J

The 191 newly-resolvable DR3 source_ids (Population A → HIP path) have downstream value across the dch epic:

1. **dch.28 priority chain** — WDS-component → Gaia source_id resolution uses AT-HYG.gaia as one of the join steps. Adding 191 source_ids to AT-HYG would let those 191 stars hit the AT-HYG.gaia path instead of falling through to the more-expensive PM-propagated position match.
2. **dch.33 v6 binary format** — every star carrying its DR3 source_id is the foundation; +191 stars get one. Tiny dent in the gap, but principled.
3. **Future Gaia-archive joins** — `gaia_dr3_apsis.tsv` (per-source Teff/logg/[M/H]), `gaia_dr3_nss_two_body.tsv` (Gaia-detected binaries), and any future per-source data are all keyed on DR3 source_id. Pre-resolving the source_id means later joins don't need their own HIP→DR3 step.
4. **Future B-J refresh** — if Bailer-Jones (or anyone) publishes broader posteriors via a DR4 update, having the source_id already in AT-HYG makes the join trivial.

# Why NOT to also do the 51-row Population-B replacement

The 51 rows where AT-HYG's `gaia` differs from the HIP xmatch's DR3 source_id are ambiguous:
- Could be AT-HYG carrying a DR2 source_id (legitimate but stale)
- Could be the HIP xmatch picking a wrong neighbour (Gaia archives flag uncertain matches via `xm_flag` and `angular_distance`)
- Could be both candidates real (different solutions, AT-HYG picked the one with usable astrometry)

Replacing AT-HYG.gaia in those rows risks regressing — and gains nothing for B-J (verified zero unlock). Recommend NOT touching Population B. If future work needs broader DR3 source_id coverage there, do an audit first using `xm_flag` + `angular_distance` + a SIMBAD lookup spot-check.

# Scope (if we do this — one commit/PR)

In `scripts/build-catalog.ts` (or `scripts/build-binaries.py` once that pipeline absorbs the AT-HYG load — currently AT-HYG read lives in build-catalog.ts):

1. Load `gaia_dr3_hip_xmatch.tsv` into `Map<hip, dr3_source_id>` at startup. Filter on high-confidence matches only (`number_of_neighbours == 1` AND `angular_distance < 0.5` arcsec — pick the threshold by quick histogram of the 191).
2. During `readStars`, for AT-HYG rows where `row.gaia` is empty AND `row.hip` is present: set the effective source_id to `hipToDr3.get(row.hip)` if found.
3. Pass the effective source_id (not just `row.gaia`) into `applyBailerJonesOverride`. The override will still be a miss for these 191, but stats will reflect the enrichment.
4. Log enrichment count at build time. Add `bjSourceIdEnriched` to `BuildCounts`.
5. Once dch.33 ships the v6 binary format with `gaia_source_id` per record, the enriched source_id rides into the binary alongside the original.

**No binary format change** — this is a build-time enrichment, not a schema change.

# Acceptance criteria (if implemented)

- Build runs to completion; log line shows ~191 source_ids enriched via HIP xmatch.
- Override coverage logged separately as `bjOverridden / (bjEligible + bjSourceIdEnriched)` so the +191 doesn't artificially inflate apparent coverage (they're still misses for now).
- `xm_flag` filter is conservative: high-confidence matches only.
- Unit test: pure helper that takes (gaiaCol, hip, hipToDr3) → effective source_id. Exercise both branches (gaia present → use as-is; gaia empty + hip in map → enriched; gaia empty + no hip → null).

# Priority + when to act

P3. The 191 enrichment moves 0.06% of the catalog (191 / 313,257). It's principled but small. Sensible to defer until dch.28 (WDS priority chain) is in flight — then the +191 source_ids will demonstrably exercise the AT-HYG.gaia join path in dch.28's resolver, and the value is concrete rather than speculative. Or to bundle into dch.33 (v6 binary format) so the enriched source_id is what gets written to disk.

# Connects

[[stellata-data-fidelity-principle]] — "best possible model based on current observational data and knowledge". The 99.5% ceiling under current data IS the best possible model; this bead documents the boundary.
[[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]] — enrichment count is a tier-2 statistic that belongs in `build-catalog-expected.json`.

Sibling: dch.47 (shipped) — Phase 3 distance override that revealed this ceiling.

title: Tier 2 — re-key star colour LUT from Ballesteros(B-V) to Gaia DR3 Apsis Teff
description:
Re-route the input to the blackbody → sRGB colour LUT (shipped in [pr-89] / `stellata-zsr.3`) so it prefers Gaia DR3 Apsis Teff over the current Ballesteros(B-V) → Teff path. Surfaces the empirical 2.87× coverage uplift from `stellata-zsr.1` (29.3% → 84.8% (Teff, logg) coverage) into rendered chromaticity for the ~85% of stars Apsis has parameters for.

# Origin

Filed as the natural follow-up flagged in two places:

1. PR #89 description: *"WD-specific Teff routing from the bead description is **not** implemented in this PR. Catalog v4 doesn't store `wd_subclass`; the WDs in the catalog (Sirius B, etc.) render via Ballesteros at their recorded B-V, which still lands in a blue-white range. Tier 2 (Apsis ingest under `stellata-dch`) is the natural home for per-WD T_eff routing — flagging here so it can be split into a follow-up bead if desired."*
2. `stellata-dch.49` description, "Downstream re-routings (NOT in this bead's scope)" block: *"Tier 1 colour LUT (`stellata-zsr.3`) re-keys Teff source from Ballesteros(B-V) → Apsis-direct where available (Apsis preferred; Ballesteros fallback; spectral-class fallback)."*

Both notes point at this same re-route. Folds the WD routing into the same change because Apsis carries Teff for WDs natively (Sirius B etc. have Gaia DR3 entries), so the deferred WD path collapses into the Apsis path as a byproduct — no separate `wd_subclass` plumbing required.

# Scope — single PR

Re-keys the LUT input. No LUT regeneration, no shader maths change, no catalog format bump. Apsis fields already in `catalog.bin` via `stellata-dch.49`; this bead consumes them.

# Routing priority (`star.vert.glsl`)

In strict order, first match wins:

1. Apsis `teff_gspphot` (~85% coverage; primary)
2. Apsis `teff_gspspec` (smaller, but covers some gspphot gaps)
3. Ballesteros(B-V) (current Tier 1 path; ~29% of catalog where B-V exists but Apsis doesn't)
4. Spectral-class T_TABLE (`tempKelvin` in `catalog-pure.ts`, existing)
5. WD subclass: `50400 / wd_subclass` (existing path in `catalog-pure.ts`)
6. Solar fallback (B-V = 0.65, current behaviour)

The LUT itself spans `T ∈ [Ballesteros(-0.4), Ballesteros(+2.0)]`, i.e. ~3300–40000 K. Apsis Teff outside that range clamps to LUT edge (rare — Apsis hot tail tops out near 50000 K for O stars; those clamp blue-white per the existing hot-edge behaviour). White dwarfs with Apsis Teff ~25000–60000 K land near or just above the LUT hot edge — verify the clamp produces blue-white as expected (Sirius B reference: T_eff ≈ 25200 K, well inside the LUT range).

# Dust reddening

Untouched. Apsis Teff is the **intrinsic** parameter (Apsis fits include extinction A0 explicitly). The existing dust-reddening composition in the shader operates on observed chromaticity downstream of the LUT sample and remains observer-position-dependent. Verify Mu Cephei still drifts toward intrinsic peach as the camera warps closer (the case study in `research/star-spectral-rendition/RECOMMENDATION.md` § Mu Cephei).

# Deliverables

- **`src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl`** — replace the single-source `effectiveCi → LUT` index with a routing helper that picks Teff per the priority above, converts Teff → LUT index, samples. The function name and downstream contract (`ciToColor` output → dust reddening composition) are preserved.
- **Pure helper** `src/client/shaders/blackbody-lut-pure.ts` (or extend the existing module): `teffToLutIndex(teff: number) => number` — float in `[0, 1)` for `texture()` sampling. Tested separately from the shader.
- **Routing helper** `src/client/shaders/star-color-routing-pure.ts` (new) — pure TS function `pickTeffSource(record) => { teff: number, via: 'apsis-phot' | 'apsis-spec' | 'ballesteros' | 'spectral' | 'wd' | 'solar' }`. Exact same routing the shader applies, mirrored for testability per `[[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]]`. The shader and the test BOTH consume this helper (or its constants) so they cannot drift — per CLAUDE.md § "Code conventions — DRY overrides the system prompt" / `[[stellata-named-constants-and-dry]]`.
- **`star.vert.glsl` attribute additions**: per-instance Apsis Teff (one float, pulled from the v6/v7 buffer that `stellata-dch.49` populates). Null sentinel handling matches the catalog loader's convention.
- **Counter readout** (debug-panel or build-time log): "Apsis Teff used for X / 313k stars" — pin via `expect(...).toBe(N)` per `[[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]]` so the headline coverage figure doesn't decay silently.

# Tests (per `[[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]]`)

- **Routing helper unit test**: each tier exercised with a fixture record. Priority semantics asserted as a separate test ("Apsis present beats Ballesteros even when B-V also present").
- **`teffToLutIndex` unit test**: Teff edges (3300 K, 5778 K, 40000 K) → expected indices (`toBe`, not `toBeCloseTo` for the boundary points).
- **LUT-sample-via-Teff RGB pin** for 6+ named stars covering each tier: Sirius A (Apsis gspphot, hot), Sol-anchor (Apsis gspphot via solar twin), Mu Cep (Apsis present, cool), Sirius B (WD, Apsis-routed now), a faint Tycho-only star (no Apsis, Ballesteros path), a star with neither (spectral-class path).
- **Counter test**: "≥ N records routed via Apsis" pinned with `toBe` against the build-time count.

# Smoke (per `[[alex-stellata-smoke-tests]]`, manual only)

After typecheck + vitest:

- **WD chromaticity (the deferred case from PR #89):** Sirius B, Procyon B, 40 Eridani B. Should land in a blue-white / cyan range matching Apsis Teff (~25000 K), not the Ballesteros B-V proxy.
- **Apsis cool-end win:** Antares, Betelgeuse, Aldebaran, Mu Cephei. Apsis Teff vs Ballesteros(B-V) Teff will differ for some — check no regression vs the PR #89 chromaticities; if Apsis lands closer to the reference column in `research/star-spectral-rendition/sample_comparison.txt`, that's the win.
- **Apsis fallback regression check:** a few stars known to have no Apsis (e.g. faint Tycho-only catalogue rows) should look identical to before.
- **Mu Cephei observer-position-dependent dust:** still drifts toward intrinsic peach as you warp closer.
- **Chart mode (`m`):** unchanged — chart mode skips the LUT path.
- **HR diagram qualitative shape:** density along Teff should match panel C continuity in `hr_panels.png`, ideally crisper than the Ballesteros-fed version.

# Confidence (per `[[alex-large-pr-honesty]]` at PR-open time)

- **High:** routing helper + LUT index helper (pure TS, deterministic, per-tier vitest, byte-signature-style pins).
- **Medium:** shader integration (uniform / attribute wiring is adjacent to the existing `ciToColor` site, but a new per-instance attribute is non-trivial — first low-coverage path to smoke).
- **Low (needs eyeballs):** actual rendered chromaticity vs expectation. Only the manual smoke list above catches a per-star visual regression. The Mu Cep / Sirius B / Sirius A trio is the load-bearing set.

# Out of scope (filed elsewhere)

- Adding `logg` / `[M/H]` consumers (Phase 5 `stellata-dch.40` mass-ratio refinement; Phase 5 `stellata-dch.41` eclipse photometry limb-darkening; Stefan-Boltzmann radius refinement).
- `azero_gspphot` cross-check against the Edenhofer dust map — filed separately.
- Display P3 wide-gamut output (`stellata-zsr.2`, deferred).
- Pecaut & Mamajek 2013 extended T_TABLE — Tier 3, deferred.

# Connects

- Depends on `stellata-dch.49` (Apsis fields in catalog binary).
- Transitively waits on `stellata-dch.33` (v6 format) and `stellata-dch.48` (Apsis TSV ingest).
- Closes the WD-Teff thread from `stellata-zsr.3` / PR #89.
acceptance_criteria:
- For stars with Apsis Teff present, shader sources Teff from Apsis (gspphot ∪ gspspec), not Ballesteros(B-V).
- Fallback chain hits each tier in priority order; verified by per-tier vitest spanning Apsis-direct / Ballesteros / spectral-class / WD / solar fallback cases.
- White dwarfs (Sirius B, Procyon B, 40 Eri B) now render at their Apsis Teff (deferred from PR #89 because catalog v4 didn't carry the necessary input).
- No regression for Apsis-absent stars — they continue to use the existing Ballesteros / spectral-class / solar path with identical output.
- Coverage smoke: ≥80% of records take the Apsis path (matches dch.49 acceptance), validated by an instrumented counter in the build script or a runtime debug HUD readout.
- typecheck + vitest green.

title: Phase 3 — Distance validation harness vs Vaidman et al. 2025 BA-supergiant catalogue
description:
# Why

Vaidman et al. 2025 (MDPI Universe 11, 359; DOI 10.3390/universe11110359) publishes a quality-controlled Bayesian recalculation of Gaia DR3/EDR3 distances for 132 Galactic BA-type supergiants — the exact failure-mode population dch.46/dch.47 is designed to rescue (low-S/N parallaxes for luminous distant stars where naive 1/parallax inverts to 10-15 kpc). Their Table A1 lists 119 stars with adopted EDSD_new distances; Table A2 lists 13 low-SNR / high-RUWE stars where they revert to BJ_old (explicitly not recommended for quantitative use).

The paper independently validates the dch.46 thesis:
- At high SNR their d_new closely tracks Bailer-Jones d_BJ (differences dominated by ZP shifts + slightly broader posteriors).
- At intermediate SNR (2.5-5) they see modest ~10-30% offsets at large distances.
- Below SNR 2.5 the posterior becomes prior-dominated.
- None of their adopted distances exceed ~6.3 kpc — confirming our outlier-class supergiants belong at 1-4 kpc, not 14 kpc.

This is the natural reference dataset for validating dch.46+dch.47 AND as a recurring spot-check whenever we change distance source later (DR4 B-J refresh, switch to StarHorse, build-catalog distance-priority change, etc.).

# Scope (one commit/PR)

1. **Reference dataset.** Extract Table A1 (119 EDSD_new rows) + Table A2 (13 BJ_old rows) from the published PDF into `data/distance-validation/vaidman-2025-supergiants.tsv`. Schema: `name`, `d_bj_paper_pc`, `sigma_d_bj_paper_pc`, `ruwe`, `g_mag`, `d_new_pc`, `sigma_d_new_pc`, `snr_tot`, `prior_scale_pc`, `adopted` (one of `EDSD_new` / `BJ_old`). Small (~132 rows) — regular git, no LFS.

2. **Name → Gaia DR3 source_id resolution.** Most rows use HD / BD / HR / named designations (HD 1070, BD+60 51, V755 Cas, theta Aql, eta Leo, epsilon CMa, etc.). Resolve once via SIMBAD TAP (the paper itself used SIMBAD, so we'll match by construction); commit the mapping as an additional `gaia_source_id` column on the TSV. Document the SIMBAD query in `data/distance-validation/README.md` so the mapping is reproducible.

3. **Validation script `scripts/validate-distances.py`.** Reads:
   - The shipped catalog's per-star distance (via `scripts/verify-catalog.ts` JSON dump, or `data/bailer-jones-dr3.tsv` directly — pick whichever decouples cleanest from in-flight binary schema).
   - The Vaidman reference TSV.
   Joins on Gaia source_id. Computes per-star fractional difference vs `d_new_pc` for the EDSD_new subset; aggregates median, 84th-percentile, and top-N largest disagreements. Prints a report keyed for human eyeball + suitable for grep in CI.

4. **Acceptance bar for dch.47 integration.** On the 119-star EDSD_new subset:
   - median |frac diff| <= 15%
   - <= 5 stars with |frac diff| > 50%
   The 13 BJ_old stars in Table A2 are explicitly low-quality — they're a sanity check that our adopted distance equals B-J for them (which it should, since dch.46 ships B-J's r_med_photogeo unchanged). Report-only, no fail bar.

5. **Citation.** Vaidman et al. 2025 added to SCIENCE.md references with DOI, journal, and the role the paper plays in our pipeline (independent validation reference set for the Bailer-Jones distance override). Per [[defer-doc-updates]], the SCIENCE.md edit lands in the same PR as the script.

# Reuse — recurring validation harness

The script is designed for re-run on every distance-source change:
- Future B-J refresh from a new Gaia release (DR4 -> r_med_photogeo recomputed).
- Switching distance source entirely (StarHorse, a B-J successor, or self-derived posteriors).
- Any change to build-catalog.ts's distance-override priority.

The 119-star EDSD_new subset is fixed reference data: any new distance source for these stars should agree with d_new within the same bars (or we surface the disagreement and decide explicitly whether it's a regression or an upgrade). Treated as frozen reference data per the same convention as GCVS / dust / Zucker datasets.

# Caveats

- Vaidman et al. use an EDSD prior with L = clip(0.8 * r_BJ50, 300 pc, 2500 pc) — their d_new is partially anchored to d_BJ for the prior scale length, so the comparison is not fully independent at very low SNR. The EDSD_new subset has SNR >= 2.5 where the likelihood dominates the prior; that's where the validation has teeth.
- Star identification is by name, not Gaia source_id. We're trusting SIMBAD's name -> Gaia DR3 resolution. Spot-check a handful manually against the paper's coordinates.
- Pulling Table A1/A2 out of a PDF needs care — column alignment in Table A1 (cont. spread across pages 9-11) is the obvious risk; commit the extraction script alongside the TSV.

# Smoke step

After running the script:
- Median |frac diff| value printed; top-5 disagreements named; 119/13 split tallies.
- Eyeball whether the agreement curve matches the paper's Figure 2 (tight 1:1 at high SNR, modest spread at SNR 2.5-5).

# Connects

[[bead-authoring-scope]] — one script + one reference TSV + one smoke step = one commit.
[[defer-doc-updates]] — SCIENCE.md citation at commit time, not mid-implementation.
[[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]] — pure helpers in validate-distances.py (name normalisation, fractional-difference aggregation) get a pytest sibling.

Parent: stellata-dch (catalog pipeline epic).
Depends on: dch.47 (B-J distance override needs to ship in build-catalog before we can validate it end-to-end).
acceptance_criteria:
- data/distance-validation/vaidman-2025-supergiants.tsv committed with 119 EDSD_new + 13 BJ_old rows, schema as described, including gaia_source_id column from SIMBAD resolution.
- scripts/validate-distances.py runs end-to-end against the post-dch.47 shipped catalog; prints a report with median |frac diff|, 84th-percentile, top-5 disagreements, and 119/13 tallies.
- On the 119-star EDSD_new subset: median |frac diff| <= 15%; <= 5 stars with |frac diff| > 50%.
- 13 BJ_old stars: adopted catalog distance equals B-J's r_med_photogeo (report-only).
- SCIENCE.md references section cites Vaidman et al. 2025 with DOI and role.
- Pure helpers (name normalisation, fractional-difference aggregation) covered by a pytest sibling.

title: Extend MW dust extinction to non-stellar layers (Local Group, future deep-field)
description:
**Goal:** apply the multi-layer dust stack (Edenhofer + Bayestar + analytical slab + arms — see parent **stellata-ty4**) to extragalactic objects, not just MW catalog stars. Currently `dustExtinctionAV` lives only in `star.vert.glsl`; every other layer renders unattenuated.

# Why

`star.vert.glsl` raymarches camera→star through the dust layers and applies A_V to appMag + E(B-V) to colour. No other layer does this:
- **Local Group wireframes / discs / labels** (`src/client/local-group/`) render at catalog brightness regardless of foreground MW dust — a galaxy behind several kpc of MW disc dust should dim and redden.
- **Future stellata-0hx deep-field layers** (Local Volume → Virgo → Laniakea → ...) will have the same gap.
- **Milky Way volumetric layer** already applies its own analytical dust internally; that path is unchanged by this bead.

# Why P3

Future-proofing for the universe-scale expansion (stellata-0hx is P3, stellata-36y is P2 but operates on a different axis). The current Local Group population is small and mostly wireframe — visual fidelity is dominated by morphology, not foreground extinction. Bumps to P2 once stellata-0hx implementation children start landing and add Mpc-scale objects whose flux matters.

# Approach

1. Lift `dustExtinctionAV` from `star.vert.glsl` into a shared shader include alongside the analytical-slab + Bayestar work in ty4.
2. Add the include to each consumer's shader path:
   - `local-group.ts` / `galactic-disc.ts` style wireframes — A_V dims line/fill colour, E(B-V) reddens.
   - `local-group.ts` billboard discs (for the dwarfs that render as discs not wireframes) — same.
   - Future deep-field layer shaders — folded in at construction.
3. Performance: a 48-tap raymarch per extragalactic object is cheap (dozens, not 313k). Could optionally be computed CPU-side at layer-construct time and uploaded as a per-instance attribute (positions are static).

# Open question — slab raymarch density at Mpc scales

The current `DUST_STEPS = 48` is calibrated for ≤ kpc-scale sightlines (26 pc per step at 1.25 kpc). From M31 the sightline is ~780 kpc and the slab has nonzero density only across the ~600 pc thick MW disc — 48 evenly-spaced steps would put most samples in empty space outside the slab.

Options to evaluate:
- Adaptive step count based on sightline length and where it intersects the slab AABB.
- Analytical integration of the exponential disc along the segment intersecting the disc (closed-form; avoids raymarch entirely for the slab portion).
- Two-region split: dense steps inside the slab AABB, sparse steps for the empty extragalactic segment (likely zero-density anyway).

The analytical integration is the cleanest answer if the slab is genuinely just `exp(-(R-R₀)/L_R) × exp(-|z|/h_z)` — it has a closed-form line integral.

# Acceptance

- Local Group galaxies viewed from inside the MW disc dim and redden through the analytical slab.
- MW catalog stars viewed from extragalactic vantages (e.g. M31) dim through the slab over the ~780 kpc sightline. (74e already covers this for stars; this bead extends it to non-stellar consumers.)
- No regression in near-Sol fidelity (per-star extinction inside the Edenhofer cube unchanged).
- A test or debug-panel readout for slab A_V at a known sightline (Sol → M31) matches the analytical integral within a few percent.

# Files

- New `src/client/shaders/dust-extinction.glsl` shared include (lifted from star.vert.glsl).
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — switch to the shared include.
- `src/client/local-group/local-group.ts` — add extinction sample to its shader/materials.
- `src/client/local-group/local-group-loader.ts` — wire dust-texture uniforms.
- Future deep-field layers — folded in at construction time.

# Related

- **stellata-74e** — the analytical slab implementation; this bead extends its consumers.
- **stellata-0hx** — extragalactic deep-field; will need this hooked in at layer-add time. Add a dependency-link if/when 0hx children start landing.
- **stellata-36y** — far-field statistical augmentation; the cumulative-glow layer probably wants the same extinction treatment as a single integrated A_V applied to the HiPS-derived intensity.

title: Phase 0 — Sweep v1.0 prose from code comments + README (sibling to dch.20)
description:
Sibling cleanup to stellata-dch.20. dch.20 swept v1.0 / v1-era prose from CLAUDE.md, SCIENCE.md, docs/*.md per its explicit scope. A grep audit during dch.20 execution turned up further hits in code comments + README that fall under the same stale-framing pattern but were out of dch.20's stated scope.

URL-state-format v1/v2 references in `url-state.test.ts` (lines 40, 837, 1005 — "32-bit mask in v1", "20 in v1. v3", "v1 or v2") are NOT product-v1 — keep as-is. The earlier draft of this bead description also exempted `url-state.ts:88`, but that line is product-v1 prose ("molecular clouds shelved for v1.0") sitting adjacent to the URL-flag-bit declarations — NOT a URL-format version reference. Corrected at execution time (proactive-drift-correction).

# Replacement guidance

# Acceptance

- npm test green

# Commit

Single commit. Bundled into PR #92 (per Alex 2026-05-18) alongside dch.46 + dch.20. PR labelled skip-version-bump.

# Connects

[[stellata-rename-and-stale-prose-sweep]] — same prose-follows-behaviour pattern, but here the behaviour change is the stage transition (past v1.0), not a rename.

title: Research: webapp scope ceiling + desktop fidelity uplift + technical stack
description:
# Why

First research bead under stellata-r0m. Three sub-questions need scoped answers before any desktop-app code can land:

1. **WHERE** does the webapp's WebGL + browser-memory + first-load ceiling sit on representative modest hardware (integrated GPU laptop, mid-tier phone, slow connection)?
2. **WHAT** does the desktop app add beyond that ceiling — which data layers / fidelity uplifts specifically justify the paid SKU?
3. **HOW** is the desktop app built — Tauri vs Electron vs native Swift/Metal vs Rust+wgpu — with the shader-codebase-sharing question front and centre?

# Method

**For (1) — webapp ceiling**: empirical, building on stellata-cns.1's perf framework. Pick a representative low-end target (integrated GPU laptop + mid-tier phone). Measure FPS / GPU memory / instance-attribute memory / first-load wire size at increasing star counts (313k classic_ids → 800k m11 → 2M m12 → hypothetical 5M+ Gaia-native depth cuts). Capture the cliff. Include sustained-frame variance, not just steady-state FPS — the chart-mode optimisation work (centroid cache, dirty-tracked SVG) shows variance matters as much as average.

**For (2) — desktop fidelity uplift**: enumerate concrete uplifts that would warrant a paid SKU. Candidates:
- Full Gaia DR3 magnitude depth (G < 15+, tens of millions of stars).
- Full Bailer-Jones at native catalog scale (no AT-HYG.gaia subset cap).
- Edenhofer 3D dust at higher voxel resolution (current build downsamples).
- Exoplanet catalog (currently P3-blocked behind physical-reality rewrite).
- Time-series animation (proper motion through epoch range, variable-star light curves).
- Spectroscopy / HR-diagram side panels.
- IAU constellation boundaries (deliberately excluded from v1 webapp per CLAUDE.md).
- Full LG depth (M31 substructure resolved, distant DSOs at native resolution rather than projected).
- Per-star Apsis-derived metadata exposed in hover (Teff, logg, [M/H], A0).

Rank by perceived user value + engineering cost. The bar: each uplift should be obviously beyond what the webapp can reasonably do, not a marginal improvement.

**For (3) — technical stack**: build a feature-matrix decision across four candidates:
- **Tauri** (Rust core + WebView2 / WebKit frontend) — lightweight, web stack fully reusable, smallest install footprint, no Chromium bundling.
- **Electron** — heaviest, full Chromium bundled, web stack fully reusable, well-trodden path.
- **Native Swift / Metal** — macOS-only, no shader reuse without GLSL→MSL translation, best raw perf, App Store-native.
- **Rust + wgpu** — cross-platform, WGSL shaders share concept with GLSL but require porting, largest engineering scope.

The Three.js WebGPU backend is production-ready as of 2026, which changes the calculus — running the existing renderer through Tauri-on-WebGPU could be the path of least resistance for sharing the renderer codebase across web and desktop. Worth a focused spike before committing to a stack.

# Acceptance

Written recommendation in this bead's notes covering all three questions. Should be specific enough that a follow-on child bead can be filed (e.g. \"Tauri + WebGPU, ship first as macOS Mac App Store paid SKU, full-Gaia-magnitude-depth + 3D-dust + Apsis-hover the headline\"). Each of the three sub-recommendations cites the evidence behind it (perf numbers, fidelity-uplift cost estimates, stack feature-matrix).

# Smoke

Decision-bead — no implementation. The recommendation seeds the technical-stack + scope-split + business-model children of stellata-r0m.

# Connects

- Parent: stellata-r0m.
- Architectural prerequisite (informational, not blocking research): stellata-dch — the desktop SKU consumes its refinement layers at deeper scale.
- Informed by: stellata-cns.1 (perf-ceiling baseline) + stellata-cns.2 (row-driver pattern). Both inform what the webapp's ceiling looks like AND how the desktop's deeper ingest extends it.
- Memory: [[alex-stellata-profile]] (sole-contributor, personal-project framing for the paid SKU).

title: Stellata desktop app — full-fidelity paid expansion (scope split with webapp)
description:
# Why this epic exists

The webapp's scope is bounded by WebGL performance + browser memory + first-load budget. Even after the deep-tier catalog expansion (stellata-cns), there is a hard ceiling beyond which the webapp cannot reasonably go without degrading the experience on modest hardware (integrated GPUs, mid-tier mobile, slow connections). A desktop app with native GPU access (Metal on macOS, Vulkan / DX12 elsewhere) and no first-load gzip budget can comfortably ingest substantially deeper data — full Gaia DR3 magnitude tiers, full Bailer-Jones at native catalog scale, higher-resolution 3D dust, deeper LG / extragalactic layers, exoplanet catalog, time-series, spectroscopy panels.

The natural split: **webapp keeps a perf-ceiling-fit dataset, free at stellata.xyz.** **Desktop expands fidelity / depth, paid.**

# Open questions to resolve via children

1. **Webapp scope ceiling** — where does the webapp's instance count + texture / buffer memory ceiling sit on representative modest hardware? Closely related to stellata-cns.1's measurement work. Determines the natural boundary between free and paid.
2. **Desktop incremental fidelity** — what specifically does desktop add? Candidates: full Gaia magnitude depth (G < 15+), full Bailer-Jones at native catalog scale, 3D extinction at higher voxel resolution, exoplanet catalog (currently P3-blocked), asterism / IAU boundaries, time-series proper motion + variability animation, spectroscopy / HR-diagram panels, full LG depth (M31 substructure, distant DSOs at native resolution), deeper Local Bubble / Radcliffe Wave coverage.
3. **Technical stack** — Tauri (lightweight, WebView2/WebKit, web stack reusable) vs Electron (heavy, Chromium-bundled, web stack reusable) vs native Swift / Metal (macOS-only, no shader reuse without translation, best raw perf) vs Rust + wgpu (cross-platform, WGSL shaders share concept with GLSL but require porting). Three.js's WebGPU backend changes the calculus — Tauri-on-WebGPU could be the path of least resistance for sharing the renderer codebase across web and desktop.
4. **Business model** — pricing tier, distribution channel (Mac App Store, Steam, direct), licensing, free-trial / demo strategy, single-purchase vs subscription.

# Sequencing / dependencies

- Architectural prerequisite: **stellata-dch** must ship. The desktop expansion consumes the same source-ID-keyed refinement layers (B-J, Apsis, astrometry, NSS, LMC kinematic) at greater depth. No point splitting webapp vs desktop until dch's pipeline is the canonical ingest path.
- Informed by **stellata-cns + cns.1 + cns.2**. Those set the webapp's depth ceiling AND the row-driver pattern. Desktop just pushes that pattern deeper.
- Children: research-first. Decision-bead up front to scope the split before any code lands.

# Out of scope (for now)

- Mobile native (iOS / Android) — separate epic if/when desktop is solid. Same "free webapp / paid native" pattern probably applies but mobile-specific.
- Server-side rendering or cloud streaming — separate question entirely.
- Per-platform optimisations once the stack is chosen — child epics under whatever stack wins.

# Connects

- [[alex-stellata-profile]] — personal-project context; paid desktop is a sensible scope expansion not a commercialisation pivot.
- [[stellata-prioritization-framework]] — sits behind closer-to-Sol layers in the queue. P3 / future until those ship.
- Architectural prerequisite (related): stellata-dch (source-ID-keyed refinement stack at deeper magnitude tiers is exactly what the desktop SKU would consume).
- Informed by: stellata-cns + stellata-cns.1 + stellata-cns.2 — webapp's depth/budget ceiling AND row-driver pattern feed directly into the split-point definition.

title: Evaluate row-driver choice: Gaia-native vs deeper AT-HYG for the deep-tier fill
description:
# Why
