   matches. Famous name? GJ designation? Unnamed?
3. Look up the candidate in SIMBAD / WDS by RA/Dec or HD.
4. Conclude: is it the same star duplicated, or a real-but-collapsed pair?

# Acceptance

Notes captured on the bead naming the AT-HYG rows + SIMBAD identification
+ conclusion. If conclusion is "should be in `multiples-overrides.tsv`",
file the override addition as a separate one-line bead.

title: Verify AI + search crawler access through Cloudflare Workers
description:
src/worker.ts is a thin passthrough to env.ASSETS — no special routing, no security middleware. In principle every crawler should be able to fetch any path. Verify in practice, because Cloudflare's account-level bot management and zone-level WAF can block bots without the Worker code knowing.

# Tasks

1. Test asset paths from a clean network (or via curl --resolve / VPN) with these User-Agents:
   - Googlebot/2.1
   - Bingbot/2.0
   - DuckDuckBot/1.1
   - ClaudeBot/1.0
   - GPTBot/1.0
   - ChatGPT-User/1.0
   - PerplexityBot/1.0
   - Google-Extended (Google's AI training UA)
2. For each, confirm 200 + correct content-type for:
   - /
   - /robots.txt
   - /llms.txt
   - /sitemap.xml
   - /og-image.png
3. Check Cloudflare dashboard:
   - Security → Bots → 'Verified bots' should be allowed (default)
   - WAF managed rules → no AI-bot block rules active (Cloudflare offers an opt-in 'block AI bots' toggle — must be OFF)
   - Page rules / cache rules don't strip user-agent or return 403/451 for bot UAs
4. If any block surfaces, file follow-up bead.

# Dependencies

Best done after the static assets (robots.txt, llms.txt, sitemap.xml, og-image.png) ship; otherwise there's nothing to verify access to.

# Acceptance

- All test UAs return 200 for all five paths
- No surprise blocks in Cloudflare console
- Findings documented in bd remember note (or attached to this bead's notes) for future re-verification post-deploy-changes

# Smoke

Run a small bash script that loops UAs × paths and asserts 200 + reasonable content-type. Capture output in PR description.

title: GEO content audit + noscript citability for AI search
description:
Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) is the practice of making content citable by AI search surfaces (Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Claude, You.com). Differs from classic SEO in that the win condition is 'the AI cites your URL when answering related questions', not 'the page ranks #1'.

Stellata's / is a near-empty HTML shell that bootstraps a WebGL app. AI crawlers that don't execute JS see nothing substantive — no description of what Stellata is, no answerable passages, no citation hooks. This bead adds a crawler-friendly content layer and audits AI-search reachability.

# Tasks

1. Add a <noscript> block inside <body> with a self-contained paragraph describing what Stellata is, what data it visualises (AT-HYG ~313k stars, GCVS variables, Edenhofer dust), what it requires (WebGL2), and a link to the (eventual) /about page or github source. Crawlers that don't run JS render this; users with JS never see it.
2. Cross-check passages with the llms.txt content so the AI agent gets coherent context whether it fetches /, /llms.txt, or both.
3. Audit citability — passages should be answer-shaped (declarative, accurate, source-attributed). Reference: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system (style of citable passages).
4. Test AI-crawler view of /:
   - curl --user-agent 'ClaudeBot/1.0' https://stellata.xyz/ → confirm the response contains noscript content
   - Repeat for GPTBot, PerplexityBot, Googlebot
5. Test citation behaviour:
   - Ask ChatGPT search 'what is stellata.xyz?' → confirm accurate answer with citation
   - Ask Perplexity 'browser-based 3D star catalog viewer' → confirm stellata.xyz appears
6. Document baseline citation rate in a follow-up note (e.g. bd remember) for future regression-check.

# Dependencies

Survey-style; ideally lands AFTER robots.txt, llms.txt, and meta-tags beads so the audit reflects the as-shipped state.

# Acceptance

- <noscript> block renders accurate, self-contained description
- crawl test with ClaudeBot UA returns the noscript content in the HTML response
- ChatGPT search returns a coherent answer when asked about stellata.xyz (manual check)

title: Add public/sitemap.xml (minimal, root-only)
description:
A sitemap is a small signal but a clean one — robots.txt points to it, search engines and AI crawlers prefer crawling sites with one, and Google Search Console / Bing Webmaster Tools want one for submission.

This bead ships a minimal sitemap.xml covering only / for now. The deferred stellata-8c1.1 build pipeline will generate a richer multi-page sitemap once /about and /docs/* exist; this bead's output is the placeholder until then.

# Tasks

- Add public/sitemap.xml with one <url> entry:
  - <loc>https://stellata.xyz/</loc>
  - <lastmod>{ISO-8601 date of last meaningful content change}</lastmod>
  - <changefreq>monthly</changefreq>
  - <priority>1.0</priority>
- Reference from robots.txt (Sitemap: https://stellata.xyz/sitemap.xml) — handled by the robots.txt bead
- Decide on hand-maintained vs. build-time generated. Default: hand-maintained one-line sitemap is fine until 8c1.1 lands. If a build script is preferred, scripts/build-sitemap.ts can read package.json and emit the file — but adds CI complexity for one entry.

# Acceptance

- curl https://stellata.xyz/sitemap.xml returns 200, content-type: application/xml (or text/xml)
- https://www.xml-sitemaps.com/validate-xml-sitemap.html reports 0 errors
- Cross-reference from robots.txt resolves

# Supersession

Mark as superseded (bd supersede) when stellata-8c1.1 lands its multi-entry sitemap generator.

title: Generate favicon, apple-touch-icon, web manifest
description:
Stellata has no favicon — the browser tab renders a blank generic icon. Add a full icon set covering:
- Desktop browser tabs (favicon.ico + favicon-16/32.png)
- iOS home-screen save (apple-touch-icon.png, 180×180)
- Android home-screen save + PWA manifest hint (192/512 PNGs + manifest)
- Theme colour for browser chrome and PWA splash

# Tasks

- Design a single 512×512 source icon (could be a simple star glyph or the Stellata 'S' on a dark backdrop; aesthetics should match the app theme — dark sky, star-disc treatment)
- Generate the standard set (https://realfavicongenerator.net/ does this from one source):
  - public/favicon.ico (multi-resolution; 16, 32, 48)
  - public/favicon-16.png, public/favicon-32.png
  - public/apple-touch-icon.png (180×180)
  - public/icon-192.png, public/icon-512.png (PWA)
- Add public/manifest.webmanifest with name, short_name='Stellata', icons array, theme_color='#000000', background_color='#000000', display='standalone' (or 'browser' if PWA install is undesired), start_url='/'
- Wire <link> tags in src/client/index.html <head>:
  - <link rel='icon' href='/favicon.ico'>
  - <link rel='icon' type='image/png' sizes='32x32' href='/favicon-32.png'>
  - <link rel='icon' type='image/png' sizes='16x16' href='/favicon-16.png'>
  - <link rel='apple-touch-icon' sizes='180x180' href='/apple-touch-icon.png'>
  - <link rel='manifest' href='/manifest.webmanifest'>
  - <meta name='theme-color' content='#000000'> (may overlap with meta-tags bead — confirm only one such meta is present)

# Acceptance

- favicon renders in Chrome / Safari / Firefox tab
- iOS Safari 'Add to Home Screen' shows the apple-touch-icon
- https://realfavicongenerator.net/favicon_checker shows green for all platforms
- manifest.webmanifest validates at https://manifest-validator.appspot.com/

# Smoke

Open https://stellata.xyz/ in a clean browser profile and confirm tab favicon. On iPhone Safari, 'Add to Home Screen' and confirm the icon and name.

title: Add Schema.org JSON-LD structured data
description:
Structured data (JSON-LD) lets search engines and AI search surfaces understand what Stellata is — software, free-to-use, in-browser, by Alex Marshall, AGPL — and surface richer snippets / knowledge-graph entries / AI citations.

# Tasks

Add a single <script type='application/ld+json'> block to src/client/index.html <head>. Use Schema.org WebApplication (correct type for a browser-based app — vs. SoftwareApplication which implies install). Fields:

- @context: https://schema.org
- @type: WebApplication
- name: Stellata
- alternateName: Stellata Star Catalog
- url: https://stellata.xyz/
- image: https://stellata.xyz/og-image.png
- description: (same one-line pitch as meta description)
- applicationCategory: EducationalApplication (or VisualisationApplication if Schema.org adds it)
- operatingSystem: Any (WebGL-capable browser)
- browserRequirements: Requires WebGL2
- author: { @type: Person, name: 'Alex Marshall' }
- license: https://www.gnu.org/licenses/agpl-3.0.html
- offers: { @type: Offer, price: '0', priceCurrency: 'USD' }  // free-to-use signal
- isAccessibleForFree: true
- inLanguage: en

(Optional second @type via @graph: CreativeWork referencing the AT-HYG and GCVS catalogues as sources.)

# Acceptance

- https://search.google.com/test/rich-results reports 0 errors, valid WebApplication detected
- https://validator.schema.org/ reports 0 errors
- View-source shows one JSON-LD block, well-indented for readability
- License URL resolves (link-check)

# Smoke

Run https://search.google.com/test/rich-results against https://stellata.xyz/ — should detect WebApplication with name, image, author.

title: Add SEO meta tags + OpenGraph + Twitter Card to src/client/index.html
description:
src/client/index.html currently has only charset, viewport, and <title>. Add the SEO/social meta-tag set to make the page indexable, shareable, and crawlable.

# Tasks (all in src/client/index.html <head>)

Add the following:
- <meta name='description' content='...'> — one-sentence pitch (≤ 155 chars). Draft: 'Stellata is a browser-based 3D star catalog viewer rendering ~313k real stars from the AT-HYG catalog with GCVS variables, galactic dust, and constellation lines.'
- <link rel='canonical' href='https://stellata.xyz/'>
- <meta name='robots' content='index,follow,max-image-preview:large'>
- <meta name='theme-color' content='#000000'> (match dark theme; consider media-query variant for light theme)
- OpenGraph (Facebook, Slack, LinkedIn, iMessage):
  - og:title, og:description, og:type=website, og:url, og:image (=/og-image.png, absolute URL), og:image:width=1200, og:image:height=630, og:image:alt, og:site_name=Stellata, og:locale=en_US
- Twitter Card (X / Twitter, also honoured by some others):
  - twitter:card=summary_large_image, twitter:title, twitter:description, twitter:image (absolute URL), twitter:image:alt
- (Optional) <meta name='author' content='Alex Marshall'>

# Dependencies

Blocked by: og-image bead (the asset path /og-image.png must exist; otherwise the card breaks)

# Acceptance

- View-source on / shows all meta tags above
- Lighthouse SEO score ≥ 95 (was below 90 before)
- https://www.opengraph.xyz/url/https%3A%2F%2Fstellata.xyz%2F renders a valid card with title/description/image
- https://cards-dev.twitter.com/validator renders a valid summary_large_image card
- Meta description ≤ 155 chars, title ≤ 60 chars (Google SERP truncation)

# Smoke

Share https://stellata.xyz/ in Slack — the unfurl should show title, description, and the OG image.

title: Create 1200×630 OpenGraph / Twitter Card image asset
description:
Stellata currently has no OG image — share links on Slack, iMessage, Twitter, LinkedIn, Discord all render as text-only previews. Add a 1200×630 PNG (the canonical OG aspect ratio; also satisfies Twitter Card 'summary_large_image') so shared URLs render as visual cards.

# Tasks

- Generate public/og-image.png (1200×630, sRGB, PNG-8 or PNG-24, target <300 KB)
  - Option A: capture a curated frame from the running app (focused on a recognisable region — e.g. Orion arm, galactic centre direction) and composite the Stellata wordmark on top
  - Option B: design a graphic in Figma/Affinity that evokes the app's visual language without being a raw screenshot
  - Default to Option A — the actual render is the headline
- Optional: public/og-image-square.png (1200×1200) for platforms that prefer square (LinkedIn sometimes; iMessage on iOS uses square in some contexts)
- The meta-tag wire-up (og:image, twitter:image) lives in the companion 'Meta tags on /' bead — this bead just ships the asset

# Acceptance

- File committed under public/
- Image visibly renders the Stellata aesthetic (dark sky + stars + wordmark)
- File size < 300 KB
- Image passes through Cloudflare Workers ASSETS at https://stellata.xyz/og-image.png with content-type: image/png

# Smoke

- After meta tags wire-up bead lands, run https://www.opengraph.xyz/ or https://cards-dev.twitter.com/validator against https://stellata.xyz/ and confirm the card renders

title: Add public/llms.txt for AI agent context
description:
llms.txt is an emerging convention (proposed by Answer.ai, adopted by Cloudflare and others) for giving LLM-powered agents a concise, well-structured summary of a site so they can answer questions or cite it accurately. Unlike robots.txt (which gates crawling), llms.txt is a content hint.

# Spec reference

https://llmstxt.org — markdown file at /llms.txt with:
- H1 site name
- Blockquote summary
- Optional sections grouping canonical URLs by purpose

# Tasks

- Add public/llms.txt with:
  - # Stellata
  - > one-paragraph description (WebGL star catalog, AT-HYG 313k stars, GCVS variables, browser-based, ecliptic / galactic / extragalactic layers)
  - ## About — link to https://stellata.xyz/ and the upcoming /about
  - ## Documentation — placeholder block for future /docs/* (mark as 'coming soon' or omit until 8c1 lands)
  - ## Source — link to https://github.com/alexmensch/stellata
  - ## License — note AGPL-3.0-only

Keep the file short (under 200 lines) — agents truncate aggressively.

# Acceptance

- curl https://stellata.xyz/llms.txt returns 200, content-type: text/plain or text/markdown
- File renders as readable markdown
- No broken links; all URLs are canonical (https, no trailing slash inconsistency)

# Smoke

Ask Claude / Perplexity to fetch https://stellata.xyz/llms.txt and summarize Stellata — the summary should accurately reflect the project (WebGL, star catalog, ~313k stars, variable stars).

title: Add public/robots.txt with crawler allowlist + sitemap pointer
description:
Stellata currently has no robots.txt at the apex domain, so crawler behaviour is implicit (allow-all by convention). Add an explicit robots.txt to:

1. Confirm crawl access for classic search engines
2. Confirm crawl access for AI search crawlers (ClaudeBot, GPTBot, PerplexityBot, ChatGPT-User, Google-Extended, etc.) — opt-in to AI citation
3. Point to /sitemap.xml once it exists

# Tasks

- Add public/robots.txt
- Default: User-agent: * Allow: /
- Add explicit Allow blocks for ClaudeBot, GPTBot, ChatGPT-User, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended, Googlebot, Bingbot, DuckDuckBot, Applebot
- Sitemap: https://stellata.xyz/sitemap.xml (forward-reference; sitemap.xml ships in a separate bead)
- Verify Cloudflare Workers ASSETS binding serves /robots.txt as text/plain (the @cloudflare/workers types should infer content-type from extension)

# Acceptance

- curl https://stellata.xyz/robots.txt returns 200, content-type: text/plain
- robots.txt linter (e.g. validator.w3.org or robotstxt.dev) reports 0 errors
- File is committed under public/ (matches existing static-asset pattern)

title: SEO + GEO discoverability for /
description:
Standalone SEO and GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) work for the WebGL app at /. Scoped to ship INDEPENDENTLY of the deferred stellata-8c1 public-site epic — the children here are surface-level meta/asset additions to the existing src/client/index.html and public/ static-asset tree, no new pages required.

# Goal

Make stellata.xyz findable and citable by:
- Classic search engines (Google, Bing) — Lighthouse SEO ≥ 95
- AI search surfaces (ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews) — accurate brand mention and citable passages

# Why now (independent of 8c1)

stellata-8c1.4 bundles meta tags + sitemap + favicon into the /about + /docs landing, but 8c1 is deferred. Meanwhile the bare / has no description, no OG card, no robots.txt, no llms.txt — every share preview is blank, no AI crawler signals exist, and there is no canonical to anchor against. These are quick wins worth shipping on their own; once 8c1 lands, its sitemap and meta work supersedes the standalone bits filed here.

# Reconciliation with stellata-8c1.4

stellata-8c1.4 is a `related` link target — when 8c1 reopens, audit which children here are already-shipped vs. still-needed and revise 8c1.4's task list to drop the overlap. The standalone work here is forward-compatible with 8c1's eventual sitemap and brand-box Docs button.

# Children

- robots.txt — explicit allow for search + AI crawlers, sitemap pointer
- llms.txt — emerging convention; concise project summary for AI agents
- OpenGraph + Twitter Card image — 1200×630 PNG asset, share-link previews
- Meta tags on / — description, OG, Twitter Card, canonical, robots, theme-color
- JSON-LD structured data — Schema.org WebApplication/SoftwareApplication
- Favicon + apple-touch-icon + web-manifest — full icon set
- Sitemap.xml — minimal one-page sitemap for / (will be superseded by 8c1.1)
- GEO content audit — citable passages for AI search; noscript fallback
- Verify AI crawler access through Cloudflare Workers passthrough

# Out of scope

- /about and /docs/* pages (covered by stellata-8c1)
- Per-docs-page schema and sitemap (stellata-8c1.1)
- Versioned docs, blog, changelog
- Multi-language / hreflang (English-only for v1)
- Paid SEO tooling / GSC submission automation (manual is fine)

title: Research: ingest pulsars (ATNF / other) — visual representation and pulsar-planet intersection
description:
# Why

Sibling to stellata-d94 (brown dwarfs / WISE). Pulsars are a class of
compact-object point sources entirely absent from Stellata today — and
they raise representation questions that the brown-dwarf bead doesn't.
Unlike Y-dwarfs, pulsars are usually invisible optically; they're
detected via radio / X-ray / gamma timing. But they're scientifically
prominent and, crucially, they intersect with the exoplanets epic
(stellata-bk5) — pulsar planets were the first exoplanets ever
detected (PSR B1257+12, 1992), and new ones still appear:

Reference example: PSR J2322−2650 — millisecond pulsar at ~230 pc
(distance from radio timing parallax), with an exoplanet
PSR J2322−2650 b (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PSR_J2322%E2%88%922650_b)
discovered via pulse-timing variations.

# Scope of this bead (research only)

Survey the landscape and decide whether (and how) to ingest pulsars in
a v1 pass. Do NOT implement.

## 1. Catalogue survey

Primary candidate: **ATNF Pulsar Catalogue** (Manchester et al. 2005,
https://www.atnf.csiro.au/research/pulsar/psrcat/) — ~3500 known
pulsars with PSR J-name, position, period, period derivative,
dispersion-measure distance, characteristic age, etc. Frozen-data
download (per frozen-external-data memory: commit raw catalogue under
data/, no live fetch in build).

Also investigate:
- EPN Database of Pulsar Profiles — pulse-shape data, not needed for
  positional rendering.
- Princeton Pulsar Group catalogues — generally subsumed by ATNF.
- Fermi LAT Pulsar Catalogue — gamma-ray detections, a useful subset
  for "visible to high-energy instruments" tagging.
- Recent millisecond-pulsar timing-array catalogues (NANOGrav, EPTA,
  PPTA) — better positional + distance precision for the MSP subset.

## 2. Distance accuracy — the unique pulsar problem

Pulsar distances come from three sources, with very different
accuracies; the catalogue ingest must carry a distance-method flag:

- **Radio timing parallax** (best, ~5-10% accuracy, only available for
  a few tens of nearby MSPs including PSR J2322−2650).
- **Dispersion measure (DM) + Galactic electron density model**
  (NE2001 — Cordes & Lazio 2002; or YMW16 — Yao, Manchester & Wang
  2017). Typical accuracy 20-40%, can be factor-of-2 wrong in
  directions where the electron-density model is poorly constrained.
- **HI absorption distance limits** — bracketing distances from
  21-cm absorption against known structures. Rare.

This is fundamentally less accurate than AT-HYG parallax for most
pulsars. Some treatment of distance uncertainty (an uncertainty halo?
a separate visual style?) should be in scope for the design — false
precision matters here in a way it doesn't for nearby stars.

## 3. Visual representation — the open questions

Pulsars are NOT stars in the conventional sense. Possible treatments:

- **Distinct disc/glow tier with non-stellar colour ramp.** Most
  pulsars have no meaningful optical counterpart; a fixed neutral or
  cyan-shifted colour (suggesting X-ray / radio emission) avoids
  implying false blackbody temperature.
- **Actual pulse animation.** Pulsars literally pulse — periods range
  from milliseconds (MSPs) to ~10 s (slow pulsars). At real time these
  are imperceptibly fast; under time-warp (per stellata-3re's `t`
  infrastructure) a 1 ms pulsar at 1e6× speed becomes a visible 1 kHz
  flicker. Possible visual hook for the existing time-warp UX, but
  needs care — variable-star pulsation in Stellata uses R ∝ √L over
  weeks-to-years periods, which is a very different regime.
- **Special glyph in chart mode.** Chart mode already has variable-
  ring and binary-wing glyphs; a pulsar-specific glyph (e.g. a stylised
  pulse arc) would fit naturally.
- **Catalog-flag bit** in catalog.bin, similar to the variable-star
  bit and the planned exoplanet-host bit. Lazy-load a sidecar
  pulsars.json with period, P-dot, DM, distance-method, age class.

## 4. Spectral class — pulsars don't have one

Stellata's render pipeline assumes a spectral classification (parsed by
`parseSpectral` in scripts/catalog-pure.ts). Pulsars don't fit that
schema — they're neutron stars, surface T can be 1e5-1e6 K (X-ray)
with no optical counterpart in most cases. The few with optical
counterparts (Crab, Vela, Geminga) are very faint. The catalog
ingest needs an "object class" field that bypasses the spectral path.

This is the same architectural question raised in stellata-d94 for
Y-dwarfs (Teff < 500 K, off the bottom of the spectral ramp) and
likely future ingest of white dwarfs, magnetars, etc. — converging
toward a unified "object class" column with class-specific render
paths. Worth coordinating the design decision across both beads.

## 5. Pulsar-planet intersection with stellata-bk5

The NASA Exoplanet Archive (stellata-bk5.1's data source) already
contains pulsar planets — PSR B1257+12 b/c/d, PSR B1620-26 b
(circumbinary in M4), PSR J2322−2650 b. If the build pipeline
ingests pulsars as hosts, those exoplanet entries naturally hang off
the pulsar host via the same hostname-cross-match logic, with no
special-casing on the exoplanet side.

Open question: does the orbital-ring + planet-body render path in
stellata-bk5.3 work visually around a tiny non-stellar host? Pulsar
planets are at AU-scale orbits around a ~20 km object; the visual
scale will be very different from Sol's planet system. May need
distinct treatment.

# Out of scope (file as follow-ups if scoping concludes "yes")

- Build-script changes for ATNF ingest.
- Rendering layer for compact-object class.
- Cross-match logic between ATNF and AT-HYG (mostly disjoint anyway).
- Pulse-animation shader work.

# Notes

- Closest known pulsars: PSR J0437-4715 (~157 pc), PSR B0950+08
  (~262 pc), Geminga (~250 pc), PSR J0108-1431 (~210 pc), PSR
  J2322−2650 (~230 pc). Far more sparse near Sol than AT-HYG stars.
- Per the prioritization framework, layer-ingest research sits at P3.
  Coordinate with stellata-d94 — both are "exotic point-of-light"
  research and should converge on a shared object-class architecture
  if implementation proceeds.

title: Research: ingest exotic point-of-light objects (brown dwarfs, sub-stellar) from WISE — and evaluate WISE as exoplanet source
description:
# Why

Stellata currently renders only stars from AT-HYG (Hipparcos/Gaia stellar
catalog). There's a class of nearby point-of-light objects that are
absent from any stellar catalog but are scientifically interesting and
visually plausible as additional discs/glows in the local volume —
notably cold brown dwarfs.

Reference example: WISE 0855−0714 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WISE_0855%E2%88%920714),
the coldest known brown dwarf, at ~2.3 pc from Sol — closer than most
named stars currently rendered. It's invisible to optical catalogs but
prominent in WISE infrared data.

This bead is to scope (not implement) two related questions:

1. Should Stellata's model include sub-stellar / exotic local objects
   (brown dwarfs, white dwarfs not in AT-HYG, etc.), and if so, from
   which catalog?
2. Is the WISE / AllWISE / CatWISE catalog family also a good source
   for exoplanet candidates (direct-imaging companions to nearby
   stars), feeding the deferred exoplanets epic (stellata-bk5)?

# Scope of this bead (research only)

- Survey WISE catalog products (AllWISE, CatWISE2020, NEOWISE) for
  coverage of nearby (<~25 pc) sub-stellar objects.
- Identify which derived/curated catalogs already collate the
  interesting subset (e.g. NASA's "Census of L, T, and Y dwarfs",
  Kirkpatrick et al. compilations) so we don't have to do photometric
  selection ourselves.
- Assess WISE's value for exoplanets — direct-imaging companion lists
  vs. the NASA Exoplanet Archive already targeted by stellata-bk5.1.
  Likely complementary, not a replacement.
- Decide which (if any) objects to ingest in a v1 pass, and the
  rendering treatment (separate disc/glow tier? merged with stars
  with an "object class" attribute? distinct point-marker layer?).
- Conform to the frozen-external-data convention: any ingest commits
  the raw catalog under data/ (LFS if >1 MB), no live fetch in the
  build pipeline.

# Out of scope (file as follow-ups if scoping concludes "yes")

- Build-script changes to actually ingest WISE.
- Rendering changes to display sub-stellar objects.
- Cross-matching WISE companions into the existing star catalog as
  exoplanet hosts (would belong under stellata-bk5).

# Notes

- WISE 0855 is at d ≈ 2.31 pc — closer than 61 Cygni and Sirius.
  Including it (and the other ~50 known Y-dwarfs within 10 pc) would
  meaningfully populate the immediate solar neighbourhood.
- WISE Y-dwarfs are extremely cold (Teff < 500 K); the existing
  spectral-class → colour mapping would need an extension or a
  separate colour ramp.
- Per the prioritization framework, layer-ingest research sits at P3
  until it's actively in scope; bumping to P2 is appropriate if/when
  scoping concludes a v1 ingest is small enough to ship as a
  follow-up to the solar-system / Local Bubble work.

title: Investigate star-colour calibration — current B-V piecewise gradient vs spectral-class Teff vs blackbody → sRGB
description:
The user (Alex) asked whether the star colour shown in the app could be calibrated more precisely. This bead captures the investigation. **It is research-only**: the deliverable is a written conclusion (worth doing / moot). An implementation bead would only be filed if the conclusion is "yes, worth it."

# Current state (as of investigation time)

- **Data available per star (binary catalog v4, 44 B/record):**
  - `ci` (B-V colour index, float32) — sourced from AT-HYG `ci` column (`scripts/build-catalog.ts:513`). Default fallback when missing: `DEFAULT_CI = 0.65` (solar-like, `build-catalog.ts:48`).
  - `spectClass` + `lumClass` (uint8 each) — parsed from AT-HYG `spect` string via `parseSpectral` (`scripts/catalog-pure.ts:39`).
  - **No effective-temperature column in the AT-HYG source CSV.** Confirmed by inspecting `data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` header — 34 columns, none named `teff` or similar.

- **What we already derive but don't use for colour:**
  - `T_TABLE` (`scripts/catalog-pure.ts:101-111`) — main-sequence Teff per spectral class O–M, interpolated by subclass. Currently consumed only by the Stefan-Boltzmann radius pipeline (drives `physRadius`), not by the colour pipeline.
  - `BC_TABLE` — bolometric corrections, also radius-only today.

- **Current colour function (`src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:137-143`):**

  ```glsl
  vec3 ciToColor(float ciVal) {
      float t = clamp((ciVal + 0.4) / 2.4, 0.0, 1.0);
      vec3 hot  = vec3(0.65, 0.78, 1.00);  // CI ≈ -0.4
      vec3 mid  = vec3(1.00, 0.98, 0.92);  // CI ≈  0.65 (solar anchor)
      vec3 cool = vec3(1.00, 0.55, 0.35);  // CI ≈  2.0
      return t < 0.5 ? mix(hot, mid, t*2.0) : mix(mid, cool, (t-0.5)*2.0);
  }
  ```

  Three-stop piecewise-linear gradient in CI space. Hand-tuned endpoints; no physical derivation. Dust reddening is applied upstream as `effectiveCi = iCi + absorbAV / R_V` (R_V = 3.1), so the gradient also serves as the reddened-colour ramp.

# Questions to answer

1. **Is there a per-star Teff source we can ingest** (Gaia DR3 `teff_gspphot`, PASTEL spectroscopic catalogue, etc.) that beats spectral-class lookup? If yes — what's the coverage % of our ~313 k AT-HYG classic-IDs subset?

   Frozen-external-data rule (`bd memories frozen-external-data`) applies: any Teff source must be a one-shot fetch committed under `data/`, not a live build dependency.

2. **If we stick with derived Teff:** is `T_TABLE`-based Teff (spectral class + luminosity class) materially better than `ci` for colour, given that B-V already encodes the dominant first-order temperature signal of the photosphere? For stars with a parseable `spect` we have both; the comparison is empirical.

3. **Is `ciToColor`'s three-stop piecewise gradient meaningfully wrong vs a physical blackbody → sRGB function?** Specifically: does CIE-tabulated Planckian locus → sRGB (gamma-corrected, white-point-anchored at D65 or at solar Teff = 5772 K) produce visibly different RGB at the same Teff/CI? If so, where — hot blue end, cool red end, or both?

4. **Is the answer to (3) moot because** (a) the renderer's super-Gaussian profile + glow + bloom dominate perceived chroma, (b) the magnitude range we cap at means most pixels are core-saturated and chroma sits in the wings, or (c) the differences are below the perceivable threshold against the dark background?

5. **Edge case: stars with missing `ci`.** Today we fall back to solar `DEFAULT_CI = 0.65`. If we have spectral class but no `ci`, deriving Teff from the spectral class and synthesising `ci` (via T_eff → B-V relation) would give a more honest colour for those stars than the solar default. How many catalog records are in that "spect ✓ / ci ✗" bucket?

# Proposed investigation steps

1. Quantify coverage: count records where `ci ≠ DEFAULT_CI`, where `spect` is parseable, and the intersection / set differences. One-line scan over the binary.
2. Pick ~20 reference stars across the HR diagram (Vega, Sirius, Sol, Arcturus, Antares, Betelgeuse, an O-type like Mintaka, an M dwarf like Proxima) — compare three colours per star: current `ciToColor`, blackbody→sRGB at Teff(spectral), blackbody→sRGB at Teff(B-V).
3. If the deltas are visible in-scene (manual smoke per `alex-stellata-smoke-tests`), write up the recommendation: either swap `ciToColor` for a blackbody LUT, add a Teff fallback when `ci` is missing, or ingest an external Teff catalogue.
4. If the deltas are not visible — close as **moot** with the comparison artefacts pinned in the bead notes.

# Files likely touched (if implementation follows)

- `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` — Teff-from-spectral helper already exists (`T_TABLE`); add Teff→B-V or Teff→sRGB if needed.
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts` — optionally derive and store synthetic `ci` for stars missing it.
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — replace or augment `ciToColor`.
- `SCIENCE.md` — document new colour derivation, cite source (CIE 1931 + Planck, or Mitchell Charity's blackbody table, etc.).
- `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts` — unit tests for the new helper.

# Related work

- `stellata-a7d.2.10` (P3, same parent `stellata-zsr`) — applies spectral/Teff correction to **appSize/brightness** (BC_photopic). Different output channel (size, not colour) but shares the per-star Teff infrastructure need. Whatever Teff source/derivation lands first should be reused by both.

# Acceptance

- Coverage stats for `ci` vs `spect` filled in (#1 above).
- Sample-star colour deltas tabulated for the three candidate functions (#2 above).
- Written recommendation: either (a) file an implementation bead with a chosen approach, or (b) close as moot with deltas documented as evidence.
- No code changes in this bead — research-only.
notes:
# Research outcome — 2026-05-17

Full writeup: `research/star-spectral-rendition/RECOMMENDATION.md` (in this PR).

## Headline findings

1. Current `ciToColor` shader is meaningfully wrong at the cool/red end
   (Antares ΔE=64, Betelgeuse ΔE=61, Aldebaran ΔE=55 vs physical
   blackbody). Visibly oversaturated orange-red against the additive-glow
   scene context.
2. T_TABLE-driven blackbody would REGRESS continuity — only 92,995 stars
   (29.3% of catalogue) have a known luminosity class, so the giant
   branch ends up the same colour as the main sequence at the same
   spectral letter. Panel B in `research/star-spectral-rendition/hr_panels.png`
   shows the discrete-banding artefact.
3. B-V-driven blackbody (Ballesteros 2012 + Planck/CIE 1931 → sRGB)
   gives continuous physical chroma AND honestly discriminates giants
   from MS via colour. Panel C in the same render.
4. Empirical Apsis probe (1000 random AT-HYG.gaia source_ids → Gaia DR3
   archive TAP): (Teff, logg) coverage goes from 29.3% (status quo) to
   **84.8% with gspphot∪gspspec** — projected ~266,690 stars vs 92,995
   today. **2.87× improvement.** Among Apsis-resolved stars: 28.5%
   giants, 30.6% subgiants, 40.9% MS.
5. Display P3 follow-up probe: **negligible benefit for blackbody star
   rendering.** DCI-P3 and sRGB share the same blue primary, and the
   Planckian locus stays inside both gamuts for all T > ~1700 K
   (Proxima at 3170 K is our coolest displayed star). See
   `research/star-spectral-rendition/p3_swatches.png` — only the
   synthetic 1500 K row clips in sRGB. Filed `stellata-zsr.2` for the
   day Stellata renders non-Planckian saturated colours (nebulae,
   auroral palettes, chart UI), where the gamut win actually appears.

## Tiered recommendation (decoupled, sequential)

**Tier 1 — Replace `ciToColor` with blackbody→sRGB LUT (ship standalone).**
Single shader change + build-time LUT generator. Routing: Ballesteros(B-V)
where `ci` present (96%), T_TABLE where parseable spect only (2%), WD
formula for white dwarfs, solar fallback for the rest. No catalogue
schema bump. No `dch` dependency. **Fixes the visible cool-end issue.**

**Tier 2 — Ingest Gaia DR3 Apsis under `stellata-dch` Phase 1.** New
`scripts/refresh-gaia-apsis.py` reusing `dch.21`'s `refresh_lib.py`
TAP client. Outputs `data/gaia_dr3_apsis.tsv` (LFS, ~15-25 MB). Surfaces
per-star Teff into the v6 binary format (rides `dch` Bead M). Tier 1 LUT
then keys on Teff directly. **Modest colour-fidelity gain over Tier 1
(B-V already proxies most logg discrimination), but broader payoff:
Stefan-Boltzmann radius refinement, Edenhofer dust cross-check via
`azero_gspphot`, metallicity-aware downstream effects.**

**Tier 3 — Deferred.** Pecaut & Mamajek extended T_TABLE for the
no-Apsis no-CI bucket; metallicity-aware colour offsets; Display P3
wide-gamut output (`stellata-zsr.2`, low-value for blackbodies but
prerequisite for future saturated non-Planckian work).

## `stellata-dch` integration points

- Tier 2 ingest script slots in as a Phase 1 sibling to `dch` Beads B/C/D/E.
- Apsis Teff carrier rides v6 binary format (`dch` Bead M) — 2 extra bytes
  per record alongside the planned 8-byte `gaia_source_id`.
- `dch` Phase 5 ("spectral / mass refinement") gets an honest Teff +
  logg input for stars with Apsis — worth surfacing in Phase 5 scoping.

## Timing

- Tier 1 is shippable today as a single PR. Independent of `dch`.
- Tier 2 implementation can't start until `dch.21` (`refresh_lib`) lands.
  Apsis can ingest as TSV-only first (lighter integration); v6 binary
  surfacing waits for `dch` Bead M.
- Tier 3 (zsr.2 P3) is filed deferred-ready; revisit when nebula or
  saturated non-Planckian work appears.

## Artefacts

All under `research/star-spectral-rendition/` in this PR. Re-runnable via
the local venv (`research/star-spectral-rendition/.venv/bin/python <script>.py`).
Apsis probe deterministic for `RANDOM_SEED=20260517`.

- `coverage.txt`, `coverage.py` — Step 1 catalogue scan
- `sample_comparison.txt`, `sample_swatches.png`, `compare_sample_stars.py` — Step 2b 20-star three-way comparison
- `hr_panels.png`, `hr_panels.py` — Step 2c full HR diagram with three colour functions
- `scene_swatches.png`, `scene_swatches.py` — Step 4 additive-glow-on-black perceptual swatches
- `apsis_coverage.txt`, `apsis_sample.tsv`, `apsis_sample.py` — Step 3 empirical Apsis coverage probe
- `p3_swatches.png`, `p3_swatches.py`, `display-p3.icc` — Tier 3 Display P3 demonstration (image is P3-ICC-tagged)
- `parse_spectral.py`, `test_parse_spectral.py` — Python port of catalog-pure.ts parseSpectral, parity-tested (14/14)
- `blackbody_color.py` — Planck + CIE 1931 (Wyman 2013) → sRGB AND Display P3

## Status

Research **complete**. Closing this bead alongside the artefact PR.
Tier 1 (under `stellata-zsr`) and Tier 2 (under `stellata-dch`) to be
filed as separate implementation beads on session-close.
Tier 3 P3 follow-on filed as `stellata-zsr.2`.
close_reason: Research complete — RECOMMENDATION.md merged in PR #86; Tier 1 filed as stellata-zsr.3, Tier 2 as stellata-dch.48, Tier 3 (Display P3) as stellata-zsr.2

title: Cross-host coverage audit — every focusable object uses camera-motion
description:
## Why this exists

Per `stellata-pattern-coverage-across-peers`: when a PR is framed as "all the Y in this layer use pattern X", the headline claim is false if even one peer is missed. The point of stellata-2br is unification — this audit confirms it.

Lands after the log-distance profile (sibling child 3) so the audit checks against the final behaviour, not the intermediate state.

## Scope

**Park-arrivals only.** Warp Phase 3 (observe→observe position lerp `pEnd → (0,0,0)`) is explicitly out of scope — it is the OBSERVE-mode handover, not a park-arrival, and stays inline by design. Phase 3 already uses cubic-Hermite `u²·(3 − 2u)` (the same shape the helper adopts in 2br.3), so the exclusion is geometric (endpoint at the focal star's local origin → `d_end = 0` is degenerate for log-d), not about easing form. See `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Inventory.

## What needs to be done

1. **Enumerate every park-arrival host** present in the codebase at audit time. From CLAUDE.md and `stellata.ts`:
   - Stars (`focusStar`)
   - Clouds (`flyToCloud`)
   - Any host added since stellata-2br was filed (check `bd ready` + recent merges)

2. **For each host, verify the park-arrival path** routes through `camera-motion.ts`:
   - `grep` for direct `camera.position.lerp` / `lerpVectors` calls in `src/client/*.ts` at arrival sites — should be zero (allowed elsewhere, e.g. UI fade-ins, warp Phase 3, observe-mode aim).
   - `grep` for inline `2 * t * t` / `1 - 2 *` piecewise-smoothstep formulas — should be zero at park-arrival sites. Two non-park-arrival matches in `stellata.ts` are expected and deliberate: `getObserveTransitionProgress` (HUD overlay readout) and the `updateObserveTransition` enter/exit fallthrough. Both are observe-mode TIME easing, not park-arrival distance easing, and stay piecewise by design. Warp Phase 3 already uses cubic-Hermite and does not match this grep.
   - `grep` for `3 * u * u - 2 * u * u * u` / `3·u²−2·u³` — should appear in `camera-motion.ts` (helper) and in `updateWarp`'s Phase 3 branch (already there, unchanged).
   - Inspect each `focus*` / `flyTo*` method and confirm it builds an `ArrivalState` and ticks via `tickArrival`.

3. **For any non-covered park-arrival host**, file a follow-up child under stellata-2br (do **not** stretch this bead's scope).

4. **PR description must enumerate the host list explicitly** with a tick-box per host. Per `stellata-pattern-coverage-across-peers` this is the canonical defence against false-headline PRs.

## Acceptance

- PR description carries an explicit, per-host checklist of park-arrival hosts.
- Each host has a one-line "covered ✓ / follow-up filed: stellata-XXX" annotation.
- `grep` audits show zero remaining inline arrival smoothsteps at park-arrival sites.
- Warp Phase 3 is documented as "deliberately not routed" with a link to `docs/camera-arrival.md` § Inventory. Note that Phase 3's `u²·(3 − 2u)` easing isn't itself an outlier — it matches the helper's adopted form — so no special "different easing" annotation is needed; the geometry (endpoint at focal-star origin) is the exclusion rationale.

## Smoke (manual)

For each host in the enumerated list: focus the host, confirm the park-arrival feel is the new log-distance profile (matches Sol/Betelgeuse from child 3).

## Out of scope

- Per-host calibration. If a specific host needs a different profile, file as a follow-up child, don't fix here.
- Future hosts not yet in the codebase (Local Bubble, Radcliffe Wave, etc.) — they inherit the helper when they land.
- Warp Phase 3 (out of scope by design).

title: Sweep codebase for astronomical-constant stragglers (shaders, build scripts, tests)
description:
# Problem

stellata-r9q.1 (the focused initial consolidation bead) establishes \`src/client/astronomy-constants.ts\` and migrates the two known TS sites (\`ephemeris.ts\`, \`stellata.ts\`). But astronomical / physical constants are scattered more widely than that — they likely live in:

- Shader source under \`src/client/shaders/*\` (GLSL can't \`import\` TS — these have to be either hard-coded with a source-of-truth comment + cross-language test, or substituted at build time).
- Build scripts: \`scripts/build-catalog.ts\`, \`scripts/catalog-pure.ts\`, \`scripts/build-clouds.py\`, \`scripts/build-dust.py\`, \`scripts/verify-catalog.ts\`.
- Tests (which may redefine constants — \`stellata-test-coverage-discipline\` calls this out explicitly: \"Tests: never redefine a constant the production code exports. Import it.\").
- Other client modules: dust loader, ephemeris helpers, planet-system, etc.

Per Alex (2026-05-11): \"For the reused 2s value... shared and defined in one place is important for code and unified feeling in the app.\" Same principle applies to AU, R_sun, arcsec-per-radian, and other physical constants.

# Scope

After stellata-r9q.1 lands the canonical constants file:

1. \`grep -rn -E \"206264|2.2543e-8|1\\.49597|6\\.957e\" --include=\"*.ts\" --include=\"*.tsx\" --include=\"*.py\" --include=\"*.glsl\" .\` and similar searches for arcsec/AU/R_sun literals. Triage each hit.
2. For TS files: replace the literal with an import from \`astronomy-constants.ts\`.
3. For Python build scripts: define a small \`scripts/astronomy_constants.py\` mirror with the same values and source comment. Add a cross-language test in vitest that loads both and asserts equality (or at least loads the TS file and asserts the values match documented Python ones).
4. For GLSL shaders: keep the literal but add a comment naming the canonical TS constant it mirrors. Optionally add a vitest unit test that scrapes the literal from the shader source and asserts it matches the TS constant (defensive against silent drift).
5. For tests: import the TS constants rather than redefining.

# Out of scope

- Adding new constants that don't currently exist somewhere.
- Refactoring math that USES the constants (only the literals are touched).

# Smoke

- \`npm run typecheck\` green.
- \`npm test\` green (including any new cross-language assertion tests).
- \`npm run build:catalog\` produces a binary numerically equivalent to before (byte-identical if possible).
- \`npm run build\` succeeds.

# Depends on

stellata-r9q.1 (the canonical constants file must exist).

title: Apply smooth focus-park to planet focus (when planets become click-focusable)
description:
# Problem

When planets become click-focusable in the codebase (currently they are rendered via `PlanetBodyField` but are not a click-focus target), the planet-focus path should route through the generic `parkDistance(...)` + focus-lerp primitives established by stellata-r9q.2 — rather than inventing a planet-specific snap.

Per Alex (2026-05-11): \"I want the same approach for future objects: clouds, planets, nebulae, etc. We MUST write code that is reusable.\"

# Status: DEFERRED

Planets aren't a click-focus target in the codebase yet — this bead is filed up-front (per `stellata-pattern-coverage-across-peers`) so the requirement is captured for when that work lands. \`bd defer\` immediately after creation; revisit when planet click-focus is being built (likely as part of this epic, `stellata-3re`).

# Scope (sketch, to be refined when picked up)

Route the planet-focus path through the shared helpers:

- \`R_pc\` = planet body radius (in pc, converted from the per-planet \`radius_earth\` / \`radius_jupiter\` data via planet-radius constants in the astronomy-constants file).
- \`dMinFloor\` = whatever planet manual-zoom floor this epic settles on (a few planet-radii would be a reasonable starting point, but TBD).
- No \`extraFloor\` for planets (no binary-companion analogue, though we may eventually want a host-star or sibling-planet floor).

Reuse \`focusLerpTo(...)\` from \`src/client/focus-transition.ts\` (created by stellata-r9q.2).

# Files (anticipated)

- \`src/client/planet-system.ts\`
- \`src/client/planet-body-field.ts\`
- \`src/client/stellata.ts\` — focus dispatch.

# Depends on

stellata-r9q.2 / Child 2 of stellata-r9q (the generic mechanism must exist).

# Related

stellata-r9q (focus-park UX epic) — this bead is the planet-peer counterpart; it lives under stellata-3re because it can only be actioned when planet-focus is being built.

title: Mask any close-disc star via spatial scan (stellata-rmo follow-up)
description:
Follow-up to stellata-rmo / PR 52. The shipped fix masks the most-recently-focused star + companion (persisted across Esc) plus every vertex of the highlighted constellation. This closes the most common case but leaves a residual gap: a close-disc star that has never been focused and is not a vertex of the highlighted constellation — e.g. drifting past a bright star post-warp without focusing it, or scrolling through a dense field where the camera is near a star that the user never clicked.

# Suggested approach

Build a coarse spatial index over the catalog at load (grid bin or k-d tree). Each frame, query stars within a bounded radius of the camera and run them through `placeCircle` in `src/client/disc-mask.ts`. `placeCircle` already filters by the 48 px disc threshold, so over-inclusion is cheap.

Bin sizing: pick a radius such that the per-frame candidate count stays in the same order as today's ceiling (~64). A few-parsec radius around the camera position should catch every star whose disc clears 48 px under reasonable FOV.

# Smoke step

After implementation: pick a path that drifts the camera near a star you never focused (e.g. warp to a target near Procyon, then orbit to drift past a neighbouring star), confirm constellation lines pass behind the never-focused close disc.

# Why deferred

The shipped option B (persist most-recently-focused) closes the user's exact reported repro at near-zero cost. Option C is the strict superset but requires spatial-index infrastructure that isn't justified by the current bug report. File for future discovery.

title: Search index: Tycho-2 (TYC) + Gaia DR3 IDs (already in AT-HYG, currently un-ingested)
description:
**Goal**

Ingest the `tyc` and `gaia` columns from AT-HYG (already present in `data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` columns 2 and 3, and they'll persist in the m12 upgrade) into the search index so users can find stars by Tycho-2 or Gaia DR3 IDs.

Filed 2026-05-10 after Alex asked whether m12 brings Gaia/TYC — the answer is no, but the columns are already there in classic_ids, untouched by `scripts/build-catalog.ts`. This is a free-data ingest, not blocked on a catalogue refresh.

**What changes**

- `scripts/build-catalog.ts`: parse `row.tyc` (string like `TYC 1234-5678-1`) and `row.gaia` (Gaia DR3 source_id as int64; needs string handling — DataView has no native u64, see url-state.ts approach to 3-byte HIP for prior art).
- `SearchEntry` (`scripts/catalog-pure.ts`): two new optional fields `tyc` (string) + `gaia` (string — int64 doesn't survive JSON's number-precision constraint cleanly; emit as a decimal string).
- `src/client/search.ts`: extend the prefix-form direct-lookup map (`TYC ...` / `Gaia ...`) alongside the existing HIP/HD/HR/Gl handling around `bindSearch` ~line 167.
- `src/client/search.ts:buildStarLabels` fallback ladder: extend to … → HIP → HD → HR → Gl → TYC → Gaia (Gaia at the end because raw 19-digit Gaia IDs are not human-readable; TYC slightly more readable).

**Watch out for**

- Gaia DR3 source IDs are 64-bit integers; JSON's max-safe integer is 2^53 - 1, so emit/store as decimal strings throughout. Internal indices stay numeric.
- TYC strings have a few variant formats in the wild (`TYC 1234-5678-1`, `1234-5678-1`, etc.). Normalise to a single canonical form for the lookup key (probably space-stripped).
- search-index.json size grows: ~280k AT-HYG rows × (TYC string + Gaia string) ≈ ~6–10 MB raw before gzip. Worth measuring against the 13 MB raw / 2 MB gzipped current size to see if compression keeps it acceptable.

**Smoke**

After build, search box should resolve:
- `TYC 1234-5678-1` → focuses the star (pick a known TYC entry in classic_ids).
- `Gaia 5853498713190525696` → focuses Proxima (Proxima's Gaia DR3 source_id).
- Existing HIP/HD/HR/Gl searches unchanged.

Pairs with stellata-04y (designator FAQ) so the doc and the search both add the same catalogue surface simultaneously.

title: Star designator reference / FAQ: catalogue provenance and scope of HIP/HD/HR/Bayer/Flamsteed/Gl/TYC
description:
**Goal**

User-facing reference content explaining what each star designator is, which catalogue it belongs to, and the rough scope/range of each. Sibling to stellata-e64 (rendering-decisions FAQ) — same audience, different topic. Filed 2026-05-10 after Alex asked the question in a session; the answer was non-trivial and the data wasn't documented anywhere except a flat list in SCIENCE.md.

**Reference content** (use as source material when authoring the page)

Display fallback ladder in our codebase (src/client/search.ts:90-119): proper name → Bayer → Flamsteed → HIP → HD → HR → Gl.

- **Proper name** — IAU-approved common names + historical (Sirius, Vega, Betelgeuse). ~450 currently sanctioned by the IAU Working Group on Star Names. AT-HYG `proper` column.
- **Bayer** — Greek letter + constellation genitive (α CMa = Sirius). Johann Bayer's *Uranometria* (1603), brightness-ranked within each constellation. ~1,500 stars, capped at the Greek alphabet (24) per constellation plus a Latin-letter tail used by Gould.
- **Flamsteed** — Number + constellation (61 Cyg, 47 UMa). John Flamsteed's *Historia Coelestis Britannica* (1725, posthumous), numbered west-to-east in RA per constellation. ~2,500 entries, naked-eye-ish.
- **HR** — *Yale Bright Star Catalogue* (Hoffleit & Jaschek 1991), originally 'Harvard Revised'. 9,110 stars all brighter than V ≈ 6.5 (naked-eye limit). Standard reference for bright stars.
- **HD** — *Henry Draper Catalogue* (Annie Jump Cannon at Harvard, 1918–1924) + the HDE extension. 359,083 stars total (272,150 in the main HD). Spectral types for everything; goes to about V ≈ 9.
- **HIP** — *Hipparcos Catalogue* (ESA 1997). 118,218 stars measured astrometrically by the Hipparcos satellite (1989–1993). Parallaxes + proper motions; the basis for almost all modern distance work shy of Gaia. Numbered 1–120404 with gaps. **Real range tops out at 120404** — anything labelled HIP above that in third-party tools is likely the HIC (Hipparcos Input Catalogue, pre-mission, ~400k entries) or a synthetic ID, not a real catalogue HIP.
- **Gl / GJ** — *Gliese Catalogue of Nearby Stars* (Wilhelm Gliese 1969) + *Preliminary Third Edition* with Hartmut Jahreiß (CNS3, 1991). Stars within ~25 pc; numbered ~1–965 in Gliese plus 1000–4388 in the Jahreiß extension. 'Gl' is the original prefix, 'GJ' the modern combined form — same numbering. Where you'll find Proxima Centauri (GJ 551), Barnard's Star (GJ 699).

Related catalogues we don't currently display (some already in AT-HYG's CSV but un-ingested — see stellata-nxb):
- **Tycho-2** (TYC) — Høg et al. 2000. 2.5M stars from the Hipparcos satellite's secondary star-mapper, format `TYC region-num-comp`. AT-HYG's name (Augmented Tycho HYG) is from this.
- **Gaia** (DR3/DR4) — current state-of-the-art, ~1.8 billion sources.
- **HIC** — Hipparcos Input Catalogue, pre-mission target list, ~400k entries. Sometimes loosely labelled 'HIP' in older tools.
- **CCDM** — *Catalog of the Components of Double and Multiple Stars* (Dommanget & Nys 1994). Used in build-catalog.ts to flag visual doubles via the Hipparcos MultFlag.

**Where it lives** (pick one)

(a) New section in SCIENCE.md (existing canonical home for catalogue/data provenance). Lowest-touch — slots into the existing 'Data sources' content. Not user-facing in-app.

(b) New top-level user doc `docs/star-designators.md`, linked from CLAUDE.md's documentation index AND from a new help-modal tab. Parallel to stellata-e64's (a) option.

(c) Embed in the brand-modal / info-modal as a 'Star names' tab so it surfaces in-app without a separate page.

(a) is the easiest first step; (b)/(c) build on it.

**Smoke**

After landing, a reader of SCIENCE.md (or the new doc) should be able to answer: 'What's the difference between HIP and HD?', 'Why does HIP 210432 sound off?', 'Is HR the same as HD?'.

Related: stellata-nxb (add TYC/Gaia/V-numbers/GJ as searchable designators).

title: Picker debug overlay: visualise hit-radius + tiers + pickScore in unified debug panel
description:
**Goal**

A debug overlay (toggle `debug.picker()`) that visualises the live pickStar state at the cursor: hit radii for each candidate, tier (prime / fallback), and current pickScore. Pays off for the next ambiguous-click report; would have surfaced the stellata-02d POI gate diagnosis directly (cursor lands on a star, tier circle goes green, but `togglePoi` reject path is then visible too).

**Scope**

- New `src/client/picker-debug-hud.ts` module + section in the unified debug panel (`debug.picker()`), following the `pin-debug-hud.ts` / `arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts` pattern.
- SVG overlay layer renders into the existing canvas-overlay container.
- For each star whose projected centre lies within `max(hitRadius, pixelThreshold)` of the cursor:
  - Circle at projected centre showing `hitRadius` (prime tier, solid) and `pixelThreshold` (fallback tier, dashed/faded).
  - Colour by pickStar state — green = prime winner, yellow = prime runner-up, blue = fallback hit, grey = fallback runner-up.
  - Hatched / red fill when togglePoi would reject this candidate (Sol or missing HIP) — surfaces the same gate as stellata-7g2 visually.
  - Live `pickScore` label next to the centre.
- Updates on `pointermove` (debounced to RAF).
- Listens to chart-mode toggle so the SVG layer participates in the chart-mode visibility gate too — most useful in chart mode where 1–2 px discs make overlapping picks ambiguous.

**Why a separate module**

`pin-debug-hud.ts` is currently scoped to the focused-star pin diagnostic; mixing the picker overlay in would blur the scope. New module + new `debug.picker()` registration matches the project convention (one HUD per concern).

**Origin**

Filed during PR #53 follow-up after Alex reported a coincident-star click failure (turned out to be a `togglePoi` gate, not a picker bug — but the overlay would have made that obvious from the page rather than the console).

**Smoke**

1. Open chart mode; `debug.picker()` toggle in the unified panel.
2. Hover near a crowded patch (Pleiades / Cygnus). Coloured circles appear, scores update on cursor move.
3. Toggle off → overlay disappears; no SVG noise left in the DOM.
4. Hover over Sol or another non-HIP star → its circle gets the hatched/reject styling.

title: Store Mallama Mercury c7 in third vec4 attribute to recover full polynomial out to 170°
description:
**Why**

Mercury is the only Mallama 2018 fit beyond degree 6. PR #56 capped MERCURY_PHASE.alphaMaxDeg at 87° because the dropped c7 = 6.592e-15 term contributes >0.25 mag past that, and the truncated polynomial would render Mercury wildly wrong (up to ~27 mag too bright at α = 170° if let to run). Past 87° the renderer falls through to anchor-scaled Lambert, which tracks the published 7th-order curve to within ~0.5 mag across 90°–170° — close but not exact.

To recover Mallama's published 170° validity range with full fidelity, we'd need to store c7 in a third vec4 per-instance attribute. That's the only way to lift αmax for Mercury back to its published 170° without re-introducing the truncation runaway.

**Acceptance**

- New per-instance attribute (e.g. `iPhaseCoefsC` carrying `(c7, _, _, _)` with three reserved slots for future expansion or a different packing).
- planet-body-field.ts plumbing: allocate / grow / write-static / flush / shift-down, mirroring the existing PhaseA/PhaseB pattern. The bead-9mm.169 read-back test pattern extends naturally.
- planet.vert.glsl: extend mallamaDV to evaluate degree-7 (one extra Horner step using the new attribute slot).
- phase-function.ts: extend PhaseCoefficients with c7; update mallamaDV. Update MERCURY_PHASE.c7 = 6.592e-15 and MERCURY_PHASE.alphaMaxDeg = 170. The other planets carry c7 = 0 (Mallama's published fits stop at degree 6 for them).
- Test: phase-function.test.ts — assert |mallamaPhaseFactor(MERCURY_PHASE, α) − full_7th_order(α)| stays sub-0.01 mag across 0°–170° (the truncation budget collapses once c7 lands).

**Smoke**

- npm test passes including a new tight-bound test on Mercury at high α.
- Manual: at αmax = 170°, Mercury should render apparent V ≈ +8 (matching reality, lost in solar glare). Prior PR #56 fix already gets this within 0.5 mag via anchor-Lambert; this bead closes the gap to within Mallama's own residual.

**Deferred reasoning**

Filed as P3 — anchor-Lambert at high α is already physically defensible (sub-0.5 mag error, indistinguishable from reality at the brightness levels involved). Worth tackling when the GPU buffer schema gets touched for another reason (e.g. exoplanet phase data under stellata-bk5).

title: Galactic grid + similar opaque-queue lines render on top of planet body in glow regime
description:
**Symptom**
Galactic sphere/grid lines (renderOrder -1, opaque queue) are visible 'on top of' Mercury (and presumably any planet in the glow regime). User reported during smoke testing of PR #48 (stellata-3re.19).

**Root cause**
The planet core depth mask (`uRenderMode == 2` at renderOrder -4) has a `vPhysRatio < PHYS_RATIO_THRESHOLD discard` gate copied from the disc pass's regime split. At any typical viewing distance for Mercury (Sol-focused at a few AU camera distance), Mercury's vPhysRatio ≈ 0.02 — well below 0.5 — so the core mask never fires.

That gate is the bug. The disc/glow regime split is meaningful for the *colour* passes (resolved disc vs distant point glow), but the core mask's purpose is different: it's a depth-only occluder for layers that should depth-fail behind the planet's bright body. The bright body exists at any vPhysRatio (the perceptual core where `glow >= uCoreThreshold`) — it just shrinks to a smaller area in the glow regime.

Render-order trap: galactic grid is opaque (renderOrder -1), so it draws BEFORE any of the planet's transparent passes (corrupt at 1.5, restore at 2.5, disc at 3, glow at 4) regardless of renderOrder. Without the core mask firing at -4 in the opaque queue, the galactic grid passes its depth test against background depth, paints colour into the framebuffer at Mercury's screen position, and the planet's later transparent passes can't replace it cleanly:
- Disc pass uses MaxEquation — keeps the brighter of (grid colour, planet colour). Mercury's night-side colour is dim → grid wins.
- Glow pass is additive — adds planet glow on top, but the grid colour stays.

Same root cause likely applies to MW (-3, opaque), molecular clouds (-2), galactic disc (-1) bleeding through Mercury at glow regime.

**Suggested approach (one-line shader edit)**
Drop the `vPhysRatio < PHYS_RATIO_THRESHOLD discard` from `uRenderMode == 2` in `src/client/shaders/planet.frag.glsl`:

```glsl
// Before:
if (uRenderMode == 2) {
  if (vPhysRatio < PHYS_RATIO_THRESHOLD) discard;  // <-- delete this
  if (vAppMag > uMaxAppMag) discard;
  if (glow < uCoreThreshold) discard;
  outColor = vec4(0.0);
  return;
}
```

Effect:
- Glow regime: core mask now fires at the perceptual core (`glow >= uCoreThreshold`), writes Mercury's actual depth at -4 in opaque queue.
- Galactic grid / MW / clouds at -1 / -3 / -2 now depth-fail behind Mercury's perceptual core.
- Halo region of Mercury (where `glow < uCoreThreshold`) untouched — background still peeks through, preserving the soft halo→MW falloff the corrupt+restore pair already preserves for orbit rings.
- Resolved-disc regime unchanged (vPhysRatio gate was redundant — disc gate at the colour passes already handles regime splits).

**Why this is filed rather than included in PR #48**
PR #48 was scoped to the orbit-ring fix and grew to include 5 9mm follow-ups, a corrupt+restore depth pair, and a stencil-then-revert iteration. Adding another shader change to a load-bearing depth-write at renderOrder -4 wants its own focused smoke pass — confirm no regression in MW haze through stellar halos, no regression in molecular cloud fall-off near close-range planets, no regression in resolved-disc viewing of bright planets (Jupiter/Saturn at host-orbit-radius). Cleaner as a follow-up.

**Acceptance criteria**
- At any viewing distance where Mercury renders, galactic grid lines passing through Mercury's screen position do NOT bleed through Mercury's bright body.
- Same for MW, molecular clouds, galactic disc background lines.
- Halo region of Mercury (the dim outer perceptual disc) still allows background MW haze to show through (unchanged behaviour, preserved by the gate on `glow >= uCoreThreshold`).
- No regression in viewing scenarios that the corrupt+restore orbit-ring fix already handles.

**Smoke step**
Focus Sol with galactic grid + galactic disc enabled. Rotate the camera so Mercury passes in front of a galactic grid line. Confirm the grid line is hidden behind Mercury's bright body. Confirm dim haze (MW background) still shows softly through Mercury's outer halo region.

**Found during smoke testing PR #48 (stellata-3re.19)**

title: Planet glow visible through host star (corrupt+restore depth bypass)
Hide planet's own orbit ring across its body when zoomed in (3re.19 follow-up)
description:
**Context**
Filed as a follow-up after the stellata-3re.19 orbit-ring fix landed (PR #48). The PR's corrupt+restore approach correctly hides:
- Far-side ring segments behind the planet (depth-test fail vs the 0.0 corrupt write)
- Other planets' rings behind the focused planet (same mechanism)
- Other planets' near-side rings in front of the focused planet (correctly drawn)

What it does NOT hide:
- The focused planet's OWN orbit ring at the planet's exact orbital tangent — i.e., the segment of the ring that geometrically passes through the planet body at the planet's current position. This segment lives at depth ≈ planet_depth, which at Mercury-class viewing distances is on the order of 1e-7 in log-depth encoding. In a 16-bit depth buffer (1.5e-5 per step) this rounds to 0, indistinguishable from the corrupt pass's gl_FragDepth = 0.0. The depth-test passes (0 <= 0) and the ring fragment draws over the planet's body.

This is a small visual artefact — a faint ring line bisecting Mercury at its current orbital position, visible only when looking right at the planet.

**Why defer**
The artefact is tolerable at typical Sol-focused viewing where the planet is small on screen. It only becomes visually distracting when the user can fly close enough to fill the viewport with the planet body — exactly the affordance stellata-2f6 is about. The fix needs the camera to actually zoom into the planet first; without that affordance, sinking complexity into hiding the artefact is premature.

**Investigation tried (all on PR #48 smoke testing)**
- vPhysRatio gate: outer-disc occluder never fired at typical viewing because Mercury is in glow regime (vPhysRatio ≈ 0.02). Removed the gate.
- gl_FragDepth = 0.0 corrupt across full halo: too generous, broke at body-only (user wanted body-only break).
- gl_FragDepth = 0.0 corrupt + restore at glow >= uCoreThreshold: works for far-side and other planets' rings; FAILS for the orbit's own tangent due to depth precision (the fundamental issue).
- Stencil-based masking (NotEqual against bit 1 written by the planet body): worked perceptually for the planet but stencil bleed appeared to mask ALL render lines on Mercury, not just the orbit ring. Reverted (commit 0678241 → 66e5bf4 on PR #48). Diagnosis incomplete — the user's report was 'every single orbit ring and render line appears on top of Mercury' which suggests stencil was being read by far more layers than just the orbit ring (e.g. constellation lines, distance vector chevrons, scale bar, focus ring) or the auto-clear-stencil between frames isn't behaving as assumed when other passes also write to it. Worth a clean re-examination from scratch under the zoom-in epic.

**Approach options for the future fix (no commitment)**

1. **Per-ring screen-space discard.** Replace the orbit ring's LineBasicMaterial with a custom ShaderMaterial. Pass per-ring uniforms: own-planet's projected NDC center + body radius in pixels. Fragment shader discards if within radius. Precision-independent, per-ring scoped (no cross-ring bleed). Requires CPU-side mirror of the planet shader's pxSize calculation per frame.

2. **Stencil with cleaner isolation.** Re-attempt the stencil approach but isolate the bit so only the orbit ring reads it (verify no other layer accidentally tests stencil). Per-ring stencil bits (one bit per planet, capped at 8 with standard stencil) if needed.

3. **Depth bias on the planet body's depth-write.** Push the planet's depth slightly toward camera (gl_FragCoord.z - epsilon). Strict-Less depthFunc on orbit ring would then fail equal cases. Bias would have to scale with camera distance — fiddly but precision-correct.

**Acceptance criteria**
- When the user is focused on a planet body filling >5% of the viewport, the planet's own orbit ring does not visibly bisect the body from any viewing angle.
- Other planets' rings depth-test normally against the focused planet's body (in front: visible; behind: hidden).
- No regression in the existing PR #48 behaviour at distant viewing.

**Smoke step**
After zoom-into-planet works, focus Mercury and rotate the camera. Confirm the orbital tangent at Mercury is hidden across the body for all rotation angles, while other planets' rings respect 3D depth normally.

title: Evaluate whether stars benefit from a planet-style outer-disc occluder pass
description:
**Confidence: Low — speculative**

**Context**
PR #48 (stellata-3re.19) added a fourth render pass to the planet pipeline — an outer-disc depth occluder at renderOrder 1.5 — so far-side orbit-ring fragments depth-fail behind the planet's full visible disc, not just the bright core. The header comment in `planet-body-field.ts` now explicitly calls out the divergence from the star pipeline (3 passes vs 4) and references this follow-up.

The star pipeline only has a core depth mask. Halo fragments deliberately push `gl_FragDepth = 1.0` so distant stars peek through additively — that behaviour is core to the look and must NOT change.

**Question**
Are there other layers that depth-test against a star and would benefit from being occluded by the full halo annulus (not just the bright core)? Candidates worth A/B-testing visually:

- Galactic-grid lines (renderOrder = -1, depthTest: true) passing through the soft halo annulus of a foreground star like Sirius. Background layers already depth-fail behind the core mask; the halo annulus currently lets them through.
- Distance-vector chevrons (SVG overlay — likely *not* affected since it's CPU-projected DOM, but worth confirming).
- Constellation stick-figure SVG lines passing close to a foreground star (same DOM caveat).
- The galactic-disc grid loop bleeding through Aldebaran-class halos.

**Likely outcome**
Probably no — the halo's far-plane depth write is intentional 'pseudo-transparent' behaviour and any layers we'd want to occlude already use SVG/DOM (immune to depth). But the question deserves a 30-minute investigation and a written 'no, because…' so the divergence is settled, not just deferred.

**Suggested approach**
1. Open the app focused on Sirius / Aldebaran / Vega.
2. Enable galactic grid + galactic disc.
3. Visually confirm whether the grid line passes 'through' the halo annulus or correctly disappears behind it. Same for distance-vector when a star sits between camera and target.
4. Write the verdict in this issue's notes and close. If the answer is 'yes, port it', file an implementation child.

**Smoke step**
Visual A/B; no code changes for the investigation itself.

title: getFocusedPlanetLocalPositions returns a stale Float32Array subarray after growCapacity
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`PlanetBodyField.getHostLocalPositions(idx)` (planet-body-field.ts:316-324) returns `this.bufLocalRel.subarray(...)` — a view into the underlying typed array. `Stellata.getFocusedPlanetLocalPositions()` (stellata.ts:1056) re-exports it; `planet-labels.ts:86` calls it on every frame. So far so good.

The risk: `growCapacity()` (planet-body-field.ts:364-391) **replaces** `this.bufLocalRel` with a fresh `Float32Array(newCap * 3)`. Any caller still holding a subarray reference is now reading a stale, never-updated buffer. Today this isn't a live bug — `planet-labels.ts` re-fetches every frame, and Sol-only attaches stay under the initial capacity of 16. But:

- The contract is silent on lifetime ("Returns a Float32Array slice…" — no warning that the view becomes stale).
- bk5 will attach exoplanet hosts dynamically, triggering grows. If any future caller caches the slice across frames (e.g. a debug HUD that polls less often, or a test that grabs the slice and asserts later), it silently reads stale data.
- The contract also doesn't forbid mutation — "overlays must not mutate" is a JSDoc plea, not a Readonly type. A typed Float32Array doesn't have a Readonly variant in TS, but returning a `.slice()` (copy) would force the immutability AND survive growCapacity at the cost of an allocation per call.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts:316-324` (`getHostLocalPositions`); `src/client/stellata.ts:1056` (`getFocusedPlanetLocalPositions`); `src/client/planet-labels.ts:86` (consumer that re-fetches each frame, hiding the issue).

**Suggested approach**
Two options for Alex:

(a) **Document the contract explicitly** — add a doc comment "WARNING: the returned view is invalidated by capacity grow / detach. Re-fetch per frame; do not cache across frames." Lowest cost, no behaviour change.

(b) **Return a `.slice()` (copy)** — survives capacity grow, costs one allocation per call (9 × 4 bytes for Sol = 36 bytes/frame at Sol-only; bk5 scale grows linearly). Trade per-frame allocation pressure for safety. Probably overkill for the current use site.

**Why**
The integration-point flag again: planet-labels and a future debug HUD would both consume this method. The current implementation is correct for the current consumer but makes a footgun for the next one. Alex's call.

**Smoke step**
For (a): doc-only change, no behavioural test needed. For (b): unit test that calls `getHostLocalPositions(idx)`, triggers a grow via additional attachHost calls, and asserts the original slice still resolves to its correct values (would fail with subarray, pass with slice).

title: PlanetBodyField.attachHost uses Date.now()/1000 directly, bypassing the time.ts abstraction
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`PlanetBodyField.attachHost()` (planet-body-field.ts:233) initialises planet positions with `this.writeHostPositions(host, Date.now() / 1000)` — reaching into wall-clock directly. Every other read of the time variable goes through `Stellata.getT()` (which is the contract `time.ts` documents in 3re.1: "Anything driven by ephemerides … reads the live value via Stellata.getT()").

Concretely this means: if the time-scrubber epic (stellata-nmu) lands `setT(t)` to scrub time backwards/forwards, the very first frame after attach shows planets at WALL-CLOCK position rather than at the scrubbed `t` — they only snap to scrubbed-`t` on the next `update()` tick. Single-frame visual artefact, but its also the only path in the codebase that reads `Date.now()` in service of an ephemeris value. The same file pulls `t` correctly in `update()` (passed in by the caller from `Stellata.getT()`).

`planet-system.ts:215` has the same smell: `getPlanetOrbitOrientations(Date.now() / 1000)` to evaluate the once-per-attach orbital orientation. Sub-arcmin-per-millennium drift makes this defensible, but its still wall-clock-by-name in a module that imports `tToJDE` from time.ts.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts:233` (`writeHostPositions(host, Date.now() / 1000)`); `src/client/planet-system.ts:215` (`getPlanetOrbitOrientations(Date.now() / 1000)`).

**Suggested approach**
Either (a) plumb the current `t` value into `attachHost()` as a parameter so the caller (`Stellata.attachHost` site at stellata.ts:944) supplies `this.getT()`; or (b) accept a `t` resolver in the `PlanetBodyField` constructor — `() => number` — and call it in attachHost. Option (a) is simpler and matches the `update(camera, t)` signature already in place. The orient case in planet-system.ts has the same fix shape.

**Why**
The 3re.1 design note explicitly puts `Stellata.getT()` as the single source of truth for ephemeris time. Wall-clock leakage at `attach` time is a small breach of that contract. At Sol-only scale this is invisible (Sol attaches at startup, nothing scrubs `t`), but the bk5 epic will attach exoplanet hosts dynamically (search-result-driven), at which point any future scrubber will produce a per-attach pop.

**Smoke step**
With the time-scrubber out of scope for v1, the smoke is: a `setT(t)` test stub before the attach call shows the first iLocalRel frame is computed at `t` not `Date.now()`. Today: typecheck + test, no visual change.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: PlanetBodyField.flushAttributeRange marks all 7 buffers dirty per frame even though only iLocalRel changes
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`PlanetBodyField.flushAttributeRange()` (planet-body-field.ts:561-575) sets `needsUpdate = true` on every per-instance attribute (iLocalRel, iHostLocalPos, iRadiusPc, iColour, iSolidity, iAlbedoP, iHostAbsmag) even though the per-frame `update()` path only ever rewrites iLocalRel. The other six are static — written once in `writeHostStaticAttributes()` at attach, never touched again until detach. The PR comment acknowledges this ("At Sol-only scale this is a 7-attr × 9-instance upload — fast. bk5 may need range-based updates.") but doesnt narrow the per-frame flush to the one attribute that actually changed.

A second smell on the same function: its parameters are `_start: number, _count: number` (leading underscore = unused), and the body comment notes three.js doesnt support partial-range updates "without `updateRanges`" — but `updateRanges` IS the documented three.js way to do partial uploads. The signature lies about what it does (range-based) and the implementation falls back to whole-buffer flush.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts:561-575` (`flushAttributeRange`); `src/client/planet-body-field.ts:480-496` (where static attributes get written once); `src/client/planet-body-field.ts:296-313` (`update()` only mutates iLocalRel).

**Suggested approach**
Split the flush into two helpers: `flushDynamicAttributes()` for per-frame iLocalRel updates and `flushStaticAttributes()` for attach/detach/recenter. The per-frame call from `update()` shrinks to one `needsUpdate = true`. The recenter case writes iHostLocalPos and would call `flushDynamicAttributes()` extended to include iHostLocalPos, or add a third helper. Bonus: switch to `attribute.updateRanges = [{start, count}]` for actual partial uploads at bk5 scale — three.js r152+ supports it.

**Why**
At Sol-only scale (9 instances × 7 floats × 4 bytes = ~252 bytes/frame extra) the cost is negligible, but the architectural argument the bead epic cares about is bk5 scale: 100 hosts × 8 planets each = 800 instances × 6 redundant attributes = ~5 MB of static buffer re-uploaded every single frame. Thats not "may need range-based updates eventually" — thats a measurable per-frame cost on hardware with constrained bus bandwidth (mobile). The fix is small enough to land before bk5 lights it up.

**Smoke step**
After refactor: confirm `npm run typecheck && npm test` clean. In the browser, panel-tune star/planet sliders and verify visual identity vs main. Add a unit test that mocks needsUpdate setters per attribute and asserts `update()` only flips iLocalRel.

title: [PR #56] mallamaPhaseFactor lacks defensive α clamp — asymmetric with lambertianPhaseFactor
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`lambertianPhaseFactor` clamps α to [0, π] defensively (and has a test for it: `lambertianPhaseFactor(-1)` returns 1). `mallamaPhaseFactor` does not — it converts `alphaRad` to degrees and drops directly into the polynomial Horner expansion if `aDeg ≤ alphaMaxDeg`. The shader is safe (acos returns [0, π]), and the only TS caller that matters routes through view-space geometry that produces a non-negative α. But the function is exported as a top-level utility next to a defensively-clamped sibling — the asymmetry is a quiet API smell.

**Location**
`src/client/phase-function.ts` lines 76–94 (`mallamaPhaseFactor`).

**Suggested approach**
Two options. (a) Add a leading `const a = Math.max(0, Math.min(Math.PI, alphaRad));` to mirror `lambertianPhaseFactor`, plus a parallel test pinning `mallamaPhaseFactor(MARS_PHASE, -1)` and `mallamaPhaseFactor(MARS_PHASE, 4)` (both should equal Lambert at the clamped α). (b) Document in the docblock that callers must pre-clamp; leave the body untouched. Option (a) is the smaller surprise.

**Why**
Defensive clamping in one function and not its sibling invites "I'll just call mallamaPhaseFactor — same shape" mistakes. Worse, the polynomial path silently gives wildly wrong values at large negative or out-of-range α (Horner happily extrapolates), so a misuse fails-loud-but-not-loud-enough.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — existing tests pass with the clamp added because they all pass valid α. The new tests (-1 → Lambert(0), Math.PI+1 → Lambert(π)) cover the clamp.

title: [PR #56] Mercury c7 truncation has no quantitative test bound
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
