`MERCURY_PHASE` drops Mallama's published `c7 = +6.592e-15` term to fit a degree-6 polynomial in two vec4 attributes per instance. The source comment says "sub-0.25 mag for α < 120°, ~2.9 mag at α = 170°" — but no test pins those bounds. `mallamaPhaseFactor` is exercised at α=30° (returns ΔV in (1.0, 1.3)) and over the full validity sweep `for finite/positive`, but neither asserts the truncation error against the reference 7th-order fit. A future edit that further truncates (drops c6 to free a vec4 slot) would silently widen the error, and a bug in the c5/c6 values could pass current tests as long as ΔV stays positive.

**Location**
`src/client/phase-function.ts` lines 122–131 (Mercury coefficient block); test file `src/client/phase-function.test.ts` (gap — only a single 30° spot-check, plus the all-curves-finite sweep).

**Suggested approach**
Add a test that compares `mallamaPhaseFactor(MERCURY_PHASE, α)` to a reference 7th-order evaluation `(0 + 6.617e-2·α + … + 6.592e-15·α^7)` over a sweep, asserting |ΔV_truncated − ΔV_full| stays under 0.25 mag below 120° and under 3.0 mag at 170°. Cheap test (~10 lines, runs over the validity range) and pins the exact assumption the source comment makes. Surfaces a Mercury-specific tradeoff if Alex ever wants to revisit.

**Why**
The truncation is a deliberate storage-vs-accuracy trade. Documenting it in prose is necessary but not sufficient — without a test, the tradeoff slowly erodes as future edits forget the original error budget. This is the only Mallama 2018 fit that's not a faithful copy of the published polynomial; the test would make that explicit.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — new test passes against current code. As a sanity step, pad the c6 by 10% and confirm the test catches it.

title: [PR #56] cullDistancePc + AttachedHost.brightestReflectance docblocks stale after peakPhaseFactor fold-in
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
Two adjacent doc comments in `planet-body-field.ts` describe the cull-distance machinery as if it still operates on `p · (R/a)²`, but the PR now folds `peakPhaseFactor` into that proxy. Specifically:

1. `AttachedHost.brightestReflectance` (line 126): `/** max over planets of `p · (R / a)²` — the geometry-independent reflectance proxy. Drives cullDistancePc. */` — no mention of the c0 boost.
2. `cullDistancePc` docblock (lines 95–98): `The actual apparent-mag formula has a φ(α) factor in [0, 1], so the real visibility threshold is at d ≤ d_cull. Using d_cull as the outer bound is conservative...` — the [0, 1] claim no longer holds (Saturn's φ(0) ≈ 1.66). The conservative-bound argument still works because peakPhaseFactor is folded into the input, but the explanation is now misleading.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts` lines 95–98 (cullDistancePc docblock), line 126 (AttachedHost.brightestReflectance JSDoc).

**Suggested approach**
Update the AttachedHost field comment to `max over planets of p · (R/a)² · peakPhaseFactor(coefs)`. Update the cullDistancePc docblock to acknowledge that φ(α) can exceed 1 for bodies with empirical c0 < 0 (Saturn), and that the cull-distance formula stays conservative because the caller folds peakPhaseFactor into the input proxy. Two small comment edits — one commit.

**Why**
These two comments are the canonical explanation for why d_cull is a safe outer bound. Once the underlying assumption changes, leaving the comment stale invites a future contributor to "simplify" by removing the peakPhaseFactor multiplication on the grounds that φ ≤ 1.

**Smoke step**
`npm run typecheck` (no behavioural change). Re-read both comments and confirm a fresh reader can derive the cull bound from the new wording.

title: hud-overlay/poi-overlay: hideArrow / hideEntry don't reset numeric sentinels — stale lastLabelX/Y survives show/hide cycle
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`hud-overlay.ts` `hideArrow` resets only `lastD` (to '') and `lastLabelDisplay` (to 'none'). It does NOT reset `lastLabelX`, `lastLabelY`, `lastLabelText`, `lastOpacity`, or `lastPointerEvents`. After a hide → show cycle (e.g. user defocuses a star then refocuses a different one), the next `updateOne` call may see new label coordinates within `ATTR_DIRTY_PX` of the old (cached, no-longer-displayed) values and skip the setAttribute. The `label.style.display` IS rewritten (sentinel was 'none'), but the `x`/`y` attributes from the previous focal session may persist on the element if the show-from-hide path lands close enough to the previous coordinates.

Same shape in `poi-overlay.ts` `hideEntry` — sets the four display states but doesn't reset the numeric `lastRingCx`, `lastRingCy`, `lastArrowLabelX`, `lastArrowLabelY`, `lastOnScreenLabelX`, `lastOnScreenLabelY`, or text fields. Re-pinning a previously-pinned star at slightly different geometry could see the same dirty-skip.

Visible impact today is small because the old `x`/`y` were what the element was last rendered at, and the new ones are within 0.05 px (otherwise the guard fails and the write happens). So you get an off-by-half-pixel label position relative to where the rest of the overlay puts it. Probably invisible, but it's the regression class the PR flags as "stale dirty-track state surviving across show/hide cycles."

**Location**
src/client/hud-overlay.ts:469-484 (hideArrow); src/client/poi-overlay.ts:189-194 (hideEntry).

**Suggested approach**
Two ways to fix:

1. **Wipe-on-hide**: extend hideArrow / hideEntry to reset every numeric sentinel back to its initial value (`-Infinity`, `-1`, `''`, etc). Mechanically simple, mirrors emptyArrowState() / createEntry() defaults.

2. **Wipe-on-show**: when transitioning from hidden → visible (detected via `lastLabelDisplay === 'none' && desired === ''`), reset the numeric sentinels before the per-attribute writes so the writes always land.

(1) is the minimum-disturbance fix; (2) is more localised. Either way: extract the reset logic into a single `emptyArrowState()` reuse path so it stays consistent.

**Why**
This is exactly the failure mode the PR description calls out ("stale dirty-track state surviving across show/hide cycles"). Worth fixing in the same module rather than relying on the visual smoke checks to catch a half-pixel drift.

**Smoke step**
Focus star A, sit idle one second, defocus, focus star B at a position whose label resolves to within 0.05 px of star A's label position — the label `x`/`y` setAttribute should fire on the show transition, not be skipped by the stale sentinel. Verifiable via DevTools mutation observer on the label element.
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (hideArrow/hideEntry wipe every per-attribute sentinel)

title: hud-overlay applyFade: pointerEvents sentinel '' equals desired-state on first apply — first-frame pointer-events write skipped
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`hud-overlay.ts` `emptyArrowState()` initialises `lastPointerEvents = ''`. `applyFade` derives `clickable = alpha >= 0.5 ? '' : 'none'` and writes only if `clickable !== state.lastPointerEvents`.

Steady-state alpha is 1, so `clickable === ''` matches the sentinel — `label.style.pointerEvents` is never explicitly written. This is fine because the inline style starts as `''` (the SVG element default is whatever CSS resolves to, and `style.pointerEvents` is the empty string when no inline rule has been applied). So the actual rendered `pointer-events` resolves to whatever CSS matches the label class.

The bug surface is when CSS is **not** the source of truth for the desired pointer-events behaviour. The pre-PR code unconditionally wrote `label.style.pointerEvents = clickable` every frame, which guaranteed inline-style precedence. The new code skips that write on the alpha=1 path, so any CSS rule that sets `pointer-events` on a parent or via cascade now wins on the first frame after `emptyArrowState` is reset (e.g. after `hideAll`, which doesn't reset `lastPointerEvents`). If the CSS happens to compute to the same effective value, no-op; if it doesn't, behaviour changes.

The `distance-vector-overlay.ts` clone of this pattern has the same shape (`lastPointerEvents = ''` sentinel + `distUi.style.pointerEvents = pe`). Same risk.

**Location**
src/client/hud-overlay.ts:66 (sentinel), :579-583 (write guard); src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:61 (sentinel), :232-236 (write guard).

**Suggested approach**
Use a sentinel that does not match either of the possible written values — e.g. `lastPointerEvents = '\0'` or change the type to `string | null` with `null` initial. Either way, force the first write to land. Same fix in both files.

Alternatively: confirm via inspection that no CSS rule writes `pointer-events` on `.gal-arrow-label` / `#dist-ui` / their ancestors, document that, and pin a test or comment. The pre-PR behaviour was "inline-style always wins"; the new behaviour is "inline-style wins after the first non-empty write."

**Why**
The PR identifies sentinel-init bugs as the highest-risk regression class for this pass. This is one — admittedly subtle, but the kind that survives all six manual smoke checks listed in the PR plan and shows up as a stray click landing on the Sol label during navigate-mode arrow fade only on a specific browser/CSS path.

**Smoke step**
Focus a star with Sol off-axis, drag the camera until the navigate-mode disc-coverage fade engages (`alpha < 1`), then drag back. Click on the Sol label location while the fade is at `alpha < 0.5` — the click should NOT slerp the camera to Sol (the label should be unclickable). Then drag back to `alpha = 1` and confirm the click DOES slerp. If the second case fails (click does nothing), the sentinel is suppressing the restore-to-clickable write.
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (applyFade pointer-events sentinel poison-init)

title: [PR #54] No test asserting the headline 10-char cam=[0,0,3.7] URL — only <=12 char upper bound
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The PR summary leads with "the canonical near-Sol orbit URL (?v= for cam=[0,0,3.7]) drops from v2's 22 base64url chars to **10**". This is the bead's headline win, mentioned in three places (PR title implicit, summary, release notes). The test at url-state.test.ts:134-146 asserts `expect(blob.length).toBeLessThanOrEqual(12)` — i.e. "≤ 12", not "= 10".

The encoder's actual byte count for cam=[0,0,3.7] is: 1 ver + 1 mask + 1 sub + 4 z = 7 bytes → ceil(7\*4/3) = 10 base64url chars. A future change that flips a default constant or adds an outer-mask byte would silently regress the headline win to 11 or 12 chars without tripping the test.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.test.ts:134-146.

**Suggested approach**
Tighten to `expect(blob.length).toBe(10)`. Add a parallel assertion that `blobBytes(blob) === 7` for the exact wire-byte count, mirroring the LEB128 block's exact-equality style at lines 836-857.

**Why**
The looser `toBeLessThanOrEqual` only catches a regression that *grows* past 12 chars. The PR is sold on a specific number; the test should pin that number.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — the assertion is a regression guard with the exact win.

title: WarpState.pEnd is mutated mid-animation; frame ownership of pStart/A/dirBack/dir0 is implicit
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
PR #50 mutates `state.pEnd` in-place at phase-3 start to convert it from the source-local frame to the destination-local frame. The other position-typed fields on `WarpState` — `A`, `pStart`, `dir0`, `dirBack` — are NOT shifted; they are still in the source-local frame for the rest of the warp. Today this is benign because `A` and `pStart` are only read in phases 1 and 2 (before the recentre runs) and `dir0` / `dirBack` are unit vectors so frame-invariant. There's no comment, no assert, and no type-level signal warning a future reader that 'after phase 3 first frame, only `pEnd` and unit vectors are valid; positional fields are stale'. A future feature that reads `state.A` or `state.pStart` during phase 3 / finishWarp would land subtly off by exactly the recenter delta.

**Location**
src/client/stellata.ts:338-384 (WarpState interface) and src/client/stellata.ts:3679-3681 (the in-place mutation)

**Suggested approach**
Two options. (1) Shift the positional fields too in the same block — three more triples of `-= dx/dy/dz`. Cheap, makes the whole struct frame-coherent, eliminates the gotcha. (2) Document the invariant on the WarpState interface and on the mutation site: a comment block calling out which fields are valid in which frame after phase 3. Option (1) is more robust; option (2) is cheaper and acknowledges that today's readers don't need them.

**Why**
The PR's correctness rests on no other code reading `state.pStart` / `state.A` after phase 3 begins. That's a non-local invariant — anyone touching updateWarp or finishWarp later has to know it without a signpost. Either fix it or sign-post it.

**Smoke step**
1. Apply chosen option.
2. Existing manual smoke from the PR test plan still passes (observe→observe long warp, no jitter; skip works in all phases).

title: EventBus.EmitArgs uses 'extends void' which traps if a payload type ever includes void
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The bus's varargs typing is `type EmitArgs<M, K> = M[K] extends void ? [name: K] : [name: K, payload: M[K]]`. This is correct for the current map (only `'state'` and `'frame'` use `void`), but `extends void` is a subtle tool: any union that *includes* `void` (e.g. a future event `pendingChange: void | { id: number }`) would match the no-payload branch and the compiler would refuse the payload. More common conventions for 'absent payload' are sentinel object types (`undefined`) or a separate map for void events.

**Location**
`src/client/event-bus.ts:7-9`

**Suggested approach**
Either (a) add a one-line comment explaining the `extends void` choice plus a guardrail comment 'do not introduce `void | T` payloads to the map — use `T | undefined` and emit explicit undefined.' Or (b) switch the convention to `undefined`-payload events (`state: undefined; frame: undefined`) with a slightly different conditional type. (a) is the cheaper docs-only fix; (b) is closer to how a TypeScript-first codebase would idiomatically express it.

**Why**
This is exactly the kind of typing-soundness footgun the migration was supposed to retire (a typo in an event name is now a compile error — this is the next concentric ring of correctness). Documenting it is enough; rewriting the conditional is judgment.

**Smoke step**
None for option (a) — comment-only. For option (b), `npm run typecheck` + `npm test` + spot-check the existing `bus.emit('frame')` call sites still compile.

title: [PR #54] currentStateOf cam-elision still uses approx() while vec3FieldV3 uses strict ===; encoder layer split
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
There are now **two** cam-elision predicates running on each URL write, with different epsilons:
- `currentStateOf` (lines 938-941, 942-944, 945-947) decides whether to populate `view.cam / view.tgt / view.up` based on `!approx(c.x, camDefault[0]) || ...` (epsilon = 1e-3).
- `vec3FieldV3.isPresent` (line 257-262) re-checks the same view with strict `===` and may then declare the field absent in the presence mask.

If `currentStateOf` populates view.cam with a value 5e-4 from default (so `approx` is true → would be omitted) — wait, the `approx` is `||` so any divergence keeps it. The interaction is: `currentStateOf` may populate view.cam even when all components are within EPS of default; vec3FieldV3 then re-checks at strict equality and may still emit a sub-mask + 4 bytes for components that are 5e-4 away from default. This is fine for floating-origin sub-µpc (`!== 0`) but means `currentStateOf`'s population check is partly redundant with the field's isPresent and `worldOffset === non-Sol` triggers force-emit at line 939, which is a third predicate.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:927-947 (currentStateOf); 257-262 (vec3FieldV3.isPresent).

**Suggested approach**
Two layers of cam-elision are not obviously wrong (the outer `approx` cheaply skips the small-noise case before the strict `!==` runs), but the comment block at 904-921 describes the contract verbally and the predicates aren't unified. Consider: collapse to one predicate (let vec3FieldV3.isPresent be the only gate; populate view.cam unconditionally as the raw float and let isPresent decide). Or document the layered design explicitly. Alex's call on which layer should own omission.

**Why**
The PR comment at 226-233 explicitly motivates strict equality for sub-µpc float-origin cam values. `currentStateOf` running `approx` first means a cam at 5e-4 pc from default would be `approx`-equal to default, populated, then strict-emitted — but a cam at 1e-6 pc (the floating-origin case) would also be approx-equal-to-default, populated, then strict-emitted with a sub-mask + 4 bytes. So both paths produce the right wire; the question is whether the redundant approx layer earns its keep or just confuses.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — floating-origin sub-µpc round-trip tests at lines 96-118 still pass.
close_reason: Resolved via documentation in PR #54: added a comment to currentStateOf explaining the two-layer (outer 1e-3 approx + inner strict ===) elision. Inner strict equality is load-bearing for floating-origin sub-µpc cam (a7d.2.11), so layers can't be collapsed. File a new bead if the actual predicate-collapse is wanted later.

title: scale-bar.ts uses whole-frame signature dirty-track; new pass uses per-attribute — divergent strategies in same overlay layer
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`scale-bar.ts` uses a coarse-grained dirty-track strategy: it builds a single per-frame signature string (`sig = 'nav|...'`) at the top of `onFrame` and bails out if it matches `lastSig`. When the signature differs, it redraws everything (every line, every text node) unconditionally. This is more aggressive than the per-attribute pattern the PR adopts: a stationary camera in NAVIGATE-mode redraws zero attributes (signature matches), and a moving camera redraws all attributes (the inner setLine helper has no dirty guards).

The newly-touched overlays (constellation, disc-mask, distance-vector, hud, poi) use per-attribute dirty-tracking instead — they always run the full per-frame computation, then skip individual setAttribute calls when the values match. This is *strictly worse than* scale-bar's approach when the signature happens to match (more JS work) but *better* when one attribute changed and the others didn't (no early-exit blocks the partial reuse).

Either strategy is defensible; having both in the overlay layer is not. After this PR there's no guidance for the next overlay's author on which to choose.

**Location**
src/client/scale-bar.ts:93, :111-113, :167-169, :471-476 (whole-frame signature, no per-attribute guards). vs. the five-overlay per-attribute pattern in this PR.

**Suggested approach**
Two reasonable resolutions:

1. **Codify the choice**: add a one-paragraph note to docs/overlays.md (or docs/performance.md) on when to use signature-vs-per-attribute, with examples. Signature wins for overlays where every attribute is keyed off the same small set of inputs (scale-bar: bar pixel count + label string drive everything). Per-attribute wins for overlays where independent attributes can change at different rates (hud-overlay: ring radius + arrow path + label all independent).

2. **Migrate scale-bar to per-attribute**: ~30 setLine + 6 setAttribute callsites; mechanical. Removes the divergence at the cost of some redraw cost on the moving-camera path (which is already cheap relative to scale-bar's geometry math).

(1) preserves scale-bar's signature optimisation and is cheaper; (2) is the consistency answer.

**Why**
Engineering manager review priority: "Architectural fit" — having two ways to dirty-track in the same overlay layer makes the next overlay author guess. A documented choice closes that. (Per-attribute is what the PR scaled to five files; signature might still be the better fit for scale-bar; documenting which-when avoids re-debating it next overlay.)

**Smoke step**
Either an updated docs/overlays.md section that the next reviewer can point at, or a scale-bar diff that shows zero behavioural change for the no-camera-movement case.

title: [PR #48] buildMaterials' four ShaderMaterial constructors duplicate boilerplate
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`PlanetBodyField.buildMaterials` (lines 437-500) now constructs four `THREE.ShaderMaterial` instances. They all share `glslVersion: THREE.GLSL3`, `vertexShader: planetVert`, `fragmentShader: planetFrag`, and `uniforms: { ...sharedPlanetUniforms, uRenderMode: { value: N } }`. Three of them set `transparent: true`. The differences are: blending (default vs `AdditiveBlending` vs `CustomBlending` via `applyDiscBlendDefaults`), depthWrite, depthTest, colorWrite — i.e. the per-pass behaviour. The shared scaffolding repeats four times. Adding a fifth pass (e.g. for chart-mode flat planets, or for planet labels' depth-occlusion) would multiply the boilerplate further.

**Location**
src/client/planet-body-field.ts:437-500

**Suggested approach**
Extract a small factory:

```ts
function makePlanetMat(
  mode: number,
  shared: object,
  per: Partial<THREE.ShaderMaterialParameters>,
): THREE.ShaderMaterial {
  return new THREE.ShaderMaterial({
    glslVersion: THREE.GLSL3,
    vertexShader: planetVert,
    fragmentShader: planetFrag,
    transparent: true,
    uniforms: { ...shared, uRenderMode: { value: mode } },
    ...per,
  });
}
```

Each pass becomes one line of intent: `makePlanetMat(3, sharedPlanetUniforms, { depthWrite: true, colorWrite: false })`. The disc-pass `applyDiscBlendDefaults` call stays separate (it's a multi-field mutation that would lose semantic clarity if folded in).

**Why**
DRY for a four-pass shape with shared scaffolding; bk5 / chart-mode might add a fifth, and the factory keeps the per-pass behaviour visible at a glance.

**Smoke step**
`npm test src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts` (renderOrder test still passes); `npm run typecheck`.

title: EventBus tests don't cover mid-emit registration ordering
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`event-bus.test.ts` has six cases: delivery, no-payload, unsubscribe, dedupe, no-subscribers, mid-emit unsubscribe. The seventh corner case — a handler registering a *new* handler mid-emit — is not pinned. JS `Set` iteration semantics include items added after iteration starts, so the new handler will fire in the same emit round (in insertion order, after all currently-iterating items). This is the implementation-defined behaviour; nothing in the bus code or test suite documents which way it goes, so a future refactor (e.g. snapshotting the Set into an array before iterating, which we'd want anyway if emit became re-entrant under flag) would silently flip the contract.

**Location**
`src/client/event-bus.test.ts` (test suite — gap)

**Suggested approach**
Add a single `it('runs handlers added mid-emit in the same round', ...)` (or `'defers handlers added mid-emit until the next emit'` if Alex wants the opposite contract). Pinning either direction makes the contract intentional. Cheap test, cheap insurance.

**Why**
The mid-emit unsubscribe test already exists, so the suite is half-aware of mutation-during-iteration. Adding the registration sibling completes the corner-case coverage and prevents a refactor from breaking re-entrant emit users. Same intent as PR #51's other test additions: pin the spec.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` passes the new case.

title: [PR #56] aOff and bOff are identical in writeHostStaticAttributes — collapse
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`writeHostStaticAttributes` declares two named offsets `aOff` and `bOff` whose initialisers are byte-for-byte identical (`baseVec4 + i * 4`). The naming suggests they index into different layouts within the same instance slot, but they don't — `bufPhaseA` and `bufPhaseB` are separate Float32Arrays that both happen to be vec4-shaped, so the per-slot offset is the same. Reader has to compute the equality by hand to be sure no off-by-one is hiding.

**Location**
`src/client/planet-body-field.ts` lines 522–531 (the write-block inside `writeHostStaticAttributes`).

**Suggested approach**
Collapse the two locals into a single `const off = baseVec4 + i * 4` (or just `const off = (baseScalar + i) * 4` for parity with the other offsets) and use it for both `bufPhaseA` and `bufPhaseB` writes. Drops the cosmetic distinction that hints at a layout that doesn't exist. Optionally, the `pc ? pc.cN : 0` repetition (8 lines) could collapse via a tiny helper or a default-zero `PhaseCoefficients` constant in `phase-function.ts` — but the offset collapse is the higher-signal win.

**Why**
DRY/elegance — repeated identical computations with different names are a low-grade reader trap. They're the kind of thing a future edit accidentally desyncs (someone adjusts `aOff` for a re-pack and forgets `bOff`). One name forecloses that possibility.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — the existing 14 lifecycle tests in `planet-body-field.test.ts` exercise attach / detach / grow / recenter / setMaxAppMag and indirectly exercise this write path; pass means the collapse didn't change behaviour.

title: [PR #54] applyDecodedView uses defaultCamForMode in worldOffset branch but spreads OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL elsewhere — pick one
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
Inside `applyDecodedView` (lines 1069-1095) two adjacent branches express the same idea differently:
- Line 1071: `const camDefault = defaultCamForMode(view.mode); stellata.camera.position.set(camDefault[0], camDefault[1], camDefault[2]);`
- Line 1093: `stellata.camera.position.set(...OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL);` — only fires when `willEnterObserve`, so functionally identical to `defaultCamForMode('observe')` but written via a constant spread.

Both expressions ultimately resolve to the same vector when mode=observe. The mixed style is small but inconsistent: one site looks the value up via the helper, the other hard-codes the const. A future change to OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL would touch one site; a change to defaultCamForMode would touch the other; a maintainer reading either branch can't tell at a glance whether the two are coupled.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:1069-1095 (applyDecodedView, worldOffset + willEnterObserve branches).

**Suggested approach**
Use `defaultCamForMode` consistently for both call sites, or extract a tiny helper `setCameraToDefault(stellata, mode)` and invoke it from both. The cam-default invariant is now defined in one place (`defaultCamForMode`) and there is no value in the OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL spread except as a way to write `[0,0,0]` symbolically — but `defaultCamForMode('observe')` already returns it.

**Why**
Closed bead 9mm.20/9mm.24's whole point was to give the cam-omission invariant a single name; the leftover constant spread at line 1093 is the last place that doesn't follow the rule.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — the mode-aware cam-fix-up tests at url-state.test.ts:155-178 still pass.

title: [PR #48] Star and planet pipelines now diverge — star has 3 passes, planet has 4
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
Before PR #48 the planet pipeline mirrored the star pipeline 1:1: core depth-mask + disc + glow, same uRenderMode 2/1/0, same gates, same gl_FragDepth = 1.0 halo trick. The PR header comment in `planet-body-field.ts` previously said "three materials share the geometry (same pattern as the star pipeline)". After the PR, planets have an outer-disc occluder (uRenderMode == 3) that stars do not — divergence with no equivalent in `star.frag.glsl`. There is no doc capturing whether the divergence is deliberate (planets need to occlude orbit rings; stars don't have orbit rings to occlude) or whether the same fix should be ported to stars (e.g. stars vs galactic-disc gridlines, stars vs distance-vector chevrons).

**Location**
src/client/shaders/planet.frag.glsl:62-75 (new uRenderMode == 3 branch); src/client/planet-body-field.ts:25-34 (header comment); compare with src/client/shaders/star.frag.glsl:116-127.

**Suggested approach**
Decide whether the divergence is permanent or transitional. If permanent: add a sentence to the planet-body-field.ts header comment explicitly calling out "unlike stars, planets carry a fourth pass because orbit rings need depth-occlusion across the full disc, not just the bright core." If transitional: file a follow-up to evaluate whether the star pipeline benefits from the same trick (do any layers depth-test against a star's halo annulus and want to be occluded? — galactic grid lines passing through Sirius, distance-vector chevrons through a foreground star, etc.).

**Why**
The 3re epic explicitly chose the star pattern for planets ("three-pass parity with stars" in docs/solar-system.md). Breaking that parity without explanation degrades the cross-pipeline mental model that makes future shader edits cheap.

**Smoke step**
Either a comment-only update (`grep -A 5 'Three materials' src/client/planet-body-field.ts` confirms updated wording) or — if porting the trick — manual A/B against galactic gridlines passing through Sirius's halo.

title: StellataEventMap doesn't document fine-grained vs catch-all 'state' relationship
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The `StellataEventMap` declaration (stellata.ts:294) has a one-line comment 'subscribers register via `Stellata.on(name, fn)` and the compiler enforces the payload type per event. `state` and `frame` are no-payload events.' What's *not* captured: every fine-grained mutation (`focus`, `vector`, `vectorCloud`, `cloudFocus`, `pois`, `filter`, `cameraMode`) emits both its specific event AND `state`, while `warp` and `planetSystem` emit only their specific event (warp emits state via `fireStateChange` after, planetSystem does not). `docs/architecture.md` documents this for `state` ('fires on any discrete state mutation') but the rule is split across the two locations and the asymmetry isn't explicit anywhere.

**Location**
- `src/client/stellata.ts:291-306` — StellataEventMap declaration
- `docs/architecture.md` §'Event bus on Stellata'

**Suggested approach**
Either: (a) extend the StellataEventMap doc-comment with a one-paragraph 'who emits what alongside state' table and the rationale (state is the URL-sync trigger, frame is the render-tick fanout, fine-grained events are for UI subscribers that want only their slice). Or: (b) introduce a typed wrapper at the Stellata level (e.g. `fireStateChange(emit?: keyof Map)`) that makes the pairing explicit at the call sites. (a) is cheaper; (b) is more invasive but ironclad.

**Why**
The 'every subscriber wired across the codebase' integration surface is the thing the PR tests don't cover (per Alex's large-PR-honesty framing). The map is now the canonical spec for that surface — anything implicit about its semantics should be moved into the type or the comment so future-Claude doesn't have to grep `fireStateChange` to discover the contract.

**Smoke step**
Doc-only change: re-read `docs/architecture.md` after the patch and confirm the relationship between fine-grained events and `'state'` is unambiguous from the doc alone.

title: disc-mask scope narrower than stellata-rmo's repro: companions of constellation vertices still leak when their disc renders
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
stellata-rmo's repro reads: 'Focus a constellation member star where a stick-figure line passes behind it … unfocus the star … same line segments now render on top of the disc.' PR #52 fixes that exact case for the *vertex* itself (every vertex of the highlighted constellation gets a cutout). It does NOT fix the analogous case where a *binary companion of* a vertex is what the camera is parked near: e.g. focus Mizar (vertex), unfocus, camera still close → Mizar gets masked (it's a vertex), but if the user instead clicks Alcor (Mizar's companion, may or may not itself be a vertex depending on Stellarium's asterism for Ursa Major) and unfocuses, only Alcor's relationship to its own `companion` field would mask it — and `companion` is directed (per scripts/catalog-pure.ts:420 'A's nearest may not be B's nearest'). Anything close enough to render a disc but not a vertex and not focused will still paint lines on top.

**Location**
src/client/disc-mask.ts:104-114 (constellation iteration). Compare scripts/catalog-pure.ts:420-440 (directedness of `companion`). The PR's bug-target stellata-rmo describes the broader class.

**Suggested approach**
Two options. (1) Document scope: add a sentence to the file's top comment naming what's *not* covered ('A close star that is neither focal, focal's companion, nor a vertex of the highlighted constellation will not be masked. In practice such a star is rare — the user has to deliberately park near a non-vertex, non-companion star.'). (2) Extend coverage: scan a small window of the catalog by squared distance to the camera each frame (top-K nearest, K=8 or so) and run them through tryPlace. K nearest neighbours of the camera is the architecturally correct generalisation; bounded scan keeps it cheap. Option (1) is a one-line doc change; option (2) is more code but closes the rmo class of bugs entirely.

**Why**
The PR title and body claim to fix stellata-rmo, but the fix is keyed to constellation-highlighted iteration. Scope-narrowing fixes are common and fine — but they should be documented so a future reader doesn't read the title and assume the broader bug is closed. Filing low-confidence because Alex may have deliberately scoped to vertices given the lines-not-rendering-without-highlight observation (no constellation highlight ⇒ no lines ⇒ nothing to paint over a disc, so the scope IS the bug class in practice).

**Smoke step**
For (1): doc-only. For (2): focus Mizar, unfocus, manually drag camera near a known non-vertex non-companion close star (e.g. one of the ~313k catalog members not in a Stellarium asterism), confirm its disc gets masked.

title: pickStar two-tier prime-vs-fallback priority untested at integration level
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`pickScore` is unit-covered with three regression tests (Double Double, Alula coincident pair, sub-pixel mag bias). What's NOT tested is the surrounding two-tier control flow in `pickStar`: the contract that 'prime hits always beat fallback hits' (line 2804: `return discIdx !== -1 ? discIdx : fbIdx`), and that the prime tier uses `hitRadius = max(pxSize/2, MIN_DISC_HIT_RADIUS_PX)` while the fallback uses `pixelThreshold`. PR description acknowledges this gap explicitly: 'The user-visible effect (clicking the right star on the canvas) needs manual smoke since pickStar lives in the Three.js scene path.' The integration is exactly where the original au3/xec bugs lived — a unit-tested pure helper plus an unverified integration is the same shape as 'tested the lemma, deployed the theorem'.

**Location**
src/client/stellata.ts:2720-2805 (`pickStar`)
src/client/star-geometry.test.ts:163-194 (existing pickScore tests, scope-bounded to the helper)

**Suggested approach**
Extract the inner-loop pick reducer to a pure function in `star-geometry.ts`: input is an iterable of `{idx, pxDist, hitRadius, appMag, inFilter}` plus the fallback `pixelThreshold`; output is the winning idx or -1. Then `pickStar` becomes 'project + filter + collect candidates' followed by 'pickFromCandidates(candidates, pixelThreshold)'. The reducer is then unit-testable with synthetic candidate arrays covering: prime-only, fallback-only, mixed prime-and-fallback (prime wins), tied scores in prime tier (same as Alula), prime hit just inside hitRadius vs fallback hit just under pixelThreshold (prime wins regardless of score). No Three.js scene needed.

**Why**
This is the same DRY pattern that PR #53 already applied to `pickScore` (lifting the score formula out of the inline body). The inner reducer is one level up: the same lift makes the priority semantics testable.

**Smoke step**
Add a test `describe('pickFromCandidates')` with 5+ scenarios. All must pass without rendering a Three.js scene. Manual smoke per the PR description still required for the projection layer (camera projection + viewport math), but the priority logic itself is pinned.

title: [PR #54] No round-trip test for the bit-21 t field (Float64); decoders handle it but encoder gate is untested
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
Bit 21 (`t`, scrubber-pinned wall-clock) appears in `FIELDS_V2` (lines 593-598) and `FIELDS_V3` (lines 672-677) as `fixed(8)` Float64 LE. `currentStateOf` gates emission on `!isLive(tNow)` (line 953). The PR test plan and code comments both note this is wired but never emitted in v1 — emission flips on with stellata-nmu.

There is no test that `encodeBlob({ t: 1234567890.123 })` round-trips through `decodeBlob` and produces the same value. The bit-21 path is genuinely dead code today (encoder never emits) but the decoder will run on stellata-nmu's first emission, and a regression in the float64 LE write/read pair would manifest only at that point.

A v3 blob built by hand with bit 21 set (analogous to the v1/v2 backward-compat tests) would also catch a future change that resizes the field.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:672-677 (FIELDS_V3 t spec); src/client/url-state.test.ts (no test).

**Suggested approach**
Add a test under the v3 round-trip block: `encodeBlob({ t: 1234567890.123 })` → decode → assert `view.t === 1234567890.123` exactly (Float64 round-trips losslessly). Also assert that `encodeBlob({ t: undefined })` does not occupy bit 21 (presence guard).

**Why**
The bit-21 t-field's purpose is in cross-PR territory — it ships in this PR's wire format but is activated by a different epic (stellata-nmu). Test coverage now is cheap insurance against a regression that would otherwise surface as a wrong wall-clock during share-link load.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` runs the new t-field round-trip test.

title: [PR #48] orbit-rings-layer.ts renderOrder ladder comment omits heliopause, dust, clouds
Heliopause geometry block duplicated verbatim in SCIENCE.md and docs/solar-system.md
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The heliopause-construction recipe (122 / 115 / 200 AU + apex direction + "unit sphere → scale (115,115,161) → offset 39 AU toward antiapex → rotate so +Z lands on antiapex" → result "+122 / -200 along apex") appears nearly verbatim in two places authored by this PR:

- SCIENCE.md "Heliopause boundary" section (under "## Solar system")
- docs/solar-system.md "Heliopause boundary" section

Both are added in PR #38, ~10 lines of identical numerical / construction prose. The two-doc convention (CLAUDE.md says SCIENCE.md = data sources + physics, docs/<topic>.md = code-level constraint surface) is sound, but the construction recipe — which is implementation detail (sphere scale, offset, rotation) — sits more naturally in docs/solar-system.md while SCIENCE.md should hold only the citation-bearing measurements (122/115/200 AU + apex direction).

**Location**
- SCIENCE.md (PR #38 add) "Heliopause boundary" subsection
- docs/solar-system.md "Heliopause boundary" section

**Suggested approach**
Trim SCIENCE.md to the four cited measurements + a one-line "Construction details: see `docs/solar-system.md` § Heliopause boundary." docs/solar-system.md keeps the full construction recipe. Single source of truth for the construction details means the next physical-asymmetry tweak edits one file, not two.

**Why**
First edit will drift the two copies — a numerical change at one site that doesn't propagate to the other turns the "ground truth" SCIENCE.md into a stale reference. Same DRY argument as the cross-doc citations elsewhere in the project.

**Smoke step**
Diff the two paragraphs verbatim; confirm SCIENCE.md after the trim still cites all four sources (Voyager 1, Voyager 2, IBEX, Frisch & Slavin 2013); confirm docs/solar-system.md still carries the construction recipe.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: projectWithNearClip module-scope scratches rely on un-enforced single-caller-per-frame contract
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input** (judgment call on encapsulation vs. perf)

**Finding**
`projectWithNearClip` in `distance-vector-overlay.ts` was changed from `.clone()` per call (three Vector3 allocations) to module-scope scratches `projVa` / `projVb` / `projEnd`. The PR description justifies this as "called at most once per frame from this module's onFrame handler — safe." The function is, however, `export`ed.

The contract is correct today: the only caller is the local `onFrame` handler, and the returned arrays `pA`, `pB` are fresh `[number, number]` so post-return reads are safe even if the scratch is later clobbered. But the contract is unenforced — a future caller (a sibling overlay, a test that drives both `projectWithNearClip` and another module-scoped projection consumer in the same tick, or a refactor that pulls the function into a shared util) would silently return stale data. The two callsites in the test file already exist and would be fine; the risk is purely future-callers.

**Location**
src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:25-27 (scratch declarations), :240-294 (the function body).

**Suggested approach**
Two reasonable resolutions, your call:

1. **Document the contract louder**: rename the scratches to `onFrameProjVa` etc. (or move them inside the closure scope if export isn't actually needed downstream beyond tests), and add a top-of-function assert in dev builds: `if (projectingFlag) throw; projectingFlag = true; ... finally { projectingFlag = false; }`.

2. **Pass scratches in**: add an optional `scratch?: { va, vb, end }` parameter that defaults to a fresh allocation when omitted, and have the `onFrame` handler create one stable scratch object for itself. Equivalent perf, no module-level state, callable from any context.

Option (2) is the principled fix; option (1) is the minimum-disturbance fix.

**Why**
Module-scope scratches in an exported function are a footgun whenever another module is one refactor away from depending on it. The current `@__PURE__` annotations also imply the values are immutable per call — they aren't. Today's behaviour is correct; the bead is about the next person who reuses the helper.

**Smoke step**
Re-run the existing `distance-vector-overlay.test.ts` suite (18 tests) — no behaviour change. After option (2), confirm two-call test that drives the function back-to-back with different inputs returns the expected results for both calls.

title: docs/chart-mode.md 'Picking under chart mode' missing pickScore third bullet
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`docs/chart-mode.md` § 'Picking under chart mode' enumerates the two pre-existing pickStar fixes (variable bright-extreme filter, MIN_DISC_HIT_RADIUS_PX floor) but doesn't mention the screen-pixel proximity tiebreak the PR introduces. Chart mode is the regime where overlapping small discs are most common (small ~1–2 px discs across a crowded sky), so it's the most user-visible context for the new pickScore behaviour and the natural place to document it. The doc is the canonical reference for 'how does the picker behave in chart mode?', and a future reader chasing a 'wrong star picked' report under chart mode lands here first.

**Location**
docs/chart-mode.md:186-200 (the 'Picking under chart mode' section)

**Suggested approach**
Add a third bullet describing the screen-pixel proximity tiebreak: 'pickScore tiebreak. Within the prime tier (cursor inside a rendered disc), the cursor's pixel distance to each candidate's projected centre wins (pickScore = pxDist + appMag·0.05). Visually-resolved pairs whose hitboxes overlap stay independently clickable — the Double Double (ε¹/ε² Lyr) is the canonical case. Sub-pixel mag bias tiebreaks coincident catalog companions sharing x/y/z, e.g. Alula Australis A/B (Gl 423A/B at identical coordinates).' Cross-link from `star-geometry.ts`'s pickScore comment.

**Why**
The existing two bullets already follow this format. Adding the third keeps the chart-mode picking section the single source of truth for picker behaviour quirks. Without this, the only place to learn about the new behaviour is the inline comment in pickStar.

**Smoke step**
After the edit, reading docs/chart-mode.md § Picking should fully describe the picker contract for chart mode — variable filter, hit-radius floor, and pickScore tiebreak.

title: [PR #48] Four-pass renderOrder test sorts before assert — wouldn't catch a disc↔outer swap
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The new test in `planet-body-field.test.ts` (lines 187-200) asserts:

```ts
const orders = f.group.children
  .map((m) => m.renderOrder)
  .sort((a, b) => a - b);
expect(orders).toEqual([-4, 1.5, 3, 4]);
```

Because it sorts before comparing, the test is satisfied as long as the *set* of renderOrder values is `{-4, 1.5, 3, 4}` regardless of which mesh holds which value. A future refactor that swaps disc↔outer (e.g. moving outer to renderOrder 3 and disc to 1.5) would still pass. The PR's own description gives the contract: outer-disc must be 1.5 (between star glow at 1 and orbit rings at 2), disc at 3, glow at 4, core at -4. The test should pin those bindings, not just the value set.

**Location**
src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts:187-200

**Suggested approach**
Either expose typed accessors (`f.meshOuter.renderOrder` etc.) and assert each individually, or reorganise the assertion to map mesh-name → renderOrder via the mesh's `name` property (set names in `buildMaterials`). A single-line change adding `mesh.name = 'outer' | 'disc' | 'glow' | 'core'` would let the test do `expect(f.group.children.find(m => m.name === 'outer')!.renderOrder).toBe(1.5)` without exposing private fields.

**Why**
PR description says "a future reorder fails CI rather than silently regressing the bug," but the sort defeats that. The test currently only catches deletion or addition of a renderOrder value — not the bug it claims to prevent.

**Smoke step**
`npm test src/client/planet-body-field.test.ts` still passes after the assertion is tightened; mutate one renderOrder in source and confirm the test fails.

title: disc-mask.ts: cutout pool grows unbounded; comment says 'we don't bother with a hard cap'
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`ensureCircles(n)` allocates new `<circle>` SVG nodes whenever `n` exceeds the pool size, and the pool is never shrunk. The new comment is explicit: 'Pool of <circle>s, grown on demand and never shrunk … we don't bother with a hard cap.' Today the upper bound is the largest constellation's vertex count + 2, which is small (Stellarium's longest-asterism constellations are around 30–40 vertices). But the bound is structural-data-dependent, not enforced. A future change that adopts a denser asterism source, or a bug that accidentally feeds the wrong list (e.g. all-constellations chart-mode iteration leaks into the disc-mask path), would cause the pool to grow to thousands of nodes silently.

**Location**
src/client/disc-mask.ts:30-43 (`ensureCircles` and the surrounding comment)

**Suggested approach**
Add a soft cap (e.g. `MAX_MASK_CIRCLES = 64`) and either silently truncate beyond it or assert in dev. The truncate option mirrors the original code's bounded-pool philosophy without re-introducing a fixed-4 limit; the assert option turns a future regression into a loud failure on next run. Either is one extra branch in `ensureCircles`. Keep the comment explaining that today's expected ceiling is ~40.

**Why**
Defensive cap costs nothing at runtime and bounds the worst case if data shape ever shifts. The current comment ('we don't bother') reads as deliberate, but isn't actually a tradeoff — there's no perf or complexity cost to a soft cap. Filing low-confidence because this may simply be a stylistic preference.

**Smoke step**
Manual PR smoke unchanged; `npm run typecheck` clean. Optional: temporarily set the cap to 4 to confirm truncation is silent (no console errors) and that visual regression is contained to over-cap stars not getting cutouts.

title: Welcome vs About modal use different headline framings ("physics-based" vs "observational")
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The Welcome modal opens with:
> Stellata is a **physics-based, empirical 3D model** of our galaxy that simulates what it would be like to actually *be there*.

The About modal opens with:
> Stellata is an **observational 3D model** of our local part of the galaxy at every scale we've measured it.

The README opens with "An observational 3D model…" and the HTML title says "Observational 3D Model of our Galaxy". Three of four intro surfaces lead with "observational"; only the welcome modal leads with "physics-based, empirical". The two phrases are not exact synonyms — "physics-based" implies *modelling*, "observational" implies *catalogue ingestion* — and the rest of the PR is explicit that Stellata is the latter ("Theoretical predictions and conjectured structures are excluded").

**Location**
- src/client/index.html line 120 (welcome modal)
- src/client/index.html line 150 (about modal)
- README.md line 3

**Suggested approach**
Rewrite the Welcome modal opening to lead with the same "observational" framing the About modal, README, and HTML title use. Drop "physics-based" — the empirical-model claim is the differentiator, not the physics machinery underneath it. A consistent tagline across the four surfaces also reads as more deliberate brand voice.

**Why**
The PR's stated goal is to align every intro surface around "the empirical-model principle". Welcome modal is the only outlier and it's the *first* surface a new visitor sees, so the inconsistency is the most visible one.

**Smoke step**
Read all four surfaces (welcome modal, about modal, README first sentence, browser tab title) and confirm the noun phrase Stellata uses to describe itself is the same across all four.

title: phase-3 recenter recomputes the worldOffset delta that recenterOrigin already has
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The phase-3 observe-warp recenter in `updateWarp` reads `catalog.positions` for the destination star and subtracts `worldOffset` to compute `(dx, dy, dz)` so `state.pEnd` can be shifted into the new frame. `recenterOrigin` (called transitively via `recenterFocusToStar`) computes the same three deltas internally on its first three lines (`newOrigin.x - this.worldOffset.x` etc.) before it shifts camera/target/instances. The PR open-codes a duplicate of that arithmetic immediately above the `recenterFocusToStar` call, then throws the deltas away after using them on `pEnd`.

**Location**
src/client/stellata.ts:3666-3681

**Suggested approach**
Either (a) snapshot `worldOffset` before the call and compute the delta after `recenterFocusToStar` returns by subtracting old from new, or (b) have `recenterOrigin` (or a `recenterOriginDelta` variant) return the `Vector3` delta it computed so the caller can apply it to other state in the same frame without re-deriving. Option (b) is the cleaner contract — the function's whole job is to shift things by a delta — and would also serve any future caller that needs to migrate auxiliary state across a recentre. One commit; non-breaking since today's only callers ignore the return value.

**Why**
The two derivations have to stay numerically identical for the lerp `pEnd → B` to land cleanly on B at `f=1`. Today they're computed twice in the same precision so they happen to match, but a future change to `recenterOrigin`'s rounding (e.g. a snap-to-float32 pass) would silently desync `pEnd` from the post-shift frame. Single-source-of-truth makes that class of bug impossible.

**Smoke step**
1. Observe→observe warp Sol → Betelgeuse: post-arrival slerp ends with the destination disc rock-solid at NDC centre.
2. `debug.pin()` HUD: phase-3 last frame shows |camera.position| ≪ source-frame B.
3. `npm test` clean.

title: docs/ux-tweaks.md MIN_DISC_HIT_RADIUS_PX warning describes removed mechanism
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`docs/ux-tweaks.md` line 88 documents the trade-off for raising `MIN_DISC_HIT_RADIUS_PX` as: 'Raise for easier hover at the cost of foreground stars stealing picks from neighbours.' That mechanism — closest-to-camera tiebreak between overlapping prime-tier hits — no longer exists after PR #53; the prime tier now scores by pixel distance from cursor to disc centre. The new failure mode for raising `MIN_DISC_HIT_RADIUS_PX` is different: a neighbour star whose centre projects nearer the cursor than the visually-targeted star can win, because hit radii now overlap more often. The doc warning misleads anyone tuning the constant.

**Location**
docs/ux-tweaks.md:85-88

**Suggested approach**
Rewrite the trade-off bullet to reference the new pickScore tiebreak. Suggested text: 'Raise for easier hover at the cost of more cases where a neighbour disc whose centre is marginally closer to the cursor wins over the visually-targeted star (pickScore tiebreak).'

**Why**
Doc drift compounds: the comment is the only place a future reader tuning chart-mode hover sees the trade-off explained; the inline pickStar comment doesn't surface here. Update is cheap and prevents a wrong mental model.

**Smoke step**
After the edit, the doc bullet should be self-consistent with the pickScore comment in star-geometry.ts. Reading both should yield the same picker-tiebreak description.

title: [PR #48] heliopause.ts comment doesn't mention new outer-disc layer between rings and shell
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`heliopause.ts` lines 94-95 read: "Behind planet bodies (renderOrder=3) and above stars (0) — sits alongside orbit rings (2) at renderOrder=1, since both are dim chrome for the same layer." PR #48 introduces a new planet outer-disc occluder at renderOrder=1.5, sitting between the heliopause shell (1) and the orbit rings (2) — the heliopause is no longer "alongside orbit rings" without an interposed depth-only planet pass. The comment doesn't reflect that.

**Location**
src/client/heliopause.ts:94-97

**Suggested approach**
One-line comment update inside the same constructor preamble. Either acknowledge the 1.5 outer-disc occluder explicitly ("Behind planet bodies (3, 4) and the planet outer-disc occluder (1.5)") or rewrite the comment to enumerate the post-PR ladder.

**Why**
The whole point of PR #48 documenting renderOrder ladders inline is that a future reorder fails. Leaving the heliopause comment stale undermines that — the next contributor reading the heliopause file will believe rings are the next neighbour above.

**Smoke step**
`grep -n renderOrder src/client/heliopause.ts` shows the updated comment matches the actual ladder; `npm run typecheck` (no behavioural change).

title: disc-mask.ts: observe-mode 'no other disc reaches threshold' assumption hardened without verification
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The pre-PR observe-mode early-return triggered when the focal star was hidden (`focus === null || observe`); the rationale was 'we only mask the focal pair, and they're hidden in observe.' The PR widens cutout sources to every constellation vertex but keeps the same blanket observe-mode skip, justifying it with: 'Other stars are always far away from a camera parked at a focal star, so they don't reach the disc threshold either.' That assumption isn't verified in code or doc — it's an empirical claim about the camera-position invariant during observe mode.

**Location**
src/client/disc-mask.ts:75-82 (the new observe early-return + comment)
docs/overlays.md:84-89 (corresponding doc update making the same claim)

**Suggested approach**
Either (a) add a smoke note to docs/overlays.md naming the invariant explicitly: 'OBSERVE camera sits at the focal star's local position, so any other catalog star is at least `min(catalog gap)` away — well beyond DISC_THRESHOLD_PX at any reasonable FOV.' Cite where in stellata.ts that camera-position invariant is enforced. Or (b) drop the early-return for the constellation-vertex pass only, keeping the focal-pair skip — i.e. let the iteration run with `focus = null` and trust the per-circle threshold check to no-op cheaply when no disc is actually rendering. Option (b) is more defensive at the cost of a few dozen `renderedSizePx` calls per frame in observe.

**Why**
Hardening an undocumented assumption to cover more cases is the kind of code that silently regresses when an unrelated change (camera-position offset in observe, FOV widen, or a new "cinematic" observe submode) violates the invariant. The original branch was load-bearing only for the focal pair; the new branch is load-bearing for every constellation vertex.

**Smoke step**
Either doc-only (a) or repro: highlight Orion, focus Betelgeuse, enter OBSERVE, then nudge the OBSERVE camera (drag controller) so it points toward another bright Orion vertex (Bellatrix / Saiph) and verify no disc crosses 48 px (or, if it does, that the lines paint through it intentionally). If (b) is chosen: run the full PR test plan + verify there's no observable cost in the perf-hud disc-mask tick row in observe mode.

title: recenterFocusToStar duplicates the focus-recenter core inside setFocus
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #50 factors a new private `recenterFocusToStar(newIdx)` out of `swapObserveAnchor` so phase-3 of an observe→observe warp can recentre the floating origin onto the destination star ahead of `finishWarp`. The new helper does the focus-recenter core (recenterOrigin to star, focusedStar = idx, controls.minDistance = minOrbitDistForStar(idx), onFocusHandlers fan-out, refreshPlanetSystem). The same five lines also live inline in `setFocus` (the `if (idx !== null)` recentre branch + `focusedStar = idx` above + the minDistance / onFocusHandlers / refreshPlanetSystem fan-out below). The PR adds the helper but doesn't route `setFocus` through it, so the duplication persists.

**Location**
src/client/stellata.ts:1410-1460 (setFocus) and src/client/stellata.ts:2466-2475 (recenterFocusToStar)

**Suggested approach**
Refactor `setFocus` so its focus-on-star branch delegates to `recenterFocusToStar`. `setFocus`'s extra responsibilities — cloud-clear, observe-cleanup, post-recenter target/camera snap, the unfocus minDistance clamp, and the trailing fireStateChange — stay inline; the shared core moves into the helper. One commit. Keep both call sites' behaviour identical (the target snap is unique to setFocus and stays under setFocus's roof; recenterFocusToStar's caller in updateWarp doesn't need it because phase-3 doesn't re-anchor controls.target the same way).

**Why**
DRY. The PR comment on `recenterFocusToStar` already calls out that navigate's setFocus(destIdx) at finishWarp does the equivalent — codifying that equivalence in code, not just prose, prevents drift if either site grows a new responsibility.

**Smoke step**
1. Focus Sol from no-focus, focus a far star (e.g. Betelgeuse), unfocus — focus state, focus-ring overlay, panel and URL update unchanged.
2. Observe→observe warp arrives without jitter.
3. `npm test` and `npm run typecheck` clean.

title: [PR #54] vec3FieldV3.encodeBytes is called twice per field per encodeBlob — recomputes sub-mask each time
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`encodeBlob` walks `FIELDS_V3` twice per call: once at line 717-719 to size the buffer (`total += f.encodeBytes(view)`) and again at line 725-729 to step `off` after writing (`off += f.encodeBytes(view)`). For `vec3FieldV3.encodeBytes` (lines 240-248) this means three component comparisons against `componentDefaults(v)` are executed twice for every present vec3, and `componentDefaults` itself runs `defaultCamForMode(v.mode)` twice for cam. The path also runs after the encode body has already done the same comparison at line 263-269.

Three identical comparisons per cam encode (size pass, write pass, sub-mask construction) × per-write debounced URL = noticeable on warp animations or rapid-camera-move, even if not catastrophic.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:713-732 (encodeBlob); 240-275 (vec3FieldV3.encodeBytes + encode).

**Suggested approach**
Have `encode` return the byte count it wrote, so the second encodeBytes call disappears: `off += f.encode(view, dv, off)`. The buffer-sizing pass either accepts the extra computation (it is unavoidable to size before allocating) or computes presence + bytes together and stashes per-field byte counts in a small array reused by the write pass. Either way the cam comparison runs at most twice (sizing + writing), not three times, and the `f.key === 'cam'` lookup happens on a fast path.

**Why**
Minor perf, but the comment on `startUrlSync` (lines 1196-1198) explicitly motivates a no-allocation steady-state path. Halving the work inside encodeBlob is consistent with that goal.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` green; no observable behavioural change. Optional: a microbenchmark counting comparator calls.

title: Outer-disc near-z write defeats disc-pass halo gl_FragDepth = 1.0 trick
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The disc pass's halo branch in `planet.frag.glsl` (line 78) writes `gl_FragDepth = 1.0` so halo fragments don't occlude later passes — same trick the star pipeline uses. PR #48 inserts a depth-only outer-mask pass at renderOrder 1.5 that writes the planet's actual near-z across the *full* visible disc, including the halo annulus. With the default `LESS` depth test, the disc pass halo fragment then has `gl_FragDepth = 1.0 < near-z = false` and gets discarded — no MaxEquation blend with the already-drawn MW/clouds/stars. The PR description and the new `docs/solar-system.md` paragraph both claim "the perceptual halo→soft background falloff still works through the disc pass's MaxEquation blending," but the depth math contradicts that claim. The unchecked manual smoke item ("a bright distant star or MW patch behind a focused planet's halo: the soft halo→background falloff should look the same as before") is the only validation Alex flagged.

**Location**
src/client/shaders/planet.frag.glsl:78 vs new uRenderMode == 3 branch at lines 62-75; docs/solar-system.md:106-117

**Suggested approach**
Eyeball the smoke step against a Mercury or Venus disc framed against a bright MW patch — compare a build with the outer mask vs without, with disc-pass halo rendered both ways. If the halo annulus is sub-pixel for v1's planets and the regression is invisible, document the deliberate halo loss in the doc paragraph instead of claiming preservation. If visible, alternatives: keep the outer mask gated on `glow >= uCoreThreshold` (only writes near-z where the disc pass would too — same as core mask but at the disc-extent radius), or apply `depthFunc: LessEqualDepth` to the rings so a 1.5/3 tie passes, or use a stencil approach.

**Why**
Star pipeline halo→soft-MW interplay was specifically called out as a load-bearing visual in `docs/rendering.md` (line 48-50: "Halo fragments push gl_FragDepth = 1.0 so they paint dim haze without occluding the later glow pass"). Adopting that pattern for planets in 3re.16 then breaking it in 3re.19 is an architectural-fit regression worth flagging even if the visual delta is small for v1's small planet discs.

**Smoke step**
Manual: focus Sol, fly inside Saturn-orbit range with Saturn framed against a bright Milky Way bulge patch; check whether the halo annulus around Saturn's disc still softens the MW colour (compare A/B with and without the new mat outer mesh).

title: pickStar: depth-occlusion semantics dropped without explicit contract note
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The old prime-tier tiebreak was `closest-to-camera wins (foreground occludes)` — a depth-ordering rule that mirrored the GPU's render order. The PR replaces it with `pickScore = pxDist + appMag·0.05`, which is dominated by screen-pixel proximity. With non-coincident overlapping discs, this means: a faint background star can win over a bright foreground star whose disc the cursor is inside, when the background star's projected centre is even ~1 px closer to the cursor (the mag bias is only 0.05 px / mag, so a 4-mag-brighter foreground is only 0.2 px of bias). The Double Double / Alula fixes are net wins, but the contract change — 'visible proximity beats depth ordering, always' — is not called out anywhere user-facing or in the helper docstring. `pickScore`'s comment mentions the Double Double rationale but doesn't acknowledge that depth-occlusion is now deliberately ignored, so a reader debugging a 'why did the picker pick the background star?' report has no signpost.

**Location**
src/client/star-geometry.ts:73-90 (`pickScore` docstring)
src/client/stellata.ts:2752-2762 (the prime-tier comment block in `pickStar`)

**Suggested approach**
Extend the `pickScore` docstring to spell out the trade-off explicitly: 'this rule deliberately ignores camera-distance / depth ordering; if a faint background star projects closer to the cursor than a bright foreground star, the background wins. The au3/xec failure mode (overlapping disc hitboxes leaving one component unclickable) was deemed worse than the rare inverse case, where a foreground bright disc is visually obvious but the cursor lands marginally closer to a background star's centre.' Same paragraph in `pickStar`'s tier-1 comment.

**Why**
Future maintainers will hit a 'why did the picker resolve to the wrong star?' report and need to know whether the answer is 'bug' or 'intentional contract'. Right now the only way to learn the contract is to git-blame the commit that removed `discBestCamDist`.

**Smoke step**
N/A (doc-only). After the docstring update, a reader of either file should be able to answer 'does pickStar prefer foreground over background when both contain the cursor?' without reading the diff.

title: disc-mask.ts dirty-track guard inverted (< ATTR_DIRTY_PX vs >=) vs rest of pass
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (disc-mask routed through canonical setNumAttr — inverted-guard form gone)

title: perf marker name 'onFrame.total' lags renamed event 'frame'
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The `animate()` loop wraps the bus emit with `perfMark('onFrame.total')` / `perfMeasure('onFrame.total')`, but the event itself was renamed from `onFrame` (the old method) to `'frame'` (the bus event). The perf section legend in `docs/performance.md` was updated to call this 'the full `'frame'` emit loop' but kept the literal section name `onFrame.total` because that's what `stellata.ts` still passes. So the user-facing label and the new event name disagree.

**Location**
- `src/client/stellata.ts:3465` — `perfMark('onFrame.total');`
- `src/client/stellata.ts:3467` — `perfMeasure('onFrame.total');`
- `docs/performance.md:46` — table entry still labelled `onFrame.total`

**Suggested approach**
Rename the marker to `frame.handlers` (or `frame.emit`) to match the event nomenclature, and update the row in the perf-table. This is the section that measures the `bus.emit('frame')` cost specifically, distinct from `frame.total` (the whole-tick wrapper).

**Why**
Naming consistency across the eleven event types is the value proposition of the migration; leaving `onFrame.total` as the perf label leaks the old vocabulary into runtime telemetry. Future readers grepping for `'frame'` won't find the perf section; future readers grepping for `onFrame` get a false hit on a renamed concept.

**Smoke step**
`debug.panel()` opens; the Perf section row reads with the new name; `npm run typecheck` and `npm test` green.

title: ATTR_DIRTY_PX duplicated 5× across overlays + chart-labels — extract shared util
description:
**Confidence: Medium** (the PR description preempts this — needs Alex's call on whether the threshold has reached extraction)

**Finding**
`const ATTR_DIRTY_PX = 0.05` plus the comment "Half a .toFixed(1) step ... Mirrors chart-labels.ts." is duplicated verbatim across five module files. Each file also reinvents an ad-hoc helper shape around it — `disc-mask.ts` uses a `Slot` object + `setCx/setCy/setR` closures, `distance-vector-overlay.ts` mixes `setLineD/setLineBgD` closures with inline `if (Math.abs(...) >= ATTR_DIRTY_PX)` blocks, `hud-overlay.ts` is purely inline, `poi-overlay.ts` mixes string-equality helpers with inline numeric guards, and `constellation-overlay.ts` has only a string-equality `setD`. Six callsites if focus-ring-overlay is added (see sibling bead).

The PR description acknowledges this and argues "three lines of constant beats premature abstraction." That argument was reasonable when the count was 1 (chart-labels alone). It now stands at 5, with 6 imminent. The "mirrors chart-labels.ts" comment becomes self-falsifying the moment any one tuning diverges.

**Location**
src/client/chart-labels.ts:138, src/client/disc-mask.ts:20, src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:21, src/client/hud-overlay.ts:42, src/client/poi-overlay.ts:49 (constant). Helper-shape divergence: src/client/disc-mask.ts:50-64, src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:69-78 + 197-215, src/client/hud-overlay.ts:256-267 + 414-440, src/client/poi-overlay.ts:147-166 + 297-326, src/client/chart-labels.ts:557-564 + 642-657 + 703-715.

**Suggested approach**
Extract `src/client/dirty-attr.ts` exporting `ATTR_DIRTY_PX = 0.05` and a small toolkit:

```ts
export function setNumAttr(el: Element, name: string, value: number, last: { v: number }, decimals = 1): void;
export function setStrAttr(el: Element, name: string, value: string, last: { v: string }): void;
export function setStyle(el: ElementCSSInlineStyle, prop: string, value: string, last: { v: string }): void;
```

(or whatever shape ends up reading cleanly — a state-bag object per element is one option, the boxed-`{ v }` cell is another). Migrate the five overlays to use it. The shape question is the bead's main risk; happy to file a follow-up that prototypes one shape and lets reviewers pick.

**Why**
Five copies of the same 3-line pattern + comment is past the point where DRY pays off — the cost of one shared module beats the cost of: (a) future drift between thresholds, (b) review burden when each new overlay reinvents a slightly different helper shape, (c) the next-overlay reviewer having to re-derive whether `>= ATTR_DIRTY_PX` or `< ATTR_DIRTY_PX` is the correct gate (disc-mask uses the inverted form — see sibling bead). The PR's stated rationale ("three lines of constant beats premature abstraction") does not survive the actual five-file body count.

**Smoke step**
After migration: `npm run typecheck`, `npm test`, and a manual stationary-camera idle in NAVIGATE + OBSERVE to confirm no regression in the dirty-skip behaviour.
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (ATTR_DIRTY_PX hoisted to src/client/dirty-attr.ts)

title: Cloud picker still 'closest-to-camera'; stars now pixel-proximity (asymmetry)
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
PR #53 fixed the same au3/xec bug class for stars by switching the prime tier from camera-distance to screen-pixel proximity (`pickScore`). The cloud picker (`Stellata.pickCloud` → `MolecularClouds.raycast`) still relies on Three.js `raycaster.intersectObjects`, which sorts hits by ray-distance from the camera and picks the first — i.e. the same 'closest-to-camera wins' rule au3/xec just rejected for stars. If the user clicks where two cloud ellipsoids overlap on screen, the foreground cloud always wins even when the cursor is visually plainly inside the background cloud's silhouette. Clouds are shelved (`FilterState.showMolecularClouds = false`), so this isn't a live UX bug today, but the picking-model asymmetry is now documented in code (one path screen-pixel-driven, the other camera-distance-driven) and will need reconciling when clouds re-enable.

**Location**
src/client/molecular-clouds.ts:197-203 (`raycast`)
src/client/stellata.ts:1627-1636 (`pickCloud`)
Compare: src/client/stellata.ts:2752-2804 (`pickStar` two-tier with pickScore)

**Suggested approach**
When the cloud layer is un-shelved, mirror `pickStar`'s contract: project each cloud centroid (or silhouette nearest-point) to screen and tiebreak overlapping cloud hits by pixel-distance from the cursor, with an analogous size-or-brightness mag bias (cloud volume / opacity). The simplest version reuses Three.js's intersectObjects to gate hit-vs-miss but resorts the hit list by projected-centroid-pixel-distance before picking the first. Alternatively, replace the mesh raycast entirely with an analytic ellipsoid screen-projection — `molecular-clouds.ts` already has `scratchDirLocal`/`scratchU`/`scratchV` projection scratch space for the silhouette overlay, so the math is partially in place.

**Why**
The whole point of au3 (Double Double) was that depth-tiebreak makes overlapping targets unclickable. Clouds are visually large and frequently overlap (Taurus / Perseus on the same line of sight). The same UX failure will reappear the moment clouds are re-enabled.

**Smoke step**
After re-enabling clouds: position camera so two clouds overlap (e.g. Perseus in front of California). Click each visible silhouette near its centre — both must be selectable independently. Today (under the current raycast logic) only the foreground is reachable.
notes: Deferred 2026-05-10 during PR #53 work. Tied to molecular-cloud un-shelve work; fix lands when FilterState.showMolecularClouds re-enables. Cloud picker still uses Three.js raycaster.intersectObjects (closest-to-camera), an asymmetry vs the new star pixel-proximity rule but inert while clouds are hidden.

title: Stale on*Change names in stellata.ts comments after event-bus migration
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
Five comments inside `stellata.ts` still refer to the pre-migration getter names (`onFocusChange`, `onWarpChange`, `onCameraModeChange`) even though those methods no longer exist on the class. The comments are correct in intent but talk about an API surface that was deleted in this PR.

**Location**
- `src/client/stellata.ts:1794` — "search box empties via onFocusChange → syncFocusUI"
- `src/client/stellata.ts:1814` — "search box clears via the onFocusChange handler"
- `src/client/stellata.ts:2190` — "overlays listening to onWarpChange are expected to hide themselves"
- `src/client/stellata.ts:2409` — "setFocus would do, triggering an onCameraModeChange flicker"
- `src/client/stellata.ts:2441` — "emit an onCameraModeChange event, briefly flickering UI"

**Suggested approach**
Mechanical s/onFooChange/the 'foo' event/ (or 'foo' bus emit) at each site. The PR already did this in `docs/architecture.md` and `docs/camera-modes.md` for the same nomenclature; these stragglers in `stellata.ts` were missed. Single commit, one file.

**Why**
Comments documenting cross-cutting state-flow are the first thing future-Claude reads — they should reference real API names. Otherwise readers grep for `onFocusChange` and find nothing, then have to reverse-engineer which event the comment means.

**Smoke step**
`grep -nE 'on(Focus|Vector|Filter|CameraMode|Cloud|Frame|State|Pois|Warp|PlanetSystem)Change' src/client/stellata.ts` returns no matches; `npm run typecheck`.

title: [PR #54] vec3FieldV3 sub-mask: high 5 bits silently ignored on decode (no defensive cap)
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`vec3FieldV3` (lines 249-256, 276-285) treats the sub-mask byte as 3 component bits + 5 reserved/unused bits, but the decoder only inspects `sub & 1`, `sub & 2`, `sub & 4` and silently ignores bits 3-7. A hand-edited or corrupt URL with `sub = 0xFF` decodes as if only the low 3 bits were set — mostly fine, but the file already takes a defensive stance elsewhere (POI count cap at decode time, fov clamp at encode time, varint length cap of 5 bytes). Reserved sub-mask bits being silently dropped is inconsistent with that stance.

Adjacent: the comment at lines 222-224 says `low 3 bits = which components diverge from default` but never names the high 5 bits as reserved — future maintainers might think they're available for repurpose, breaking the v3 wire format.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:222-256 (vec3FieldV3 comment + decodeBytes/decode); also affects every vec3 in v3.

**Suggested approach**
Two options. (a) Spell the reservation in the comment: `bits 3-7 reserved (must be 0); decoder ignores for forward-compat with future per-component encodings`. Optional: assert in dev that `sub & 0xF8 === 0` to catch hand-edits early. (b) If forward-compat for the high bits matters (e.g. a future per-component f64 escape), document the layout now so future v4 doesn't have to bump the schema version unnecessarily.

**Why**
The wire format's bit-budget should be readable from the code without inferring it from the encoder. A test that hand-builds a v3 blob with `sub = 0xFF` and asserts it still decodes the low 3 bits correctly would also pin the silent-drop behaviour in code rather than prose.

**Smoke step**
Add a test that hand-builds a v3 blob with cam sub-mask 0xF7 (only x set among low 3, plus all reserved bits) and asserts cam decodes as `[x_value, 0_default_y, 0_default_z]`. `npm test` green.

title: disc-mask.ts: no unit-test coverage for dedup / pool-growth / threshold branching
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #52 substantially extends `disc-mask.ts` per-frame logic: a `seen` Set deduplicates indices across focus + companion + every constellation-vertex iteration, an `ensureCircles` pool grower replaces a fixed-size pool, the observe-mode early-return now clears only the prior-frame slice, and `renderedSizePx` is now invoked per vertex of the highlighted constellation (vs at most 2 stars). None of this branching has unit coverage — there are no test files for `disc-mask` anywhere in the repo (`find . -name 'disc-mask*'` returns only worktree copies of the source). The PR's own test plan is a manual smoke checklist (Orion + Betelgeuse), so a regression in dedup, pool growth, or the lastUsed-tail clear would only surface visually on next manual smoke.

**Location**
src/client/disc-mask.ts (whole module, currently uncovered)

**Suggested approach**
Extract a pure helper that, given a focused-star idx (nullable), companion lookup fn, highlightCon idx, constellation polyline list, and a `renderedSizePx(idx) > threshold` predicate, returns the ordered list of indices to mask + a Set of seen indices. Cover: focus-null + highlight-set, focus-set + companion = -1, focus is a vertex of the highlighted constellation (dedup), polyline shares an endpoint with another polyline (dedup across polylines), all vertices below threshold (used = 0), conIdx out of bounds. Keep the DOM-touching half (`ensureCircles`, `placeCircle`) inside the factory — purity isn't required there, only for the index-selection pipeline.

**Why**
The dedup pipeline replaces an explicit, hand-verifiable focus + companion sequence with a Set-based fan-in across an arbitrary-length data structure. That's exactly the shape of code that can silently drop or double-count stars when the constellation data shape changes (e.g. polylines stored differently, or a vertex appears in three polylines). Pinning the index-selection contract via vitest matches the project's pattern for newly-introduced branching logic (see `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts`, `src/client/perceptual-magnitude.ts` + test).

**Smoke step**
