`npm test` covers new disc-mask.test.ts; `npm run typecheck` clean; the manual PR test plan still passes (Orion / Betelgeuse focus + unfocus + zoom-out).

title: [PR #54] varintLen and writeVarint duplicate the same shift-loop; encodeBlob recomputes mask twice
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`varintLen` (lines 371-379) and `writeVarint` (lines 343-354) implement the same `do { x >>>= 7; n++; } while (x !== 0)` loop. `writeVarint` already returns the byte count it wrote — so the byte count is computable in both directions, but `encodeBlob` calls `varintLen` to size the buffer, allocates, then calls `writeVarint` and trusts (silently) that the length matches.

Adjacent issue: `writeUrl` (line 1123) calls `computePresence` to decide between '' and `?v=`, then `encodeBlob` (called by writeUrl) immediately calls `computePresence` again at line 714. Two full FIELDS_V3 walks with isPresent() per-field on every URL write.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:343-354 (writeVarint), 371-379 (varintLen), 705-732 (computePresence + encodeBlob), 1123-1131 (writeUrl).

**Suggested approach**
Either drop `varintLen` and pre-write the varint to a 5-byte scratch `Uint8Array`, then concat with payload — or accept the duplication but unit-test that `varintLen(m) === writeVarint(_, _, m)` for masks 0, 1<<6, 1<<7, 1<<13, 1<<14, 1<<20, 1<<21, 0xffffffff so a future change to either function trips a test instead of producing a malformed blob. For computePresence: have encodeBlob return both blob and mask, and writeUrl use mask !== 0 from that single call.

**Why**
Duplicate length math is the kind of thing that drifts without notice — a bug in either function produces an off-by-one buffer over/underread that the existing round-trip tests would catch only because the version+mask path happens to be dead-fixed. Direct unit coverage of the helpers (or removing the duplication) makes that intent load-bearing.

**Smoke step**
Add a unit test asserting `varintLen(m)` equals the byte count returned by `writeVarint` for the masks listed above; `npm test` still green.

title: [PR #54] vec3FieldV3 hard-codes cam-mode special case via key-string check; decodeV3 mirrors it
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`vec3FieldV3` (line 234) is a generic helper for cam/tgt/up/worldOffset, but inside it `componentDefaults` branches on `key === 'cam'` to return mode-aware defaults (line 237). The other three keys take the static `def` path. Symmetrically, `decodeV3` (line 792) re-introduces the same special case: `if (f.key === 'cam') camSub = dv.getUint8(off);` to capture the sub-mask for the post-pass cam fix-up.

The same per-key-string check appears in both encode and decode, in two different files-of-the-mind, and renaming the cam key (or adding a second mode-dependent vec3 like a future observe-mode tgt or up) requires editing both sites.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:234-286 (vec3FieldV3 with embedded `key === 'cam'` branch); 780-805 (decodeV3 with embedded `f.key === 'cam'` branch).

**Suggested approach**
Pass `componentDefaults` as a callback parameter to `vec3FieldV3(bit, key, getDefault)` instead of branching on key. The cam call site supplies `v => defaultCamForMode(v.mode)` and tgt/up/worldOffset supply `() => def`. `decodeV3`'s post-pass can be replaced by storing the sub-mask on the field spec or by exposing a `postDecode(view)` hook so cam's mode-dependent fix-up lives next to its encoder rather than as scattered `f.key === 'cam'` checks.

**Why**
The cam-default-mode-aware behaviour is a single conceptual rule (closed bead 9mm.20 already extracted the const `OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL` / `defaultCamForMode`), but the code that *uses* it still looks like three coincidences: vec3FieldV3 inlines a key check, decodeV3 inlines another key check, and applyDecodedView inlines a third call to defaultCamForMode (line 1071). Pushing the rule into the field spec localises it.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — observe-mode cam fix-up tests (lines 155-178 of url-state.test.ts) still pass and `?v=` still round-trips a focused observe pose at < 12 chars.

title: [PR #54] FIELDS_V2 / FIELDS_V3 differ only in the four vec3 entries — duplicate ~70 lines
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`FIELDS_V2` (lines 515-599) and `FIELDS_V3` (lines 607-678) are byte-for-byte identical for bits 3-19 and bit 21 — that is 16 of 20 field specs duplicated verbatim, including the inline preset/con/flags/focusCleared/pois/t entries with their full encode/decode bodies. The only differences are bits 0/1/2/20 swapping `vec3Field` for `vec3FieldV3`. A field-shape change in one (say, repurposing the 23-bit ID space, fixing a bug in pois encode) silently has to be applied twice or the two tables drift.

**Location**
src/client/url-state.ts:515-678

**Suggested approach**
Build the two tables from a single shared base. One option: `buildFields(vec3Spec: (bit, key) => FieldSpec)` returning the full 20-entry list parameterised on the vec3 helper. `FIELDS_V2 = buildFields(vec3Field)` and `FIELDS_V3 = buildFields(vec3FieldV3)`. The frozen-decoder concern is preserved because v2's vec3Field stays unchanged; the dedup is on the 16 shared entries. FIELDS_V1 stays separate (different scalar shape) per the existing comment, but the v2/v3 split is a copy.

**Why**
The file's own comment block at the top (`Adding a field… freeze the old one verbatim so its decoder stays correct`) only argues for *not retiring* old tables — it doesn't argue for textual duplication. A shared builder keeps v2 frozen *by construction* while eliminating ~70 lines of code that would otherwise need to be edited in lockstep for any non-vec3 field change.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — all 503 v3 round-trip + v1/v2 backward-compat tests should still pass.

title: MAGIC and BINARY_VERSION coupling not enforced (currently 'HYG4'/4)
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`MAGIC = 'HYG4'` and `BINARY_VERSION = 4` are currently in lockstep — the trailing digit of MAGIC matches the version. The PR's instructions to future contributors (catalog-pure.ts:215, docs/build-and-data.md) say 'bump BINARY_VERSION + MAGIC' as if they're a coordinated pair, but nothing enforces the convention. A future PR could bump version to 5 while leaving MAGIC at 'HYG4', and only the runtime version check (`Bad magic` is fine, `Unsupported catalog version: 5 (expected 4)` is the only catch — but only against an old catalog.bin file).

The risk is small (any divergence is loudly caught at next `npm run build:catalog` + dev load), but the convention is a load-bearing one and would benefit from a one-line test.

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:261-262

**Suggested approach**
Add to the `binary-format constants` describe block: `expect(MAGIC.endsWith(String(BINARY_VERSION))).toBe(true)`. One line, pins the documented convention. If the convention isn't worth keeping (e.g. v10 makes the digit-suffix scheme awkward), drop it from prose and do nothing.

**Why**
The PR's own description says 'bump BINARY_VERSION + MAGIC in catalog-pure.ts' — i.e. they are in sync by convention. Tests should pin convention if it's load-bearing.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` after adding the assertion. Bump only one of MAGIC/BINARY_VERSION and verify the test catches it.
close_reason: v5 scaffolding landed in 24c34cb; coupling assertion folded in

title: startUrlSync lastCam seed/update duplicates the same 9-line snapshot pattern
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`startUrlSync` writes the same 3-vec3 (camera.position, controls.target, camera.up) into the lastCam Float64Array in three places: the new initial seed (lines 933-939), the per-frame change-detector body (970-972), and (implicit) every comparison block at 963-967. The seed and the update bodies are textually identical. A small extracted helper would document the layout and prevent index-mismatch drift.

**Location**
- src/client/url-state.ts:933-939 (new initial-seed block — added in PR #39)
- src/client/url-state.ts:963-972 (existing per-frame compare + write)

**Suggested approach**
Extract `function snapshotCam(stellata: Stellata, out: Float64Array)` (or method on a tiny class) that writes the 9 components with a single line per axis triple, e.g. via three Vector3.toArray() calls into out at offsets 0/3/6. Call once for the seed and once per-frame after the change check fires. Optional: a parallel `function camChangedSinceSnapshot(...)` for the comparison block, but that's a bigger refactor — keep this bead scoped to the snapshot helper.

**Why**
The change-detector layout (`[0..2] camera.position, [3..5] controls.target, [6..8] camera.up`) is now embedded in two places. Adding a fourth axis (e.g. fov, future zoom) means editing two parallel sections that must stay aligned by index. A helper centralises the layout and makes additions one-line.

**Smoke step**
`npm test`: existing url-state tests cover the per-frame writer; manual smoke is bare-URL load → URL stays empty until first interaction (the contract this seed protects).
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: perf-hud colour ramp uses unnamed thresholds (4 ms, 0.7×budget, 2× cap)
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`colourForAvg` uses a literal `4` (ms) threshold for the green→amber transition; the histogram bar ramp uses `MS_PER_FRAME_60 * 0.7 ≈ 11.67` for the same green→amber transition; the visible-height cap uses `MS_PER_FRAME_60 * 2`. Three magic numbers, two of them encoding similar visual semantics ("approaching the budget") at different scales, neither named.

**Location**
src/client/perf-hud.ts:262-264 (`colourForAvg`: `avg > 4`), :317 (`const cap = MS_PER_FRAME_60 * 2`), :333-335 (histogram colour ramp: `MS_PER_FRAME_60 * 0.7`)

**Suggested approach**
Hoist named constants near the existing `MS_PER_FRAME_60` block. Suggested:
- `AVG_AMBER_MS = 4` — "row colour amber threshold (avg ms)"
- `HISTO_AMBER_RATIO = 0.7` — "histogram bar amber threshold as fraction of MS_PER_FRAME_60"
- `HISTO_HEIGHT_CAP_MULT = 2` — "histogram bar visible-height cap as multiple of MS_PER_FRAME_60"

Optionally collapse the row + bar colour ramps to a shared helper that takes an amber threshold and a red threshold parameter, since both ramps are 3-stop linear (green / amber / red).

**Why**
DRY + readability. The colour-ramp logic is duplicated almost verbatim between row and histogram; the only difference is the amber threshold (one absolute, one ratio). A reader has to triangulate three sites to understand the calibration.

**Smoke step**
`npm run typecheck && npm test`. Manual: `debug.panel()`, induce sustained \>16.7 ms frames (toggle a heavy filter), confirm bars and rows transition through amber → red as before.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: modal-dismiss: querySelectorAll once at bind time — late-added dismiss buttons aren'\''t wired
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`bindModalDismissal` runs `modal.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>("[data-modal-dismiss]")` once at bind time and attaches click handlers. If a modal's contents are later mutated (e.g. dynamic close button injected into a content area), the new dismiss button won't be wired. The pre-refactor code had the same behavior, but consolidating into a helper is the right time to decide on the contract.

**Location**
src/client/modal-dismiss.ts:36-38

**Suggested approach**
Two options:
1. Leave as-is and document "modal contents must not change after bind" on `bindModalDismissal`.
2. Switch to a single delegated click listener on `modal`: `modal.addEventListener("click", e => { if ((e.target as Element).closest("[data-modal-dismiss]")) close(); })`. Late-added dismiss buttons just work, and the per-button listener overhead disappears.

Option 2 is the single-line change that future-proofs the helper.

**Location**
src/client/modal-dismiss.ts:36-38

**Why**
Today every modal's dismiss buttons are static, so the bug doesn't exist in production. But the helper is going to be the modal pattern going forward; pinning the contract now (or making it robust) saves a debugging session later.

**Smoke step**
After applying #2, open about modal, run `document.getElementById("about-modal").querySelector(".modal-card").insertAdjacentHTML("beforeend", "<button data-modal-dismiss>extra</button>")`, click the new button, confirm modal dismisses.

title: readPipeDelimited's existsSync check duplicates main()'s outer gate
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`readPipeDelimited` checks `if (!existsSync(path)) return` internally, but `main()` already gates the only two call sites (`parseGcvsMain()` and `parseGcvsCrossref()`) behind `if (existsSync(SRC_GCVS) && existsSync(SRC_GCVS_XREF))` (build-catalog.ts:682). The inner check fires three times per build (twice in the helper + once in the outer gate) for the same files; under normal flow it's dead defensive code. `parseHipCcdm` is structured the opposite way — it self-gates (line 323) and `main()` also gates (line 699), but that's at least symmetric across both GCVS-style paths.

**Location**
scripts/build-catalog.ts:226-233 (helper), 682 (outer gate around the two GCVS parsers)

**Suggested approach**
Either: (a) drop the `existsSync` from `readPipeDelimited` and rely on the outer gate (simplest; matches the helper's stated 'pure pipe-delimited reader' purpose); or (b) drop the outer `existsSync` pair in main() and rely on the helper's per-file no-op (lets `parseGcvsMain`/`parseGcvsCrossref` be safely callable in any context). (a) is preferred because the outer gate also wraps the log-line / timing block, so `main` would still need to know whether the data files were present to print 'GCVS files not found; skipping…'. Keep the outer gate, drop the inner one.

**Why**
The PR's framing is dedup, but the dedup leaves two 'is the file there?' checks side-by-side. One is the system-of-record (the outer gate decides whether to even enter the GCVS branch and emits the user-facing log message); the other is a no-op fallback. Tightening the inner helper to assume its caller already checked makes the contract clearer and removes a read of the filesystem on a build hot path (~hundreds of thousands of stars × N file stats is negligible, but the principle is cleaner).

**Smoke step**
`npm run build:catalog` reports the same record counts (313242 records, 11316 Hip + 13926 HD GCVS xrefs, 3677 variables matched) before and after the change. `npm test` unaffected (helper still pure inside).
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Section builder API split: star/milkyway return HTMLDivElement, perf/pin return {element, dispose, setVisible} — unify
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The four section builders wired into `debug.ts togglePanel` have two distinct shapes:

- `buildStarSection(stellata): HTMLDivElement` and `buildMilkywaySection(layer): HTMLDivElement` both build the `makeCollapsibleSection` themselves and return the wrapped section ready to append.
- `buildPerfSection(): {element, dispose, setVisible}` and `buildPinSection(stellata): {element, dispose, setVisible}` return a builder triple, and `togglePanel` does the `makeCollapsibleSection` wrapping at the call site, manually wires `onCollapseChange → setVisible`, AND manually calls `setVisible(!isCollapsed())` once after wiring (because `onCollapseChange` doesn't fire on init).

The result is ~25 lines of duplicated boilerplate in `togglePanel` per dispose-needing section, and adding a fifth section means replicating the pattern again. The asymmetry leaks into the docstring at debug.ts:24 ("either a section element or a {element, dispose, setVisible} triple") which is itself a design smell.

**Location**
src/client/debug.ts:55-94 (togglePanel), src/client/star-tuning.ts:18-22 + src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts:31-35 (build their own section), src/client/perf-hud.ts:117 + src/client/pin-debug-hud.ts:57 (return a triple).

**Suggested approach**
Pick one shape. Two options:

1. *All sections return a triple.* Push `makeCollapsibleSection` into `togglePanel` (or a single-line helper `mountSection({title, storageKey, build})`) for every section. Star/milkyway gain a no-op `dispose` and a stub `setVisible` (or `setVisible` becomes optional). `togglePanel` reduces to a four-element array iteration.

2. *All sections handle their own collapsing.* Star/milkyway already do. Perf/pin take a `storageKey` and `makeCollapsibleSection` themselves, returning `{section, dispose}` or just `HTMLDivElement` plus a separate dispose registration callback. `togglePanel` collects disposers via a `disposers: (() => void)[]` array.

Option 2 keeps the current return-shape of star/milkyway but loses the "each section shape mirrors its needs" intentional split. Option 1 unifies and is more iteration-friendly but slightly more ceremony for the sliders-only sections. I lean (1) — easier extension and lets the dispose/visibility lifecycle be a first-class capability.

**Why**
Per CLAUDE.md priority (Architectural fit). The unification is the explicit purpose of this PR ("Unify debug panel"); having two builder shapes inside the unified panel undercuts that goal and creates the exact "copy this paragraph" tax the next dev will hit when they add a section. Also tightens `togglePanel` so it stops being a per-section transcription.

**Smoke step**
`npm run typecheck`, `npm test`. Open panel, confirm all four sections render + collapse + persist. Close + reopen, confirm sessionStorage state survives. Reload, confirm collapse state resets.

title: AU_PC redefined locally in first-load.test.ts and heliopause.test.ts instead of imported from ephemeris
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
Two test files redefine the AU_PC constant inline (`const AU_PC = 1 / 206264.80624709636;`) rather than importing the canonical export from ephemeris.ts. If the canonical value were ever refined (e.g. to a higher-precision IAU figure), the test constants would silently diverge from production code and tests could pass against a wrong reference value.

**Location**
- src/client/first-load.test.ts:5 (new in PR #39)
- src/client/heliopause.test.ts:5 (pre-existing; now joined by a second copy)
- canonical source: src/client/ephemeris.ts:23 (re-exported at ephemeris.ts:324, also re-exported from orbit-rings-layer.ts:35)

**Suggested approach**
Replace the local `const AU_PC = ...` in both test files with `import { AU_PC } from './ephemeris';`. One line of import, one line of redefinition removed each. Apply to both files in a single bead since they are the same fix and currently the *only* sites of redefinition.

**Why**
Tests verifying physical-unit conversions should reference the same constant the production code uses; otherwise a typo or precision update in ephemeris.ts could silently desynchronise the test reference. This is also the project's existing convention — see orbit-rings-layer.ts:35 where AU_PC is even re-exported for downstream callers, exactly to avoid each module redefining its own copy.

**Smoke step**
`grep -rn 'const AU_PC' src/` returns zero matches outside ephemeris.ts; `npm test` still green.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: No regression test for the name-table layout (zero-sentinel + length-prefixed UTF-8)
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The catalog binary's name-table format is described in catalog-pure.ts:255-259 ("Two zero bytes of padding so name offset 0 reads as the 'no name' sentinel, followed by length-prefixed UTF-8 strings: uint16 byteLen, then byteLen bytes"). The PR added pin tests for `HEADER_LAYOUT` and `RECORD_LAYOUT` but not for this third layout component. `catalog-loader.test.ts` exercises the table indirectly (the 'name table' describe block, lines 295-356) but each test instantiates names through the synthetic `buildCatalog` helper, which itself is a parallel implementation of the writer's chunk-builder logic in build-catalog.ts:711-732 — drift between the two would not be caught.

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:255-259 (spec); scripts/build-catalog.ts:711-732 (writer); src/client/catalog-loader.ts:145-169 (reader); src/client/catalog-loader.test.ts:36-100 (synthetic builder)

**Suggested approach**
After the round-trip helper in 9mm.80 lands, extend it to cover the name-table portion: writer emits the canonical encoding, reader resolves names by offset, and a property-style test fuzzes name lists (including UTF-8 multi-byte chars, empty list, very long names near the 65535 cap). Alternatively, add direct constants for the name-table contract: `NAME_TABLE_PADDING = 2`, `NAME_LENGTH_PREFIX_BYTES = 2`, and a focused unit test that pins them.

**Why**
Name-table layout is part of the 'binary format' the PR centralised but only two of the three components (header, record) got the regression block. The third one stays pinned only by the prose comment.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` after the change. Deliberately omit the 2-byte zero-sentinel padding from the writer and verify the new test fails.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: No-reverse-sync caveat duplicated across star-tuning.ts and milkyway-tuning.ts
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #47 added a near-identical "No reverse sync" header comment to both src/client/star-tuning.ts (lines 13-16) and src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts (lines 14-17). The wording is essentially the same — both warn that makeSlider's `initial` is a snapshot at build time and external writes will not move the thumb. Ironically a cleanup PR introduced a small DRY violation: any third tuning section will copy the same caveat again.

**Location**
src/client/star-tuning.ts:13-16
src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts:14-17
(Pattern lives in src/client/debug-panel.ts:302-344 — makeSlider / SliderOpts.initial.)

**Suggested approach**
Hoist the caveat onto makeSlider (or SliderOpts.initial) as a JSDoc, where the snapshot semantics actually live. The per-section header comments can collapse to one line ("No reverse sync — see makeSlider."). A future tuning section then inherits the contract via the JSDoc instead of copy-pasting.

**Why**
The "no reverse sync" property is a property of makeSlider, not of the star-disc or Milky Way sections. Locating the caveat at its source is the standard fix for "same comment in multiple consumers" — it survives Cmd-click, IDE hover, and grep. The current state will only get worse: PR #47 itself acknowledges "presets" as a hypothetical future writer, and adding presets is the kind of follow-up that will spawn more tuning sections each carrying their own copy of the same comment.

**Smoke step**
None needed — pure comment relocation. npm run typecheck clean.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: modal-dismiss: per-modal keydown listener — multiple-open modals all close on one ESC
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
The new `bindModalDismissal` adds a fresh `keydown` listener every time a modal opens, and the listener only checks `if (!modal.hidden) close()`. If two modals using this helper are open simultaneously (e.g. some future flow opens credits while about is still open), an ESC keypress fires both listeners and closes both. The previous brand-modal had a SINGLE shared listener that walked both modals — same problem under the same hypothetical, but the failure mode was visible at one site. With the refactor it's now a contract of `bindModalDismissal` itself.

**Location**
src/client/modal-dismiss.ts:23-25

**Suggested approach**
Two reasonable fixes; pick whichever fits the design intent:
1. Document the helper as "single-modal-at-a-time" and add a runtime warn if a second `open()` is called while another bound modal is still showing.
2. Make the helper aware of an "active modal stack" so ESC only closes the top one (last-opened wins).

Today every caller (info / about / credits / help) opens exactly one modal at a time so this is theoretical. Worth filing because the consolidated helper now silently bakes this behavior into every future modal user.

**Why**
The previous code had the same coupling but a future maintainer adding a fifth modal had to look at brand-modal.ts's shared-listener pattern and reason about it. Now it's buried in a generic helper; the gotcha is invisible.

**Smoke step**
Open about-modal then credits-modal via dev-console (`document.getElementById(credits-modal).hidden = false`); press ESC and confirm both close together. If that's acceptable, document it; if not, implement #2.

title: docs/build-and-data.md: search-index section doesn't reference SearchEntry
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
The PR makes `SearchEntry` in scripts/catalog-pure.ts the single source of truth for the search-index.json wire format and adds in-file comments framing it as the shared writer↔reader contract. `docs/build-and-data.md` has a 'Search index (`public/search-index.json`)' section (lines 69-89) that still describes the format procedurally — 'Short keys (`i/p/b/f/hip/hd/hr/gl/c/s`) to keep wire size down' — without pointing to where the canonical TypeScript definition lives. Future maintainers reading the doc won't know there's a single interface to update.

**Location**
docs/build-and-data.md:69-89 (Search index section)

**Suggested approach**
Add a one-line pointer in the search-index section: 'Field shape pinned in `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` as the `SearchEntry` interface — the writer (build-catalog.ts) and the reader (src/client/search.ts) both import it; drift = compile error.' Same convention this doc already uses for the binary record layout ('Layout consistency is pinned by the binary-format constants block in scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts').

**Why**
Discoverability. The file-format paragraph in docs/build-and-data.md is the natural place a new contributor goes when extending the search index (e.g. adding alternate designations per stellata-nxb). Without the pointer, they'll re-introduce drift by editing one side without the other.

**Smoke step**
Visual review of the rendered markdown; the pointer should sit alongside the existing 'pinned by ... .test.ts' note for the binary layout.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Stale 'Orion-framed' / 'Orion highlighted' comments document a dropped draft
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
Two comments still describe an earlier draft of the first-load view in which Orion was highlighted. The shipped feature parks at the galactic centre with NO constellation highlight (FIRST_LOAD_VIEW.con is undefined and the test at first-load.test.ts:32 explicitly asserts this). The stale comments contradict the code and the docs.

**Location**
- src/client/main.ts:154-155 — "…canonical first-load view (Sol focus, parked at 5 AU framing Orion, HUD on, Orion highlighted) — stellata-vjm."
- CLAUDE.md:162 — "5 AU Orion-framed park via first-load.ts"

**Suggested approach**
Replace both with the galactic-centre framing language used in the PR body, README, first-load.ts module header, and docs/solar-system.md ("5 AU Sol park aimed at the galactic centre, HUD on, no constellation highlight").

**Why**
Comments documenting load-bearing behaviour need to match the code; future maintainers reading main.ts will look at these to decide whether to preserve Orion-framing on changes that touch the first-load path. CLAUDE.md is the always-loaded entry point for Claude-Code sessions and a wrong description there propagates into every downstream session.

**Smoke step**
grep -n 'Orion' CLAUDE.md src/client/main.ts and confirm zero hits in the first-load region; visual smoke (load /) still shows galactic-centre framing.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: HEADER_LAYOUT shape loses field-type info — only inline comments capture uint32 vs ASCII
description:
**Confidence: Low — needs Alex's input**

**Finding**
`HEADER_LAYOUT` and `RECORD_LAYOUT` are typed as `{name: number}` (offset only). Field-type information (`uint8`, `uint16`, `uint32`, `float32`, `ASCII4`) lives only in `// uint32`-style comments next to each entry. Consequence: writers and readers must remember which `view.setXxx` / `view.getXxx` to call per field, the test must mirror the type info into a parallel `sizes` table (see 9mm.71), and an automated reader/writer pair (see 9mm.80) can't be expressed without re-stating the type info a third time.

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:267-293 (HEADER_LAYOUT, RECORD_LAYOUT)

**Suggested approach**
Promote each layout entry to `{ offset: number; kind: 'u8' | 'u16' | 'u32' | 'f32' | 'ascii4' }`. Provide tiny `readField(view, base, spec)` / `writeField(view, base, spec, value)` helpers that switch on `kind`. Writer and reader then read/write by name — `writeField(view, off, RECORD_LAYOUT.physRadius, s.physicalRadius)` — and the per-call `setFloat32` boilerplate vanishes. Trade-off: a small per-call switch overhead in two hot loops (writer is one-shot at build, but reader runs 313k×). Worth measuring before adopting.

The 'maybe unnecessary scaffolding' angle: the current shape is concise and the PR's regression test pins offsets. Promoting kind into the const may be over-engineering for a 4-month-old format with one writer and one reader.

**Why**
Files this as 'needs input' because it is fundamentally a tradeoff between elegance (single source for offset+type) and reading-loop cost. Alex should decide whether the reader-loop cost is acceptable. A middle path: keep the offset-only consts, but export a `RECORD_FIELD_KINDS` const next to them so the test (and any future round-trip helper) imports it instead of re-stating sizes.

**Smoke step**
If pursuing the kind-aware shape: `npm test` plus a perf-hud measurement of `parseBinary` cold-load time, comparing before/after on the full ~313k catalog (target: no measurable regression beyond noise).

title: Test gap: extract perf-hud pure helpers (summarize, insertSorted, colourForAvg) for vitest coverage
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`perf-hud.ts` has three pure helpers used per-tick by `renderPanel()` that have non-trivial behaviour and zero test coverage: `summarize()` (avg+max over a partial-fill ring buffer — must respect `s.count`, not `s.ring.length`), `insertSorted()` (partial top-N sort: keeping `MAX_TABLE_ROWS=8` descending-by-avg with bounded splice), and `colourForAvg()` (threshold step at the 60Hz frame budget and the 4ms warning).

The current `perf-hud.test.ts` only covers the "no-op API stays callable" contract. The three helpers above are exactly the kind of "obvious-looking but easy to off-by-one" code that benefits most from a 10-line test file. Specifically: insertSorted with arr.length === MAX_TABLE_ROWS and a value smaller than every element should be a no-op, not a splice that walks past the cap; summarize with count=0 must return zeros (currently does), with count<RING_SIZE must average over count not RING_SIZE (currently does — but a regression here would silently halve early-frame averages).

**Location**
src/client/perf-hud.ts:248-258 (summarize), :262-264 (colourForAvg), :358-366 (insertSorted), :260 (fmtMs).

**Suggested approach**
Extract the four helpers into `perf-hud-pure.ts` and re-import them from `perf-hud.ts`. Add `perf-hud-pure.test.ts` covering: summarize over a partial-fill ring (count=3, ring partially populated); insertSorted top-3 with ascending and descending input streams; insertSorted no-op when below smallest existing avg at cap; colourForAvg at 4.0/4.0001/16.6/16.7 boundary samples; fmtMs(9.99) vs fmtMs(10.0) format flip.

**Why**
Per CLAUDE.md priority (Tests). The previous PR #41 (stellata-r48) optimised this exact path — preserving its correctness through future refactors needs lock-in via tests. The functions are textbook-pure; cost of extraction is trivial.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` shows new module covered. Run a session, expand Perf section, confirm headline + table + histogram still update at ~5Hz with same numbers as pre-refactor (eyeball comparison).

title: perf-hud test only covers no-op contract; insertion-sort + dirty-track + section-GC untested
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
PR #41 added `src/client/perf-hud.test.ts` with two cases, both covering only the "no-op until installed" API contract (`mark/measure/frame` don't throw before install). The actual code-quality value of the PR — single-pass insertion sort, per-row / per-bar dirty-tracking, the panel rebuild→mutate switch — has zero regression coverage. Pre-existing logic that's also uncovered: section garbage-collection after `RING_SIZE` silent frames (commented at perf-hud.ts:90-95 as critical for chart.* dropoff after exit) and `summarize` (sum/avg/max).

**Location**
src/client/perf-hud.test.ts (entire file), src/client/perf-hud.ts:90-95 (GC), :248-258 (summarize), :355-365 (insertSorted)

**Suggested approach**
Add tests against the pure helpers without DOM: export `insertSorted` and `summarize` (or a test-only entrypoint). For `insertSorted`: descending-by-avg ordering, fixed cap behaviour (9th input drops the lowest), tie-breaking semantics, empty-input handling. For `summarize`: count=0 returns {0,0}, ring-buffer wraparound. For section GC: install, fire `mark/measure` for two labels, fire `RING_SIZE+1` frames without re-marking one, assert that label is gone (would require exposing `sectionsForTest()` or similar). Skip the DOM-render path tests if the cost of jsdom setup outweighs the value — but the dirty-track contract is the core deliverable so probably worth a smoke that confirms repeated render() calls with stable inputs don't emit redundant style writes (Proxy on style or count setter calls).

**Why**
Test-coverage gap matches the priority of the change. The PR's own description claims "+2 for perf-hud no-op API contract" — accurate, but the no-op contract was the lowest-risk piece. The new sort + dirty-track is the part that could regress silently (already does in part — see colour-comparison bead).

**Smoke step**
`npm test` shows new green cases for insertion-sort, summarize, and section GC. CI parallel runs unaffected.

title: chart-labels.ts projectVec comment misleading: shared scratch is module-level not 'fresh'
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The new `projectVec` in chart-labels.ts has a comment claiming "The overlay-project scratch is fresh on every call, so the shared module-level scratch costs no allocation." That contradicts itself: overlay-project.ts uses a module-level `scratch` Vector3 that is reused across ALL callers (chart-labels, hud, poi, focus-ring, disc-mask, constellation), not "fresh". The intended meaning was probably "the shared scratch is owned per-call (no caller-supplied scratch needed)" but as written it reads like an incorrect denial of the sharing.

**Location**
src/client/chart-labels.ts:744-749

**Suggested approach**
Rewrite to: "// projectToScreen owns its own module-level scratch; passing `p` as input is safe because projectToScreen does scratch.copy(p)." That accurately describes the contract callers depend on (input is preserved, no per-call allocation).

**Why**
Future maintainers reading this comment may believe the scratch is per-call and try to interleave projectToScreen calls with concurrent reads of an unrelated Vector3, which is fine — but they may also write back to the scratch assuming exclusivity, which is wrong. Accurate comments are the contract.

**Smoke step**
None — comment-only change.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Test gap: SearchEntry wire-format keys not pinned
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The PR promotes `SearchEntry` to `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` as the shared writer↔reader contract for `search-index.json` (~13 MB raw across hundreds of thousands of entries) and explicitly frames it the same way the binary record layout is framed: 'drift here ships a broken index'. The binary record layout has a regression-pinning test (the `catalog-pure / binary-format constants` describe block in catalog-pure.test.ts: header offsets non-overlap, record offsets non-overlap, fields cover RECORD_SIZE, flag bits are distinct powers of two). The new `SearchEntry` interface gets no equivalent pin.

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:295-313 (interface); scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts (no covering describe block)

**Suggested approach**
Add a small `describe('catalog-pure / search-index wire contract', ...)` block that asserts the field-name set and required-vs-optional shape — for example: build a sample `SearchEntry` exercising every optional field, JSON.stringify it, and check the key set against an explicit literal (`['i','p','b','f','c','s','hip','hd','hr','gl']`). Bonus: assert that the writer-side optional-field omission convention round-trips (i.e. no `undefined` keys end up in the JSON). The test should fail loudly if anyone renames a key or drops one without a coordinated reader change.

**Why**
The whole motivation in the PR description — 'drift is now a typecheck failure rather than a broken index at runtime' — only holds for typecheck-time changes within the same TypeScript build. A runtime change (e.g. some future build script writing `{ index: ... }` instead of `{ i: ... }`) sails past tsc but breaks every consumer. Mirroring the binary-format pinning closes that gap.

**Smoke step**
New test case fails when a key in the interface is renamed; passes against current code. `npm test` still reports 485+ passing tests (no regression in existing suite).

title: No round-trip test from build-catalog writer to catalog-loader parser
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The PR moves writer + reader onto shared `RECORD_LAYOUT` constants — but there is no test that actually runs the writer's record-emit logic and feeds it to `parseBinary`. `catalog-loader.test.ts` has its own synthetic `buildCatalog()` that mirrors the writer (lines 36–100); `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts` doesn't write binaries at all. So the writer side of the layout sharing is exercised only at build time (verified manually via the catalog.bin md5 in the PR description) — not under `npm test`.

If a future change to `build-catalog.ts` introduces a writer-only bug — e.g. a stray `view.setUint16` where `setUint8` was intended, or an off-by-one in the variable-fields branch — vitest catches nothing because the writer is in a side-effecting CLI script, not a tested function. The next deploy would silently ship a corrupt binary.

**Location**
scripts/build-catalog.ts:749-783 (record write loop); src/client/catalog-loader.ts:124-143 (record read loop)

**Suggested approach**
Extract the per-record write and the per-record read into pure functions in catalog-pure.ts:
- `writeRecord(view: DataView, off: number, record: BinaryRecord): void`
- `readRecord(view: DataView, off: number): BinaryRecord`

The current writer/reader keep their loop structure but call those helpers. Add a vitest property-style test that fuzzes records, writes via `writeRecord`, reads via `readRecord`, and asserts equality (with quantisation tolerance for ampUnits/period). Bonus: `writeRecord` and `readRecord` are the natural seam where the layout const becomes load-bearing — drift in either direction (writer OR reader) gets caught.

**Why**
The PR's correctness argument is 'md5 matches main', i.e. byte-for-byte identical to the un-refactored writer. That's a one-shot fact about today, not a regression guarantee for tomorrow's changes to either side. A round-trip property test pins the contract for the lifetime of v4.

**Smoke step**
`npm test`; deliberately swap two field assignments in build-catalog.ts (e.g. `physRadius` ↔ `ci`) and verify the round-trip test catches it.

title: Test gap: extract debug-panel.ts pure helpers (clampToViewport, rgbToHex, hexToRgb) to a tested module
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`debug-panel.ts` has three small pure functions that are easy to break and impossible to unit-test in their current form: `clampToViewport`, `rgbToHex`, and `hexToRgb`. None are exported. The colour round-trips are particularly bug-prone (off-by-one rounding at channel boundaries; NaN propagation when hex is malformed). `clampToViewport` is the only thing keeping a saved position from being unreachable on a smaller window — also worth pinning.

Additionally, `loadPosition` uses `typeof === 'number'` which lets NaN pass through; a once-saved bad position would clamp to the corner forever. `Number.isFinite` is safer.

**Location**
src/client/debug-panel.ts:23-53 (clampToViewport, loadPosition), src/client/debug-panel.ts:371-383 (rgbToHex/hexToRgb).

**Suggested approach**
Extract the four helpers into `debug-panel-pure.ts` (or a small `color-util.ts` for the colour pair if they get reused) and add a vitest `debug-panel-pure.test.ts`. Cover: clamp at all four edges + below-margin + viewport-smaller-than-panel; rgb→hex→rgb round-trip preserves ~3-decimal precision; hex with shorthand or bad input fails predictably; loadPosition rejects NaN-shaped JSON. Keep `makeDebugPanel`/`makeSlider`/`makeColor` as the DOM-coupled exports they already are.

**Why**
Per CLAUDE.md priority (Tests). Pure functions with no test coverage are exactly the easy wins — once extracted they cost ~30 lines of vitest and prevent the low-frequency-but-confusing "my saved panel position is gone after a window resize" class of bug from regressing silently.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` passes with new file present; coverage report shows new module at >90%; manually drag panel near viewport edge, resize window smaller, reopen, confirm panel re-clamps in.

title: Test gap: readPipeDelimited has no unit coverage
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The PR extracts `function* readPipeDelimited(path: string): Iterable<string[]>` to dedupe the existsSync→readFileSync→split-lines→split-on-`|` boilerplate previously duplicated in `parseGcvsMain` and `parseGcvsCrossref`. The helper has zero unit tests despite being the new shared seam between the two GCVS parsers. The pure parts of its behaviour (skip empty/whitespace lines, split on `|`, trim each cell, missing-file → empty iteration) are exactly the kind of input-shape contract that the rest of catalog-pure.test.ts pins down with vitest cases (cf. `normalizeGcvsName`, `parseGcvsNumber`).

**Location**
scripts/build-catalog.ts:226-233 (helper definition); scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts (no covering describe block)

**Suggested approach**
Move the trim/split/skip-empty logic into a pure helper in `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` (e.g. `splitPipeDelimited(text: string): string[][]` or a generator that takes a string), keep the file-IO shell (`existsSync` + `readFileSync`) in build-catalog.ts wrapping it, and add a vitest describe block covering: blank lines skipped, whitespace-only lines skipped, trailing whitespace inside cells trimmed, CRLF line endings, empty input, single-line input. This mirrors the existing pure/impure split where build-catalog hosts file IO and catalog-pure hosts the deterministic transforms with tests.

**Why**
The pipe-reader is the new single point of failure for both GCVS parses. A regression in cell-trimming or line-skipping would silently corrupt the variability cross-match (period/amplitude=0 for affected stars). Pin the contract with tests before any future caller adds further format quirks.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` passes new describe block; `npm run build:catalog` still reports the same 11316 Hip + 13926 HD GCVS xrefs and 3677 variables matched as the PR's test plan.

title: Reserved flag bits documented in comment but no test asserts they stay reserved
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
catalog-pure.ts:323-324 lists `0x08, 0x20, 0x40, 0x80` as 'reserved bits available without a binary-version bump.' This is prose. If a future commit adds `FLAG_FOO = 0x08` and forgets to update that comment, no automated check catches the staleness. The new `distinct powers of two` test would still pass (0x08 is single-bit and distinct from the others).

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:322-328

**Suggested approach**
After consolidating flags into a registry (see sibling bead 9mm.74), add a complementary assertion: `expect(usedFlagBits & RESERVED_FLAG_BITS).toBe(0)` where `RESERVED_FLAG_BITS = 0x08 | 0x20 | 0x40 | 0x80` is exported as a named const next to the `FLAGS` registry. Forces any reservation change to be a deliberate edit at one site rather than a silent drift between code and comment.

**Why**
The 'available bits' comment is a load-bearing piece of project knowledge — the PR description and `docs/build-and-data.md` both reference it as the path to add functionality without a binary version bump. If it drifts, future Claude / Alex sessions will believe a bit is free when it's already used.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` after the change. Add a fake `FLAG_FOO = 0x08` and verify the reservation test fails.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Headline render walks DOM by index instead of using captured span refs
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
`renderPanel()` updates the headline via DOM index gymnastics: `headlineEl.childNodes[0].nodeValue = ...`, `headlineEl.children[0].firstChild!.nodeValue = ...`, `headlineEl.children[1].firstChild!.nodeValue = ...`. This mixes `.children` (element-only, indexes `lowSpan` and `gpuSpan`) with `.childNodes` (all nodes, includes text nodes). The build path already created typed locals `lowSpan` and `gpuSpan` but they're discarded — only `headlineEl` is retained. A future change that adds another text node or reorders the headline shape silently scrambles the writes (the `!` suppresses the type warning).

**Location**
src/client/perf-hud.ts:149-156 (build creates `lowSpan`, `gpuSpan` as locals), :277-281 (renderPanel walks by index)

**Suggested approach**
Lift `lowSpan` and `gpuSpan` to module-level refs (typed `HTMLSpanElement | null`) alongside `headlineEl`. Set them in build, null them in dispose, write to them by name in render. Better still, also keep a ref to the leading text node (currently `childNodes[0]`) — `document.createTextNode('')` returns a Text and TS will type it. Then `renderPanel` becomes three named writes against typed nodes with no `!` and no `.children` / `.childNodes` interleave.

**Why**
Same elegance issue as the row pool — the build path already chose to keep typed handles for the rows (`{ line, label, values }`), so the headline should follow suit. Removes the stale comment "(3 children: textNode, lowSpan, textNode, gpuSpan)" which under-counts (4 children).

**Smoke step**
`npm run typecheck && npm test`. Manual: `debug.panel()`, confirm "FPS NN low NN gpu N.NNms" still renders and updates.

title: Flag-bit regression test hand-lists FLAG_* — adding bit needs test edit
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The new test `flag bits are distinct powers of two` (catalog-pure.test.ts:677–684) hardcodes `[FLAG_HAS_NAME, FLAG_IS_SOL, FLAG_HAS_BAYER, FLAG_BINARY_PRIMARY]`. Adding a 5th flag (FLAG_PLANET_HOST, FLAG_HAS_EXOPLANET, etc.) silently gains zero coverage — the new bit isn't in the array, so distinctness/single-bit-ness isn't checked. There's no central `FLAGS = { hasName: 0x01, ... } as const` that the test could iterate.

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:325-328 (FLAG_* exports as separate consts); catalog-pure.test.ts:677-684 (hand-listed array)

**Suggested approach**
Group the flags into a single `FLAGS = { ... } as const` object (or an exported `FLAG_REGISTRY` array) and re-export the individual `FLAG_HAS_NAME` etc. as named aliases for callsite readability. The test then walks `Object.values(FLAGS)` so any new flag is automatically pinned to be a single bit and distinct from the others. Bonus: the registry is the natural place to assert against the 'reserved bits' comment (`0x08, 0x20, 0x40, 0x80` should not appear as values).

**Why**
The PR's thesis is 'adding a bit means adding a name here, not sprinkling another magic number' — same logic should apply to the test. Right now adding a flag means TWO edits (the new const + the test array) and skipping the second one passes CI silently.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — add a deliberate `FLAG_FAKE = 0x10` colliding with FLAG_BINARY_PRIMARY and verify the iteration-based test catches it.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Drop dead rowsContainer / histoBarsContainer module refs in perf-hud
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
PR #41 introduced module-level `rowsContainer` and `histoBarsContainer` nullable refs but they're only ever read in the null-guard at the top of `renderPanel` (`if (!panelEl || !headlineEl || !rowsContainer || !histoBarsContainer) return;`). They're never dereferenced thereafter — the per-tick writes go through `rowPool[i].line` and `histoBars[i]` directly. The container element refs add bookkeeping noise, an extra null-init/null-out path in dispose, and tempt readers into thinking they participate in render.

**Location**
src/client/perf-hud.ts:49 (`let rowsContainer`), :51 (`let histoBarsContainer`), :194 (assignment), :221 (assignment), :237-238 (nulled in dispose), :267 (only read site)

**Suggested approach**
Drop both let-bindings. Build still appends `rowsParent` / `histo` to `div` directly (already does); no other site needs the container handles. Remove from the dispose null-out and from the renderPanel null-guard. The `panelEl` and `headlineEl` guards are sufficient — if the panel exists, its child containers exist.

**Why**
DRY-ish — the guard duplicates the `panelEl` non-null check in two redundant ways. Removing the dead refs makes the section's lifecycle (build → render → dispose) clearer: only `panelEl`, `headlineEl`, `rowPool[]`, `histoBars[]` matter.

**Smoke step**
`npm run typecheck && npm test`. Manual: `debug.panel()` in dev console, expand Perf section, confirm histogram + rows still render and update; collapse + re-expand still works.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: No test pins inline byte-plan comment to RECORD_LAYOUT consts
description:
**Confidence: Medium**

**Finding**
`scripts/catalog-pure.ts` lines 219–253 contain a 35-line ASCII-art comment block describing every header + record byte ("32 spectClass uint8", "36 ampUnits uint8 GCVS amplitude in 0.05 mag units", etc.) right above the `HEADER_LAYOUT` / `RECORD_LAYOUT` consts. The comment is the human-readable spec, the consts are the machine-readable spec. There's no automated check that they agree. If `RECORD_LAYOUT.hip` moves to byte 36 but the comment still says `40..43 hip`, future readers get a wrong story — and the regression-prevention block in the test file can't catch it because it doesn't read the comment.

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.ts:219-253 (block comment); catalog-pure.test.ts:620 (block where pin would go)

**Suggested approach**
Either (a) drop the redundant comment and rely on `RECORD_LAYOUT` + per-field inline comments as the spec, or (b) add a regression-prevention test that does a regex/parse pass over the block comment, extracts `<lo>..<hi> <name>` triples, and asserts each matches `RECORD_LAYOUT[name]` and the implied size. Option (a) is cleaner — the inline `// uint8 (×0.05 mag)` annotations next to each layout entry already convey the same information.

**Why**
The PR rightly centralised offsets but left a parallel English description of those offsets that can drift independently. Either delete the duplication or pin it.

**Smoke step**
`npm test` after the change. If pinning is chosen, deliberately edit one line of the comment to wrong byte range and verify the new test fails.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Test sizes table parallels RECORD_LAYOUT — drift not caught
description:
**Confidence: High**

**Finding**
The new `binary-format constants` regression block in `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts` validates non-overlap by walking `RECORD_LAYOUT` entries against a hand-coded `sizes: Record<string, number>` map (4 for floats, 1 for uint8, 2 for uint16, 4 for uint32). That map is a second copy of per-field type info that already lives in inline comments next to `RECORD_LAYOUT` (`// float32`, `// uint8`, etc.) — not in a machine-readable form. If a field is added to `RECORD_LAYOUT` and the test `sizes` map is forgotten, `expect(sizes[name]).toBeDefined()` fails — loud, fine. But if the wrong size is supplied (e.g. a new `uint16` mistakenly added as 4 in the test), the non-overlap check passes against an inconsistent type, which is a parallel-implementation footgun. Same shape duplicated across the header test (line 624–626) and record test (line 645–650).

**Location**
scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts:624-650

**Suggested approach**
Promote the layout shape from `{name: offset}` to `{name: {offset, kind}}` where `kind` is one of `'u8' | 'u16' | 'u32' | 'f32' | 'ascii4'` (or a numeric byte size). Keep `RECORD_LAYOUT.x` etc. accessor aliases (or a derived const) so writer/reader sites read unchanged. The test then walks the same shape to derive sizes — single source of truth for both layout and pin-test. Alternatively, expose a `recordFieldSize(name)` helper from catalog-pure that the test imports.

**Why**
The PR's whole thesis is 'no inline byte literals across writer/reader/verify' — but the regression test reintroduces the duplication on the type-size axis. Promoting kind into the layout const is a one-edit change for both directions (add field, get test coverage automatically).

**Smoke step**
`npm test` — existing 65 tests stay green; intentionally introduce a 2-byte field with size 4 in the test map and verify the non-overlap test now flags the type mismatch.
close_reason: Closed via PR #58 (9mm code-quality: low-hanging fruit) — merged 2026-05-11

title: Extract DustParticleLayer subsystem (particleMesh + material + dispose + attach helpers)
description:
Sibling refactor to stellata-9mm.43 (StarPipeline extraction). Independent — different file, different concerns, no shared blockers.

# Why

The dust-particle layer in stellata.ts owns its own resource cluster:
- `particleMesh: THREE.Mesh | null`
- `particleMaterial: THREE.ShaderMaterial | null`
- `disposeParticles({ removeFromScene })` helper
- `attachDustParticles(...)` constructor
- `setParticleStrength(v)` runtime knob
- Dust uniforms wired into the material at attach time

These were made individually disposable in PR #30 (stellata-9mm.35 disposeParticles helper, stellata-9mm.38 DustField.dispose), but the encapsulation itself is still inlined in Stellata. Same architectural smell as the disc/glow/coreMask cluster covered by 9mm.43 — a sub-system without a dispose() contract of its own.

Note: the dust-particle layer is **shelved for v1.0** per CLAUDE.md (rendering disabled by default; uParticleStrength = 0). Decoupling it now keeps the re-enable path simple and prevents the shelved code from accumulating tangle as Stellata.ts evolves.

# Scope

- New `src/client/dust-particle-layer.ts` class owning `{ mesh, material, scene-attach, dispose }` plus the existing setParticleStrength / setParticleSize debug knobs.
- Stellata holds it as one optional field; the existing inline particleMesh / particleMaterial / disposeParticles / attachDustParticles / setParticleStrength move into the class.
- DustField (the per-star dust raymarch CPU helper) stays where it is — that's a separate subsystem already covered by stellata-9mm.38.

# Smoke

- typecheck + vitest pass.
- Manual: temporarily set uParticleStrength > 0 in the debug HUD (window.debug...), confirm particle layer attaches and renders. Toggle back to 0; confirm it disappears. Reload page; confirm shelved-default (strength=0) state still works.

title: Migrate strict-equivalent camera-busy sites in stellata.ts to isCameraBusy()
description:
Follow-up implementation bead for the audit landed in stellata-9mm.59. Depends on that bead's per-site annotations + decision matrix.

# Scope

For each site flagged `// equivalent: isCameraBusy()` by the audit:
- Replace the inline `(this.warpState || this.aimState || ...)` predicate with `this.isCameraBusy()`.
- Drop the site's local annotation now that the call is self-documenting.

Sites flagged `// narrower: <reason>` are left as-is. The audit's reason annotation stays on those sites permanently.

Risk: per the audit, each surviving narrower site has a deliberate reason. The migration only touches strict-equivalent sites — but a strict-equivalent flag in the audit is itself a judgment call, so a per-site smoke pass is required (don't trust the audit blindly).

# Smoke

Per-site browser smoke for each migrated entry point. The user-visible interactions to exercise depend on which sites the audit identifies; expected coverage:
- warp interrupt during aim (start a warp, then click another star — should not steal focus mid-warp)
- aim interrupt during warp finish (click during the warp's slowdown phase)
- observe enter/exit transition click handling (click during the navigate→observe lerp and reverse)
- pickStar / pickCloud / aimAt / setCameraMode / focusStar / onPointerUp — whichever the audit migrates.

# Acceptance

- All sites flagged 'equivalent' in stellata-9mm.59 now call `isCameraBusy()`.
- typecheck + vitest pass.
- Browser smoke for every migrated site.

title: Retire legacy URL-state schema decoders
description:
The URL-state encoder in src/client/url-state.ts ships a frozen FieldSpec table and decoder for every prior schema version, plus dispatch in decodeBlob. Each version costs maintenance overhead (~80–100 lines of code, plus regression tests) to keep legacy share URLs and bookmarks decoding correctly.

Eventually those legacy URLs will have aged out of circulation enough that the cost stops being worth it. At that point we should drop the older FieldSpec tables, their decoders, the version dispatch branches in decodeBlob, the SCHEMA_VERSION_V<n> constants for retired versions, and the corresponding backward-compat test blocks in url-state.test.ts.

Not urgent — gated on circulation timeline. The auto-upgrade rewrite path in applyFromUrl means every load of an old URL silently rewrites the address bar to the current schema, so the legacy decoders are mostly serving cold paths (someone opening a bookmark for the first time in months/years).
design:
At pickup time, decide:

1. Which version(s) to retire. Look at the gap between the oldest still-supported version and the current SCHEMA_VERSION. A reasonable heuristic: only keep N-1 (one version back) so the auto-upgrade rewrite still has a fallback for users who haven't loaded the site since the most recent schema bump.

2. Whether to drop the corresponding backward-compat test block. Keeping the manually-built blob tests for retired versions has no cost beyond the test file size, but it also serves no purpose once the decoder is gone.

3. Any URL-state-adjacent constants or tag-bit aliases that exist only for retired versions (e.g. FOCUS_HIP_TAG vs FOCUS_HIP_TAG_V2 — the V2 alias has continued existence even though v2's id space matches v3's).

Touch points:
- src/client/url-state.ts — FIELDS_V<n>, decodeV<n>, SCHEMA_VERSION_V<n>, dispatch in decodeBlob
- src/client/url-state.test.ts — "v<n> backward compatibility" describe block
- docs/architecture.md — the "wire formats coexist" paragraph
acceptance_criteria:
- Retired version's FIELDS table, decoder function, and SCHEMA_VERSION constant are removed from url-state.ts.
- decodeBlob's dispatch no longer references the retired version; throws "Unsupported view version" for it as it would for any unknown.
- Backward-compat test block for the retired version is removed from url-state.test.ts.
- docs/architecture.md "wire formats coexist" paragraph updated to reflect remaining supported versions.
- npm test + npm run typecheck still pass.

title: MC: HII emission overlay near hot embedded stars
description:
**Goal:** faint emission/scattering term near embedded stars hot enough to produce visible HII regions — a red/pink tinge fading with distance from the host. Threshold from **stellata-c7u.1**. Spec context in parent **stellata-c7u.5**.

### Scope
- Threshold: typically embedded stars ≥ early B (per c7u.1 research).
- Per qualifying embedded star: a faint emission term in the cloud-rendering layer that adds (not modulates) a pink/red contribution near the star, fading with `r/r_HII`.
- Cloud-type variation: quiescent dark cloud (no/few hot embeddings) renders unchanged; active SF region (multiple hot embeddings) acquires the emission term.
- Reflection nebulosity remains explicitly **deferred** to **stellata-9hp** which covers RNe as discrete blob objects.

### Files
- `src/client/shaders/cloud.frag.glsl` (or successor shader landed by `stellata-c7u.6`) — emission contribution.
- Possibly `src/client/molecular-clouds.ts` — uniform plumbing for the per-cavity emission descriptor.

### Acceptance / visual gate
- Orion A around the Trapezium reads with subtle red/pink emission overlay.
- Quiescent clouds (Taurus, Chamaeleon) are unaffected.
- Emission never competes with the physical extinction signal — it stays clearly secondary, matching the c7u-epic constraint that physical signals dominate.

### Depends on
**stellata-c7u.5.1** (cavity descriptors define where HII regions live).

title: MC: embedded young-star cavities + density modulation
description:
**Goal:** model cavity-carving by embedded O/B + YSO stars inside Zucker clouds. Density modulation only — no emission yet. Spec context in parent **stellata-c7u.5**.

### Cross-match (build time)
- Identify O/B stars and known YSOs whose 3D positions fall inside any Zucker cloud bbox.
- Use ATHYG spectral types for O/B identification.
- Per cloud, list embedded hot stars: position, spectral type, luminosity proxy.
- Output: per-cloud cavity descriptor in the dust manifest or a new cloud JSON field.

### Density modulation
- For each embedded hot star, reduce local density inside a radius scaled by stellar luminosity (Strömgren-sphere-style; constants from **stellata-c7u.1**).
- Soft falloff at the cavity edge — `density × (1 − cavity_strength × falloff(r/r_cav))`.
- Bake into the same chunked 3D-texture pipeline that **stellata-c7u.2** populates with Zucker densities (single source of truth, no double-counting).

### Acceptance / visual gate
- Orion A (multiple O stars) renders visibly differently from quiescent dark cores like Taurus — Orion's density feels "hollowed".
- Per-star extinction `star.vert.glsl` automatically picks up the modulation from the existing voxel-volume raymarch (no shader change required).
- Cavity edges read as soft, not stepped.

### Files
- `scripts/build-clouds.py` — emit cavity descriptors per cloud.
- `scripts/build-dust.py` — apply cavity modulation when overlaying Zucker into the voxel volume.

### Out of scope
- HII emission overlay — sibling **stellata-c7u.5.2**.
- Reflection nebulosity — deferred to **stellata-9hp** (already covers RNe as discrete blob objects).

### Depends on
**stellata-c7u.2** (data pipeline) + **stellata-c7u.4** (substructure landscape into which cavities are carved).

title: Search index: GJ / Gliese + CCDM letters + Greek-letter abbreviations
description:
**Goal:** broader coverage of alternate-designation classes after V-numbers land in **stellata-nxb.1**. Spec context in parent **stellata-nxb**.

### Scope (in priority order, each independently shippable)
- **GJ / Gliese** — common in nearby-stars literature. Source: AT-HYG `gl` field is already in the binary; just thread it into `search-index.json` if not already.
- **Multi-component CCDM letters** (Alpha Centauri C, A, B). Coordinate with the binary/multiple-star work in **stellata-dch** — the override-list output there may inform CCDM-letter resolution.
- **Greek-letter abbreviations** (Alf Cen C alongside Alpha Centauri C) if not already handled by nxb.1's audit.

### Out of scope (per parent)
LFT, LHS, LPM, LTT, NLTT — esoteric proper-motion catalogues; low search value.

### Files
- `scripts/build-catalog.ts` / `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` — extend search-index entry generation.
- Tests in `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts`.

### Acceptance
- "GJ 551" and "Gliese 551" both resolve to Proxima Centauri.
- "Alpha Centauri C" and "Alf Cen C" both resolve.
- Multi-component CCDM letters surface the right star.
- No regression on typeahead behaviour.

### Depends on
**stellata-nxb.1** (audit + V-numbers establish the pattern).
### Related
**stellata-dch.3** (CCDM-letter overrides may share pipeline).

title: Variable pulsation: implement chosen fix path (a/b/c)
description:
**Goal:** implement the option (a / b / c) chosen by sibling **stellata-fe4.1**. Spec context in parent **stellata-fe4**.

### Implementation depends on chosen option
- **(a)** Cap-multiplier table — touch `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` (per-type multiplier) + `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` (apply cap to `radiusFactor`). One-line per type.
- **(b)** R+T amplitude split — extend the variable-star uniform pipeline with a per-frame T-shift uniform; touch `star.vert.glsl` + `star.frag.glsl` (colour/temperature output). Per-type amplitudes live in `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` and SCIENCE.md.
- **(c)** No code change; close this issue with rationale, leave a brief note in SCIENCE.md / CLAUDE.md.

### Doc updates
- SCIENCE.md — variable-pulsation section: cite the interferometric source (Lacour 2009 / Woodruff 2009 / Wittkowski 2016) and document the chosen amplitude split.
- CLAUDE.md — update or remove the "Things deliberately kept out" bullet about variable-star T-swing.

### Acceptance
- For (a)/(b): Mira full-amplitude swing on screen reads as believable against the published interferometric gap.
- For (c): bullet in CLAUDE.md updated with reasoning + interferometric citation.
- No regression on Cepheid / RR Lyrae / eclipsing pulsation behaviour (unless option (b) intentionally re-tunes them).

### Depends on
**stellata-fe4.1** (research conclusion drives the implementation).

title: Variable pulsation: quantify Mira R-swing vs interferometry (research)
description:
**Goal:** decide whether the constant-T `R ∝ √L` pulsation model is physically defensible at high amplitude. Spec context in parent **stellata-fe4**.

### Method
- For Mira (the type-defining example) and a few other high-amplitude AAVSO archetypes — chi Cyg, R Hya, R Leo — compute the on-screen angular-diameter ratio between minimum and maximum light under the current `R ∝ √L` assumption used in `src/client/shaders/star.{vert,frag}.glsl` (`R_eff = R · radiusFactor`).
- Compare to **interferometric measurements** of the actual physical radius swing:
  - Lacour 2009 (CHARA Mira disc).
  - Woodruff 2009 (VLTI Mira).
  - Wittkowski 2016 (VLTI Mira-class long-period variables).
  - Other CHARA/VLTI/IOTA Mira papers from the ~2008–2020 window.
- Quantify the gap: ratio of modelled R-swing to measured R-swing per archetype.

### Decision among options
- **(a)** Cap R-swing per variable type (simple multiplier table). Brightness amplitude on V-band then under-served. Cheapest.
- **(b)** Split amplitude: per-type fixed fraction of luminosity change goes to R, rest encoded as colour/temperature shift in the disc + glow shader (the spectral colour uniform path is already there; extending to per-frame T-shift is contained).
- **(c)** Keep current model, document limitation in SCIENCE.md as a known stylistic choice.

### Decide
- Whether option choice differs by GCVS variable type (Mira, Cepheid, RR Lyrae, eclipsing). Cost of (b) goes up if type-specific.
- Whether the fix is worth the complexity given that variable pulsation is an aesthetic-correctness concern, not a critical-path bug.

### Deliverable
Quantified discrepancy + chosen option (a/b/c) recorded in this issue's notes. Drives sibling **stellata-fe4.2** (implementation) — if (c), close fe4.2 with the rationale and update the CLAUDE.md "kept out" bullet to cite this research.

title: Warp arrival projection drift / pin-snap mitigation
description:
**Goal:** address Effect B from parent **stellata-a7d.2.9** — float32 projection drift then pin snap visible at warp arrival. Only ship this if the drift is still perceptible after the flyby fix in sibling **a7d.2.9.1**.

### Effect to address
During warp the floating origin sits on the source star, so the destination's worldPos magnitude is ~|AB| (e.g. ~265 pc for Sol→Rigel). GPU float32 precision at that magnitude is ~|AB|·1e-7 ≈ 2.6×10⁻⁵ pc — comparable to endOffset (~3.4×10⁻⁵ pc for Rigel). The projected centre can drift a noticeable fraction of a screen in final approach. The pin (`uPinFocusToCenter`) does NOT engage during warp, so the drift is visible. At `finishWarp`, `recenterOrigin` moves origin to the dest → projection precision becomes essentially exact → pin engages → star snaps to mathematical NDC position.

### Mitigation
- Generalise `uPinFocusToCenter` to non-origin pin targets.
- Pin the destination star to its `lookAt`-projected position during the final phase of warp (requires passing target NDC or world position as a uniform).

### Files
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — `updateWarp` final-phase pin engagement.
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — generalised pin uniform.

### Acceptance
- Warp arrival has B's disc grow at perceptibly uniform angular rate; no pop at parking.
- No regression on short warps where the prior arrival already felt fine.

### Depends on
**stellata-a7d.2.9.1** (only do this if the flyby fix doesn't already eliminate the drift visually).

title: Warp flyby curve: arc fillet (or clothoid) at source-side pop
description:
**Goal:** replace warp's in-line departure with a curve that takes the camera *off* the A↔B line so the user perceives a flyby of the source star A, not a teleport through it. Spec context in parent **stellata-a7d.2.9**.

### Approach
- Smooth join between two line segments at A:
  - Start tangent: camera's current direction (or current radial-from-A — depends on launch pose).
  - End tangent: +forward along A→B.
- Implement as **arc fillet** for C¹ continuity at A. If centripetal jerk at the waypoint is perceptible, upgrade to **clothoid** (Euler spiral, linearly varying curvature) for C² continuity.
- Arc-length parametrise the warp position-time profile so the angular size of A grows smoothly as the camera curves past — no perceived teleport.

### Reference: failed prior attempt preserved on archive branch
`archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease` (pushed to origin). Single commit on top of a7d.2 worktree HEAD at archival.
```
git fetch origin
git log archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease -1
git diff archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease~1 archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease -- src/client/stellata.ts
```
Don't reuse the in-line angular-ease shape — it treats A as a point on the warp axis the camera passes through, which is the wrong mental model.

### Files
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — `startWarp`, `updateWarp`, `finishWarp`.

### Acceptance
- Warp start reads as a continuous flyby of the source star — no perceived teleport across A.
- A's apparent size grows smoothly as the camera curves past.
- C¹ continuity at the waypoint(s); C² preferred if centripetal jerk is otherwise perceptible.
- No regression on short warps (Sol → α Cen) where the existing profile already feels fine.

### Out of scope
- Effect B: float32 projection drift / pin-snap on arrival — sibling **stellata-a7d.2.9.2**. Re-evaluate after this lands; the flyby reframe may already eliminate it.
- Destination-side ease — keep the existing 1/d_B-linear angular-ease arrival.

title: Wander: runtime state in stellata.ts (G key + updateWander + recenter)
description:
**Goal:** runtime wander mode integrated into the existing stellata.ts state machine. Spec in parent **stellata-8r1**.

### Scope
- Add `'wander'` mode alongside warp. Same disable pattern: `controls.enabled = false`, `body.classList.toggle('wandering', true)`, `pointerup` short-circuited, URL writer skips camera-hash updates while active.
- Entry: keyboard shortcut `G` (verify free; W is warp; Esc/Space skip warp). Exit: any `pointerdown`, `Esc`, or `G` again.
- Focus interaction: activating wander **unfocuses** any current focal star (`setFocus(null)`), recentring origin to Sol — clean absolute frame for the duration.
- Particle state: `_wanderActive`, `_wanderVelocity`, `_wanderDistanceSinceRecenter`.
- Per-frame `updateWander(dt)` per parent's algorithm (sample, accel, integrate, clamp, step, recenter every ~1 pc).
- Initial kick: field-scaled with floor — `v0 = max(KICK_FLOOR, KICK_TIME × |g| × GRAVITY_SCALE)`. Defensive guard for degenerate `target == position` direction.

### Floating-origin contract — CRITICAL
After unfocus on entry `worldOffset = (0,0,0)`. Once `recenterOrigin` fires, **all gradient samples must be at `camera.position + worldOffset`**, never `camera.position` alone. Same for lookahead and any read-back. Call this out in code comments around every sampling site.

### Tuning levers (define as named constants at top of wander section)
- `WANDER_SOFTENING_PC = 2.0` — **build-baked**, must match `build-gravity.ts`. Comment.
- `WANDER_MASS_EXPONENT = 0.5` — **build-baked**, must match.
- `WANDER_GRAVITY_SCALE = 0.1` — runtime
- `WANDER_VELOCITY_DAMPING = 0.99` per-frame at 60 fps; applied as `pow(damping, dt × 60)` for frame-rate independence
- `WANDER_MAX_SPEED_PC_PER_S = 20.0`
- `WANDER_SIM_TIME_SCALE = 5.0`
- `WANDER_LOOKAHEAD_PC = 2.0`
- `WANDER_KICK_TIME_S = 2.0`
- `WANDER_KICK_FLOOR_PC_PER_S = 1.0`

### Files
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — wander state + per-frame update + key handler + exit handling.
- `src/client/main.ts` — pass loader to `Stellata`.
- `src/client/styles.css` — optional `body.wandering` style (cursor change / subtle vignette).
- `CLAUDE.md` (post-landing): extend floating-origin section with the absolute-frame sample contract; add a "Wander mode" section.

### Acceptance / smoke
- G enters wander; pointerdown / Esc / G exits.
- URL writer does not serialise intermediate poses while wandering.
- Camera drifts plausibly through dense regions; voids still produce motion via the kick floor.
- No visible jitter at recentre boundaries.

### Depends on
**stellata-8r1.2** (loader).

title: Wander: src/client/gravity-loader.ts (parse + trilinear sampleGradient)
description:
**Goal:** runtime loader for `public/gravity.bin` per the spec in parent **stellata-8r1**.

### Scope
- New `src/client/gravity-loader.ts`, mirroring `catalog-loader.ts` shape.
- Fetch `gravity.bin`, validate magic ("GRV1") + version. Hard fail on mismatch.
- Expose `gridN`, `halfExtent`, `softening`, `massExponent`, `gradients: Float32Array` (length `N³ × 3`).
- Export `sampleGradient(absolutePos: THREE.Vector3): THREE.Vector3` — trilinear interpolation.
  - Return zero vector for positions outside the bounding sphere.
  - Index clamping: `floor + 1` must not read past `N − 1`. Test fractional voxels at the cube edge.
- Wire parallel-load in `main.ts` alongside `catalog.bin` and `search-index.json`.

### Tests (vitest)
- Synthetic GRV1 file fixture: validate header parsing.
- Sample at grid-aligned positions returns the stored gradient.
- Sample at a position halfway between two voxels returns the average.
- Sample at the corner voxel does not throw or read past the array end.
- Sample at a position outside the bounding sphere returns zero.

### Depends on
**stellata-8r1.1** (binary format + file must exist).

### Out of scope
Runtime wander state machine; that's the next sibling.

title: Wander: scripts/build-gravity.ts precompute (gravity.bin + isUpToDate)
description:
**Goal:** offline precompute that emits `public/gravity.bin` per the spec in parent **stellata-8r1**.

### Scope
- New `scripts/build-gravity.ts`. Reads star positions from `catalog.bin` (inline a small reader; no shared module).
- Bounding volume: cube of side `2R` centred at origin where `R = max distance from Sol`. Voxel side ≈ `2R / 128` (128³ grid → ~25 MB).
- Mass proxy: `luminosity = 10^((4.74 − absmag)/2.5)`; `mass = max(0, luminosity^MASS_EXPONENT)`. Default `MASS_EXPONENT = 0.5`.
- Gradient computation per voxel per star with `SOFTENING²` regularisation; vectors NOT normalised.
- Run in `worker_threads`, chunked one Z-slice per task. Target <5 min on a modern laptop. Optional pre-cull beyond ~500 pc for speed; measure before adding.

### Output binary format (32-byte header + N³ × 12 bytes)
- `0–3`: ASCII "GRV1"
- `4–7`: uint32 grid dimension N (128)
- `8–11`: float32 grid half-extent in parsecs (= R)
- `12–15`: float32 softening
- `16–19`: float32 mass exponent
- `20–31`: reserved (zeros)
- Body: float32 x, y, z gradient per voxel, index `x + y*N + z*N*N` (x varies fastest).

### Build wiring
- Add `build:gravity` to `package.json`, chained after `build:catalog` in both `dev` and `build`.
- Use the same `isUpToDate` pattern as `build-catalog.ts`, gated on `catalog.bin` mtime + script mtime + build-baked constants.
- Add `/public/gravity.bin` to `.gitignore`.

### Build-baked constants (must match runtime sampler)
- `WANDER_SOFTENING_PC = 2.0`
- `WANDER_MASS_EXPONENT = 0.5`
- `GRID_DIM = 128`

### Acceptance
- `npm run build:gravity` produces `public/gravity.bin`.
- Re-run is idempotent (no-op when up to date).
- Header round-trips: magic = "GRV1", dimension = 128, half-extent ≈ catalog max-distance.
- Spot-check: a voxel within ~5 pc of Sol has nonzero gradient pointing roughly toward the local stellar density centre.

title: Per-planet empirical phase curves (Mallama 2018) replacing Lambertian default
description:
**Goal:** swap the v1 Lambertian phase function `φ(α) = (sin α + (π−α)·cos α)/π` for per-planet empirical phase curves. Mallama et al. 2018 publishes V-band phase coefficients for each major planet, including Saturn's ring contribution and Venus's phase asymmetry.

# Why

Lambertian over-darkens at large phase angle for atmospheric planets — Venus is brighter at large α than Lambert predicts because of forward scattering through its atmosphere. Saturn at all phases benefits from including the ring system contribution to total brightness. For Mercury and Mars, Lambert is an adequate approximation, but the empirical curves are still more correct.

# Source

Mallama, Krobusek, Pavlov 2018 — "Comprehensive wide-band magnitudes and albedos for the planets, with applications to exo-planets and Planet Nine," *Icarus* 282 (2017) 19–33. DOI 10.1016/j.icarus.2016.09.023.

Per-planet empirical fit forms vary; coefficients are tabulated for each planet. Implementation likely adds `phaseFunctionCoefficients` to `Planet` and a per-planet `phaseFunction(α)` method, defaulting to Lambert for hosts that don't supply coefficients (which means: every exoplanet under stellata-bk5).

# Files likely touched

- `src/client/planet-system.ts` — add coefficients to relevant SOL_PLANETS entries; default Lambert when absent.
- `src/client/shaders/planet.vert.glsl` (or the perceptual-disc chunk) — branch on a per-instance flag, or use a series form that collapses to Lambert when coefficients are zero.

# Priority

P3. Visual refinement, not a correctness gate. Lands after the v1 Lambert phase function (stellata-3re.17) ships.
