notes: PR: https://github.com/alexmensch/stellata/pull/56 (open, awaiting manual smoke + merge). Bead stays in_progress until merge — close with bd close after the PR lands.

title: Document and rescue silently-dropped famous stars (KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS pattern)
description:
**Goal:** rescue famous stars silently dropped by the AT-HYG → catalog-binary build, and document the rescue pattern for future cases.

# The trap

`scripts/build-catalog.ts` drops any AT-HYG row with empty `x0/y0/z0/plx` columns *without warning*. AT-HYG v3.3 has several famous stars in this state — the row exists but the 3D position fields are blank, so the star never reaches the renderer or the search index.

`KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS` in `scripts/build-catalog.ts` is **not** a rescue list. It's only consulted when a Stellarium constellation-line endpoint can't be resolved — to suppress the warning. Arbitrary missing famous stars don't surface there; the build still succeeds cleanly.

# Known cases

- HIP 5165  — alpha Phe / Ankaa (in `KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS`)
- HIP 89341 — mu Sgr / Polis (in `KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS`)
- HIP 107259 — mu Cep / Herschel's Garnet Star (NOT in the list — currently silently dropped, action item below)

# Investigation pattern (when a famous star is reported missing)

1. Grep `data/athyg_33_classic_ids.csv` for the HIP. Empty `x/y/z` cells → AT-HYG lacks parallax for that star.
2. Don't conflate "missing from render" with "missing from search" — the same CSV feeds both.
3. Rescue options:
   - Patch a synthetic row at build time using a hand-measured parallax (Gaia DR3, Hipparcos re-reduction).
   - Switch data sources for that subset of stars.
4. Document the rescue source + retrieval date in SCIENCE.md per the `frozen-external-data` convention.

# Tasks

- Rescue HIP 107259 (mu Cep / Herschel's Garnet Star).
- Audit AT-HYG for other named stars with empty x/y/z columns; either add to `KNOWN_MISSING_HIPS` (if it's an expected-missing constellation-line endpoint) or rescue (if it's a user-facing famous star).
- Optional: surface a build-time warning when an AT-HYG row with a non-empty `bayer`/`flamsteed`/`proper` field is dropped due to empty x/y/z, so future cases don't go silent.

# Out of scope

- Re-pulling AT-HYG itself. Per `frozen-external-data` the source CSV is committed and refresh is a manual one-shot, not a build dependency.

title: Phase-angle planet illumination (φ(α)) — proper reflected-light geometry
description:
**Goal:** replace the v1 full-illumination assumption (`φ = 1`) in planet apparent-magnitude with a proper phase function. A planet's brightness should depend on its phase angle relative to the viewer.

# Why

Currently, Jupiter on the far side of the Sun from camera = full phase = bright; Jupiter between camera and Sun = ~zero illuminated face = ~zero brightness. The v1 formula treats both the same. Visually wrong, especially obvious from inside the solar system or from a nearby star where geometry matters.

# Implementation sketch

Phase angle `α = ∠(viewer–planet–host)`.

For a Lambertian sphere:
```
φ(α) = (sin α + (π − α) · cos α) / π
```

For more realistic planetary phase curves, use the empirical `H, G` magnitude system or per-planet phase coefficients (Mallama et al. 2018 give published phase curves for all major planets — including Saturn's ring contribution, Venus's phase asymmetry).

Substitute into the apparent-magnitude formula from the parent v1 bead:
```
m_planet = m_host − 2.5 · log10( p · φ(α) · (R / d_host_planet)² · (R / d_viewer_planet)² )
```

# Affects

- Apparent-magnitude visibility cutoff for planets (parent bead, in spirit) — phase function tightens / loosens visibility geometrically.
- chart-mode planets (stellata-m40.3) — chart-mode rendering should also respect phase brightness so symbology / disc-size / opacity matches what's visible.

# Files likely touched

- `src/client/planet-system.ts` — add `phaseFunction(α)` per planet (default Lambert; override per-planet for empirical).
- Wherever apparent magnitude is computed (the new planet pipeline) — pipe in viewer position to compute `α`.

# Priority

P3. Visual refinement, not a correctness gate. Ship after the v1 apparent-magnitude work lands.
design:
# Scope split

Lambertian φ(α) = (sin α + (π−α)·cos α)/π lands in the planet pipeline
as part of the 3re.15 + 3re.16 + 3re.17 PR. This bead closes when
Lambert ships.

Per-planet empirical phase curves (Mallama 2018, including Saturn's
ring contribution + Venus's phase asymmetry + atmosphere-aware
Earth/Titan) are filed as a separate P3 follow-up — see the related-
linked bead.

# Phase angle derivation in the planet vert shader

α = ∠(viewer→planet, host→planet). With iHostLocalPos and iLocalRel
giving us host- and planet-positions in the floating-origin local
frame, and viewer = camera position (also local frame):

  vec3 vph = cameraPos − planetWorldPos;       // viewer → planet
  vec3 hph = iHostLocalPos − planetWorldPos;   // host → planet
  // (planetWorldPos = iHostLocalPos + iLocalRel)
  float cosA = dot(normalize(vph), normalize(hph));
  float alpha = acos(clamp(cosA, -1.0, 1.0));
  float phi = (sin(alpha) + (PI - alpha) * cos(alpha)) / PI;

Multiply brightness by φ → 2.5·log10(φ) added to m_planet inside the
planet-side appMag calculation.

# Cross-cutting architecture

See parent stellata-3re --design for the planet pipeline architecture
and shared perceptual-disc abstraction.
close_reason: Landed in PR #45 (v1.4.0): planet apparent-magnitude pipeline + Lambertian phase + global PlanetBodyField architecture.

title: Evaluate WebGPU migration (renderer + shader port)
description:
## Goal

Decide if/when Stellata should migrate from `THREE.WebGLRenderer` (WebGL2 + GLSL ES 3.00) to `THREE.WebGPURenderer` (WebGPU + WGSL/TSL). Capture context here so the decision is informed when a triggering need surfaces — not a P2 active scope.

## What WebGPU unlocks

- **Compute shaders** with shared memory, atomics, storage buffers. Many Stellata problems become trivial: visible-star count = one compute dispatch + atomic counter (~10 lines), GPU-driven culling for big catalogs, particle physics, on-GPU dust resampling.
- **Storage buffers (SSBO equivalent)** — could simplify the dust raymarch attribute juggling and search-index uploads.
- **Indirect draws + GPU-driven culling** — pull the per-frame `hasStarWithin` / `pickStar` / `countVisibleStars` family entirely off the CPU.
- **Better state model** — pipeline objects + bind groups instead of WebGL's global state machine. Far fewer footguns.
- **Lower CPU-side draw overhead.** Stellata is GPU-bound so the gain is modest, but real for per-frame overlay + label code.

## Browser support (2026-05)

- Chrome / Edge: stable since v113 (May 2023). Fully shipped.
- Safari (macOS + iOS): stable since 17.4 (March 2024). iOS 17.4+ is now the majority.
- Firefox: shipped to release in v141 (mid-2025).

Effectively generally available on evergreen browsers; long tail (older mobile, locked-down work browsers) is still WebGL2-only. Not a blocker for a hobby project audience.

## Three.js story

`WebGPURenderer` is no longer "examples-only" — first-class renderer. Shaders are authored in **TSL (Three Shading Language)**, a JS DSL that compiles to WGSL or GLSL — write once, run on either renderer. Rough edges to verify before committing:

- **Logarithmic depth buffer** (Stellata uses this; see `stellata-a7d.1`) — historically a sore spot on WebGPURenderer. Status as of early 2026 is "supported but verify" — needs a smoke pass before migration.
- **Custom GLSL3 shaders** — keep working unchanged on WebGLRenderer; on WebGPURenderer must port to TSL or WGSL. **This is the bulk of the migration work.** Stellata surface area: `star.vert/frag.glsl`, `dust-particle.vert/frag.glsl`, `cloud.vert/frag.glsl`, `milkyway.vert/frag.glsl`, plus any non-trivial chunks in `galactic-overlay.ts`.
- **InstancedBufferAttribute, 3D textures, MRT, Line2** — all supported.

## Migration cost estimate (eyeballed)

- ~1 week: port the four shader pairs to TSL + verify each pass renders identically.
- ~3 days: cross-browser smoke (Chrome/Safari/Firefox × Mac/iOS/Win — driver differences matter on WebGPU).
- ~2 days: perf comparison and tuning (log depth, dust raymarch step count, etc.).
- ~1 day: build/Vite config + `RawShaderMaterial` consumer migration.

Call it ~2 weeks elapsed for someone who's done a Three.js WebGPU port before; more for a first-time port.

## When this becomes worth doing

- **A critical mass of compute-shader-shaped problems** stacks up. Visible-count alone is not enough; need 3+ to justify the migration cost.
- **stellata-cns lands and 313k → 1M+ stars stresses the WebGL2 pipeline.** That's the natural moment to bundle a WebGPU port with the perf rework — see stellata-cns.1 perf research; if m12 doesn't run on integrated GPUs / mobile, WebGPU + GPU-driven culling is one of the alternatives to "fall back to m11."
- **GPU-bound features land that compute would simplify**: nebulae raymarch on compute, particle physics, exoplanet orbital integration GPU-side, etc.

## When it's NOT worth doing

- Stellata is feature-complete-ish and stable — migration for migration's sake is a 2-week tax with no user-visible win and a few imperceptible regressions.
- The visible-count alone (stellata-9mm.67) is the smallest possible reason to migrate.

## Triggering signals (when you see one of these, revisit this bead)

1. m12 catalog upgrade (stellata-cns) hits a perf wall on integrated GPUs / mobile.
2. Any GPU-bound feature surfaces where compute + atomics is the obvious tool (and CPU-mirroring or texture-RT-readback is the WebGL2 workaround).
3. Three.js drops `WebGLRenderer` (not anytime soon, but track).
4. Stellata picks up multi-platform deployment goals where WebGPU's lower CPU overhead matters.

## How to revisit

1. Re-validate browser-support and Three.js WebGPU feature-completeness numbers (this bead's data ages fast).
2. Spike: port `star.vert/frag.glsl` to TSL on a throwaway branch, verify on Chrome/Safari/Firefox, confirm log-depth works.
3. If the spike succeeds, scope the full port as an epic with one shader pair per task.

## History

Filed 2026-05-08 from the stellata-9mm.67 review session, where the visible-count CPU mirror was shelved because dust extinction can't be cheaply applied on CPU and WebGL2 has no cheap GPU-side counter. WebGPU's atomic-counter compute shader would solve that in ~10 lines. See stellata-9mm.67 notes for the full WebGL2 GPU-counting alternative (counting RT + reduction + async fence readback — viable but ~100 lines of plumbing).

title: Major moons: rendering integration (parent-relative bodies + visibility heuristic)
description:
**Goal:** render the major moons as parent-relative billboarded bodies in the focused planet's local frame. Mirrors `star-system.ts`'s per-planet body mesh, scaled down to N=1–7 per parent.

### Render layer

Likely a new `MoonSystem` class alongside `StarSystem`, or an extension of `StarSystem` that handles per-planet child bodies. Decision deferred until 2f6's focal-frame design lands — the natural shape depends on whether planet focus is a sub-state of star focus or a separate type.

If a separate class:

```typescript
class MoonSystem {
  setMoons(moons: readonly Moon[], parentPole: ParentPole): void;
  // Per-frame: pull moon positions from the resolver (2f6.1.2),
  // rotate by the parent-equatorial-to-ICRS quaternion, write to
  // iPosition InstancedBufferAttribute, set needsUpdate.
  update(t: number): void;
  setVisible(on: boolean): void;
  setMonochrome(on: boolean): void;
}
```

### Visibility heuristic

Major moons orbit at sub-AU scales — Earth's Moon at ~0.0026 AU, Triton at ~0.0024 AU, Iapetus at ~0.024 AU. At any zoom outside the planet-zoom-affordance from 2f6, every moon projects sub-pixel and is invisible.

Recommended gate: render moons only when the camera is within some multiple of the parent planet's heliocentric distance from the parent (e.g. closer than ~0.5 AU to the parent). Cheaper than a per-moon angular-separation heuristic and avoids the "dot cluster on top of the planet disc" failure mode at far framings.

A second per-moon visibility test (à la `ringVisibility` in `star-system.ts`) suppresses moons whose pixel separation from the parent disc collapses below threshold. Galilean moons are well-separated visually at typical Jupiter-zoom; rocky moons (Phobos, Deimos) and Saturn's inner moons (Mimas, Enceladus) need closer zoom to pull off the parent.

### Layered visibility

Same gate as the rest of the solar-system layer:
- Visible only when the focused star has planets AND a planet is the focal sub-object (per 2f6 focus model).
- Hidden in chart mode.
- Hidden during warp (consistent with planet labels, distinct from planet bodies which we now keep visible per `30eccc7` — moons at warp scale are unambiguous noise).

### Body shading

Reuse `shaders/planet.{vert,frag}.glsl`. The fragment shader's `solidity` knob already differentiates rocky / icy / gas-giant edges; for moons:
- `rocky` (Phobos, Deimos): 1.0 (hard edge).
- `icy` (most outer moons — Europa, Enceladus, Titan, etc.): 0.7 or so.
- `irregular` (very small captured asteroids — Phobos, Deimos do double-duty here): 1.0 with the caveat that 3D shape rendering is a 2f6 follow-up.

Earth's Moon phase is the only moon whose phase is visually obvious; defer to 2f6's general planet-phase work — same shading model applies uniformly.

### Tests

- `MoonSystem` toggles visibility correctly via `setVisible`, `setMonochrome`.
- `MoonSystem.update(t)` calls the resolver and re-uploads the iPosition attribute.
- The visibility-by-camera-distance gate works at threshold edges.

### Dependencies

- Blocks on `stellata-2f6.1.2` (resolver) for the position pipeline.
- Blocks on `stellata-2f6` itself for the focal-frame design (where `MoonSystem` mounts in the scene graph and how its host position is set per frame).

title: Major moons: per-parent positionsAt(t, out) resolver (Kepler, parent-relative)
description:
**Goal:** compute moon positions in the parent planet's local frame at any wall-clock `t`. Mirrors the `PlanetSystem.positionsAt(t, out)` shape introduced in stellata-3re.3.

### API

For each major-moon-bearing parent:

```typescript
// Returns moon positions in the parent planet's equatorial frame (xy
// planar, z perpendicular). The renderer then applies the parent
// planet's pole-orientation quaternion to land them in ICRS, mirroring
// the per-host-plane dispatch from stellata-3re.8 / star-system.ts.
function moonPositionsAt(parent: PlanetName, t: number, out: Float32Array): void;
```

`out` length = 3 × (count of moons for that parent), in the order the data-ingest bead (`stellata-2f6.1.1`) defines.

### Implementation per moon

1. `T = (tToJDE(t) - J2000_JD) / 36525` — centuries since J2000 (same convention as planet-positions resolver).
2. `M = M₀ + Ṁ·T + n·(t - t₀)` — mean anomaly, where `n = 360°/period`.
3. Solve Kepler's equation `M = E − e·sin(E)` (lift the solver from `ephemeris.ts:solveKepler`).
4. In-plane cartesian: `x = a·(cos E − e)`, `y = a·√(1 − e²)·sin E`, `z = 0` (parent equator).
5. Rotate by ω (in-plane), then by I (about x'), then by Ω (about parent z) → parent-equatorial cartesian.
6. km → parsecs (or stay in km until rendering — pick once, document once).

### Caching

Moon orbital periods range from ~7 hours (Io's parent-relative motion at planet-zoom can be visible) to ~16 days (Titan) to ~360 days (Iapetus). The minute-bucket cache from 3re.3 (planet positions) is too coarse here — moons at planet-zoom complete an orbit in days, and a smooth scrubber (stellata-nmu) wants per-frame motion.

Recommendation: cache per ~1 second (or even per-frame for the focused parent). Tune empirically once 2f6 affordances exist.

### Why parent-equatorial frame, not direct ICRS

Each parent planet's equator is tilted relative to the ecliptic by a different amount (Earth ~23.4°, Mars ~25°, Jupiter ~3°, Saturn ~27°, Uranus ~98°, Neptune ~28°). Moons orbit close to the parent equator (Laplace plane for outer giants), not the ecliptic. Returning positions in the parent-equatorial frame and letting the renderer rotate to ICRS via the parent's pole quaternion (data from `stellata-2f6.1.1`) is the natural decomposition.

This mirrors the per-host orbital-plane dispatch already in `star-system.ts:orbitalPlaneNormalFor` — Sol's planets get the ecliptic, exoplanets get the galactic plane (3re.8), parent planets' moons get the parent's equator. Same shape.

### Tests

- Per-moon: distance from parent stays in `[a(1-e), a(1+e)]` over ±3000 years.
- Per-moon: position varies measurably over a fraction of one orbital period (e.g. quarter-period sample displaces by ~√2·a).
- Per-moon: position returns to start after one full period.
- Triton specifically: orbit is retrograde — moon-cross-velocity dotted with parent's spin axis is negative.

### Dependencies

- Blocks on `stellata-2f6.1.1` (data ingest — supplies the elements + parent poles).
- Reuses `solveKepler` from `ephemeris.ts`.

title: Major moons: data ingest (per-planet IAU pole + per-moon orbital elements)
description:
**Goal:** assemble the static catalog the moon-position resolver and renderer need. Snapshot at build time per the `frozen-external-data` rule.

### Per-planet data (parent reference frame)

Most moons orbit close to their parent planet's equatorial plane (or Laplace plane for outer moons). The parent's equatorial plane is defined by the IAU rotational pole — RA, Dec of the planet's north pole in ICRS, plus a per-planet rotation rate W₀ + W·d. For our visualisation purposes only the pole matters (we don't need to track surface-fixed reference longitude).

Source: IAU/IAG Working Group on Cartographic Coordinates and Rotational Elements, latest published recommendation. Bundled in the JPL ephemeris file headers; also tabulated in NASA fact sheets.

Per-planet additions:
- `poleRaDeg`, `poleDecDeg` — north pole orientation in ICRS J2000.
- (Optional) per-century pole drift, if any planet's pole moves enough to matter at our zoom — Mars/Earth precess slowly, all are sub-arcsec/century, almost certainly noise at our scale; skip unless trivial.

### Per-moon data

For each major moon (~20 total per `stellata-2f6.1`):
- `name` (display string)
- `parentPlanet` (enum: 'earth' | 'mars' | … | 'neptune')
- `radiusKm` (mean radius for billboard sizing; same convention as `Planet.radiusKm`)
- `colour` (representative single-tone RGB; same convention as `Planet.colour`)
- `type` ('rocky' | 'icy' | 'irregular' — irregular for Phobos / Deimos)
- **Orbital elements at J2000 in the parent's equatorial frame:**
  - `semiMajorAxisKm` — keep in km (not AU) since moon orbits are sub-AU
  - `eccentricity`
  - `inclinationDeg` (relative to parent equator; Triton ≈ 157° = retrograde)
  - `longAscNodeDeg` (Ω)
  - `argPeriDeg` (ω)
  - `meanAnomalyDeg` at J2000 (M₀)
  - `periodDays` (or mean motion n in deg/day) — derivable from a + parent GM but precomputing avoids the lookup
- (Optional) per-century secular rates for Ω, ω, M₀ where the moon's orbit precesses fast enough to matter (most major moons: yes for Ω, ω; small for M₀ if a, e are roughly constant)

### Sources

JPL Solar System Dynamics maintains canonical tables:
- Per-planet IAU pole: https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/?planet_pos
- Per-moon mean elements: https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sats/elem/
- Per-moon physical properties (mass, radius, colour estimates): https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sats/phys_par/

The build-time approach decision (bundle CSV + parse, or hand-curate, or generate from astronomia) lives in the **related** bead `stellata-2f6.2`. The output schema this bead specifies is independent of the source.

### Output

A new module under `src/client/`, e.g. `moon-system.ts`, exporting:

```typescript
export interface Moon {
  readonly name: string;
  readonly parentPlanet: PlanetName;
  readonly radiusKm: number;
  readonly colour: readonly [number, number, number];
  readonly type: 'rocky' | 'icy' | 'irregular';
  readonly elements: KeplerElements;  // J2000 osculating, parent-equatorial frame
}

export const MAJOR_MOONS: readonly Moon[] = [...];

// Per-planet pole orientation in ICRS — drives the parent-equatorial frame.
export interface ParentPole {
  readonly raDeg: number;
  readonly decDeg: number;
}
export const PLANET_POLES: Record<PlanetName, ParentPole> = {...};
```

### Test coverage

- Each moon has positive radius / period / a.
- Eccentricities in [0, 1).
- Inclinations in [0, 180] degrees (Triton confirms the retrograde range works).
- Names match the `stellata-2f6.1` scope (no minor moons accidentally included).
- Element tables cross-check published values (e.g. Earth's Moon a ≈ 384,400 km; Io a ≈ 421,800 km).

### Related

- `stellata-2f6.1` (parent epic).
- `stellata-2f6.2` (astronomia build-time investigation — informs the source-of-data choice).

title: Investigate astronomia as build-time ephemeris-table source
description:
**Goal:** evaluate whether to use the [astronomia](https://www.npmjs.com/package/astronomia) npm package at *build time* to generate small runtime ephemeris tables, particularly for the moon ephemerides needed by the major-moons epic (sibling stellata-2f6.1).

### Backstory

We rejected astronomia at runtime three times now (planet positions in stellata-3re.3, etc.) because shipping the full VSOP87 / ELP coefficient set is ~500 KB gzipped — way past what billboarded planet/moon discs actually need. The astronomia tarball is 18.7 MB unpacked, with per-body coefficient files in the hundreds-of-KB range.

But astronomia is well-tested, semi-actively maintained, and bundles solid Meeus implementations of:
- VSOP87D for planets (we replaced with JPL Standish 1992 → no value to redo from astronomia)
- Abridged ELP for the Moon (~70 KB; Meeus chapter 47)
- Galilean satellites (jupitermoons.js, ~10 KB; Meeus E2x3 truncation)
- Major Saturnian moons (saturnmoons.js, ~10 KB; Meeus chapter 45)
- Lacks: Uranian moons (we'd need GUST86 from elsewhere), Neptunian moons

**The angle worth pursuing:** instead of bundling astronomia at runtime *or* hand-rolling each formulation, run astronomia in the build pipeline and emit smaller domain-specific tables.

### Investigation tasks

1. **Tree-shake test.** Import only `astronomia/jupitermoons` and `astronomia/saturnmoons` and `astronomia/moonposition` in a build target. Measure resulting bundle size. Astronomia's package.json may not expose tree-shaking-friendly entry points; if so, copy the relevant files directly.

2. **Build-time table-generator pattern.** Sketch a `scripts/build-ephemeris.ts` that:
   - imports needed astronomia modules
   - samples positions / elements at chosen epochs (J2000 ± centuries, fine-grained around now)
   - fits truncated polynomials per element per body (e.g. 5th-order over ±3000 years)
   - emits TypeScript const tables to `src/client/ephemeris-data/` or a build artifact like `public/ephemeris.bin`
   - covers: Earth's Moon, the four Galileans, the seven major Saturnians

3. **Compare with manual JPL CSV ingest.** JPL's [satellite-elements table](https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sats/elem/) publishes mean elements + secular rates directly — no astronomia needed. The build-pipeline angle is only valuable if astronomia gives us *better* coverage / accuracy than the JPL CSV at the same effort. Need to assess this per-system.

4. **Output format choice.** Consider TypeScript const tables (typecheck-friendly, compiled into bundle), JSON file (lazy-loaded, smaller bundle), or a binary format mirroring `catalog.bin` (smallest, opaque). The pattern from `scripts/build-catalog.ts` is the natural reference.

### What this is NOT

- **Not** about runtime astronomia. Runtime use is rejected.
- **Not** about replacing JPL Standish for planets — that work is done; astronomia and Standish use the same upstream VSOP series, so re-deriving from astronomia would reinvent the wheel.

### Considered and rejected: fork / upstream a lightweight astronomia

Idea was floated to PR a "lite" build target into astronomia, or maintain a stellata fork. Rejected as out of scope for this project:

- astronomia's tarball is ~18 MB raw because it bundles *all* the coefficient sets — a "lite" build is at odds with the package's positioning as a complete reference.
- The actual code we need is small enough to write or generate ourselves.
- Forking commits us to maintenance of a JS library we'd otherwise just consume.
- astronomia release cadence is sparse (8+ months since last release at time of writing); upstream PR may sit indefinitely.

If anything emerges from the build-time investigation that *would* benefit the broader community, an upstream PR could be a follow-up; but the investigation should not block on that path being viable.

### Output of this bead

A short recommendation: which moon systems benefit from build-time astronomia (vs manual JPL CSV vs hand-rolled Meeus formulas), what the build pipeline looks like, and whether stellata-2f6.1's data-ingest child should adopt this approach or skip it.

### Related

- `stellata-2f6.1` (major moons epic) — primary consumer of the recommendation.
- `stellata-1gh` (deep-time accuracy research) — orthogonal but related: the same investigation could inform whether astronomia helps the planet ephemerides at deep-time edges.

title: (EPIC) Major moons of Sol's planets
description:
**Goal:** render the major moons of Sol's planets as planet-relative bodies once the user can zoom into a planet (gated on stellata-2f6).

**Scope: major moons only.** Approximately 20 named, roughly-spherical satellites:

- **Earth (1):** Moon
- **Mars (2):** Phobos, Deimos (both small captured asteroids; included for completeness since Mars only has these two)
- **Jupiter (4):** Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto (the Galileans)
- **Saturn (~7):** Mimas, Enceladus, Tethys, Dione, Rhea, Titan, Iapetus (skip Hyperion — irregular shape, less major)
- **Uranus (5):** Miranda, Ariel, Umbriel, Titania, Oberon
- **Neptune (1):** Triton (retrograde orbit, i ≈ 157°)

### Deliberately excluded

- **All minor / irregular moons (~270+).** Saturn jumped from 83 → 146 in 2023 from the Subaru survey; the vast majority are sub-5km outer irregulars with provisional designations like `S/2003 J 2`. No visual value at any zoom we'd render at; mostly potato-shaped and unnamed; continually being rediscovered. Snapshot-and-freeze a constantly-changing catalog of meaningless-at-disc-scale rocks is poor ROI.
- **Pluto's moon system.** Filed as a separate epic if/when Pluto becomes a focal body.
- **Asteroid moons** (e.g. Dactyl orbiting Ida) and **comet structures**. Out of scope.
- **Saturn / Jupiter / Uranus / Neptune ring systems.** Sister bead — same gating epic (stellata-2f6) but very different render approach. To be filed separately once moons take shape.

### Why gated on stellata-2f6 (planet-as-object)

At any current zoom, planet bodies floor-clamp to ~2 px discs. Moons that orbit at sub-AU distances would project sub-pixel and be invisible. Implementing moon rendering before planet-zoom affordance lands violates the new `stellata-defer-detail-until-zoom-affordance` rule. Once 2f6 lets the user fly within hundreds of thousands of km of a planet, moons become first-class visual elements.

### Why gated, also, on the time scrubber feeling right

Planet positions move sub-arcsec/sec at our zoom — invisible, cached for 60s in 3re.3. Moons at planet-zoom move *visibly*: Earth's Moon completes an orbit in 27.3 days; at planet-zoom, that's degrees per minute of perceptible sweep. The minute-bucket cache from 3re.3 needs to drop way down for moons under any "play time" affordance the scrubber introduces (stellata-nmu).

### Children (to file)

1. **Data ingest.** Per-planet IAU pole orientation (RA, Dec of north pole) + per-moon J2000 osculating Keplerian elements + secular rates where available. JPL satellite-elem table is the canonical source. See related bead on the astronomia build-time investigation.
2. **Per-parent moon-position resolver.** Same shape as `PlanetSystem.positionsAt(t, out)` from 3re.3, but operating in the parent planet's equatorial frame. Returns moon positions relative to the parent planet's centre.
3. **Rendering integration.** Extend the body-mesh pattern from star-system.ts to support per-planet child bodies. Per-moon visibility heuristic similar to per-planet orbit rings (3re.7).
4. **(Coupled to 2f6 phase work)** Earth's Moon phase rendering. Moon phase is the most visually obvious property of any moon; once 2f6 introduces planet phase shading, Earth's Moon inherits it.

### Other considerations the children pick up

- **Non-equatorial orbits.** Most moons orbit close to the parent's equatorial plane (Laplace plane for outer moons). Parent equatorial plane ≠ ecliptic. Need each planet's IAU pole. Triton is the famous exception — retrograde at i ≈ 157°.
- **Tidal locking.** Most major moons are tidally locked to their parent. If 2f6 introduces 3D body meshes (vs current billboards), moons inherit the same shading model; tidal lock means the same face always toward parent.
- **Co-orbital pairs.** Saturn's Janus and Epimetheus literally swap orbits every 4 years; Tethys has Trojan companions at L4 / L5 (Telesto, Calypso). Independent Kepler around the parent gives them all the right (independent) positions; no special handling needed.
- **Earth-Moon barycentre.** Our JPL Standish table (3re.3) computes EM Bary, not Earth itself. Earth's centre lies ~4700 km off-barycentre — sub-pixel at planet-disc scale, visible at Earth-zoom. The Earth-Moon split would naturally fall out of moon ephemerides if we add them.
- **Searchability and focusability.** Should "Europa" in the search box jump to it? Should moons be focal objects? Belongs to 2f6's "what's a focal object" rework, not here.

### Out of scope for v1 of this epic

- Phase / illumination shading other than Earth's Moon (the rest defer to 2f6's general planet-phase work — apply to all bodies uniformly when it lands).
- Moon-on-moon eclipses, transits, mutual events.
- Saturn ring shadow on Saturn's moons.
- Moon-of-moon (none currently confirmed in our system anyway).

title: Research: planet position accuracy beyond ±3000 years from J2000
description:
Beyond the validity window of JPL Standish 1992 (used by stellata-3re.3), planet positions become unreliable. JPL Standish 1992 with cubic Jupiter–Neptune corrections is good to ~arcminute over ±3000 years; outside that window the linear+cubic element model breaks down (mean-motion resonances and longer-period perturbations dominate).

If the time-scrubber epic (stellata-nmu) wants to allow scrubbing further than ±3000 years from J2000:

### Options to investigate

1. **Cap the scrubber range at ±3000 years.** Simplest. Documented in the scrubber UX as 'historical-era only'. No code changes here.
2. **Switch to VSOP87 (full series).** ~1.5 MB raw / ~500 KB gzipped via the astronomia npm package. Designed validity ±4000 years. Even VSOP87 is undefined beyond that window.
3. **Truncated VSOP87 inline.** Pick the largest-amplitude terms per planet from the VSOP87 series, ship a trimmed coefficient table inline. ~50 KB code. Lets us tune the size/accuracy tradeoff.
4. **Numerical integration.** Symplectic integrator (e.g. WHFast) seeded from JPL DE4xx initial conditions. Good for arbitrary epochs but blows the bundle and adds CPU cost.

### Decision points

- What is the maximum scrubbable  range the scrubber should expose?
- What is acceptable accuracy at the scrubber's far edges? (E.g. arc-minute vs degree-scale).
- Bundle size vs accuracy tradeoff for stellata's overall page-weight budget.

This task is research-only — produces a recommendation that informs the scrubber's range constraints (stellata-nmu) and any future planet-position upgrade.

title: Planet-as-object: select, focus, and zoom into individual planets
description:
**Goal:** treat planet bodies as selectable, focusable objects so the camera can fly arbitrarily close to any of them and reveal per-planet visual detail.

**Why now:** the current solar-system layer (stellata-3re) renders planets as physically-sized billboarded discs. At any reasonable host-relative camera distance the bodies project below the pixel-size floor and clamp — Jupiter and Mercury read as identical 2-px dots. A full set of per-planet refinements (texture maps, atmospheric haloes / scattering, banding, ring systems, moons, axial tilt, day-side / night-side shading) all depend on the user being able to get close enough to a single body to see them. Without that affordance, those details are wasted code.

**Scope (open — refine before scheduling):**

1. Planet picking. The existing star-pickStar pipeline returns a star catalog index; this needs an analogue for planet bodies: cursor → planet identifier (host star + planet index in PlanetSystem). Hover tooltip + click select.

2. Planet focus. setFocus(starIdx) currently snaps the floating origin to the focal star and clamps controls.minDistance to the per-star physical floor. For planets, the focal frame becomes (host star, planet index); the floating origin recenters on the planet's local position; controls.minDistance drops below the planet's physical radius (e.g. ~5e3 km for Earth orbit, ~2e4 km for Jupiter). Need to think through the implications for the floating-origin precision invariants.

3. Camera framing. Arrival pose, default orbit radius for a focused planet (analogue of the per-star d_min(R) from stellata-a7d.2), warp-from-host-to-planet animation.

4. URL state. Today the URL encodes a focused star index; planet focus needs an additional sub-identifier.

5. UI plumbing. Search-by-planet-name, planet entries in the focus search box, breadcrumb indicator ("focused on Earth orbiting Sol"), Esc step-back semantics (planet → host star → unfocused).

6. What it unblocks (separate beads filed once this epic clears the design gate):
   - **stellata-3re.10**: texture research → texture rendering, since you can now actually see them.
   - **Atmosphere / banding / scattering**: per-body shader work, deferred from stellata-3re.4 because the bodies currently floor-clamp to a 2-px disc.
   - **Ring systems** (Saturn, faint rings of the other giants), **moons**, **axial tilt and rotation**, **day/night phase shading**.
   - **Exoplanet planet inspection** (ties to stellata-bk5.3): the same selection/zoom affordance carries to focused-host exoplanet bodies.

**Design gate (suggested first child):** decide whether planet focus is a refinement of the existing star-focus state (a sub-index added to focusedStar) or a separate orthogonal focus type (focusedPlanet alongside focusedStar / focusedCloud). Implications for setFocus, URL encoding, observe mode, and the Stellata.getFocusedPlanetSystem() event ripple from this choice.

**Defer:**
- Anything exoplanet-specific (covered under stellata-bk5).
- Solar system aside-from-planets — heliopause is in 3re.5, deep-space probes in stellata-ywn.

LABELS: phase-future, solar-system, ui, design-gate

title: Unit tests for v1.1.4 hot-path helpers (peakAmplitudeFactor, minDistForStar, pickStar, getWarpInfo, url-state EPS)
description:
PR #35 (v1.1.4) shipped six bd-tracked changes — two bug fixes (warp no-op for co-located stars, variable peak overshoot) and four perf wins (dust prefilter, url-state EPS, pickStar binary-search window, hot-path scratch Vector3s). All verification was manual smoke tests; no new unit tests were added.

Coverage gaps worth filling so future refactors of these helpers have a regression net:

- peakAmplitudeFactor(idx): `pow(10, amp/10)` for a variable, `1` for non-variables (no period or no amplitude). Pure helper, easy to test with a hand-rolled catalog stub.
- minDistForStar / minOrbitDistForStar: that Reff = R · peakAmplitudeFactor is what gets fed into distAtFillFraction. distAtFillFraction itself is already covered by star-geometry.test.ts; the wrapper is not.
- Warp no-op branch in startWarp: distPc < 1e-6 takes the setFocus / setFocusedCloud path (no warp animation). Could be covered by a Stellata harness test or extracted to a pure helper.
- pickStar windowed-scan: behavioural test that the windowed scan returns the same picks as the (now-removed) full-catalog scan over a representative slider range. Would catch off-by-one in sortedDistRange.
- url-state startUrlSync EPS comparison: that the new component-wise epsilon comparator schedules a write iff any of the 9 components changes by ≥ 1e-3.

Acceptance: vitest module(s) covering at least the first three above. The pickStar one is harder (needs camera + catalog harness) and may be a follow-up.

Source PR: #35.

Filed during the post-merge review cleanup that extracted sortedDistRange / destLocalPositionInto helpers — those helpers are also untested and could be in scope here.

title: Revisit variable-star pulsation: radius-only vs radius+temperature, validate Mira amplitude realism
description:
Revisit whether modeling variability via radius change alone is physically defensible. Today Stellata uses the constant-T assumption `R ∝ √L` (so brightness amplitude lands entirely on R), which is explicitly listed in CLAUDE.md under 'Things deliberately kept out':

> Temperature-swing component of variable-star brightness change. We use `R ∝ √L` (constant-T assumption); real pulsating variables split the brightness change between R and T swings. Modelling T changes per variable type is more complexity than the visualisation warrants.

The concern reopening this: for high-amplitude pulsators (Mira itself being the type-defining example), full-amplitude variability under the constant-T model implies an angular-diameter swing that may be extreme relative to what real stellar physics produces. Real Miras split the change between R-swing and T-swing (and the T-swing dominates V-band amplitude more than people often assume).

# What needs investigating

1. **Quantify the discrepancy.** For Mira (and a few other high-amplitude AAVSO archetypes — chi Cyg, R Hya, R Leo) compute the on-screen angular-diameter ratio between minimum and maximum light under the current `R ∝ √L` assumption, and compare to interferometric measurements of the actual physical radius swing (CHARA / VLTI / IOTA Mira-disc papers — Lacour 2009, Woodruff 2009, Wittkowski 2016, etc.). If the modeled R-swing is far larger than the real R-swing, the visual is not 'physical reality with perceptual exaggeration only where the eye demands it' — it's just wrong.

2. **Decide on a fix path.** Three plausible options, in increasing complexity:
   - (a) Cap the R-swing per variable type (simple multiplier table), accept that brightness amplitude is then under-served on V-band.
   - (b) Split the amplitude: a fixed per-type fraction of luminosity change goes to R, the rest is encoded as a colour/temperature shift in the disc + glow shader (we already pass spectral colour via uniforms — extending that to per-frame T-shift is a contained change to the variable-star uniform path).
   - (c) Keep the current model, document the limitation in SCIENCE.md as a known stylistic choice, do nothing in code.

3. **Decide if the answer differs by GCVS variable type** (Mira-types, Cepheids, RR Lyrae, eclipsing binaries are all already in the build). The cost of (b) goes up if it has to be type-specific; (a) scales fine.

# Pointers

- Variable-star data flows from GCVS via scripts/catalog-pure.ts → binary v4 → vert/frag uniforms.
- Pulsation lives in src/client/shaders/star.{vert,frag}.glsl (`R_eff = R · radiusFactor` plus glow scaling).
- Documented choices: SCIENCE.md, CLAUDE.md ('Things deliberately kept out' bullet quoted above), docs/rendering.md (variable pulsation section).
- Loosely related: stellata-a7d (physical-scale rewrite — this bead is downstream of that landing) and stellata-a7d.2.10 (V-band → perceived brightness correction, the static-spectral analogue of what (b) above does dynamically).

# Acceptance

- Mira's full-amplitude angular-diameter swing in the renderer is verified against published interferometric measurements; gap quantified.
- Decision recorded: implement (a), (b), (c), or 'something else' — and either the CLAUDE.md 'kept out' bullet is updated/removed, or the bead is closed with rationale to leave it.
- If implementing (b): the temperature-swing path lands in the variable-star shader uniform pipeline with per-type amplitudes documented in SCIENCE.md.
notes:
# Visual reference — Wikipedia Cepheid pulsation animation

Alex flagged this animation as a possibly useful visual reference for the option-(b) split (R-swing + T-swing in phase) the epic is considering. NOT a physics-validation source — useful only for getting an eye on what coupled R+T pulsation looks like rendered.

**URL:** https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cepheid_animation_5_rend_1.gif
**Author:** Merikanto, 2023-01-09, CC BY-SA 4.0
**Rendering stack:** POV-Ray 3.7, 100 frames, 500×500, 5 s loop

## Animation parameters (verified against the file page metadata)

- **Radius:** sphere scale oscillates 0.8 ↔ 1.2 — ±20% (40% peak-to-peak)
- **Temperature:** blackbody 5500 K ↔ 6800 K, synced in phase with radius
- **Surface:** procedural granite + wrinkles overlay (purely aesthetic)
- **Brightness:** ambient lighting varies with pulsation phase

## How physics-grounded is it?

- **Radius amplitude is exaggerated.** Real classical Cepheids vary by ~5–10% in radius (e.g. δ Cephei itself ≈ 7%), not 40% peak-to-peak. RR Lyrae and W Vir sit in similar ranges. The animation's ±20% radius is well above observed values.
- **Temperature range is plausible.** 5500–6800 K spans a believable classical-Cepheid range across phase (typical F-G excursions). The qualitative direction — hotter at minimum radius / near maximum light — matches the standard κ-mechanism phase relation.
- **The R+T phase coupling is the right qualitative model** — and is precisely what option (b) in this epic proposes for the renderer.

## Why keep this on the bead

When fe4.1 lands on a decision and fe4.2 starts on option (b), this is a quick reference for what a published procedural rendering of the coupled R+T animation looks like — useful for calibration intuition. The actual physics inputs still come from the interferometric citations already listed in fe4.1 (Lacour 2009, Woodruff 2009, Wittkowski 2016) and the standard Cepheid photometric amplitude tables. Treat the Wikipedia animation as visual mood-board, not a parameter source.

title: Decision: time control widget design (slider, jump-to-date, FF/RW, format)
description:
**Goal:** decide what the scrubber actually looks like before building it.

### Design surface to settle

1. **Continuous slider vs jump-to-date input vs both.** A slider gives the "scrub the universe" feel; a date input lets the user be precise. Both is probably right, with the slider as the primary affordance and the date input as a click-to-edit detail.
2. **Slider range.** `±10 yr`? `±10,000 yr`? Or two-tier: a log-scale that maps a single-track slider over `±100 Myr`?
3. **Auto-play.** Forward/back buttons that march `t` at adjustable speed (e.g. 1 day/sec, 1 yr/sec, 1000 yr/sec). Pause/resume.
4. **Keyboard nudges.** Arrow keys = fine step; shift = coarse; alt/option = jump? Use the existing keyboard-shortcuts.ts dispatch.
5. **Time format on the readout.** Inherits from stellata-3re.H — both this widget and the static readout should agree on the format. Leading recommendation in 3re.H is UTC Zulu (`2026-05-07 18:23Z`).
6. **Where it lives on the screen.** `.ui-bottom` next to the scale bar, or a dedicated affordance further from the scale bar so they do not visually compete?
7. **Live mode.** "Live" means `t = Date.now() / 1000`, refreshed each frame. The widget needs a clear "Live" state when scrubbed back to now; should the slider snap to "Live" when within tolerance, or does the user have to click a button?
8. **URL parameter.** `t=<unix>` only when scrubbed away from "Live"; absence in URL = live. Already specified in stellata-3re.1; confirm here that the widget honours it.

### Out of scope

- The actual implementation of the widget — that becomes its own task once the design lands.

LABELS: time-scrubber, design-decision, ui

title: Decision: hide-vs-keep policy for non-time-aware objects at extreme t
description:
**Goal:** decide what happens to scene objects without time data when `t` is far from now.

### The problem

Some objects (molecular clouds, dust, distant catalogue stars without proper-motion data) cannot be correctly positioned at non-current epochs. Two extreme behaviours:

- **Always show.** Render them at their J2000 positions regardless of `t`. Easy. Misleading at million-year timescales (the local dust cloud morphology at t = -10 Myr is nothing like today's).
- **Always hide.** Render only objects that have time data. Honest. Empty / sparse for `t != now` since most layers have no time info.

### Options between the extremes

- **Soft fade.** Each layer carries a "validity timescale" — within it, render normally; outside it, fade to invisibility (linear / log fade window).
- **Hard cutoff.** Past a single global `|t - now|` threshold (say 10,000 years), hide every layer that does not have time info.
- **Banner.** Always show everything, but display a banner ("at this scale only the solar system is correctly positioned") when `|t - now|` is large.

### Recommendation (tentative)

Soft fade per layer:
- ATHYG stars without proper-motion data fade above ~10,000 yr offset.
- Molecular clouds + dust fade above ~100,000 yr offset.
- The solar-system layer is always valid.

Combined with the audit from the sibling task, this gives a coherent story: the user can sweep millennia and the scene gracefully degrades to "only what we know" at the extremes.

### Open question

Where do constellation lines fit? They are derived from star positions, so they trail whatever ATHYG does. If half the stars in Orion fade out, the stick-figure breaks — probably the right behaviour (the constellation literally changes on long-enough timescales).

LABELS: time-scrubber, design-decision

title: Audit: catalogue object motion data + decide what moves with t
description:
**Goal:** answer the central design question for the time scrubber — when `t` moves, which objects in the scene move with it?

### Audit

For each layer / dataset, document the proportion of records carrying time-relevant motion data:

| Layer | Field(s) needed | Question to answer |
|---|---|---|
| ATHYG stars | `pmra`, `pmdec`, `rv` | What % have all three? Just proper motion (pmra/pmdec)? Just radial velocity? |
| GCVS variables | (already animated via uTime) | Period of pulsation already drives variable-star size. Does scrubbing `t` correspond to phase shifts? |
| Zucker molecular clouds | (none expected) | Confirm: clouds are snapshot-only. |
| Edenhofer dust | (none expected) | Confirm: dust map is current-epoch. |
| Stellarium constellation lines | (N/A) | Constellations are derived from star positions; they move iff stars move. |

### Decision options

- **All-or-nothing:** every object that *can* move, moves; everything else freezes in place. Pro: consistent. Con: clouds in their J2000 positions at t = 1500 CE is misleading; at t = 0.1 Myr it is wrong.
- **Honest motion:** move only objects with motion data; visually fade out (or hide) objects without it as `|t - now|` exceeds their reasonable lifetime. Pro: honest. Con: complicated UX.
- **Solar-system-only:** the time scrubber only affects planets / probes; everything else is frozen. Pro: simplest. Con: limits the scrubber's narrative power (no "see Polaris drift").

### Recommendation (tentative)

Branch on time scale:
- `|t - now| < ~10,000 yr`: all stars with proper-motion data move; everything else stays in place (clouds / dust / constellations look the same on this timescale anyway).
- `|t - now| > ~10,000 yr`: only solar-system objects move; stars freeze and a banner explains.

This connects to the "hide-vs-keep" sibling task — they share the same time-scale logic.

### Deliverable

A documented decision in this issue's closing note + the audit numbers (so future-you can revisit if the catalogue refresh changes the data coverage).

LABELS: time-scrubber, design-decision

title: Decision: planet type indication (gas giant vs rocky vs unknown)
description:
**Goal:** decide how planet type is communicated visually. Lands before stellata-bk5.3 (rendering).

### What we have

NASA `pscomppars` does not carry a single canonical "type" column. We can derive type heuristically from radius and mass:

- **Rocky:** `pl_rade < ~1.6` (the "radius valley" boundary from Fulton 2017)
- **Mini-Neptune / sub-Neptune:** `1.6 < pl_rade < ~4`
- **Neptune-like ice giant:** `4 < pl_rade < ~10`
- **Gas giant:** `pl_rade > ~10` (or `pl_radj > ~0.8`)
- **Unknown:** radius is null

Mass can disambiguate (ρ = M/V) but radius alone is sufficient for visual buckets.

### What to decide

(a) **Skip type entirely.** Render every planet as a uniform disc. Loses information.
(b) **Colour-code by type.** Rocky = warm grey/brown, sub-Neptune = pale blue-green, Neptune = deep blue, gas giant = banded yellow. Quick to ship, schematic. Matches the same approach used for Sol planets in 3re.E.
(c) **Per-planet representative colour.** When NASA carries colour info (rare — mostly atmospheric albedo for transiting planets), use it; otherwise fall back to the type-bucket colour. Marginal improvement over (b).

### Unknown type (null radius)

Use a neutral grey + the "unknown" indicator from bk5.3's null-radius treatment. Same visual; the indicator does the explaining.

### Recommendation (tentative)

Option (b) — type-bucketed colour from radius, derived once in the build script and stored as a categorical enum (`rocky | sub_neptune | neptune | gas_giant | unknown`) on each planet record. Renderer maps enum → colour. Cheap, informative, consistent with how Sol's planets get representative colours in 3re.E.

### Format the renderer consumes

`PlanetSystem.planets[i].type: "rocky" | "sub_neptune" | "neptune" | "gas_giant" | "unknown"` — derived in build script from `pl_rade` (with `pl_radj * 11.2` as fallback). Single enum, single colour table.

LABELS: exoplanets, phase-11, design-decision
notes: Created 2026-05-07 — completes the trio of bk5 design decisions Alex flagged. Together with the orbital-fallback and size-representation decisions, bk5.3 (rendering) inherits a fully-specified data contract.

title: Decision: planet size representation format (absolute vs relative-to-host)
description:
**Goal:** decide how exoplanet sizes are represented in the renderer when radius is known. Lands before stellata-bk5.3 (rendering).

### The problem

NASA `pscomppars` carries radius as either `pl_rade` (Earth radii) or `pl_radj` (Jupiter radii), with one or both populated depending on the source. Stellata's star renderer (post stellata-a7d.2) sizes discs by true angular diameter `θ = 2·atan(R/d)` using `R` in parsecs. To plug planets into the same pipeline we need `R` in parsecs — straightforward unit conversion either way.

### What to decide

The user-visible question is whether the planet's on-screen size is anchored to:

(a) **Absolute physical scale.** A 1 R⊕ planet always renders at the same angular diameter from a given camera distance, regardless of its host's size. This is what the angular-diameter pipeline naturally produces. Real-world result: most planets at typical viewing distances are sub-pixel; only gas giants resolve.

(b) **Relative-to-host visual scale.** Multiply planet radius by some perceptual factor (or render at `min(physicalRadius, hostRadius × 0.05)` to keep the visual ratio readable). Loses physical accuracy but the planet:host visual ratio is informative.

(c) **Hybrid.** Physical when zoomed close (planet resolves geometrically), exaggerated minimum when zoomed far.

### Recommendation (tentative)

Option (c) — same `max(appSize, physSize)` pattern stars already use. For planets, `appSize` would be a small floor (e.g. 2 px) so they remain visible at typical exoplanet-viewing zoom levels even when the true angular size is sub-pixel. The physical-disc threshold takes over at close approach.

### Format the renderer consumes

`PlanetSystem.planets[i].radius_pc: number | null` — a single canonical field, derived in the build script from whichever NASA column is populated. Build script handles unit conversion (`R⊕ = 4.2588e-5 AU`, `R♃ = 4.7790e-4 AU`, `1 AU = 4.8481e-6 pc`) so the runtime never sees the source-column ambiguity.

### Null radius

Already covered in bk5.3 — render at default mid-range size with a visual indicator. Reaffirm here so the contract is consistent.

LABELS: exoplanets, phase-11, design-decision
notes: Created 2026-05-07 — Alex flagged that the size-representation format was not pinned down in the existing bk5 description.

title: Decision: orbital fallback when pl_orbsmax is null
description:
**Goal:** decide what to do when NASA Exoplanet Archive has no semi-major axis for a confirmed planet. Lands before stellata-bk5.3 (rendering) so the renderer inherits a settled rule.

### The problem

The NASA `pscomppars` table has `pl_orbsmax` (semi-major axis in AU) populated for the majority of confirmed planets but not all. Some entries carry only `pl_orbper` (period) or `pl_bmasse` (mass) without orbit geometry. The renderer cannot draw an orbit ring without a radius.

### Options

1. **Compute from period via Kepler's third law.** When `pl_orbper` is present and `st_mass` is approximately known (or default to the host's `st_lum`-implied mass), `a = (G·M·P²/4π²)^(1/3)`. Pros: physically grounded. Cons: requires guessing host mass when missing; minor planets get smeared.
2. **Fixed default.** Show every null-orbit planet at a fixed visual radius (e.g. 1 AU) with a clear visual indicator. Pros: simple. Cons: misleading.
3. **Hide null-orbit planets.** Skip rendering entirely. Pros: honest. Cons: loses entries.
4. **Synthetic spacing.** When N planets exist around a host with no orbit data, space them at the geometric mean of the system's known orbits. Cons: invented physics.

### Recommendation (tentative)

Option 1 (Kepler's third law) when `pl_orbper` is present, falling back to option 3 (hide) otherwise. Surface a separate metric in the build log: how many planets get orbit, how many get period→orbit conversion, how many are dropped.

### Out of scope

- The null-radius case (already covered in bk5.3 — different problem, different fallback).
- Eccentricity null — already handled (default to 0 / circular).

LABELS: exoplanets, phase-11, design-decision
notes: Created 2026-05-07 — flagged by Alex as an open decision the existing bk5 description did not address.

title: Time scrubber: catalogue motion, hide policy, and control widget
description:
**Status:** Standalone, low priority. Deferred until the v1 solar-system layer (stellata-3re) and exoplanets (stellata-bk5) ship.

**Goal:** build a user-facing time scrubber on top of the wall-clock `t` variable from stellata-3re.1. The v1 solar-system layer only displays `t = now` — no scrubbing. This epic covers the design + UX work to let the user move time forward and back.

The complexity is not the scrubber UI itself (small widget). It is the three open questions about what time means at scene scale:

### Question 1: catalogue motion (proper motion + velocity)

When `t` moves significantly, should ATHYG stars move along their proper-motion / radial-velocity vectors? The visual coherence question — at t = 1500 CE, do nearby stars sit where the scrubber would put them, or do they freeze at their J2000 positions?

To answer:
- Audit what fraction of ATHYG carries proper-motion (`pmra`, `pmdec`) and radial-velocity (`rv`) data.
- Audit what fraction of GCVS variables, Zucker molecular clouds, and Edenhofer dust have any time information.
- Decide whether to (a) move only objects with velocity data, (b) move all stars and freeze everything else, (c) freeze everything outside the solar system.

### Question 2: hide-vs-keep policy at extreme epochs

Over centuries: probably fine to leave non-time-aware objects in place (clouds, dust, distant stars look the same).
Over millions of years: the Milky Way has rotated, the local dust morphology has changed completely, the molecular clouds may not have existed. Showing them in place is misleading.

Where is the cutoff? Do we cap the scrubber range? Do we fade objects out as `|t - now|` grows past their reasonable lifetime? Do we show a banner ("at this scale, only the solar system is correctly positioned")?

### Question 3: control widget design

What does the scrubber look like? Open shape questions:
- Continuous slider, or jump-to-date input, or both?
- Forward/back nudges (±1d, ±1y, ±1000y keys)?
- Auto-play (FF/RW with adjustable speed)?
- What time format does the readout use — UTC/Zulu, ISO 8601, JD, plain English? (This decision is shared with stellata-3re.H and should land there first.)
- Where on the screen does it live — `.ui-bottom` next to scale bar, or a dedicated affordance?

### Files likely touched (when implementation begins)

- New: `src/client/time-scrubber.ts`
- Touch: `src/client/stellata.ts` (extend `t` setter to fan out to catalogue rendering when motion is enabled), `url-state.ts` (add `t=` URL param when scrubbed away from now), `index.html`/`styles.css`.

### Out of scope

- The `t` variable itself (lives in stellata-3re.1).
- The "live" / "now" time readout when no scrubbing is happening (lives in stellata-3re.H).
notes:
Spun out of stellata-3re during a 2026-05-07 session: solar-system v1 only needed t=now display, not a scrubber. The deferred design questions deserve their own epic so future work can sequence the audits / decisions before implementation.

Children include the original 3re.2 (time-scrubber UI in .ui-bottom), reparented from stellata-3re.

title: Log zero-result search queries to surface catalogue gaps
description:
Capture and persist a record of search queries that return zero results, so we have signal for which alternate designations / catalogue entries are worth supporting.

User-stated category: defer. This is much more involved than the related catalogue-expansion work and should sit below it on the queue.

Why: today we have no telemetry on what users *try* to search for. If someone types 'V645' and gets nothing, we'll never know. A zero-result log would feed prioritisation of stellata-nxb (alternate designations) and any future catalogue additions.

Design considerations to think through before implementing:

- **Privacy / scope.** Stellata is a static Cloudflare Workers site with no backend currently. Logging implies either (a) a small Worker endpoint with a KV/D1 store, or (b) a third-party analytics SDK. Either way, what we're logging is just-typed text — needs care around accidentally capturing PII (low risk, but think it through).
- **Frequency / debounce.** The typeahead fires on every keystroke. Don't log per-keystroke. Options: log only on final blur or Enter; log only after N seconds of no further keystrokes; log only queries of length >=4; sample. Probably some combination.
- **Junk filtering.** Random keymashing, single characters, extremely long strings — exclude.
- **Signal threshold.** Need traffic to make this useful. Stellata has very low traffic right now, so the practical value is months-out.

Tasks (when un-deferred):
1. Decide infrastructure — Worker endpoint + Cloudflare D1/KV vs. minimal third-party analytics (Plausible, etc. — both already privacy-minded).
2. Wire client-side debounce + filter logic into search.ts.
3. Build a simple admin view (or just SQL queries) to surface frequent zero-result terms.
4. Feed findings back into stellata-nxb.

title: typeahead-util scrollRowIntoView: offsetTop semantics depend on offsetParent
description:
scrollRowIntoView in typeahead-util.ts uses row.offsetTop, which is relative to the row's offsetParent — not necessarily the list element. If the dropdown's CSS ever changes the list's position/transform such that it stops being the offsetParent of its <li>s, the scroll math would silently drift (rows would scroll to wrong offsets). The unit tests use a stub that hardcodes offsetTop, so they wouldn't catch this.

Fix options: (1) use row.offsetTop - list.offsetTop (works when list is also positioned), (2) use getBoundingClientRect deltas (more robust but layout-thrashes), (3) document the offsetParent assumption in the helper.

Surfaced in the PR #30 review (epic 9mm). Nit-level — confirm via the existing manual smoke (arrow-nav through long search dropdown).

title: Stellata.attachDust: dispose previous DustField on re-attach
description:
Stellata.attachDust(d) stores this.dust = d so dispose() can release the ~128 MiB Data3DTexture. Today attachDust is called exactly once, but the contract isn't enforced — a second call with a different DustField would leak the previous one (no dispose).

Fix: in attachDust, if this.dust is non-null and not equal to the incoming dust, call this.dust.dispose() before reassigning. Or document the single-call contract explicitly and assert in non-prod builds.

Surfaced in the PR #30 review (epic 9mm). Nit-level — no observable bug today.

title: DRY: angularToPx (viewport.y / fovYRad) divide duplicated at 4 sites in stellata.ts
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 review of commit 32077d7 (stellata-a7d.3 — distance vector lands on cloud silhouette).

The pixel-per-radian conversion `viewport.y / Math.max(fovYRad, 1e-9)` is now computed inline at four sites in src/client/stellata.ts:

1. `renderedSizePx` (line ~2550) — star disc diameter.
2. `renderedCloudSizePx` (line ~2613) — cloud silhouette diameter (added by this commit).
3. `renderedDiscPxAtPeak` (line ~2780) — variable-star peak disc, used by arrow-fade.
4. (older) — verify with `grep -n 'viewport.y / Math.max(fovYRad'` for the canonical count.

Each site reads the same two uniforms (`uViewport`, `uFovYRad`) and applies the same 1e-9 floor on fovYRad. A single `Stellata.angularToPx()` accessor would unify the lookup and the floor.

Sibling to:
- stellata-9mm.52 (broader physical-size formula triplication — that bead's `physSizePx(R, d, viewport_y, fovYRad)` helper would consume an internal angularToPx, but the divide is also used from contexts where the full physSize formula isn't appropriate, so worth a separate accessor).
- stellata-9mm.57 (1e-30 distance clamp duplicated at 5 sites — same DRY pattern).

Acceptance:
- New private/internal accessor `Stellata.angularToPx(): number` returns `viewport.y / Math.max(fovYRad, 1e-9)`.
- All four call sites use the accessor.
- No behaviour change; existing tests pass.
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: Prolate-cloud silhouette: largest-axis approximation overshoots end-on view
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 review of commit 32077d7 (stellata-a7d.3 — distance vector lands on cloud silhouette).

`renderedCloudSizePx(cloud, dCamPc, angularToPx)` in src/client/molecular-clouds.ts approximates a cloud's projected silhouette by picking the largest semi-axis as the radius. This matches `cloudViewingDistancePc`'s convention and guarantees the chevron tip never lands inside the visible silhouette — but for strongly prolate clouds viewed end-on it can overshoot substantially.

Example: cloud axes [10, 1, 1] viewed along the long axis. True silhouette is a circle of radius 1 pc; helper returns the diameter that 10 pc would project to — a 10× overshoot. The distance-vector chevron tip then floats noticeably off the rendered cloud edge.

Zucker 2021 catalog clouds are not all spheres; several are elongated enough for this to be visible at certain viewing angles.

Fix sketch: rotate the camera direction into cloud-local space using `cloud.quat`, then compute the silhouette ellipse semi-axes from the two axes perpendicular to that direction. Take the larger of those two as the silhouette radius (still conservative for non-axis-aligned views, but tight for axis-aligned ones). Pure math; no Three.js dependency in the helper.

Test: extend molecular-clouds.test.ts with a prolate-end-on case asserting the silhouette matches the perpendicular axis, not the longitudinal one.

Acceptance:
- `renderedCloudSizePx` accepts a viewing direction (or camera-relative position) and produces silhouette diameter using the projected ellipse, not max axis.
- Existing tests still pass (sphere → unchanged; max-axis aligned-view → unchanged).
- New test covers prolate-end-on case.
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: Test gap: worldOffset URL round-trip only covers pc-scale anchors; no kpc/Mpc test guards Float32 cam/tgt precision claim
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review.

src/client/url-state.test.ts:59 round-trips worldOffset with the pc-scale value `[51.6, 257, -37.7]`. The encoder's design comment at url-state.ts:135 explicitly justifies the float32 choice via "Float32 ULP at megaparsec absolute scale is ~10⁻² pc — invisible in any view because the user-visible pose is the cam/tgt offset *within* the local frame, and that's encoded at full Float32 precision relative to the anchor."

That precision claim isn't tested. The current test asserts the worldOffset itself round-trips at 3 decimal places, but doesn't pin the *cam/tgt* fidelity at large worldOffset magnitudes — which is the actual operational concern (user warps to galactic-centre vicinity, cam/tgt are tiny local-frame numbers, share URL, recipient lands in the same pose).

Fix: add a test that:
1. Sets worldOffset to a kpc-scale value (e.g. `[8500, 0, 0]` — galactic-centre direction) AND a Mpc-scale value (`[1e6, 0, 0]`).
2. Encodes a small local-frame cam/tgt at sub-pc precision (e.g. `cam = [0, 0, 5e-7]`, `tgt = [0, 0, 0]` for a close-approach pose).
3. Round-trips through encodeBlob → decodeBlob.
4. Asserts the decoded cam/tgt match the input within float32 ULP relative to the worldOffset magnitude — i.e. the *combination* (cam + worldOffset reconstructed in absolute space) lands within ~10⁻² pc at Mpc anchor.

This guards the architectural claim that worldOffset + small cam is the right way to handle large-coordinate poses.
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: Audit camera-busy guard sites in stellata.ts: document the decision matrix (no behavioural change)
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review. Re-scoped from the original implementation bead (split 2026-05-10): this bead is the audit-only deliverable; the migration follows in a child bead.

`Stellata` exposes two near-but-not-equivalent activity predicates:
- `isObserveTransitionActive()` — observe enter/exit, excludes 'unfocus' kind.
- `isCameraTransitionActive()` — warp || any observeTransition (incl. unfocus).
- `isCameraBusy()` — warp || aim || isObserveTransitionActive (added in PR #30 batch 2).

The manual `if (this.warpState || this.aimState) return;` (and variants) appears in 6+ places in src/client/stellata.ts:
- 1075, 1079 (setCameraMode — already migrated cleanly in PR #30 batch 2)
- 1267-ish (setFocus's observe-cleanup branch — checks via cameraMode)
- 1880, 1977 (focusStar / startWarp)
- 2267 (aimAt)
- 2774 (onPointerUp)

Each site has its own subset of (warp, aim, observeTransition, cameraMode==='observe', focusedStar !== null) checks, with no consistent ordering and no shared explanation.

# Scope (audit-only)

Output is a comment-only PR. No code-path changes:

1. Comment block above the `isCameraBusy()` definition documenting the decision matrix: which combinations of (warp, aim, observeTransition, cameraMode==='observe', focusedStar !== null) gate which classes of UI action.
2. At each of the 6+ sites, an inline annotation flagging the predicate as either:
   - `// equivalent: isCameraBusy()` — strict-equivalent, queued for migration
   - `// narrower: <reason>` — deliberately divergent, with the reason captured (e.g. 'cosmetic cloud picking suppressed during warp only — aim-while-pointing should still update the cursor cloud').
3. The site list at the top of this bead's description converted into a structured table inside the comment block (or a section in docs/architecture.md if it's better placed there).

The output of this bead is read by the implementation child to drive the migration without re-litigating each site.

# Smoke

- typecheck + vitest pass (no behavioural change, so neither should drift).
- Manual diff: confirm only comment lines changed; no expression / control-flow edits.
notes: Re-opened 2026-05-06 after partial completion in PR #30 batch 2. The isCameraBusy() helper and documented decision matrix were added on Stellata; one site (setCameraMode lines 1124-1128) was migrated cleanly (the pre-migration check was provably equivalent to isCameraBusy). The other 5+ sites remain on their original narrower predicates because each picks its own subset deliberately (e.g. cosmetic cloud picking is suppressed during warp only, not during aim) — those still need a per-site semantic audit before migration. Continue from here when the audit is scheduled.

title: DRY: 1e-30 distance clamp magic number repeated in 5 sites within stellata.ts
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review.

The literal `1e-30` is used as a close-distance floor in five sites in src/client/stellata.ts:
- line 2221 (aimAtConstellation, projection guard)
- line 2447 (pickStar, dCam guard for log10)
- line 2545 (renderedSizePx, dCam guard)
- line 2753 (renderedDiscPxAtPeak, dCam guard)
- line 3003 (shouldEnableCoreMask, tan(halfAngle) guard)

The comment at line 2543 explains the choice: "keeps log10(dCam) finite without clamping atan(R/d) at close approach." That reasoning is invisible at the other four sites.

Fix: name it once at the top of stellata.ts, e.g. `const DCAM_LOG_FLOOR_PC = 1e-30;` with the explanatory comment. Replace the five literals.

The unrelated `1e-9` floor used as the physicalRadius minimum in minOrbitDistForStar/minDistForStar (lines 2701, 2724) is a different magic number with different rationale — leave separate, but consider a parallel `MIN_PHYSICAL_RADIUS_R_SUN` if that ever gets a third site.
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: DRY: binary-companion floor block duplicated between minOrbitDistForStar and minDistForStar
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: DRY: pin-engagement guard (target.lengthSq() < 1e-12) duplicated across isPinEngaged, animate, and pin-debug-hud
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review.

The focused-star pin guard `controls.target.lengthSq() < 1e-12` is reproduced in three places:

1. `Stellata.isPinEngaged()` — src/client/stellata.ts:956 (canonical)
2. `Stellata.animate()` — src/client/stellata.ts:3089 (per-frame, picks uPinFocusToCenter value)
3. `pin-debug-hud.ts:102` — `const guard = t.lengthSq() < 1e-12;`

The threshold (`1e-12`) is the same magic number in all three; the surrounding focus / mode / animation predicates are also duplicated between (1) and (2).

Worse: pin-debug-hud already calls `stellata.isPinEngaged()` on line 103 to populate `pinNow`. The `guard` variable on line 102 is then a redundant standalone calc that just exists to display the predicate result. Trivially removable.

Fix:
- Have `animate()` call `isPinEngaged()` instead of re-implementing the predicate.
- Drop the `guard` local in pin-debug-hud.ts; display `pinNow` (and optionally surface the threshold constant) without re-deriving the bool.

Stretch: extract the threshold itself to a named constant `PIN_ENGAGE_THRESHOLD_SQ_PC = 1e-12` so the magic 1e-12 isn't a literal at three sites. Comment-rot risk if the threshold ever moves.
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: DRY: SHAFT_START=28 hardcoded in computeDiscCoverageAlpha shadows HudOverlay.getShaftStartPx()
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review.

`Stellata.computeDiscCoverageAlpha` at src/client/stellata.ts:2656 declares:
```
const SHAFT_START = 28;  // FOCUS_RING_RADIUS_PX + RING_HALO_GAP_PX
```
duplicating the live value already exposed by `HudOverlay.getShaftStartPx()` (src/client/hud-overlay.ts:243) and reconstructed independently as `FOCUS_RING_RADIUS_PX (24) + RING_HALO_GAP_PX (4)` in computeShaftStartRadius (hud-overlay.ts:441). `distance-vector-overlay.ts:14` also uses `SOURCE_OFFSET_PX = 28` for the same geometric reason.

The arrow-fade debug HUD already consumes the right getter (`stellata.hud.getShaftStartPx()` at arrow-fade-debug-hud.ts:81). `computeDiscCoverageAlpha` should do the same.

Fix: replace the local `const SHAFT_START = 28` with `this.hudOverlay.getShaftStartPx()` so the alpha calc tracks whatever shaft start the HUD actually used last frame (which is mode-aware — focus ring rim in nav, HUD ring rim in observe).

Caveat: `getShaftStartPx()` is 0 immediately after construction before the first `update()` runs. `computeDiscCoverageAlpha` is gated to navigate mode and a focused star, so by the time it runs HudOverlay has already updated at least once. Worth a defensive fallback to `FOCUS_RING_RADIUS_PX + RING_HALO_GAP_PX` if 0 — but ideally export those two constants from hud-overlay so they're the single source.

`distance-vector-overlay.SOURCE_OFFSET_PX` should also share the same constant since it has the same geometric meaning (start the shaft past the focus-ring rim).
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: DRY: ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION (0.9) and VAR_TROUGH_FLOOR_FRACTION (0.2) duplicated as bare numbers in star.vert.glsl
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review.

Two close-approach fraction constants are defined as named consts in src/client/stellata.ts and then re-typed as bare numeric literals in src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:

| Name                            | TS site                | GLSL site                  |
|---------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| `ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.9`     | stellata.ts:189        | star.vert.glsl:233 (`0.9 * min(uViewport.x, uViewport.y)`) |
| `VAR_TROUGH_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.2` | stellata.ts:2568      | star.vert.glsl:235 (`const float VAR_TROUGH_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.2;`) |

The TS comment at line 188 explicitly says: "Both the vertex shader (variability headroom) and minOrbitDistForStar use this constant; keep them in sync." That's the canonical smell — should be a uniform.

Fix: pass either a single precomputed `uMaxPhysSizePx` uniform (= `ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION * min(viewport.x, viewport.y)`, recomputed on resize) or two scalar uniforms `uMaxPhysFrac` / `uVarTroughFrac`. Either way the shader reads from the uniform instead of a bare literal, and the TS const becomes the single source of truth.

`renderedSizePx` already consumes `ZOOM_FLOOR_FRACTION` directly (stellata.ts:2553), so it would just stay using the const. The shader is the only consumer that loses the literal.
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: DRY: physical-size formula triplicated across TS + GLSL (renderedSizePx, renderedDiscPxAtPeak, star.vert.glsl)
description:
Surfaced in PR #22 (stellata-a7d.2) review.

The angular-diameter-to-pixels formula `2·atan(R·radiusFactor / dCam) · viewport.y / fovYRad` now lives in three places:

1. `renderedSizePx` in src/client/stellata.ts:2534-2592 (full app/phys blend with variability headroom).
2. `renderedDiscPxAtPeak` in src/client/stellata.ts:2745-2762 (physSize-only with peak amplitude factor — used by arrow-fade only).
3. `star.vert.glsl` line 332 (the actual shader physSize, plus the headroom calc at lines 228-243).

The variability headroom block (`maxUpLog10` / `maxDownLog10` / `ampLimitMag` / `ampEff`) is reproduced word-for-word between TS:2569-2576 and GLSL:236-243.

Risk: any future tweak to the size math (e.g. switching to a smoothstep blend at the pxSize boundary, or revisiting the trough floor) has to be applied in three locations, with no single source of truth and no test guarding the equivalence.

Fix: extract a single `physSizePx(R_pc, dCam_pc, viewport_y, fovYRad)` pure function in TS and a parallel GLSL routine inside star.vert.glsl. Have `renderedSizePx` and `renderedDiscPxAtPeak` both call it. Same for the variability headroom — one helper, two call sites.

Out of scope for this bead: TS↔GLSL sharing of the actual function body (would require a build-time include or a constants module emitting GLSL). The two `physSizePx` definitions can stay separate, but with paired comments + a regression test asserting they produce the same numeric output for representative inputs (R=Sol, R=Betelgeuse at typical d/FOV).
close_reason: Addressed in PR #30 batch 2 (9mm.51 surgery follow-up). 9mm.59 helper added + one-site migration; remaining sites left as documented per the bead's audit-first warning.

title: (EPIC) Visual refinements — perceptual polish on top of the physical model
description:
Aesthetic / perceptual polish that sits on top of the a7d physical-scale rewrite. Each child is a refinement, not a fix — visual quality wins that aren't on the critical path for shipping the physical model itself.

Initial children (moved out of a7d.2 once that epic finished its core scope):

- stellata-a7d.2.9 — Cinematic flyby of source star with arc/clothoid fillet (smooth warp transition).
- stellata-a7d.2.10 — Spectral/temperature correction to appSize (V-band → human-eye perceived brightness via BC_photopic).

Both are P3: small visual deltas, no critical-path dependency. Add new perceptual / aesthetic refinements here as they surface (e.g. limb darkening, atmospheric dispersion at the disc edge, etc.).

title: docs/rendering.md doesn't describe the depth-encoding pipeline (logarithmic depth + per-pass overrides)
description:
docs/rendering.md is the canonical reference for the star pipeline — three passes, super-Gaussian profile, depth-mask vs disc vs glow, manual gl_FragDepth writes. After PR #7 it has nothing to say about how depth is actually encoded:

- The `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` setting and its prerequisite role for stellata-a7d.2 are not mentioned.
- The interaction between manual `gl_FragDepth` writes and the `<logdepthbuf_fragment>` chunk in star.frag.glsl (the default-then-overwrite-then-conditional-halo pattern at lines 81/82/155) isn't documented.
- The fact that all four custom shader pairs (star/cloud/dust-particle/milkyway) require log-depth chunks isn't called out, so a future shader added without them would silently mis-sort against the rest of the scene.

Why it matters: rendering.md is in the always-loaded CLAUDE.md doc index — future Claude sessions read it before touching shaders.
