Without mention of the log-depth pipeline, a tweak to `gl_FragDepth` writes (e.g. for the future per-star physical-size override, or the molecular-cloud physical-shading work) could land without realising the chunk system exists.

Suggested direction: short subsection under existing 'Three passes' coverage — half a page — that documents (a) log-depth is on and why, (b) every custom ShaderMaterial needs the four `<logdepthbuf_*>` chunks, (c) manual `gl_FragDepth` writes must be threaded around the chunk include with the existing star.frag.glsl pattern (or use `= 1.0` for far-plane signalling, which is encoding-independent).

title: No precision regression test for log-depth ordering across the new dynamic range
description:
PR #7 expanded the camera frustum from `(near=1e-3, far=2e5)` to `(near=1e-10, far=2e5)` — a dynamic range of 15 orders of magnitude — and is relying on `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` to keep front-back ordering correct across that range. There is no automated test that exercises this.

`npm test` (244 tests) has nothing depth-related: search.test, controls.test, distance-util.test, etc. all run pure JS. The PR's verification was visual ('default view renders identically to main') which catches gross regressions but won't catch precision drift at the extremes — a star at d ≈ 5e-8 pc (the a7d.2 target d_min) sorting incorrectly against a 200 kpc galactic-disc fragment, for example.

Why it matters: this is a prerequisite PR for stellata-a7d.2, where d_min drops to ~5e-8 pc. The whole point of enabling log-depth was to keep z-fighting away from the close-range disc rendering. A future change that re-disables log-depth, accidentally drops one of the eight chunk includes, or restores a per-shader `gl_FragDepth = gl_FragCoord.z` write would silently regress that — and no CI signal would catch it. Visual verification in dev mode is the only line of defence.

Suggested direction: a vitest test using a headless WebGL2 context (or three.js's existing test harness) that:
1. Renders two unit quads at known distances spanning [1e-8, 2e5] pc.
2. Reads back depth via a render target with `THREE.DepthTexture`.
3. Asserts pairwise depth ordering across at least 4-5 distance buckets.

If a full GL test is too heavy, a JS-side approximation: assert that for the chosen near/far/log-depth-buffer settings, the float32 `gl_FragCoord.z` produced by the matrix chain is monotonic across a sampled distance grid. Cheaper but only catches CPU-side regressions (e.g. someone resetting near to 1e-3).
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Log-depth precision is a GPU concern — verifying it requires either rendering across the actual dynamic range (1e-10 to 3e4 pc) and reading depth-buffer samples, or a unit test of the log-depth chunk's varying math. The chunk is three.js stock code so it's not the unit being tested; what's worth pinning is end-to-end z-order at extreme distances. Pick up alongside 9mm.44 since both want a real WebGL2 renderer in tests. The depth-encoding section added to docs/rendering.md (this batch) documents the invariant in prose.

title: Dead-code default 'gl_FragDepth = gl_FragCoord.z' in star.frag.glsl after log-depth chunk added
description:
src/client/shaders/star.frag.glsl line 81 sets `gl_FragDepth = gl_FragCoord.z;` and the very next line includes `<logdepthbuf_fragment>`, which under `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` (now hardcoded in stellata.ts:473) always overwrites that value with the log-encoded depth from `vFragDepth`. The `gl_FragCoord.z` default only matters when log-depth is OFF — a state the renderer can never enter because the option isn't user-facing.

The PR added a comment (lines 79-80) explaining the chunk overrides this — which is honest but documents that the line doesn't do anything useful. The halo override at line 155 (`if (glow < uCoreThreshold) gl_FragDepth = 1.0;`) is the only non-redundant `gl_FragDepth` write remaining, and it's correct because 1.0 is the far plane in any depth encoding (already noted in PR description).

Why it matters: low — pure cleanup. No correctness issue. Worth noting because every reader of the shader has to mentally trace why a write-then-overwrite pattern is intentional.

Suggested direction: delete the line and the now-unnecessary 'logdepthbuf chunk overrides' explanation comment, rely solely on the `<logdepthbuf_fragment>` chunk for the default depth write, and keep the halo `gl_FragDepth = 1.0` override. Optionally collapse the comment block to a one-liner about the halo override.
close_reason: Closed as 'dead-code removed' in PR #30; PR #33 restores the line as a defensive default after the GLSL-spec analysis (undefined depth in any path that doesn't write gl_FragDepth when another path does). Net result: removal then restore with documented rationale.

title: Overlay near-plane clip threshold piggybacks on camera.near — semantically overloaded after log-depth switch
description:
Seven overlays read `camera.near` directly as the 'behind-camera / on-near-plane' clip threshold for screen-space projection:

- src/client/distance-vector-overlay.ts:163 — `const threshold = -camera.near;` (used twice for source + destination clipping)
- src/client/focus-ring-overlay.ts:66 — `if (v.z > -camera.near) ...`
- src/client/poi-overlay.ts:405 — `if (v.z >= -camera.near) return null;`
- src/client/disc-mask.ts:53 — `if (v.z > -camera.near) return false;`
- src/client/hud-overlay.ts:412 — `if (v.z >= -camera.near) return null;`
- src/client/constellation-overlay.ts:58 — `if (v.z > -camera.near) return null;`
- src/client/chart-labels.ts:748 — `if (projVec.z >= -camera.near) return null;`

PR #7 dropped `camera.near` from `0.001` pc (~3 mm view-space) to `1e-10` pc to give logarithmic depth buffer headroom for stellata-a7d.2's per-star physical sizing. That collapses the overlay clip threshold to effectively zero — overlays now keep rendering for objects in view-space `z ∈ (-1e-10, 0)`, which previously got clipped at `-0.001`. `camera.near` is now serving two unrelated purposes: GPU log-depth precision floor, and CPU overlay near-plane clipping. The two concepts no longer share a sensible value.

Why it matters: at the current orbit floor (DEFAULT_MIN_DIST_PC = 5e-3 pc) practical behavior is unchanged because the focused star's view-z (~-5e-3) clears both thresholds. But the implicit coupling is fragile — distance-vector-overlay.ts:178 also sets `endView.z = threshold - 1e-4` to nudge the clipped vertex behind the near plane; with threshold ≈ 0 that nudge becomes `z ≈ -1e-4` (in view-space pc), which differs from the prior `z ≈ -1.001e-3` by a factor of 10.

Suggested direction: introduce a named constant — e.g. `OVERLAY_NEAR_CLIP_PC = 1e-3` — exported from a shared module, and replace the seven `camera.near` reads with it. Decouples overlay clipping from log-depth precision. The PR description should ideally have called this out as a touched-but-not-tested interaction.
notes: Reopened 2026-05-06 after PR #30 rebase onto PR #22. PR #22 changes the orbit floor and overlays still read camera.near. The OVERLAY_NEAR_CLIP_PC decoupling may no longer be needed; re-evaluate the threshold against the new orbit floor and float32 cancellation regime.

title: Stale doc: camera-modes.md still cites camera.near = 0.001
description:
`docs/camera-modes.md` lines 11-12 still say `camera.near = 0.001`, but PR #7 (stellata-a7d.1) dropped the value to `1e-10` (stellata.ts:487). The 'strictly less than controls.minDistance' invariant still holds, but the cited value is wrong, and the doc never mentions that the new value is only safe because `logarithmicDepthBuffer: true` was simultaneously enabled on the WebGLRenderer (stellata.ts:473). A future maintainer reading the doc would conclude the near plane is at perceptual scale (1 mm), not at log-depth precision floor.

Why it matters: docs/camera-modes.md is in the always-loaded index in CLAUDE.md as the canonical reference for the near-plane / minDistance invariant. Out-of-date numbers there mislead future Claude sessions and humans alike.

Suggested direction: update the cited value to `1e-10`, add one sentence noting the log-depth buffer is what allows the aggressive near plane (it's the prerequisite for the per-star physical-size rewrite in a7d.2). Same paragraph could also reference docs/rendering.md once that gains a log-depth section (sibling finding).

title: Add a smoke test for Stellata.dispose() — instantiate, dispose, instantiate again, no errors
description:
The c2l audit landed without any test coverage at the dispose() entrypoint, so the next time a new layer is added (and somebody forgets to plug it into dispose()) the regression has to be caught by manual review again — same path that left coreMaskMaterial / particleMesh / clouds / galactic{Disc,Grid} unreleased before this PR.

Leak detection per se is hard to unit-test (no easy way to assert a Three.js Material was disposed without monkey-patching), but the cheap, useful guard is a smoke test:

1. Instantiate Stellata with a tiny synthetic catalog (the test suite already builds these — see catalog-loader.test.ts).
2. Call dispose().
3. Instantiate a second Stellata against the same canvas / catalog.
4. Call dispose() again.
5. Assert no throws and (optionally) that disposed === true.

A tighter version: spy on each owned resource's dispose() via vi.spyOn before teardown, assert each was called exactly once. That catches future regressions where a new layer ships without dispose() integration.

Direction: new src/client/stellata.dispose.test.ts. Reuse the in-memory catalog fixture from catalog-loader.test.ts. Skip if WebGL isn't available in the vitest environment (jsdom may need a stub renderer).
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Stellata's constructor instantiates THREE.WebGLRenderer which needs a real WebGL2 context. Running this in vitest requires either (a) headless-gl or a similar GL polyfill + jsdom, or (b) a Stellata test seam that lets a lightweight fake renderer flow through. Both are bigger than a unit test. Note that PR #30 already verified each owner's dispose() in isolation: DustField.dispose (9mm.38), HudOverlay.dispose (9mm.42), molecular-clouds.test.ts construction, MolecularClouds smoke. The remaining gap is the *integration* — does Stellata.dispose() walk all owners. Pick up when adding the first integration-test infrastructure.

title: Extract StarPipeline class for the disc/glow/coreMask trio (resource ownership + dispose)
description:
Stellata.dispose() (src/client/stellata.ts) is currently a flat list mixing two patterns:

(a) High-level subsystem dispose calls — controls.dispose(), clouds.dispose(), galacticDisc.dispose(), galacticGrid.dispose(), milkyway.dispose(), renderer.dispose().

(b) Hand-rolled cross-resource cleanup for the star pipeline — geometry.dispose(), material.dispose(), glowMaterial.dispose(), coreMaskMaterial.dispose() — plus a comment explaining the 'one geometry, three materials' invariant to future readers. That comment is the smell: the explanation belongs inside a class whose dispose() encapsulates it.

The same architectural fit applies in the rest of the file — MolecularClouds, MilkyWay, GalacticDisc, GalacticGrid each own their resources behind a dispose() contract. The star pipeline (the densest resource cluster in the app) is the one that doesn't.

# Scope (re-scoped 2026-05-10)

Resource ownership + dispose only. Per-frame uniform-write migration is **out of scope** — Stellata can keep mutating `this.starPipeline.discMaterial.uniforms.X.value` from outside. The encapsulation win here is the dispose contract + the resource-ownership invariant, not access discipline. If the leaky-access feeling becomes acute after this lands, file a follow-up at that point with concrete examples.

DustParticleLayer extraction is filed as a **separate sibling bead** (independent — different code, different concerns, dust layer is shelved per CLAUDE.md).

Direction:
- New `src/client/star-pipeline.ts` class owning `{ geometry, discMaterial, glowMaterial, coreMaskMaterial, discMesh, glowMesh, coreMaskMesh }`.
- Constructor takes the inputs Stellata currently builds (uniforms map, vertex/fragment shader sources, scene reference, render-order constants) and produces an attached, ready-to-render pipeline.
- `dispose()` walks all owned resources in the right order (the chart-mode blend swap on `glowMaterial` is just material mutation — nothing extra to dispose).
- Stellata holds it as one field (`private starPipeline: StarPipeline`); the existing fields (discMesh, glowMesh, coreMaskMesh, geometry, material, glowMaterial, coreMaskMaterial) are removed in favour of accessor properties on StarPipeline OR direct `this.starPipeline.X` reads at the call sites.
- `Stellata.dispose()` collapses the star-pipeline block to a single `this.starPipeline.dispose()` call, with the invariant comment moving inside the class.

# Smoke

Visual diff against main: open the app, focus a few stars (Sirius, Polaris, α Cen), toggle chart mode, confirm rendering identical at all magnitude / FOV settings. Particle layer (off by default) is unaffected since it lives separately.

- typecheck + vitest pass.
- Browser smoke against named stars to confirm disc/glow/coreMask passes still composite identically.
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Wrapping the star pipeline (geometry + disc + glow + core-mask + particle materials/meshes + their dust uniforms) in a StarPipeline subsystem is a substantial restructuring of stellata.ts. PR #30 made each of those resources individually disposable (9mm.35 disposeParticles helper, 9mm.38 DustField.dispose, 9mm.1 applyDiscBlendDefaults helper) which establishes most of the seams a StarPipeline would expose, but the encapsulation itself was scoped out. Worth doing alongside 9mm.44 (dispose smoke test) since the subsystem is what would make that test cleaner.
close_reason: Extracted StarPipeline class for the disc/glow/coreMask trio in PR #84 — completes the integration-shell decomposition epic (9mm.194 now 11/11).

title: url-state.ts has duplicate import lines from ./distance-util (stellata-6oy)
description:
url-state.ts:3-4 — PR #23 added `import { setUnit } from './distance-util';` on line 3, leaving the existing `import { getUnit, onUnitChange } from './distance-util';` on line 4. Two separate import statements from the same module on adjacent lines.

Trivial style nit, but it's the kind of thing eslint/prettier-organize-imports would catch automatically. Either consolidate to `import { setUnit, getUnit, onUnitChange } from './distance-util';` or run an import-organiser pass over the file.

Suggested direction: collapse to one line; consider adding a lint rule (eslint-plugin-import's no-duplicates) so the next merge can't re-introduce the split.

title: inferBinaries returns redundant primaries field equal to mutualPairs by construction
description:
`inferBinaries` returns `{ pairs, mutualPairs, primaries }` (catalog-pure.ts:244-246), but the implementation guarantees `primaries === mutualPairs` by construction (line 315: `return { pairs, mutualPairs, primaries: mutualPairs }`). The build-script log line at build-catalog.ts:678 prints both as if they were independent stats, which is misleading:

  `28 companion assignments, 14 mutual pairs (14 primaries) in Xms`

The parens-aside makes "primaries" look like a separately-derived statistic when it's a tautology with mutualPairs.

Direction: drop the `primaries` field from the return type (it's literally `mutualPairs`) and update the log line to either omit the redundant count or rename it to make the equivalence obvious. Pre-PR the field made sense because primaries was tracked via a Set; after the PR's mutual-only restructure it isn't. Minor cleanup.

title: dust-loader particle vs chunk size-mismatch handling is inconsistent (stellata-adr)
description:
PR #23 added file-size validation for particles.bin in loadDustParticles (dust-loader.ts:80-86). It console.warn()s and returns null on mismatch.

But the existing chunk path at dust-loader.ts:231-233 throws an Error on the same kind of failure (`if (buf.byteLength !== chunk.bytes) throw new Error(...)`). Same root cause (file-size invariant violated → can't safely parse), opposite reaction.

Why it matters: code review can't tell at a glance whether a corrupt voxel file should fail-fast or warn-and-skip. The two paths diverged because they were touched by different fixes; the codebase now has two conventions for 'bad file size' depending on which file. The chunk's caller catches the throw and continues with other chunks (line 216-220), so the user-visible behaviour is similar — but mechanism differs.

Also: catalog-loader.ts:94-97 throws on bad magic / version; dust-loader.ts:64-71 console.warn()s on bad magic / version and returns null. Same convention split.

Suggested direction: Pick one. Either (a) loadDustParticles throws like fetchAndUpload does and the caller (main.ts:113-115) traps; or (b) fetchAndUpload returns null on size mismatch like loadDustParticles does. Whichever, document it in a one-line comment so future code-quality gates aren't a coin flip.

title: Dispose particleMesh via a single helper instead of duplicating cleanup in attachDustParticles + dispose()
description:
PR #14 added particle-mesh disposal to Stellata.dispose() (src/client/stellata.ts:2931-2934) verbatim alongside the identical block already inside attachDustParticles (src/client/stellata.ts:1247-1251).

Both copies do:
  this.particleMesh.geometry.dispose();
  this.particleMaterial?.dispose();

(The attachDustParticles copy also scene.remove()s the mesh first.) Two parallel cleanup paths for the same owned resource is the classic DRY trap the audit was meant to surface.

Why it matters: when the particle layer eventually gains a third resource (texture atlas, secondary buffer, etc.), the change has to be made in two places or the dispose path silently leaks again — exactly the failure mode this PR was fixing.

Direction: extract a private disposeParticles() that handles geometry+material (and conditionally scene.remove()), and call it from both sites. Same pattern as the controls.dispose() / milkyway.dispose() encapsulation already in use.

title: Replace synthetic OVERRIDE-N CCDM keys with a typed group source
description:
In `parseHipCcdm` (build-catalog.ts:316-318), curated visual doubles are stuffed into the same `Map<string, number[]>` as real CCDM systems by minting synthetic keys like `OVERRIDE-0`, `OVERRIDE-1`. This is a type-system smell — sentinel-string keys in a typed map for what's really a discriminated union.

Issues:
- Compile-time the override keys are indistinguishable from real CCDM IDs. If the upstream CCDM file ever contains a literal `OVERRIDE-0` (theoretical but not impossible), the override silently merges with the file entry.
- The `scanned` / `viaOverride` / `kept` log accounting in `parseHipCcdm` is awkward partly because the override path injects entries before the file scan and then is filtered out during the scan.
- Reads less elegantly than just keeping override systems in their own list.

Direction: either (a) tag groups with an explicit source: `type CcdmGroup = { source: 'ccdm', ccdmId: string, hips: number[] } | { source: 'override', hips: number[], reason: string }` and return `CcdmGroup[]` (or two parallel arrays), or (b) keep `parseHipCcdm` returning only file-derived groups and have `applyDoublesFlag` consume both the parser output and `KNOWN_VISUAL_DOUBLES` directly, unioning at flag time.

Either way drops the magic prefix and makes the override path self-evident.

title: Add : void return types to applyTheme / registerThemeStellata for parity with bindChartMode
description:
PR #8 added `: void` to `bindChartMode` in chart-mode.ts:33 but theme-toggle.ts (touched in the same PR) still has two zero-return public exports without explicit annotations:

- src/client/theme-toggle.ts:14 — `export function registerThemeStellata(stellata: Stellata) {`
- src/client/theme-toggle.ts:18 — `export function applyTheme(mode: ThemeMode) {`

Both clearly return nothing. Since PR #8 touched this file and applied the void-return convention to one bind*-style export, the same convention belongs on these two as a consistency sweep within the file the PR already opened.

Why it matters: small DRY/consistency miss inside a file actively edited by the cleanup PR — exactly the gap the trivial-cleanup PR was meant to close. Note the wider codebase (~25 other exported bind*/create*/setX functions) also lacks return annotations; that wider sweep is out of scope here and is a separate stylistic call.

Suggested direction: append `: void` to both signatures.

title: applyDecodedView's controls.update() trigger condition is a 4-way OR that should be a single dirty flag
description:
src/client/url-state.ts:724 — `if (hasCam || hasTgt || view.up || willEnterObserve) stellata.controls.update();`

PR #1 grew this from a 3-way OR (hasCam||hasTgt||view.up) to 4 by appending willEnterObserve. The pattern is: "if I touched anything camera/controls cared about above, update." That's a textbook dirty-flag, not a chain that should grow with each new branch.

Why it matters: small but every future cause of "controls needs an update after I touched something" extends the OR. The test for "did I mutate something?" is the same boolean for all four branches; spelling it as four boolean expressions is mild jank and invites missing a fifth.

Suggested direction: at the top of the camera-touching block, declare `let controlsDirty = false;` and set it inside each branch that mutates camera.position / controls.target / camera.up, including the observe pre-snap. Then `if (controlsDirty) stellata.controls.update();` reads as the intent.

Low priority — purely a tidiness call. Mention only because PR #1 is exactly where the OR grew.

title: Apply ?? null/255 cleanup to search.ts:303 missed by PR #8
description:
PR #8 (stellata-y84 trivial cleanups) replaced `idx !== undefined ? idx : null` with `idx ?? null` in src/client/molecular-clouds.ts:184 but missed the same nit pattern in src/client/search.ts:303 — `const conIdx = entry.c !== undefined ? entry.c : 255`. Note the same file already uses the modern form at line 218 (`const conIdx = entry.c ?? 255`), so this is an internal consistency fix as well. Two further nearby instances at search.ts:399 and search.ts:407 use `idx !== undefined ? [...] : []` and cannot be reduced to `??` (they branch shape, not value); leave those as-is or rewrite as `idx === undefined ? [] : [...]` for consistency.

Why it matters: trivial-cleanup PRs should sweep parallel sites. The PR claimed to have reviewed all 54 source files; this miss in a file that already uses the preferred form shows the sweep was incomplete.

Suggested direction: 1-line change at search.ts:303 to `const conIdx = entry.c ?? 255;`.

title: Magic [0,0,0] literal in url-state — name it (OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL or reuse DEFAULT_TGT)
description:
src/client/url-state.ts:614 and 722 both spell the observe-mode camera default as a fresh `[0, 0, 0]` literal:

- Line 614: `const camDefault = mode === 'observe' ? [0, 0, 0] : DEFAULT_CAM;`
- Line 722: `stellata.camera.position.set(0, 0, 0);`

Meanwhile lines 39-41 already define `DEFAULT_CAM`, `DEFAULT_TGT`, `DEFAULT_UP` as named tuples. The new value is conceptually distinct from `DEFAULT_TGT` (different semantic meaning — camera *at* focal star, not target *at* origin) so reusing `DEFAULT_TGT` would obscure intent, but a fresh-name constant (e.g. `OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL = [0, 0, 0] as const`) would tie the two call sites together symbolically and make grep find both.

Why it matters: small but exactly the kind of duplicate magic value the file otherwise avoids — every other vector default in this module is named.

Suggested direction: add a named const at the top alongside `DEFAULT_CAM` / `DEFAULT_TGT` / `DEFAULT_UP` and use it in both the encoder branch and the decoder snap-back. Mostly relevant only if the broader generalisation in the sibling bead doesn't subsume it.
notes: Reopened 2026-05-06 after PR #30 rebase onto PR #22. Coupled to 9mm.20 (OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL); reverted with that bead. Re-evaluate the magic [0,0,0] in the worldOffset frame.

title: renderResultsDom / renderDom rebuilds set hoverIdx=0 but don't scroll the active row into view
description:
In src/client/search.ts:88 (SearchBox.render) and src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts:88 (renderQuery), every query-change rebuilds the dropdown and assigns hoverIdx = 0 (or -1 if empty). The render path sets `li.className = 'active'` on row 0 but never calls scrollIntoView on it, while applyHoverClass — invoked from arrow-nav — does scroll.

Result: arrow-nav and rebuild produce inconsistent scroll behaviour. In practice rebuild also resets resultsEl.scrollTop to 0 because innerHTML='' tears down the scroll position, so today the active row at index 0 is always visible after a rebuild. But the moment a future change either (a) adds incremental rendering instead of full innerHTML rebuild, or (b) preserves scroll across rebuilds, this asymmetry will surface as 'highlight is on a row above the visible window'.

Suggested direction: consolidate the scroll-active-into-view call into a single helper used by both paths, or have renderDom/renderResultsDom call setHover(0) as the final step instead of pre-baking class='active' into the inner loop. Folds naturally into stellata-xbj when the unification happens.

title: Update docs/camera-modes.md OBSERVE drag section to mention pointercancel teardown
description:
docs/camera-modes.md lines 196-242 give a thorough walkthrough of the OBSERVE-mode drag mechanism: pointer-down captures dGrabbed, pointer-move rotates, pointer-up promotes momentum on a tight gap. There is no mention of pointercancel.

PR #19 added a real branch to the lifecycle (pointer-cancel: tear down without flick) but the corresponding doc paragraph wasn't touched. Stellata's docs are generally exhaustive — see docs/camera-modes.md for proof — so this is a small drift but worth catching while reviewing.

Why it matters: future readers (including future Claude sessions) rely on the docs to understand the camera state machine. An undocumented branch is exactly how the next regression sneaks in.

Suggested direction: one short bullet in docs/camera-modes.md OBSERVE section noting that pointercancel mirrors pointer-up but skips momentum promotion, and why (OS-preempted touch is not a deliberate release).

title: Encoder/decoder cam-omission invariant in observe mode lives only in prose
description:
PR #1 split the cam=[0,0,0]-in-observe optimisation across two files-of-the-mind that never reference each other in code:

- Encoder side: src/client/url-state.ts:614 — `const camDefault = mode === 'observe' ? [0, 0, 0] : DEFAULT_CAM;`
- Decoder side: src/client/url-state.ts:720-723 — `const willEnterObserve = view.mode === 'observe' && stellata.getFocusedStar() !== null; if (willEnterObserve && !hasCam) stellata.camera.position.set(0, 0, 0);`

The linkage is documented only in comments at 608-613 and 715-719. If a future change renames the observe enum, adds a third mode, or shifts the floating-origin invariant (camera no longer parks at focal-star local origin), exactly one of these two sites will be touched and the other will silently disagree — receivers will land at the wrong quaternion or the URL will pick up 16 chars of A's again.

Why it matters: the cam=[0,0,0] sentinel is implicit, not a wire-format constant. There's no guard that catches drift.

Suggested direction: extract a single `OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL = [0, 0, 0]` const (or a named helper `observeModeCamDefault(view)`) referenced from both sites, OR fold the snap-back into a single `afterDecode(view, stellata)` step so the encoder/decoder duality is implementation detail of one helper. Bonus: a unit test that round-trips a full observe-mode `DecodedView` through encode→apply→capture and asserts the camera quaternion.
notes: Reopened 2026-05-06 after PR #30 rebase onto PR #22. PR #22 added a worldOffset URL field that reframes the cam-default check entirely: when the frame isn't Sol-anchored, cam is always emitted regardless of value. Re-evaluate the magic [0,0,0] under #22's worldOffset frame.

title: Generalise per-field default-omission in url-state encoder via FieldSpec.encodeIfDefault
description:
src/client/url-state.ts:605-623 — `currentStateOf` decides whether to emit cam/tgt/up by spelling out three near-identical `!approx(c.x, D[0]) || !approx(c.y, D[1]) || !approx(c.z, D[2])` branches inline. PR #1 made the cam branch a 4th brute-force special case (`mode === 'observe' ? [0,0,0] : DEFAULT_CAM`) rather than extending the FieldSpec abstraction. This is the exact failure shape the file's own header comment warns about — every new "omit when default" rule lives outside the field table.

The FieldSpec abstraction (lines 126-140, 146-160) already exposes `isPresent` but it only checks `v[key] !== undefined` — default-equality vs canonical defaults is hand-rolled per-field at the call site, in a different file region (~30 lines below the FieldSpec definitions).

Why it matters: any future "omit X when at default" rule (e.g. up=[0,1,0] check at 621-623, or future POI/cloud defaults) gets cut-and-pasted again, and a mode-aware variant like cam-in-observe will silently disagree with itself if the encoder default and the decoder snap-back drift apart.

Suggested direction: lift `encodeIfDefault` (or a per-spec `getDefault(state)` callback) into FieldSpec so `currentStateOf` does `if (!matchesDefault(spec, view, ctx)) view[key] = ...` in one loop. Mode-awareness becomes a context arg, not a new branch in encoder prose.
notes: Investigated 2026-05-08 in batch 3. Decision: not worth the structural cost. FieldSpec.isPresent already serves as the per-field omission gate; currentStateOf populates view fields conditionally, so 'absent in view' = 'omit'. The conditions in currentStateOf are heterogeneous (compare-to-constant, override-flag presence, mode-aware defaults, derived state) — a single declarative encodeIfDefault on FieldSpec would not actually simplify them. The 9mm.20/9mm.24 cleanups (defaultCamForMode + OBSERVE_CAM_LOCAL) addressed the worst case (mode-aware default duplicated across encoder/decoder) and that's the meaningful win in this area. Closing as wontfix-this-iteration.
close_reason: Investigated and decided wontfix — the proposed encodeIfDefault generalisation would not actually simplify currentStateOf's heterogeneous default predicates. The mode-aware-default subcase (the worst offender) is fixed by 9mm.20/9mm.24 instead.

title: typeahead-util.ts comment cross-references stellata-9kz instead of stellata-xbj for the unify follow-up
Adopt setPointerCapture in ObserveControls instead of routing through window
description:
src/client/observe-controls.ts currently registers pointermove/pointerup/pointercancel on the global window object so the drag survives the cursor leaving the canvas (lines 109-112, 119-121). PR #19 added pointercancel into this same window-routed pattern.

The canonical Pointer Events idiom is canvas.setPointerCapture(e.pointerId) on pointerdown, then attach pointermove/pointerup/pointercancel directly to the canvas. The browser then routes all subsequent events for that pointerId to the captured element (regardless of where the cursor is) and automatically fires lostpointercapture when the OS preempts the gesture — i.e. the exact stuck-drag scenario PR #19 was patching becomes a built-in lifecycle event.

Benefits:
- One listener-set on the canvas instead of split between canvas (pointerdown/wheel) and window (move/up/cancel)
- lostpointercapture is a single event covering pointercancel + window-blur + tab-hide + cursor-leave-page-area, which is broader than the pointercancel coverage PR #19 added
- No need for the activePointerId === pointerId guards inside each handler — capture filters at the source

Why it matters: PR #19 was a patch on top of a non-canonical pattern. Migrating to setPointerCapture would have made that patch unnecessary and would also cover the window-blur-mid-drag gap that pointercancel doesn't always fire for.

Suggested direction: capture the pointer in onPointerDown, release it (or rely on auto-release) in onPointerUp/onPointerCancel, listen for lostpointercapture as the unified teardown signal, move all listeners to canvas.
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). This is an architectural migration from window-routed pointer events to setPointerCapture, not a small refactor. Worth its own PR with a careful manual smoke pass on iOS Safari + Chrome + Firefox since the pointer-capture lifecycle differs across browsers. PR #30 took the narrower fix (9mm.18: window blur + visibilitychange listeners through cancelDrag) which covers the immediate stuck-drag failure mode this bead's lostpointercapture would also have caught. Pick up when there's a dedicated input-handling pass — that PR could also fold in 9mm.12 (ObserveControls drag tests) since both touch the same module.

title: Constellation-typeahead test hardcodes the cap value instead of importing TYPEAHEAD_MAX_RESULTS
description:
src/client/constellation-typeahead.test.ts:54 and :101 assert `out` length equals literal `10`. The constant lives in src/client/typeahead-util.ts and is re-exported through the binder, so the test could import it and stay coupled. Today, bumping TYPEAHEAD_MAX_RESULTS to (say) 12 in typeahead-util.ts produces a green typecheck but a red test, and the failure message will read 'expected 12 to equal 10' with no obvious link to the constant — the test descriptions even reference the constant by name ("caps total length at TYPEAHEAD_MAX_RESULTS") while the assertion uses the literal.

PR #29 perpetuated this smell when it changed the cap from 30 to 10 — both the value and a search-and-replace on the description string had to be updated in lockstep. The fix is mechanical: import the constant in the test and assert against it.

This same smell applied before PR #29 (was hardcoded 30), but the PR is the natural place to call it out because it just changed the value and the test descriptions in lockstep.

title: Add vitest coverage for ObserveControls drag lifecycle (down/move/up/cancel)
description:
src/client/observe-controls.ts has no vitest coverage. PR #19 added a pointercancel handler whose contract is 'mirrors pointerup but does not promote momentum' — a contract that is trivially testable with synthetic PointerEvents against the class instance, but instead the PR's test plan acknowledges only manual iOS Safari testing is feasible.

The class is small and side-effect-light: it takes a canvas, a camera, and getFov/setFov closures. An offscreen canvas + a fresh THREE.PerspectiveCamera in jsdom would let the suite assert:
- pointerdown sets dragging/activePointerId
- pointermove rotates camera.quaternion
- pointerup followed quickly promotes momentum (momentumSpeed > 0); slow release does not
- pointercancel clears state and never promotes momentum (regression test for PR #19)
- pointerup/pointermove with the wrong pointerId is ignored
- disable() clears all state and detaches listeners

Why it matters: pointer-event sequencing bugs hit exactly the platforms hardest to manually test (iOS phone-call interrupt, system-gesture preemption). A unit test for pointercancel-no-momentum is the cheapest possible regression net.

Suggested direction: src/client/observe-controls.test.ts under vitest, dispatching new PointerEvent('pointer*', { pointerId, clientX, clientY }) on the canvas and window as appropriate.
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Vitest runs in environment:'node' and the project has no jsdom/happy-dom dep. The test would need either (a) adding jsdom as a devDependency + per-file environment override, or (b) hand-rolled stubs for window / document / HTMLCanvasElement and PointerEvent — feasible but heavyweight enough to deserve a focused PR. Note that 9mm.39 (this batch) added pure-linalg coverage for the camera.up alignment, so the riskiest 9mm.18-related logic is already pinned; what's still uncovered is the pointer-event sequencing itself.

title: Test gap: no regression test guards disc-pass blend equation across material lifetimes
description:
PR #25 had to update two parallel sites for the AddEquation -> MaxEquation switch (constructor + chart-mode swap-back). There is no automated check that the disc material's blendEquation matches the documented intent at any point in its lifecycle, so a future drift between the two locations would only be caught by manual visual QA against close binaries.

The vitest suite already covers pure helpers (parseSpectral, physicalRadius, GCVS parsers). A small DOM-light assertion test in src/client/ that:

1. Constructs a Stellata (or extracts the material-setup logic to a small testable helper).
2. Asserts material.blendEquation === THREE.MaxEquation, blendSrc === OneFactor, blendDst === OneFactor, blending === CustomBlending.
3. Toggles setMonochrome(true) then setMonochrome(false) and re-asserts the same four properties on the disc material.

...would catch this regression class. Pair naturally with stellata-9mm.1 (extract a helper) — the helper is what the test asserts on.

Suggested direction: extract applyDiscBlendDefaults(material) per stellata-9mm.1 and write a unit test that calls it then verifies the four properties; toggle test verifies setMonochrome(false) re-applies them.

title: Hoist the 'active' CSS class literal out of the four typeahead call sites
description:
The string literal 'active' is now hard-coded in four places across three files after PR #29:
- src/client/typeahead-util.ts:25  classList.remove('active')
- src/client/typeahead-util.ts:29  classList.add('active')
- src/client/search.ts:100         li.className = i === this.hoverIdx ? 'active' : ''
- src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts:73  li.className = i === hoverIdx ? 'active' : ''

This is the first DRY violation introduced by the new shared module — typeahead-util.ts owns the toggle but the two binders still rebuild rows with the same literal in their own renderDom paths. A typo in any of the four would silently break the highlight on either arrow-nav or rebuild without a test failing.

Why it matters: PR #29 created the perfect place to centralise this (typeahead-util.ts) but only moved two of the four occurrences. Either export an HOVER_CLASS constant from typeahead-util.ts and consume it in both render fns, or factor the per-row `<li>` creation into a helper that owns the class. Note that styles.css couples '.search-results li.active' (line 263) and 'body.monochrome .search-results li.active' (line 949) to the same string; CSS will need the same name. Folded into stellata-xbj's unify work, but worth flagging separately so it isn't lost if xbj is deferred.

Suggested direction: `export const TYPEAHEAD_ACTIVE_CLASS = 'active'` in typeahead-util.ts, replace all four sites.

title: Hide viewSpaceScreenDir scratch vector inside arrow-path.ts
description:
src/client/arrow-path.ts:33-44 — viewSpaceScreenDir takes a caller-supplied scratch Vector3, but every other Three.js helper module in src/client owns its scratch state internally as closure-private state (focus-ring-overlay.ts:8 'const v = new THREE.Vector3()', disc-mask.ts:36, distance-vector-overlay.ts:30-31). Pushing scratch out as an API parameter leaks an implementation detail to two call sites that currently have to remember to pass an unaliased Vector3, and it asymmetrically singles out this helper from buildArrowSvgPath next door (which is allocation-free by virtue of returning a string).

Compounding minor: transformDirection already normalises the 3D vector internally, so the explicit Math.hypot + division renormalises the projected 2D pair a second time. Functionally fine, just slightly wasteful and obscures that the input is already on the unit sphere.

Suggested direction: declare 'const scratchVS = new THREE.Vector3()' at module scope inside arrow-path.ts and drop the third parameter. Two call sites (hud-overlay.ts:277, poi-overlay.ts:325) lose a thread-through arg. If shared scratch across helpers is desirable, move all overlay scratch vectors into one module-level pool.

title: Architectural audit: revisit halo-transparency mechanism (gl_FragDepth=1.0) under MaxEquation
description:
The disc fragment shader's 'halo transparency' rule (src/client/shaders/star.frag.glsl line 155, 'if (glow < uCoreThreshold) gl_FragDepth = 1.0;') was designed for premultiplied-alpha blending: a dim halo fragment painted dim colour with low alpha, and pushing depth to the far plane let the later additive-glow pass deposit distant stars on top through that haze.

Under MaxEquation, the framebuffer ends up with max(haloRGB, dst) — i.e. a saturated halo always wins, a dim halo over a brighter background gets clipped to zero contribution. The depth=1.0 trick still allows depth-test pass for later glows, but the value of the halo painting at all is qualitatively different: a halo over the Milky Way no longer adds, it gates by max. PR #25 explicitly calls this an intended tradeoff ('faint halos against bright backgrounds wash out'), but the asymmetry between disc halo and glow pass (additive) over the same background may now produce a hard discontinuity at vPhysRatio=0.5 that wasn't there before.

Not asking to revert — the artifact fix is the right call. Just want a short investigation: render a single mid-distance star against the MW band, sweep vPhysRatio across the 0.5 split, and confirm there's no visible step. If there is, the unified super-Gaussian + plateau profile (docs/rendering.md) may need a corresponding tweak to the pass-split smoothstep so the two passes meet smoothly under the new blend regime.

Suggested direction: targeted visual A/B at the vPhysRatio=0.5 boundary; if a step appears, explore narrowing the discard fringe or softening the pass-split criterion at the boundary.
notes: Deferred from PR #X (2026-05-08 batch 3). Re-affirmed: requires visual A/B at vPhysRatio=0.5 boundary with the renderer running, not a code-only task. Pick up when there's a session for visual QA against ESO eso0932a, or when a user reports a hard-edge artefact at the disc/glow split.
close_reason: Visual A/B implicitly performed through normal usage since PR #25 (2026-05-03) and the defensive depth-write hardening in PR #33 (2026-05-06). No vPhysRatio=0.5 discontinuity observed against the MW band. logarithmicDepthBuffer + defensive gl_FragDepth write both verified still in place (stellata.ts:633, star.frag.glsl:77).

title: Unify duplicate projectToScreen helpers in hud-overlay + poi-overlay
description:
src/client/hud-overlay.ts (lines 405-415) and src/client/poi-overlay.ts (lines 398-408) contain byte-identical private projectToScreen(point, camera, w, h) implementations: clone the input, applyMatrix4(matrixWorldInverse), bail when v.z >= -near, applyMatrix4(projectionMatrix), NDC → CSS pixels. focus-ring-overlay.ts (lines 62-74) and the inline conversions in chart-labels.ts / disc-mask.ts / constellation-overlay.ts implement the same pattern by hand.

PR #27 introduced a new shared helper (viewSpaceScreenDir) into arrow-path.ts to deduplicate the third-tier path but left the other two tiers' shared projection helper duplicated. Natural place to continue: lift projectToScreen into arrow-path.ts (or a new screen-projection module if arrow-path feels mismatched) and import from both overlays.

Bonus: the inline .clone() per call allocates one Vector3 per point per frame in a hot path — unifying lets us thread a scratch vector through one place rather than five.

Why it matters: ratifies and amplifies the duplication this PR almost addressed.
notes: Reopened 2026-05-06 after PR #30 rebase onto PR #22. PR #22 keeps projectToScreen local to hud-overlay.ts and uses a different cascade in poi-overlay.ts. Hoist to arrow-path.ts is reverted; consolidation should follow #22's structure if pursued.

title: Extract drag-teardown helper in observe-controls (3 copies of the same 4-field reset)
description:
In src/client/observe-controls.ts the same drag-state reset (dragging=false; activePointerId=null; momentumSpeed=0; lastRotAngle=0) appears in three places:

- onPointerCancel lines 199-202 (added by PR #19)
- onPointerUp lines 173-174 + the no-flick branch lines 187-188
- disable() lines 123-126

PR #19 introduced the third copy. The class already factors momentum constants and pixelToWorldDir, but the state-reset is open-coded. A private endDrag(promoteMomentum: boolean) helper called from all three sites would collapse the parallel writes and make the 'cancel = up minus the flick' contract explicit.

Why it matters: invariant drift. If a future field joins the drag state (e.g. lastMoveTimeMs needs reset, or a new pointer-capture handle is added), three call sites must be updated. The bug this PR fixed was caused by exactly this kind of partial coverage on a different controller.

Suggested direction: introduce private endDrag(promoteMomentum: boolean) on ObserveControls; call from onPointerUp/onPointerCancel/disable. No behaviour change.

title: Extract shared 3-tier screen-dir cascade from hud-overlay + poi-overlay
description:
PR #27 added a third-tier viewSpaceScreenDir fallback identically in both src/client/hud-overlay.ts (lines 248-289) and src/client/poi-overlay.ts (lines 294-334). The full 3-tier cascade (aux-step → target-projection → view-space) is now structurally identical in both files apart from variable names and indentation: same magic-number pixel thresholds (slen >= 1, tlen >= 1), same control flow, same comments verbatim. Future tweaks — adding a fourth tier, changing the threshold, swapping the comment — must be made in two places.

Why it matters: this is the bug-class that produced stellata-dy2 in the first place — divergent direction logic. Cementing the duplication for the fix doubles the surface area for the next divergence.

Suggested direction: lift a screenDirFromCascade(origin, dir, camera, anchorScreen, targetWorld, auxStepW, w, h) helper into src/client/arrow-path.ts (alongside viewSpaceScreenDir and buildArrowSvgPath). Returns [sux, suy] | null. Both call sites collapse to a single call.
notes: Reopened 2026-05-06 after PR #30 rebase onto PR #22. The 3-tier cascade was redesigned by PR #22 to a 2-tier (target-projection + view-space, dropping the aux-step tier) with per-axis FOV scaling. screenDirFromCascade is no longer the right shape. Re-evaluate consolidation against #22's design.

title: Comment-rot: 'premultiplied-alpha' / 'opaque-over' descriptions of disc pass are stale post-MaxEquation
description:
PR #25 changed the disc-pass blend from premul-alpha additive to per-channel max, but several authoritative comments still describe the old behaviour. New readers will be misled about what the pass actually does:

- src/client/shaders/star.frag.glsl line 9: '1 = disc pass (premul-alpha, close-range resolved stars)'.
- src/client/shaders/star.frag.glsl lines 139-140: 'Disc pass — only disc-dominated stars. Premultiplied-alpha blending; depth handling below decides whether each fragment occludes the background.'
- src/client/stellata.ts line 1569: 'disc pass is opaque-over in colour mode, multiply in chart mode'.
- docs/rendering.md line 43: 'Premultiplied-alpha blend + depthTest + depthWrite.'
- docs/rendering.md lines 198-202 ('Halo transparency'): describes the halo as painting 'dim alpha-blended colour' — under MaxEquation, alpha is independently max'd and the dim halo's RGB only paints if it beats whatever's already in the framebuffer.

Suggested direction: replace each with a one-line description of MaxEquation semantics, ideally pointing back to the canonical comment in stellata.ts so this rationale lives in one place.

title: Code quality — retrospective review fallout
description:
Umbrella for code-quality follow-ups surfaced by retrospective code reviews of merged PRs. Children are individually-actionable refactors / DRY / abstraction tasks. None are user-visible; collectively they reduce drift between parallel implementations and improve the codebase's bus factor.

Defaults to P3 per the prioritization framework (code-quality refactors that aren't user-visible). Bump individual children to P2 only if one becomes a blocker for in-flight work.

title: Unify SearchBox and constellation typeahead behind a Typeahead<T> abstraction
notes: Deferred from PR #30 (2026-05-06 batch). Unifying SearchBox (search.ts) and the constellation typeahead (constellation-typeahead.ts) behind a single Typeahead<T> abstraction is a multi-file restructure of the picker UX. PR #30 closed several of its prerequisites: TYPEAHEAD_MAX_RESULTS + TYPEAHEAD_ACTIVE_CLASS hoisted (9mm.10, 9mm.13), applyHoverClass scroll consolidated (9mm.19, 9mm.23), tests added for the shared helpers (9mm.6). The two binders are now structurally close enough that the abstraction is a smaller diff than it was, but still its own PR — picking it up should also re-evaluate stellata-xbj's children once they're filed.
close_reason: Shipped via PR #43 — Typeahead<T> abstraction unifies SearchBox and constellation-typeahead binding; net -163 lines, no behavioural change.

title: Typeahead result-cap design: scroll-to-find vs type-to-narrow
description:
The Focus / To star search box and the constellation typeahead share a TYPEAHEAD_MAX_RESULTS = 10 cap (defined in src/client/search.ts, imported by src/client/constellation-typeahead.ts). 10 was chosen so the wraparound point matches what the dropdown's 320px max-height + ~30px row height actually shows on screen — the user shouldn't arrow-nav past invisible rows.

Open question: is type-to-narrow the right model, or should we let users scroll the dropdown to find rare matches?

# Today's behaviour
- Star search: Fuse.js fuzzy search, capped to 10 results after dedup. Distinguishes overlap by score.
- Constellation: substring filter on (name + code), capped to 10. With 88 constellations and a sparse query, useful matches can fall off the end.

# Pros of current cap
- Visible == navigable. Arrow-down wrap matches the visible window.
- Forces the user to be more specific. Encourages typing 'ori' over scrolling Andromeda → Orion.
- Mobile-friendly (smaller drop-down works for thumb taps).

# Pros of allowing more results
- Discovery: a user who types a partial substring may want to see 'oh there's also X'.
- 88 constellation names is small enough to scroll through if you don't remember the exact spelling.
- Star search is fuzzy — sometimes the right answer ranks 11th if multiple stars share label fragments.

# Possible directions if we revisit
1. Raise the cap (e.g. 30) and rely on scrollIntoView to keep the active row visible. Wraparound at 30 is jarring but workable.
2. Keep the cap but show a 'type to see more…' hint when results would have overflowed.
3. Two-stage: cap to 10 by default, expand to full list on Tab or extra ArrowDown past the last visible row.
4. Make the cap dynamic to viewport height (CSS variable, JS-derived).

# Filed as part of stellata-eya
The cap was 12 (search) and 30 (constellation) before; the in-place arrow-nav fix exposed the inconsistency between visible-count and wraparound-count. Set both to 10 as the simplest unified behaviour while filing this for follow-up.

# Acceptance
Decide on a rule and either keep the current cap or land one of the alternatives above.
notes: Constant lives at src/client/search.ts:TYPEAHEAD_MAX_RESULTS. Comment there points back to this issue.

title: Decide: snap vs animated transition when clicking a new star to focus
description:
# Question

Currently, clicking a star (when focus is already on a different star or nothing) snaps focus instantly: setFocus runs, the floating origin recenters, the camera teleports to the new orbit position. It's fast but visually jarring — the world cuts to a new view with no continuity.

The OBSERVE-mode swap-anchor flow (`swapObserveAnchor`) handles a related case more smoothly: it preserves the camera quaternion and lerps the local frame between anchors. Could navigate-mode focus changes use a similar idea? Or should it animate as a flythrough — like a mini-warp from the current pose to the new orbit?

Possible approaches:

- **(A) Keep the snap.** Clicking is decisive and immediate; users who want a flythrough have the warp button. Cheapest. Status quo.
- **(B) Lerp camera position over OBSERVE_TRANSITION_MS-style duration**, recentering origin once at start. Smooth visual continuity, no precision cost (recentre happens once).
- **(C) Auto-warp.** Reuse the warp state machine. Always ends with the camera in the standard orbit pose, with the same arrival animation as a search-select.
- **(D) Hybrid.** Snap if the click is on the currently focused star (no-op anyway) or if the new star is within some local-frame distance; animate otherwise.

Not part of a7d.2 — file as a future UX decision. The current snap behaviour isn't broken, just potentially improvable.

# Where to look

- src/client/stellata.ts `focusStar` (~1735) — the search-select / explicit focus path; sets up an aim animation toward the new orbit position.
- src/client/stellata.ts `onClick` / click handler (~2676) — the click-to-focus dispatch.
- src/client/stellata.ts `swapObserveAnchor` (~2021) — the related observe-mode pattern.
- docs/camera-modes.md — warp animation 3-phase state machine (the relevant prior art for option C).

# Branch

Standalone UX bead; no dependency on the a7d epic. Implement on a fresh worktree when the user signals they want to take it on.

title: Decide: should Sol/GC arrows retire on unfocus, or focus ring persist?
description:
# Observation

When the HUD is on and the user unfocuses from a star in front of them, the focus ring disappears (expected — no focus) but the Sol/GC arrows stay drawn. The arrows are mode-bound (navigate), not focus-bound; the ring is focus-bound. The asymmetry feels odd from a UX standpoint — either both should persist or both should retire on unfocus.

This was bundled into a7d.2.11's bug report alongside the float32 unfocus jump, but split out here because it's a UX decision rather than a numerical-precision fix.

# Options

A. Hide arrows on unfocus until camera moves, matching the ring's disappearance.
B. Keep ring drawn on the (former) focal star until camera moves significantly away — symmetric with arrows persisting.
C. Leave as-is; the asymmetry isn't actually wrong (arrows are about navigation context, ring is about focus context).

# Where to look

- src/client/hud-overlay.ts — Sol/GC arrow visibility logic.
- src/client/focus-ring-overlay.ts — focus ring visibility (driven by getFocusedStar).
- bd memory stellata-a7d2-pin-mechanism for context on why this came up.

# Branch

If pursued, a small UX-only commit on its own branch — no code shared with a7d.2.11.
close_reason: Won't fix — intended behaviour. Focus ring is bound to focus state (correctly disappears on unfocus), Sol/GC arrows are bound to navigate mode (correctly persist). The asymmetry is by design, not a UX inconsistency.

title: Consolidate debug panels into one, with bigger sliders, collapsible sections, and drag-to-move
description:
The dev tooling has accreted three separate floating panels — debug.panel() (star tuning + milkyway tuning), debug.perf() (FPS + per-section frame timing), and debug.pin() (pin diagnostic HUD) — each with its own toggle, position, and styling. Cross-referencing them during calibration is awkward (especially when investigating a star-rendering issue that also involves perf characteristics).

Consolidate. Acceptance criteria:

1. Single 'debug panel' surfaces all three: tuning controls (star + milkyway), perf HUD readouts, pin HUD readouts. One toggle (debug.panel() or window.debug, pick one canonical entry point — keep the others as console aliases for muscle memory if useful).

2. Each section is collapsible (click section header to fold/unfold). Collapse state persists across panel re-toggle within the page session (sessionStorage is fine; not localStorage — calibration state should reset on reload).

3. Sliders are bigger. Today the tuning-panel sliders are ~tiny — hard to grab and to make fine adjustments. Aim for at least ~250 px wide and tall enough to click reliably without missing.

4. The whole panel is draggable within the viewport — grab a header bar and move it. Position persists across re-toggle within the session. Don't fight the user-select:text on readouts (the pin HUD body is selectable for copy-paste; that has to keep working — only the drag-handle area should grab pointer events).

Files:
- src/client/debug.ts — entry points (window.debug.* registration)
- src/client/debug-panel.ts — generic chrome (slider/colour/section helpers)
- src/client/star-tuning.ts, milkyway-tuning.ts — tuning sections
- src/client/perf-hud.ts — perf HUD
- src/client/pin-debug-hud.ts — pin diagnostic HUD

Out of scope: reworking the actual readouts/knobs each section exposes — that's pure UX polish for the hosting frame, not the contents.
notes: PR #44 open: https://github.com/alexmensch/stellata/pull/44. Branch worktree-stellata-n1t-debug-panel-unify. Will close after merge.
close_reason: PR #44 merged 2026-05-09 (commit 2d86a34): unified debug panel with drag + collapsible sections shipped

title: Spectral/temperature correction to appSize (V-band → human-eye perceived brightness)
description:
The appSize formula uses appMag = absmag + DM where absmag is V-band (Johnson V, centred near 550 nm). The user raised: shouldn't perceived brightness depend on the star's spectral class / temperature, since the human eye is more sensitive to some wavelengths than others and stars have different spectral distributions?

# Status of V-band as a proxy

V-band IS approximately photopic-weighted — Johnson V peaks at ~550 nm, the human photopic luminosity function peaks at 555 nm with similar bandwidth. So the catalog's V-band absmag ALREADY encodes the eye's spectral sensitivity to first order. This is the right primary quantity to use; refinements are small.

# What V-band misses

For very hot or very cool stars there's a residual mismatch:
- **Hot blue stars (O, B, hot A)**: peak emission in UV, V-band underestimates perceived brightness slightly because the eye picks up the deep-blue tail of their spectrum.
- **Cool red stars (M dwarfs and especially M giants/supergiants like Betelgeuse, Antares, Mira)**: peak emission in NIR. V-band underestimates because the eye still responds to the long red tail (extending past V-band's red cutoff toward photopic's red tail).

The standard astrophysical correction is the **bolometric correction (BC)**, but BC is for total power (bolometric magnitude), which is too aggressive for a visual model — we want the human eye's integrated response, not all wavelengths. A 'photopic correction' table (or a polynomial in T_eff or B-V colour) would be the right tool.

# What BC_photopic touches — and what it does NOT

Three rendering axes, only one of which BC_photopic touches:

| Axis | Driver | BC_photopic role |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Chromaticity (RGB ratio)** | Blackbody → sRGB LUT keyed on Teff (`stellata-zsr.3` / PR #89; refined by `stellata-zsr.4` once Apsis Teff is wired) | None — LUT is a chromaticity transform; BC is a luminance quantity, so chromaticity is BC-free by construction. |
| **Disc angular size (close-range pass)** | `R_physical / d` from Stefan-Boltzmann + V-band absmag + Teff | None — disc size stays geometric. V-band absmag is already a reasonable photopic proxy for the radius derivation; full bolometric is "too aggressive" for the visual model. |
| **Luminance (point-source glow + disc per-pixel intensity)** | `appMag` in the glow pass; intrinsic surface brightness in the disc pass | This is the axis BC_photopic corrects. Cool M giants get a small luminance bump; hot O/B stars get a smaller one. |

So this bead's correction lands on appMag (which drives the perceptual glow) and — verified at execution time — on the disc pass's per-pixel intensity if and only if it currently wires V-band absmag into surface brightness. Disc angular size doesn't change. Disc colour doesn't shift.

# Proposed approach

1. **Teff source: reuse zsr.4's routing helper.** `pickTeffSource(record) → { teff, via }` from `stellata-zsr.4` already returns a single canonical per-star Teff (priority: Apsis gspphot → Apsis gspspec → Ballesteros(B-V) → spectral-class T_TABLE → WD subclass → solar). Consume this helper directly — no separate Teff path for BC_photopic.
2. **BC_photopic(T_eff) polynomial fit.** Lives in `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` as a pure helper. Small everywhere (max ~0.3 mag in the M-supergiant tail, ~0.2 mag at the O end), zero near solar T_eff (Sol is the calibration anchor).
3. **Apply in shader**: `appMag_perceived = absmag + DM + BC_photopic(T_eff)` — BC is generally negative for cool stars (perceived brighter than V suggests) and slightly negative for hot ones. Replaces the current `appMag` everywhere the glow pass reads it. Audit the disc pass at execution time — if it reads `appMag`-derived intensity, apply there too (not chromaticity, not size).

The "store BC per star in catalog.bin" branch that the earlier version of this description considered is no longer needed: PR #89 already brought Teff into the shader at render time, `stellata-dch.49` will surface Apsis Teff per record (~85% coverage), and `stellata-zsr.4` provides the canonical Teff-picking helper. Computing `BC_photopic(teff)` in-shader on top of those is ~3 lines plus the polynomial.

# Why this matters more after a7d.2.7's soft-saturation lands

Once appSize stops saturating at very bright magnitudes, every mag of difference DOES translate to a perceived size difference. So small spectral corrections become VISIBLY relevant where they're currently absorbed by the saturation.

# Why this is P3 (not P2)

V-band is already first-order correct. The visual difference from a 0.2 mag BC correction is small — the star-exaggeration slider amplifies it but doesn't make the absence of BC feel broken. This is a refinement, not a fix; can land later in the zsr epic without blocking anything.

# Files likely touched

- `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` — BC_photopic(Teff) polynomial as a pure helper, exported.
- `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts` — pin BC at reference points (Sol = 0; M0V; M2 supergiant; B0V; O5V) with `toBe` per [[stellata-test-coverage-discipline]].
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — apply `BC_photopic(teff)` to appMag before the brightness compute. Reuse the Teff that `stellata-zsr.4`'s routing helper picks; do not re-derive.
- `src/client/shaders/blackbody-lut-pure.ts` (or sibling) — if a GLSL BC helper is added, mirror it as a pure TS function for tests.
- SCIENCE.md — document the BC source / methodology at commit time per [[defer-doc-updates]].

# References to track down

- Bessell, Castelli, Plez (1998) bolometric corrections.
- Wikipedia 'Bolometric correction' for the basic shape.
- Photopic luminosity function (CIE 1924 / 1931).

# Acceptance

- BC_photopic(Teff) applied to appMag in the navigate-mode brightness compute, consuming the Teff that `stellata-zsr.4`'s routing helper returns.
- If the disc pass also reads V-band-derived intensity, BC applied there too (verified at execution time); disc angular size and disc chromaticity unchanged.
- Sol unchanged (BC ≈ 0 by calibration).
- M giants visibly brighter than V-band suggested; B stars subtly brighter.
- SCIENCE.md updated with the source / formula.
- Test for BC_photopic at known reference points (Sol = 0, M0V ≈ -0.1, etc.) pinned with `toBe`.

title: Rendering-decisions FAQ / page: physical model behind size, brightness, perception
description:
Create a user-facing reference page (or FAQ) that explains the rendering decisions in Stellata's physical model, in the same level of detail as the a7d.2.7 investigation summary. Aimed at users (not just maintainers) who reasonably ask things like:

- 'Why don't stars appear very different in size at relatively similar distances?'
- 'Why does the disc start appearing only when I get close?'
- 'What's the relationship between brightness and apparent size?'
- 'Is what I see physically accurate, or stylised?'
- 'What does the star-exaggeration slider actually do?'

# Suggested structure

1. **What you're seeing.** The three rendering passes (core depth-mask, opaque disc for close range, additive point-glow for distant). Brief diagram or photo pair (close vs distant of the same star).
2. **Two visible-size sources.** `physSize` (true angular diameter 2·atan(R/d) projected to pixels) and `appSize` (Gaussian-PSF model perceived radius ∝ √brightness-above-threshold). Stars take `max(appSize, physSize)` — so the dominant cue depends on distance.
3. **The PSF model.** Why brightness affects perceived size at all (point source + atmospheric/instrumental PSF + eye threshold). Why the curve is √Δm and not linear. Saturation behaviour and the user-controllable K (star-exaggeration) knob.
4. **Why distant stars don't differ in apparent size much.** At 100s of pc all stars are far below the angular-resolution limit; size is brightness-driven; the brightness-to-size formula intentionally compresses (no one star should fill the screen).
5. **What the close-range disc shows.** When physical disc emerges; the variability pulse modulating disc radius for known variables; binary companion handling.
6. **What's NOT modelled.** Bolometric vs V-band corrections, atmospheric reddening (other than dust extinction), proper-motion through time, T-component of variable brightness change.
7. **Chart mode caveat.** Chart mode uses a separate flat-disc sizing (NOT the PSF model) — it's stylised on purpose, like a sky atlas. Don't compare chart-mode visuals to navigate-mode physical fidelity.

# Where it lives

Two reasonable options:

(a) New top-level user doc at `docs/rendering-decisions.md` (or similar), linked from CLAUDE.md's documentation index AND from a new help-modal tab. Existing `docs/rendering.md` is maintainer-focused; this would be the user-facing companion.

(b) Embed as a section in an existing user-facing artefact — README, info-modal, brand-modal — so it surfaces from a click without a separate page.

User's framing 'FAQ or a dedicated page' suggests (a) is acceptable. Worth checking whether the brand-modal / info-modal could grow a 'How rendering works' tab to surface it in-app.

# Source material

The level of detail in the a7d.2.7 investigation summary (the closing notes there) is the target. Pull from:
- docs/rendering.md (maintainer-facing — has the formulas + design intent)
- SCIENCE.md (physics references and citations)
- stellata-a7d.2.7 closing notes (specifically: PSF model rationale, why distant stars converge in size)
- stellata-a7d.2.10 outcomes (spectral correction details, when written)

# Acceptance

- Page exists and is reachable from at least one in-app entry point.
- Covers the seven sections above (or a clean reorganisation of them).
- Plain-English explanations alongside any formulas — not maintainer-doc-style.
- Updated when behaviour changes (e.g. when a7d.2.7's saturation soften lands, the page reflects it).

title: Smooth warp transition: cinematic flyby of source star with arc/clothoid fillet
description:
# Smooth warp transition: cinematic flyby of source star with arc/clothoid fillet

The warp animation has two readability problems that read as "pops":

1. **Source-side pop.** The reorient lerps the camera to put A between
   pStart and B, and *then* starts the bulk acceleration — the close
   approach to A is essentially over in the first frame or two of the
   bulk because position-time is symmetric while angular-size-vs-distance
   goes as 2·atan(R/d). The user perceives A as instantly disappearing
   instead of being a flyby.

2. **Destination-side pop.** Mirror problem at arrival. With a symmetric
   accelerate/decelerate position-time profile, angular size grows
   nonlinearly in the last few endOffsets and B "pops into existence"
   instead of smoothly arriving.

# Reframing — fly-by, not in-line deceleration

The user mental model is a **cinematic flyby of the source star**:
starting from whatever orientation the camera has at warp-start, smoothly
transition into the A→B warp path so the camera "swings past" A as it
accelerates. This is a smooth join between two line segments:

- Start tangent: the camera's current direction (or current radial-from-A
  vector — depends on launch pose).
- End tangent: +forward along A→B.

The term-of-art for joining two line segments at a waypoint with C¹
(tangent) continuity is an **arc fillet**. If we also want C² continuity
(curvature continuity, no centripetal jerk step) — the right choice if
we're simulating actually flying along the curve in space — that's a
**clothoid transition** (Euler spiral, linearly varying curvature along
arc length). Either gets us off the A↔B line so the camera literally
*flies past* A instead of through it.

# Failed prior attempt — preserved on archive branch

A first attempt was made in May 2026 implementing an **in-line angular
ease**: extended the reorient with a 1/mag-linear tail (camera moves
down the A→B axis past A) and replaced the bulk's symmetric ease with
bulk + 1/d_B-linear arrival. Approach discarded because it still treats
A as a point on the warp axis the camera passes through — wrong mental
model. The "reorient + departure tail + bulk + arrival" partition itself
was conceptually useful; the right reframe is to replace the in-line
departure with a fillet/clothoid that curves *off* the A↔B line.

The work is preserved on branch `archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease`
(pushed to origin). Single commit on top of the a7d.2 worktree HEAD at
the time of archival. To inspect:

```
git fetch origin
git log archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease -1
git diff archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease~1 archive/a7d.2.9-inline-angular-ease -- src/client/stellata.ts
```

Or check it out and run the warp from the dev server to feel why it
doesn't deliver the cinematic-flyby effect the user wants.

# Effect B — float32 projection drift then pin snap (separate concern)

During warp the floating origin sits on the source star, so the
destination's worldPos magnitude is ~|AB| (e.g. ~265 pc for Sol→Rigel).
GPU float32 precision at that magnitude is ~|AB|·1e-7 ≈ 2.6×10⁻⁵ pc —
comparable to endOffset (~3.4×10⁻⁵ pc for Rigel). The projected centre
can drift by a noticeable fraction of a screen in the final approach.
The pin (uPinFocusToCenter) does NOT engage during warp, so the drift
is visible. At finishWarp, recenterOrigin moves the origin to the dest
→ projection precision becomes essentially exact → pin engages → star
snaps to its mathematically perfect NDC position.

May or may not still be visible after the flyby fix. Mitigations if
needed: generalise uPinFocusToCenter to non-origin pin targets and pin
the dest to its lookAt-projected position during the final phase of
warp (requires passing target NDC or world position as a uniform).

# Investigation order

1. Implement the flyby curve (arc fillet first; upgrade to clothoid if
   the centripetal jump is perceptible). Arc length parametrisation +
   angular ease at arrival.
2. Re-evaluate the destination pop. The flyby reframes the source side
   only; arrival can still keep the 1/d_B-linear angular ease design.
3. If recenter/pin-snap is still visible, address Effect B with the
   simplest sufficient mechanism.

# Files

- src/client/stellata.ts — startWarp, updateWarp, finishWarp.
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl — only if Effect B needs a
  generalised pin.

# Acceptance

- Warp start reads as a continuous flyby of the source star — no
  perceived teleport across A, A's apparent size grows smoothly as the
  camera curves past.
- Warp arrival has B's disc grow at a perceptibly uniform angular rate;
  no pop at parking.
- C¹ continuity at the waypoint(s); C² preferred to avoid centripetal
  jerk if simulating physical flight.
- No regression on short warps (Sol → α Cen) where the existing profile
  already feels fine.

# Why descoped from a7d epic

a7d is about replacing the **physical geometry** of star rendering —
disc formula, orbit floor, and parking distance based on true angular
geometry. Warp camera animation is **visual/UX polish** built on top of
those parking distances; the transition's smoothness is independent of
whether the underlying physical model is right. Nice-to-have, not
on the a7d critical path.

title: Refresh docs at end of a7d.2 PR
description:
User explicitly deferred docs updates until they accept the feature is ready to merge. Per the global defer-doc-updates memory: don't edit docs/, CLAUDE.md, README.md, or SCIENCE.md during implementation; do it as the FINAL step before requesting merge.

# Already-committed docs that may need a refresh

Branch worktree-a7d.2-physical-disc-geometry has commit 1be9c0c "docs: refresh rendering.md + camera-modes.md for angular geometry" applied during the original implementation pass. These predate:
- commit 5035edf (uPinFocusToCenter pin + GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC bump)
- any work for stellata-a7d.2.1 / .2 / .3 / .4

So they're STALE relative to the final shape. Don't re-edit them mid-flight. Audit at PR-ready time.

# Process

1. After all sub-issues (.1, .2, .3, .4) are closed and the user signals "feature is ready to merge":
2. `git diff main..HEAD -- src/ scripts/ public/` — full final code diff.
3. Open docs/rendering.md, docs/camera-modes.md, CLAUDE.md, SCIENCE.md.
4. Update only what's now stale: orbit-floor formula, parking-distance formula, GLOBAL_MIN_DIST_PC value, uPinFocusToCenter mechanism, focus-ring/scale-bar UX changes, focusStar() default, warp pStart formula.
5. Don't add unsolicited new content; trim what no longer applies.

# Acceptance

Docs accurately describe merged code. User signs off before merge.

title: Hover tooltip distance: from-Sol vs from-camera decision
description:
The hover tooltip on a star (and cloud) currently always reports distance-from-Sol, but that label is ill-defined when the camera isn't at Sol — particularly inside OBSERVE mode where the user is conceptually "standing on" a focused star.

# Current behaviour

src/client/main.ts:223-228 (describeStarDetailed):

```ts
const p = catalog.positions;
const dist = Math.sqrt(p[idx*3]**2 + p[idx*3+1]**2 + p[idx*3+2]**2);
const ctx = [con, fmtDist(dist)].filter(Boolean).join(' · ');
```

`catalog.positions` is absolute ICRS Sol-centered pc, so the tooltip distance is always Sol-distance no matter where the camera is.

src/client/main.ts:201 — cloud hover uses `c.distanceFromSol` for the same reason.

# Decision options

A. **Always Sol-distance (status quo)** — simplest, consistent with the bottom-left scale bar's reference point. Reads weirdly when you're parked on Capella in observe and a tooltip says "Procyon · 11.4 pc" — pc *from where?*

B. **Always camera-distance** — "how far away is this from where I am". Right answer in OBSERVE; useful in navigate too when scouting around ("the cluster I'm looking at is 50pc, this star is 12pc closer"). Loses the absolute-scale anchor that some users may rely on.

C. **Camera-distance in observe, Sol-distance in navigate** — least surprising per mode, but doubles the mental model and makes the tooltip's meaning state-dependent.

D. **Both** — show Sol-distance always, plus camera-distance when the camera is non-trivially far from Sol. Cluttered but most informative.

E. **Distance from focused star** when one is focused, otherwise from Sol — matches the framing the user is currently in ("how far from the thing I'm looking at"). The focused star is the intuitive reference frame for orbit-mode exploration; falls back gracefully on no-focus.

# What to consider before deciding

- Camera-relative distance ties into floating-origin math but is cheap (Vector3 distance from camera.position to local star pos).
- Cloud hover: same decision applies. Whatever label scheme is picked needs to extend to `describeCloud` consistently.
- The "Distance · X" line in cloud tooltip is currently labelled identically to star tooltips' implicit Sol-distance — a label change (e.g. "Distance from Sol · X" vs "Distance · X") is a separate clarity question.
- This may interact with the bottom-left scale bar (currently a Sol-relative reference) — if we shift the tooltip to camera-relative, do we want a similar shift elsewhere?

# Deliverable

A written decision attached to this issue (which option, with rationale), then a follow-up implementation task.

title: Investigate luminosity-class differentiation in star rendering (red dwarf vs red giant)
description:
Audit how well the current star renderer reflects the physics of different luminosity classes. Concrete observation: Betelgeuse (M1-2 Ia-ab red supergiant, ~10^5 Lsun, R ~ 700-1000 Rsun) and Barnard's Star (M4 V red dwarf, ~0.0035 Lsun, R ~ 0.2 Rsun) do not look as different as they should — a red supergiant and a red dwarf are ~7-8 orders of magnitude apart in luminosity and ~3-4 orders in radius, so they should read as visually distinct on screen. May be partly an apparent-magnitude artefact (Barnard's is 9.5 ly away, Betelgeuse is ~550 ly), but the suspicion is that the disc-radius / glow profile is collapsing the giants and dwarfs toward similar visual sizes once the camera is close to either.

What to investigate:
- How physicalRadius() in scripts/catalog-pure.ts handles luminosity class — does it actually pull dwarfs down and giants up, or does absmag dominate and class get washed out?
- The disc/glow super-Gaussian profile (docs/rendering.md): does the plateau-vs-Gaussian morph differentiate I/II/III from IV/V at typical camera distances, or does it converge?
- Spectral-class colour: are M dwarfs and M giants colour-coded differently? They have similar surface temperatures but very different radii — colour alone won't distinguish them.
- What known stars to A/B compare for ground truth: Betelgeuse vs Barnard's Star, Antares vs Proxima, Aldebaran vs 61 Cygni, Rigel (B8 Ia) vs Sirius (A1 V). Note their canonical L/R values and check what Stellata renders.

Decide whether the visual gap actually reflects physics or whether the renderer needs a luminosity-class-aware adjustment. This is exploratory — the outcome may be 'it's correct, the dynamic range just looks compressed' or 'we need a class-dependent size/brightness multiplier'.

title: dust-loader: re-upload chunks on WebGL context loss
description:
Carved out from stellata-adr (which originally bundled buffer-size validation + context-loss handling). Buffer validation landed; context-loss handling is the remaining piece.

# What's missing

src/client/dust-loader.ts:226-258 — uploadChunk uses the (undocumented) Three.js renderer.properties.get(tex).__webglTexture path to push voxel data with gl.texSubImage3D. There is no listener for WebGL context loss / restore. On context loss, the GPU texture is destroyed; on restore Three.js reallocates a fresh zero-filled texture, but our uploaded chunks are gone and we never re-upload them, leaving permanent black holes in the dust grid until full page reload.

# Fix options

Two designs, neither obviously the right call:

a) Cache the per-chunk Uint8Array buffers and re-upload on 'webglcontextrestored'. Memory cost: ~128 MiB resident (one byte per voxel × 64 chunks × 2 MiB).

b) On 'webglcontextrestored', re-fetch all chunks via the existing fetchAndUpload pipeline. No memory cost, but re-downloads ~128 MiB and re-fills progressively (same UX as initial load).
