Option (b) is probably right: dust is decorative, and context loss is rare enough that an extra fetch is fine. The browser disk cache should also serve the chunks back without hitting the network.

# Acceptance

- canvas listens for webglcontextlost (preventDefault) + webglcontextrestored
- on restore, all previously-loaded chunks repopulate the texture
- particles.bin is also re-uploaded (or its mesh re-attached) — relevant for stellata.attachDustParticles consumers
- repro by triggering loseContext via the WEBGL_lose_context extension in dev console

title: Toliman at min distance: dark halo ring around disc — likely companion overlap
description:
Visual artifact observed when zooming to minimum distance on Toliman (α Cen B, HIP 71681) in navigate mode.

# What you see

A single bright disc fills the lower-left of the viewport (per user screenshot). Just outside the bright core is a clearly-visible darker gray ring/halo, then the falloff resumes normally. The halo reads as a separate object's outline rendered behind/inside the focused star's disc.

# What's happening (working theory)

α Cen is a tight system: A (Rigil Kentaurus) and B (Toliman) sit ~24 AU apart on average — within the 0.005 pc geometric-binary radius. At minimum-distance focus on Toliman, the camera ends up inside or extremely close to the *other* component's render volume, so we're rendering the companion's disc geometry on top of (or coincident with) the focused star.

The dark ring is consistent with the *companion's* core depth-mask being written under the focused star's outer falloff: the depth mask occludes the additive glow contribution from the other star, leaving a dark band where one star's mask sits inside the other's halo.

# Likely resolves under stellata-a7d

stellata-a7d (Physical-scale rewrite of star sizing & close approach) replaces the close-approach pipeline with true angular geometry. Under that model, two stars 24 AU apart at any reasonable zoom are spatially resolved — they won't share screen pixels at min-distance focus the way they do today. So this bug should fall out for free once a7d lands.

# Verify before fixing

- Confirm the dark ring is the companion's core mask (toggle the companion off / hide its disc and see if the halo disappears).
- If a7d is months out and this is still annoying, a smaller fix is to push the focused star's disc-pass forward in depth or skip rendering the companion when it's within the focused star's screen extent.

# Repro

Focus 'Toliman' in navigate mode, manual-zoom all the way in until the disc fills ~½ the viewport's smaller dimension. The dark halo ring is visible just outside the bright core.

title: Touch gestures: pinch-to-zoom + multi-touch behaviour on iPad / touch devices
description:
The mobile epic body already flags 'no pinch-to-zoom, two-finger roll is desktop-Safari-only by design' as a known gap. Even on iPad-sized viewports — which 47c.3 may decide is the only fully-supported mobile target — the current camera doesn't support pinch-to-zoom, so 'works on iPad' is currently aspirational.

WHAT'S THERE TODAY (src/client/stellata.ts + observe-controls.ts):
- TrackballControls (Three.js) drives the navigate camera. Its built-in touch handling is single-finger orbit + (we believe) two-finger pan. Pinch-to-zoom is NOT a TrackballControls feature.
- observe-controls.ts handles the OBSERVE camera (drag yaw + pitch, wheel FOV). No touch pinch.
- Two-finger roll exists ONLY on desktop Safari (gesturestart/change events) per docs/camera-modes.md.
- touch-action: none is set on #scene to prevent default browser gestures on the canvas.

WHAT NEEDS WORK:
1. Verify on iPad: do single-finger orbit + double-tap-zoom + the pan gesture actually work via TrackballControls' touch path, or are they broken too?
2. Pinch-to-zoom (navigate mode):
   - Map pinch span change to camera distance (matches scroll-wheel behaviour).
   - Implement via TouchEvent (iOS-friendly) since gesture* events are Safari-desktop-only.
3. Pinch-to-FOV (observe mode):
   - Same gesture, mapped to camera.fov instead of distance.
4. Decide what the two-finger drag does:
   - On desktop today: nothing in canvas (mouse drag = orbit, wheel = zoom).
   - On touch: Three.js TrackballControls treats two-finger drag as pan. We may want to keep that, or override to roll, or disable.
5. Single-finger drag in OBSERVE mode:
   - Currently observe-controls.ts uses pointerdown/move; should already work on touch since pointer events fire for touch. Verify.
6. Confirm the touch-action: none on #scene doesn't kill the gesture handlers we add.

CRITICAL FILES:
- src/client/stellata.ts — TrackballControls instance, existing onTouchStart/Move/End (currently used only for two-finger roll detection).
- src/client/observe-controls.ts — OBSERVE camera; pointer-event-based.
- node_modules/three/examples/jsm/controls/TrackballControls.js — to understand existing touch behaviour before extending.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing the desktop control scheme; replacing TrackballControls wholesale (the epic explicitly excludes that).

title: Decision: minimum viewport policy + mobile gating UX
description:
Decide how Stellata behaves on viewports too small for the desktop-tuned layout. The current settings panel + topbar overlap the canvas badly on phone-width devices, and full-bleed is impractical in browser iOS Safari (see 47c.2 notes). Several distinct UX paths are possible — pick one before sinking effort into per-control mobile redesigns.

OPTIONS to evaluate (these are the design space; deliverable is a chosen direction + threshold + copy):

A. Hard block below threshold
   - At <X width, replace the app with a simple gate: 'Stellata is built for tablet and desktop. Open this on a larger screen, or Add to Home Screen for an immersive full-screen experience on this device.'
   - Pros: zero effort tuning per phone breakpoint; one consistent fallback.
   - Cons: refuses users; may feel aggressive.

B. Soft warning modal (dismissable)
   - First-launch detect: small viewport → show modal explaining caveats + offering 'Add to Home Screen' instructions, dismissable to continue with degraded UX.
   - Pros: respects user agency.
   - Cons: more code, easy to dismiss-and-forget.

C. Banner/toast (always visible)
   - Persistent thin banner: 'Best on tablet/desktop · Add to Home Screen for full-screen'.
   - Pros: low-effort.
   - Cons: takes vertical space; nags.

D. Just polish best we can, no gating
   - Spend the effort making the panel collapsible, controls touch-friendly, modals reflow.
   - Pros: most users get a usable experience.
   - Cons: a lot of work, never quite as good as desktop, harder to QA.

E. Hybrid: D for tablet (≥768 width), A or B for phone (<480 or <600 width)

THRESHOLD QUESTION: where's the cutoff?
- iPad mini (portrait) is ~768 width × 1024 height — likely OK with desktop layout.
- iPhone Pro Max (portrait) is ~430 width × 932 height — settings panel covers ~65% of width.
- iPhone SE (portrait) is ~375 width — even worse.
- Landscape phones (e.g. 932 × 430) have ample width but very short height.

Recommend deciding on width-based gating in portrait; treat landscape phone as 'works but tight'.

ADD-TO-HOME-SCREEN COPY:
- If we recommend AtHS in the gating UI, draft the instructions inline. Apple's icon for the share sheet has changed across iOS versions; either show a generic step-by-step or a screenshot.

INPUTS to this decision (gather before deciding):
- Walk through panel + modals on real iPhone (covered by 47c.1).
- Render perf at iPhone resolution: how bad is it really? (Also 47c.1.)
- Telemetry: do we have any data on phone visits today? (Probably not; treat as unknown.)

DELIVERABLE: a written decision (which option + threshold + AtHS copy) attached to this issue, then file follow-up issues under 47c that *implement* that decision.

title: Roll-up: small comment/WHY/dead-code cleanups from code review
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84). Roll-up of small findings worth fixing in one cleanup pass; each is 1-3 lines and low-risk.

**Comment / WHY gaps**
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:201-216 — magic constant `VAR_TROUGH_FLOOR_FRACTION = 0.2` has no comment. Add a one-line WHY ("perceptual floor below which a variable reads as 'disappeared' rather than 'dimmed'").
- scripts/catalog-pure.ts:34 — comment says "C/S/W (cool carbon)" but code also matches N and R; extend to "C/S/W/N/R".
- scripts/catalog-pure.ts:113-123 — `interpolate` fallthrough is unreachable in practice; comment as such or assert.
- src/client/galactic-coords.ts:36 — note that `galZ` ends up parallel-to-but-not-equal-NGP after re-orthogonalisation.
- src/client/catalog-loader.ts:130 — note "byte 37: padding" after the amplitude read.
- src/client/catalog-loader.ts:144 — extend the existing nameOffset=0 sentinel comment with "(allows nameOffset=0 to mean 'no name')".

**Small dead/redundant**
- src/client/main.ts:74 — declare `window.stellata` in src/client/globals.d.ts instead of `as unknown as` cast.
- src/client/url-state.ts:142-144 — `fixed(n)` returns two functions both ignoring args; signatures are misleading.
- src/client/url-state.ts:32 + src/client/stellata.ts:73 — `ALL_SPECT_MASK` defined twice; export from one and import in the other.
- src/client/warp-button.ts:11 — `as unknown as SVGGElement` is a type lie; type as `Element` (no cast needed for `addEventListener`).
- src/client/observe-controls.ts:62-65 — fields could be `readonly`.

**Shader micro-wins**
- src/client/shaders/cloud.frag.glsl:29 — replace 4-component dot product with `abs(normalize(vNormalView).z)`.
- src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl:203-217, 291-294 — `baseSize0`, `logSpan0`, `logRatio0`, `sizeAtRef0` are computed twice (variability branch and non-variable branch); compute once outside.

**Galactic disc**
- src/client/galactic-disc.ts:131-156 — `makeRing` allocates a fresh `THREE.LineBasicMaterial` per ring (6 rings, all visually identical). Use one shared material; opacity update writes once.

**Star tuning panel**
- src/client/star-tuning.ts:13 + src/client/milkyway-tuning.ts:97 — closure-captured initial values become stale if `setStarRenderParams` / `setReddeningRGB` is called from elsewhere (URL state, future presets). Either document the no-reverse-sync, or read live state on each `onChange`.

**Style**
- src/client/molecular-clouds.ts:48-50 — `meshIndex: Map<string, number>` keyed by `mesh.uuid`. Store `mesh.userData.cloudIdx = i` at construction; raycast reads `hits[0].object.userData.cloudIdx`. Drops the Map.
- src/client/poi-overlay.ts:373 — `catalog: { hip: Uint32Array }` structural-typed param hides dependency; use `Catalog`.
- src/client/keyboard-shortcuts.ts:99-112 — `cTapTimer` non-null sentinel for "first tap pending" reads better as `pendingFirstCTap` boolean alongside the timer.

Severity: LOW/MEDIUM (cleanup pass; safe single-line fixes).
notes:
PR #47 open: https://github.com/alexmensch/stellata/pull/47. Branch worktree-stellata-nqb-finish. Will close after merge.

Final batch (PR #47, 2026-05-10): closing out remaining nqb roll-up items.

Done this PR:
* catalog-pure.ts T_TABLE row-7 comment: 'C/S/W' → 'C/S/W/N/R' to match the case statement at line 34.
* catalog-pure.ts interpolate(): added a comment that the post-loop fallthrough is unreachable for in-range subclass keys (0-9 inclusive across all tables), noting it's a defensive clamp.
* cloud.frag.glsl: replaced abs(dot(normalize(vNormalView), vec3(0,0,1))) with abs(normalize(vNormalView).z) — mathematically identical, one fewer op.
* poi-overlay.ts: imported Catalog and replaced the structural { hip: Uint32Array } param on labelFor() with the named type.
* star-tuning.ts + milkyway-tuning.ts: added a header comment documenting the no-reverse-sync (sliders snapshot getters at build time, won't update if URL state / presets write the params later).

Skipped as stale or cosmetic-only:
* VAR_TROUGH_FLOOR_FRACTION WHY comment — already documented in stellata.ts:212-216 since 9mm.53 lifted the constants out of GLSL.
* main.ts:74 globals.d.ts cast — already done in earlier work; no cast remains.
* url-state.ts fixed(n) signature lie — current signatures use _-prefixed unused params, the idiomatic 'I'm ignoring this' marker.
* star.vert.glsl baseSize0/logSpan0/logRatio0/sizeAtRef0 dedupe — bead's named locals don't appear in current code; the variability vs non-variability split was refactored.
* keyboard-shortcuts.ts cTapTimer rename to pendingFirstCTap — current code uses the timer handle's null/non-null state as the single source of truth, which is cleaner than a parallel boolean.
close_reason: Merged in PR #47 — comments/WHY gaps, type narrows, DRY star.vert.glsl, cloud.frag micro-win, star-tuning no-reverse-sync caveat all shipped.

title: Remove dead showMolecularClouds plumbing post-shelving
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

`FilterState.showMolecularClouds` was removed from the user UI when the molecular-cloud overlay was shelved for v1.0 (CLAUDE.md), but the field remains threaded through:

- src/client/stellata.ts:55, 334 — interface and DEFAULT_FILTER (always false)
- src/client/stellata.ts:1180-1189 — `pickCloud` checks the flag (always-false branch)
- src/client/stellata.ts:2693, 2739 — `clouds.update(worldOffset, false)` is always called with literal false
- src/client/main.ts:286 — `pickStar`-then-`pickCloud` chain runs through dead path

Fix: One of:
- (a) Remove the flag and the threading through. URL bit 2 stays reserved with a one-line comment.
- (b) Keep the flag but mark with a single comment "dead until clouds layer is re-enabled" and remove the conditional pluming so `clouds.update()` takes no arg.

Either way, drop the `pickCloud` invocation from main.ts since it's currently unreachable.

Severity: MEDIUM (cleanup; eliminates real dead code paths).

title: build-catalog: type CSV rows + SearchEntry + dedupe GCVS pipe-reader
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

scripts/build-catalog.ts uses untyped Records throughout:

1. **CSV row access** — `csv-parse` runs with `cast: false` and rows accessed as `row.x0`, `row.absmag`, `row.spect` etc. Every column is implicitly `any`. A typo'd column name yields `undefined` and silently corrupts the binary.

   Fix: Declare `interface AthygRow { x0: string; y0: string; z0: string; absmag: string; ci: string; spect: string; con: string; ... }` and cast the parser stream once.

2. **Search-index entries** (line 758-769) — built as `Record<string, string | number>` with stringly-keyed shorthand fields (`p`, `b`, `f`, `c`, `s`, `i`, `hip`, `hd`, `hr`, `gl`). The keys are a public wire contract with src/client/search.ts; one typo here ships a broken index.

   Fix: `interface SearchEntry { i: number; p?: string; b?: string; f?: number; c?: number; s?: string; hip?: number; hd?: number; hr?: number; gl?: string; }`. Build with that type to catch drift between writer and reader.

3. **Related**: scripts/build-catalog.ts:184-214 (`parseGcvsMain`) and 216-256 (`parseGcvsCrossref`) both implement: existsSync → readFileSync → split lines → trim-skip → split on `|`. Extract `function* readPipeDelimited(path)` yielding trimmed-field arrays.

Severity: MEDIUM (type-safety + DRY in build path).

title: Catalog binary format: extract shared RECORD_LAYOUT constants + flag-bit registry
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

The binary catalog format constants (`RECORD_SIZE = 44`, `HEADER_SIZE = 32`, `NO_COMPANION = 0xffffffff`) are duplicated between scripts/build-catalog.ts and scripts/verify-catalog.ts:11. The byte-offset map exists only as scattered comments at scripts/build-catalog.ts:708-714 and 727; e.g. `setUint8(off + 36, ampUnits)`, `setUint16(off + 38, periodUnits)` are bare numeric literals.

A single change to the layout requires touching two files plus the runtime reader (src/client/catalog-loader.ts). Drift between writer and reader silently ships corrupt data.

Fix:
- Extract `const RECORD_LAYOUT = { x: 0, y: 4, z: 8, absmag: 12, ci: 16, physRadius: 20, companion: 24, nameOffset: 28, spectClass: 32, lumClass: 33, conIndex: 34, flags: 35, ampUnits: 36, period: 38, hip: 40, SIZE: 44 }` to a shared module (e.g. `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` or new `scripts/binary-format.ts`).
- Import from build-catalog.ts, verify-catalog.ts, and src/client/catalog-loader.ts.
- Use the named offsets in DataView reads/writes.

Bonus: a single canonical record-layout block-comment listing every byte (used and reserved) so adding flag bit `0x08` later doesn't collide silently. Currently flag bits `0x08`, `0x20`, `0x40`, `0x80` are unused with no documented reservation.

Severity: MEDIUM (no current bug, but it's the most realistic vector for a future corrupt-binary regression).

title: Stellata event-bus consolidation: replace eight on*Handlers arrays with typed bus
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/stellata.ts has eight `on*Handlers` arrays and matching `on*Change` getters / `fire*Change` firers (focus, cloud focus, frame, camera mode, pois, vector, filter, ...). ~150 lines of mechanical boilerplate.

Fix: Replace with a single typed `EventBus<EventMap>` with `on(name, handler)` / `emit(name, payload)`. Concrete payload types follow through to subscribers in url-state.ts, controls.ts, etc. Eliminates duplication and tightens types at every callsite.

Optional sub-improvement: dedupe handlers on register (Set instead of Array) — currently nothing prevents double-registration.

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY — biggest single readability win in stellata.ts).

title: Unify SearchBox and constellation-typeahead; share escapeHtml
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Two related cleanups around search and the typeahead:

1. **`escapeHtml` duplicated** — defined verbatim in search.ts:557-563 and constellation-typeahead.ts:143-149. Move to a shared util and import.

2. **Two parallel implementations of the same UI** — `SearchBox` class in search.ts and standalone `bindConstellationTypeahead` in constellation-typeahead.ts are nearly identical: same input/results/active-row pattern, same listeners, same race-deferred blur, same render markup (`<span class="sub">`). The typeahead is essentially a `SearchBox` with a different filter function.

Fix: Have `bindConstellationTypeahead` instantiate `SearchBox` with a constellation-specific filter and item renderer. Removes ~150 LOC and a parallel implementation. (This is removing duplication, not adding abstraction.)

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY + reduces drift risk).
close_reason: duplicate of stellata-xbj (Unify SearchBox and constellation typeahead behind a Typeahead<T> abstraction)

title: Arrow shaft + screen-direction helpers shared by hud-overlay and poi-overlay
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/hud-overlay.ts (Sol/GC arrows) and src/client/poi-overlay.ts (observe-mode pinned-star arrows) implement near-identical algorithms:

- aux-step calculation: `auxStepW = (ARROW_PIXEL_LENGTH * dist) / focalPx` with `focalPx = h / (2 * tan(fov*π/360))`
- screen-direction derivation from world-space dir + camera (with target-projection fallback)
- shaft-shrink + `buildArrowSvgPath` + label-clamp block (~80 lines duplicated almost verbatim between hud-overlay.ts:283-318 and poi-overlay.ts:328-352)

The shared utility module `arrow-path.ts` already exists for the SVG path builder; the screen-direction and aux-step math are the natural next thing to live there.

Fix: Extract:
- `screenDirectionFromWorldDir(camera, origin, dir, target, w, h)` → arrow-path.ts
- `renderArrowAndLabel(path, bg|null, label, ...)` for the shaft+label block

Migrate both overlays to use the helpers.

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY — bug-fix risk: changes today require touching two places).

title: perf-hud: cache histogram spans, drop unused Stellata arg, single-pass row build
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

src/client/perf-hud.ts has two issues:

1. **Per-tick array thrash** (line 147): `Array.from(sections.keys()).filter(...).map(...).sort(...).slice(0,8)` allocates 4+ arrays at 5Hz. Single-pass build would suffice.

2. **innerHTML rebuild** (line 180): `panelEl.innerHTML = ...` reparses the entire HUD HTML, including 60 histogram spans, 5 times per second when the HUD is open.

3. **Unused parameter**: `installPerfHud(stellata: Stellata)` (line 81) doesn't use the parameter; it can be dropped along with the import at line 1 and the call site in debug.ts:64.

Fix: Build histogram bars as cached span elements once; update only `style.height` and `style.background` per tick. Build the rows array in one loop. Drop the unused Stellata argument.

Severity: MEDIUM (gated behind `debug.perf()` so impact is bounded).

title: Modal dismissal helper across info-modal/brand-modal/help-modal
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Three modal files implement essentially the same dismissal lifecycle (ESC, backdrop click, close button, keydown listener add/remove) with subtle drift:

- **info-modal.ts** uses ID-targeted listeners (`#info-backdrop`, `#info-close`).
- **brand-modal.ts** uses `[data-modal-dismiss]` selector AND uniquely couples two modals via cross-checks of `.hidden` (line 17): when closing one modal, it inspects the other to decide whether to detach the keydown listener. Adding a third modal breaks this.
- **help-modal.ts** uses `[data-modal-dismiss]` like brand.

Fix: Extract `bindModalDismiss(modal: HTMLElement, onDismiss: () => void)` that wires ESC, `[data-modal-dismiss]` clicks, and per-open keydown lifecycle (closure-scoped, so each open's listener is disposed on close). Migrate all three modals. Tag `#info-backdrop` and `#info-close` with `data-modal-dismiss` so they unify with the others.

Note: info-modal still owns its localStorage opt-in — keep that local; only the dismissal mechanics are shared.

Severity: MEDIUM (DRY win + the brand-modal cross-check is an actual fragility).

title: SVG overlays: dirty-track attribute writes + scratch-vector projection helpers
description:
Surfaced in code review (stellata-y84).

Several SVG overlays unconditionally rewrite attributes / textContent every animate tick. The dirty-tracking pattern from chart-labels.ts (compare proposed value to lastWritten, skip identical) is missing across:

- **hud-overlay.ts:308-318** — `path.setAttribute('d', d)`, `bg.setAttribute('d', d)`, `label.setAttribute('x'/'y', ...)`, `label.textContent = ...` per frame for both Sol and GC arrows.
- **poi-overlay.ts:88-92, 73-86, 191-204, 328-352, 362** — text and class writes every frame for every POI.
- **disc-mask.ts:46-61** — focused star + companion `cx/cy/r` writes every frame.
- **distance-vector-overlay.ts** — same kind of per-frame attribute writes; plus `getComputedTextLength()` at line 139 forces SVG layout every frame even when the destination label is unchanged.
- **constellation-overlay.ts:50-72** — segment string `setAttribute('d', ...)` per frame for every constellation in chart mode.

Also: **projection helpers in poi-overlay.ts:391, hud-overlay.ts:400, distance-vector-overlay.ts:161-179** all `.clone()` Vector3 inputs per call. Per frame × per POI/arrow this is the biggest non-obvious allocation cluster outside stellata.ts itself.

Fix: Adopt `lastD`, `lastText`, `lastX`, `lastY`, `lastR` fields per element; gate writes on change. Replace `.clone()` projection helpers with module-scope scratch `Vector3`s (poi-overlay already has the pattern for `tmpStarLocal` / `tmpDir` etc). Cache `getComputedTextLength` keyed on text content.

Severity: HIGH (multiple files, but each small; together they're the steady-state per-frame DOM cost).
close_reason: merged via PR #55 (SVG overlays dirty-track pass + scratch-vector projection helpers)

title: Chart-mode: distant DSO glyphs (galaxies, globular clusters)
description:
**Goal:** when chart mode is active, render the projected distant DSOs from `stellata-2q8` as type-coded chart glyphs at their RA/Dec — mirroring the symbology used by nebulae in `stellata-m40.2`.

**Scope:**
- Galaxies — traditional ellipse outline (oriented to angular major axis if known)
- Globular clusters — circle with crosshair (Sky Atlas 2000.0 convention)
- Other extragalactic types — fall back to galaxy ellipse or generic outline

**Constraints (from the m40 epic):**
- Chart mode is monochrome — line style and shape carry the type encoding
- Outlines should read at the same visual weight as constellation lines

**Implementation:**
- Extend `chart-mode.ts` / `chart-labels.ts` to register a DSO-overlay path
- Use the same projected-at-infinity celestial-sphere positions from `stellata-2q8`
- Symbology lookup from `stellata-m40.1`'s design reference

**Visibility:** active when chart mode is engaged. Picks up the same magnitude-floor filter that drives `stellata-2q8` in non-chart mode.

**Depends on:** `stellata-m40.1` (symbology design) + `stellata-2q8` (DSO data + base rendering).

title: Projected distant DSOs on celestial sphere (Andromeda, Messier objects, etc.)
description:
**Goal:** include the catalogued naked-eye / brighter-than-floor objects that exist *beyond* the model's spatial volume — Andromeda (M31), Triangulum (M33), distant globular clusters, other Messier galaxies — projected onto the celestial sphere at effective infinity. Filter visibility by the existing magnitude UI control.

**Why:** completes the perceptual model. A user looking at the sky should see Andromeda where Andromeda actually is, even though it's at ~780 kpc — well outside the model's volume. Computationally near-free since each object is a single point with no real 3D position to maintain.

**Scope:**
- **In:** extragalactic objects + distant globular clusters + any catalogued DSO that's *beyond the model's spatial volume* but brighter than the model's magnitude floor (see `stellata-cns` — the chosen m11 or m12 catalog cutoff defines the floor for all object categories).
- **Out:** objects already rendered in the model in 3D — M42 Orion Nebula (local SF region, comes via `stellata-9hp`), local star clusters, anything within the catalog's spatial range. Avoid double-rendering.
- **Wavelength:** human-visible spectrum only. No IR/radio sources unless their visible-band counterparts are visible.

**Magnitude semantics:** lower number = brighter. Magnitude *floor* = dimmest cutoff (highest number we render). 'Brighter than the floor' means apparent magnitude ≤ floor.

**Implementation:**
- Catalog ingest at build time. Candidates: OpenNGC (well-curated, commonly available), the Messier catalogue as a starting subset, or SIMBAD TAP for unified type-filtered fetch.
- Per-object data: name, RA/Dec, apparent magnitude, type (galaxy / globular cluster / etc.), angular size if known.
- Render: projected at fixed large distance (effectively infinity) on a celestial-sphere overlay. Direction = RA/Dec. Each object is a small glyph/disc + optional label. Type-coded visuals can match `stellata-9hp`'s nebula symbology where overlap exists (galaxies vs PNe vs globulars).
- Visibility filter: rides the existing magnitude control; objects with apparent mag > current display limit are hidden.

**Files likely touched:**
- New `scripts/build-distant-dsos.{py|ts}` for catalog ingest → `public/distant-dsos.json`
- New `src/client/distant-dsos.ts` rendering layer
- Wired into the magnitude-filter pipeline in `stellata.ts` / `controls.ts`
- Search index (`search-index.json`) gains entries with `kind: 'dso'` discriminator (parallels the cloud / nebula patterns)

**Out of scope:**
- Chart-mode treatment — handled separately via `stellata-m40` (decide there whether each type gets a chart symbol).
- True 3D positioning of these objects. They live on the celestial sphere; any user 'travel toward' them is a no-op (they recede with the camera).

**Relates to:** `stellata-cns.1` for the magnitude-floor decision that determines the catalog's apparent-mag cutoff.

**No hard dependencies — can be implemented before `cns.1` resolves; just parameterise the floor and let the magnitude UI filter.**

title: Wireframe outlines for nearby galactic structures (Magellanic Clouds, etc.)
description:
**Goal:** add wireframe outlines for visible Local Group members within 250 kpc of the Milky Way, plus distance-gated labels for both the MW (newly added — currently no label) and each labelled Local Group object. The MW label fixes a longstanding inconsistency with the heliopause's labelled apex. Together these give the user spatial context for *where* the Milky Way sits when fully zoomed out.

Refines [[stellata-data-fidelity-principle]]: rendered geometry is representational, but positions / distances / structural parameters come from peer-reviewed sources committed under `data/` per [[frozen-external-data]] — no live fetches.

# Blocked by

stellata-5gq (camera envelope bump to 500 kpc far plane / 250 kpc maxDistance). The 100 kpc max maxDistance today is too cramped for the layer to be useful.

# Visibility (no UI, no URL flag — explicit user decision)

The MW disc has no toggle today; this layer inherits that model. Each object's wireframe opacity tracks the SAME distance-from-Sol fade curve `galactic-disc.ts` uses today (`smoothstep(FADE_INNER_PC=500, FADE_OUTER_PC=5000, |camera-Sol|)`). Past 5 kpc from Sol, MW + Local Group outlines are at full base opacity; close to Sol they're invisible (same as the existing MW disc).

Per [[stellata-named-constants-and-dry]], `FADE_INNER_PC` / `FADE_OUTER_PC` hoist to a shared module (e.g. `src/client/galactic-fade.ts`) since they now have two call sites. Update `galactic-disc.ts` to import the hoisted constants in the same PR.

# Objects rendered (within 250 kpc; distance filter is the build-script's source of truth, not hand-curated)

- **Sagittarius dSph** (~26.5 kpc) — triaxial ellipsoid, the closest dwarf, tidally disrupting.
- **LMC** (49.59 kpc — Pietrzyński 2019, Nature 567, 200) — inclined disc + thickness rings. i ≈ 32°, PA_nodes ≈ 135°, scale length 4.5 kpc, scale height 1 kpc (van der Marel & Kallivayalil 2014, ApJ 781, 121; confirmed Ripepi et al. 2022, MNRAS 512, 563).
- **SMC** (62.44 kpc — Graczyk 2020, ApJ 904, 13) — triaxial ellipsoid with axes 1 : 1.33 : 1.61 (Subramanian & Subramaniam 2012, ApJ 744, 128). Line-of-sight elongated ~12 kpc.
- **Draco, Ursa Minor, Sculptor, Sextans, Carina, Fornax, Leo II, Leo I** — classical dSphs, oblate ellipsoids driven by LVDB's per-row ellipticity + half-light radius.
- Ultra-faint dwarfs that LVDB lists within 250 kpc — render whatever the filter catches. Geometry is essentially free (LineLoop with ~64 segments × ~3 rings × N is trivial); they're useful when zoomed in close to a satellite even if invisible at coarse zoom.

# Data pipeline (per [[frozen-external-data]])

- `data/local-group/lvdb-snapshot.csv` — committed snapshot of the [Pace et al. 2024 Local Volume Database](https://github.com/apace7/local_volume_database) `dwarf_all` table. **CC0 licence**, peer-reviewed (Open Journal of Astrophysics, arXiv:2411.07424). LFS-track if > 1 MB; uncompressed likely well under.
- `data/local-group/overrides.tsv` — hand-curated structural detail + label policy for objects where LVDB's summary row is too coarse for meaningful 3D rendering. Schema:
  `name, kind (disc|ellipsoid), inclination_deg, pa_deg, a_pc, b_pc, c_pc, label_threshold_pc, ref_doi`
  Three core rows initially: LMC (disc), SMC (triaxial), Sgr dSph (3D axis allocation since LVDB's projected ellipticity doesn't capture line-of-sight extent). Other dSphs use LVDB-only and need no override row.
- `scripts/build-local-group.ts` — reads CSV + overrides → filters by distance ≤ 250 kpc → merges per-object structural detail → writes `public/local-group.json`. Idempotent (mtime check, mirror `scripts/build-clouds.py`). Schema parallels `RawCloud` from `cloud-loader.ts:24–32` plus a `kind: 'disc' | 'ellipsoid'` discriminator and a `label_threshold_pc?: number` field.

# Client rendering (mirrors `galactic-disc.ts` LineLoop pattern)

- `src/client/local-group-loader.ts` — fetch + parse `public/local-group.json`. Shape parallel to `cloud-loader.ts`.
- `src/client/local-group.ts` — new layer module. Per object:
  - Position via RA/Dec/d_helio_pc → ICRS heliocentric XYZ (build script does the conversion so the runtime sees ready-to-use ICRS XYZ; no `GAL_TO_ICRS` rotation since LVDB + the LMC/SMC structural papers are sky-frame).
  - **Discs** (LMC only at 250 kpc cap): one midplane LineLoop ring + a thickness pair offset ±c along the disc normal, all rotated by `(inclination, PA_nodes)`.
  - **Ellipsoids** (everything else): three orthogonal LineLoop meridian rings on the principal axes, ~64 segments each.
  - Floating-origin rebase per frame: `group.position.copy(worldOffset).negate()` (same pattern as `molecular-clouds.ts:96`).
  - Material: `LineBasicMaterial`, base opacity comparable to `DARK_BASE_OPACITY` in `galactic-disc.ts`. Per-frame opacity = `smoothstep(FADE_INNER_PC, FADE_OUTER_PC, |camera-Sol|) × baseOpacity`.

# Distance-gated label helper

Heliopause's per-frame projection pattern at `src/client/heliopause.ts:217–315` is the template. Extract a generic helper:

```ts
// src/client/distance-gated-label.ts
createDistanceGatedLabel(stellata, {
  elementId: string,             // SVG <text id="..."> in markup
  samplePointsLocal: Vector3[],  // silhouette samples for support-point search
  labelDir: { x, y },            // screen-space direction (e.g. bottom-right)
  visible: () => boolean,        // per-frame gating predicate
  lerp: number,                  // screen-position smoothing
});
```

Per [[stellata-named-constants-and-dry]] second-usage rule, the helper IS the extraction: heliopause's label call site moves to it (predicate stays `stellata.anyOrbitRingVisible()`), MW + Local Group label call sites use it (predicate becomes `() => cameraToObject.length() > object.labelThresholdPc`). If predicate divergence makes the heliopause refactor noisy, leave heliopause alone and document the asymmetry in a comment.

# Per-object label policy

- **Milky Way**: `label_threshold_pc` ~10 kpc — label appears once camera is outside the disc.
- **LMC**: ~30 kpc — visible once you can see the whole inclined disc.
- **SMC**: ~30 kpc.
- **Sagittarius dSph**: ~10 kpc (the closest, smaller threshold OK).
- Classical dSphs: ~20 kpc default unless overridden; ultra-faints unlabelled.

SVG markup additions in `src/client/index.html`:
- `<text id="mw-label">Milky Way</text>` in the same SVG layer that hosts `heliopause-label`.
- One `<text id="lg-<slug>-label">` per labelled Local Group object, emitted dynamically at layer construction (mirror `constellation-overlay.ts`).

# Acceptance

- `npm run typecheck` + `npm test` green.
- Build script idempotent: re-running the build doesn't re-rewrite `public/local-group.json` if inputs unchanged.
- Manual smoke (per [[alex-stellata-smoke-tests]] — alex tests in his own browser, do not start a dev server):
  1. Zoom out from first-load. Past ~500 pc from Sol, MW disc fades in (existing behaviour). Past ~10 kpc from galactic centre, "Milky Way" label appears anchored to the galactic-centre projection, lerping into place.
  2. Continue zooming to 50+ kpc. LMC + SMC + Sgr dSph + classical dSph outlines fade in on the same curve. Each labelled object's label appears once camera is past its `label_threshold_pc`.
  3. Sky-position sanity check: LMC + SMC sit south-east of the galactic centre as in photographs; Sgr dSph sits ~6 kpc below the galactic plane.
- Reload at zoomed-out view (URL captures camera via existing v3 envelope, not via new flags) → outlines + labels restore to the same state.

# Test gaps to add (per [[alex-large-pr-honesty]])

- `build-local-group.ts`: distance filter, override-merge precedence, idempotency.
- `local-group-loader.ts`: parse round-trip on generated JSON.
- `distance-gated-label.ts`: support-point selection, near-plane-clip bail-out, lerp-snap on first show.
- Renderer + label engine are "low (needs eyeballs)" — flag the manual smoke list explicitly in the PR description per [[alex-large-pr-honesty]].

# Files likely touched

- New: `data/local-group/lvdb-snapshot.csv`, `data/local-group/overrides.tsv`, `scripts/build-local-group.ts`, `src/client/local-group.ts`, `src/client/local-group-loader.ts`, `src/client/distance-gated-label.ts`, `src/client/galactic-fade.ts`.
- Edited: `src/client/index.html` (SVG label slots), `src/client/galactic-disc.ts` (import hoisted fade constants), `src/client/heliopause.ts` (move to shared helper), `src/client/stellata.ts` (instantiate layer), `package.json` (build script wire-up if needed).

# Out of scope

- M31, M33, NGC 205, M32, IC 10, NGC 6822, Leo A, IC 1613, WLM — **separate deferred bead** (the new Bead C filed alongside this one; needs the 1.5–2 Mpc envelope).
- Magellanic Stream — invisible HI gas, user-confirmed exclusion.
- M31 / M33 / dSph chart-mode glyphs — `stellata-m40.4`.
- Galactic-disc fade-curve rework — current curve (full opacity past 5 kpc from Sol) is correct for this work.
- Local Group objects as focus targets — no plan now; revisit visibility rules if/when that changes.
- Star-catalogue extension to LMC/SMC/Sgr stellar populations — AT-HYG depth doesn't reach LMC/SMC reliably; Sgr dSph red giants marginal (see [[stellata-cds-data-sources-and-detail-gradient]]). File a separate follow-up if relevant.

# Data sources (committed under `data/local-group/`)

- Pace et al. 2024, *Local Volume Database*, Open Journal of Astrophysics (arXiv:2411.07424). CC0. https://github.com/apace7/local_volume_database
- Pietrzyński et al. 2019, *Nature* 567, 200 (10.1038/s41586-019-0999-4) — LMC distance.
- van der Marel & Kallivayalil 2014, *ApJ* 781, 121 (10.1088/0004-637X/781/2/121) — LMC structure.
- Ripepi et al. 2022, *MNRAS* 512, 563 (10.1093/mnras/stac495) — LMC Cepheid 3D map.
- Graczyk et al. 2020, *ApJ* 904, 13 (10.3847/1538-4357/abbb2b) — SMC distance.
- Subramanian & Subramaniam 2012, *ApJ* 744, 128 (10.1088/0004-637X/744/2/128) — SMC structure.

DOIs go in `overrides.tsv` `ref_doi` column AND in code comments at the call sites that consume per-object structural data, per [[stellata-named-constants-and-dry]].

# Documentation deferral (per [[defer-doc-updates]])

Don't touch CLAUDE.md / SCIENCE.md / docs/ during implementation. At commit time, audit the diff: SCIENCE.md gains a Local Group data-source section; CLAUDE.md module roster gains the new files + `data/local-group/`. Possibly a new `docs/local-group.md` if the layer earns its own doc — decide at commit time.

# Reference

Planning session (this bead's full design context): /Users/alexm/.claude/plans/let-s-start-plan-mode-nested-bentley.md
close_reason: merged via PR #67 (Local Group wireframes + MW label)

title: Chart-mode: planets of the focused host star at current t
description:
**Goal:** render the planets of the focused host star in chart mode at their angular positions for the current `t`, using the symbology + labeling rules resolved in `stellata-m40.1`. Currently this means Sol's planets only, since Sol is the only host with a planet table; the implementation must be host-generic so that any focal host with a planet table picks this up automatically when `stellata-bk5` (exoplanets) ships.

**Implementation:**
- In chart mode, the focused host's planet positions (currently from `stellata-3re.3` VSOP87 for Sol) project to angular sky coordinates
- Render per `m40.1`'s resolved symbology (glyph vs. magnitude-disc; non-Sol depiction convention)
- Labeling flows through the existing named-star path in `chart-labels.ts` (same dirty-track / collision contract)
- Time-aware: when the user scrubs `t`, planet positions update

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/chart-mode.ts`
- `src/client/chart-labels.ts`
- `planet-system.ts` / `planet-body-field.ts` may grow a chart-mode branch (or a sibling reader consumes the same per-host table)

**Visibility:** active when chart mode is engaged AND the focal star has a planet system. Outside that, planets don't render in chart mode. Filter mechanism (focal-host gate vs. apparent-mag self-filter) is decided in `m40.1`.

**Acceptance:**
- Sol focused + chart mode → planets render at correct angular positions for `t`, labeled per the named-star convention from `m40.1`
- No Sol-specific assumptions in the rendering path: when `bk5` ships planet tables for other hosts, focusing one of those hosts in chart mode produces planets without further code change
- Symbology matches `m40.1`'s output

**Depends on:** `stellata-m40.1` (symbology + open questions resolved), `stellata-3re.4` (Sol planet bodies; closed), `stellata-3re.3` (positions API; closed), `stellata-3re.16` (host-generic apparent-mag visibility cutoff; closed).

title: Chart-mode: nebulae outlines per type symbology
description:
**Goal:** render catalogued nebulae from `stellata-9hp` as chart-mode outlines using the per-type symbology defined in `stellata-m40.1`.

**Implementation:**
- Extend `chart-mode.ts` to register a nebulae-overlay path active in chart mode
- Per-nebula glyph in `chart-labels.ts` (or a new `chart-nebulae.ts` if the existing label engine isn't a clean fit)
- Type-specific symbology from `m40.1`: PNe symbol, H II outline, RNe outline, SNR outline
- Picking + label coordinates with existing chart-mode label engine

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/chart-mode.ts` — orchestrator hook for the nebulae layer
- `src/client/chart-labels.ts` — glyph integration (or new module)
- Possibly `src/client/shaders/nebula.frag.glsl` (from `stellata-9hp.2`) gets a chart-mode branch like the cloud shader's `uChartIsobar` path

**Visual gate:** a region rich in catalogued nebulae (e.g. Orion's belt + sword) reads as a star atlas — outlines indicate nebula presence and approximate extent without dominating the page.

**Depends on:** `stellata-m40.1` (symbology design) + `stellata-9hp` epic shipping (nebulae data + base rendering).

title: Design: chart-mode visual language for catalogued structures (Sky Atlas 2000.0 reference)
description:
**Goal:** produce the design reference that drives `m40`'s child rendering tasks. Output is a written section added to `docs/chart-mode.md` covering per-structure-type chart symbology.

**Reference:** Sky Atlas 2000.0 conventions (user has a scanned reference page available on request). Other classical atlases (Norton's, Uranometria) are secondary references if useful.

**Per-type symbology to define:**
- **Nebulae** — by type (PNe, RNe, H II, SNR), each has traditional chart symbol conventions:
  - PNe: small green-tinged circle with cross or shell-style outline
  - H II / diffuse: dashed or solid outline approximating the angular extent
  - RNe: outline biased toward illuminator, often dashed
  - SNR: filamentary or shell outline
- **Planets of the focused host star** — labelled symbol or filled disc at current angular position, sized appropriately vs. chart star labels. See open design questions below — this section drives `m40.3` and is host-generic (Sol now, exoplanets when `stellata-bk5` ships).

**Open design questions for chart-mode planets** (resolve here, then `m40.3` implements):

1. **Filter mechanism.** Two candidate framings: (a) gate on the focal-host system — planets render only for the star the camera is observing-from; (b) gate on apparent magnitude — planets outside the focal host fall below the visibility cutoff anyway (per `stellata-3re.16`'s host-generic apparent-mag math) and self-filter. (a) and (b) likely produce identical visible output; pick the one whose framing matches how chart-mode planets are *intended* to read (a focal-system affordance vs. a sky-photometry artefact).
2. **Labeling.** Treat each planet like a named star, so it flows through the existing named-star path in `chart-labels.ts` (same font, same collision rules, same dirty-track contract). Confirm or specify divergences.
3. **Glyph vs. magnitude-disc.** Two options: (a) special chart symbol per planet (e.g. ♀ ♂ ♃ ♄ for Sol); (b) same disc-glyph as a star, sized by the planet's apparent magnitude through the existing star-disc pipeline. (a) gives traditional-atlas legibility; (b) is host-generic and works for any focal star.
4. **Non-Sol depiction.** The traditional ♀ ♂ ♃ ♄ symbols are Sol-specific — they don't exist for exoplanets. The chart-mode convention must work for any focal host. Candidate options: (a) generic filled-disc glyph + designation, used uniformly including for Sol; (b) Sol gets the traditional symbols as an explicit override, exoplanets get the generic glyph; (c) astronomical host-letter-index labelling (b/c/d/…) for exoplanets, named labels for Sol's planets. Decision interacts with question 3 — if (3b) wins, depiction is just the disc + label, no glyph alphabet to design. Decision goes in `docs/chart-mode.md`.

**Constraints:**
- Chart mode is monochrome (existing convention) — colour can't differentiate types; line style and shape carry the encoding
- Outlines should read at the same visual weight as constellation lines, neither dominating nor disappearing
- Coordinate with the per-frame label engine in `chart-labels.ts` so symbols don't fight existing star/constellation labels for space

**Output:** new subsection in `docs/chart-mode.md` with reference symbology table + opacity/weight values + resolved answers to the four open planet questions above. Drives `m40.2` and `m40.3` implementation.

**No code in this phase.**

title: Chart-mode rendering of catalogued structures (nebulae, planets, etc.)
description:
**Goal:** as new structures land in the model (nebulae, solar-system planets, etc.), they need a chart-mode representation when they're visually relevant in the real sky. Traditional star atlases (Sky Atlas 2000.0 is the canonical reference) render bright/catalogued objects as light outlines so observers can use the chart against the actual sky.

**Per-structure visibility decision** (made up-front so each structure's chart-mode treatment is clear):

| Structure | Visually relevant? | Chart-mode? |
|---|---|---|
| Stars (existing) | yes | ✓ shipped |
| Constellation lines (existing) | reference | ✓ shipped |
| Galactic disc (existing) | yes (band of MW) | ✓ shipped (isobar) |
| **Nebulae (PNe, RNe, H II, SNR)** — `stellata-9hp` | **YES** | **add** |
| **Planets of focused host star** — `stellata-3re.4` (Sol), `stellata-bk5` (exoplanets) | **YES** — host-relative (Mercury through Saturn naked-eye from Sol; exoplanet bodies are naked-eye from their own host) | **add** (`m40.3`, host-generic — Sol now, exoplanets pick up the same path when `bk5` ships) |
| Molecular clouds — `stellata-c7u` | no (extinction is the signal, dark patches at most) | skip |
| Local Bubble — `stellata-dk3` | no (3D cavity, invisible) | skip |
| Radcliffe Wave — `stellata-n2x` | no (3D structure) | skip |
| Heliopause / scale rings — `stellata-3re.5` | no | skip |
| Deep-space probes — `stellata-ywn` | no | skip |

**Children:**
- `.1` Design language — chart-mode visual conventions per structure type (line weights, fills, opacity, label conventions). User has a scanned Sky Atlas 2000.0 reference page available on request. Includes the four open planet design questions (filter / labeling / glyph-vs-disc / non-Sol depiction) that gate `.3`. Output: written reference in `docs/chart-mode.md` covering the per-type symbology rules.
- `.2` Nebulae chart-mode rendering (depends on `stellata-9hp` shipping + `.1` design)
- `.3` Planets-of-focused-host-star chart-mode rendering, host-generic (depends on `stellata-3re.4` shipping + `.1` design; auto-extends to exoplanets when `bk5` ships)

**Future:** any new structure added to the model needs a chart-mode decision — visible? render. invisible? skip. Add a child here when the call is 'render'.

**Out of scope:**
- Any change to existing chart-mode shipped behaviour (stars, constellation lines, galactic disc isobar)
- The constellation-name label-size tweak (filed separately as its own task)

title: Chart-mode: enlarge constellation name labels + increase transparency
description:
**Goal:** in chart mode, constellation name labels currently render small enough that individual letters can be occluded by adjacent star labels. Increase size by ~50% and increase transparency to compensate (so the larger labels don't dominate).

**Tweak target:**
- Size: ~50% larger
- Opacity: more transparent than today (since the larger size will compensate for legibility)

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/chart-labels.ts` — per-frame label engine + glyphs; constellation-name rendering lives here
- `src/client/chart-mode.ts` — orchestrator if any size/opacity uniforms live there
- Possibly `src/client/styles.css` if labels are SVG/HTML-based

**Verification:** load chart mode, check that overlapping constellation-name + star-label cases now read clearly without the constellation name dominating sparse regions of the sky.

title: Time-scrubber UI in .ui-bottom
description:
**Goal:** the user-facing time scrubber widget. Lives in `.ui-bottom`. Final implementation task in the time-scrubber epic — depends on the three design decisions (motion audit, hide policy, widget design) being settled first.

### Brief

Discreet, unobtrusive. Visible only when the solar-system layer is active (focused star has planets per stellata-3re.6).

### Likely shape (refined by stellata-nmu.3)

- Short text readout showing current `t` (matches the format chosen in stellata-3re.11 — likely UTC Zulu).
- Click-to-edit affordance for jump-to-date.
- Forward / back nudges (e.g. ±1 day, ±1 year keys via keyboard-shortcuts.ts dispatch).
- Optional auto-play (FF/RW) per stellata-nmu.3.
- "Live" state when scrubbed back to now.

### Files likely touched

- `src/client/index.html`, `styles.css` for the chip.
- `src/client/stellata.ts` for visibility wiring + `t` setter integration.
- New module `src/client/time-scrubber.ts`.

### Depends on

- `stellata-3re.1` for the `t` state + setter.
- `stellata-3re.11` for the time-format decision (the static readout settles the format; the scrubber inherits it).
- `stellata-nmu.1`, `stellata-nmu.2`, `stellata-nmu.3` for the design questions about catalogue motion, hide policy, and widget shape.
notes: Reparented from stellata-3re to stellata-nmu on 2026-05-07. v1 solar-system layer ships only a static time readout (stellata-3re.11); this scrubber is deferred to the time-scrubber epic.

title: Rendering: orbiting planets, orbit rings, null-radius indicator
description:
**Goal:** when the user focuses a flagged host star, render its known exoplanets as orbiting bodies — orbital rings sized to `semi_major_axis_au`, planet discs sized to `radius_earth` / `radius_jupiter` where known.

**Visual decisions deferred to implementer** (see parent `stellata-bk5` for context):
- Orbit ring style (solid / dashed / fade-with-distance)
- Planet disc shading (flat / lit / temperature-tinted via `st_teff`)
- Null-radius indicator (an unknown-radius planet still has a known orbit; show the orbit ring + a default-size marker?)

**Leverage `stellata-a7d`:** disc sizing should use the same physical-angular-diameter formula `θ = 2·atan(R/d)` from the rewrite, not the legacy log-mapped formula. Planets at correct angular size at the camera distances expected when focused on a host star.

**Files touched:** likely a new `src/client/exoplanets.ts` rendering layer + shader pair, plus integration into `stellata.ts` focus path.

**Depends on:** `stellata-bk5.2` for data, and **strongly benefits from `stellata-a7d.2` having landed** so disc sizing is physically consistent.

title: Client loader: lazy-load exoplanets.json on first focus of a flagged host star
description:
**Goal:** when the user focuses a star whose 'has-exoplanets' flag bit is set, fetch `public/exoplanets.json` (once, cached for the session) and surface its `ExoplanetSystem` for the focused host.

**Implementation:**
- New `src/client/exoplanet-loader.ts` mirroring the structure of `catalog-loader.ts`
- Singleton fetch promise — first call kicks off the load, subsequent calls reuse the result
- API: `getExoplanetsForStar(idx: number): ExoplanetSystem | null`
- Wired into `stellata.ts` focus path: when a flagged star is focused, kick the fetch and (on resolve) update the rendering layer

**Out of scope:** any UI affordance is part of `stellata-bk5.3`. This issue ends at `getExoplanetsForStar()` returning data.

**Depends on:** `stellata-bk5.1` for the binary flag bit + json file.

title: Build pipeline: NASA Exoplanet Archive ingest, cross-match, catalog flag bit + lazy json
description:
**Goal:** ingest the NASA Exoplanet Archive `pscomppars` table at build time, cross-match host stars to ATHYG, set a 'has-exoplanets' flag bit per star in `catalog.bin`, and emit `public/exoplanets.json` keyed by host-star catalog index for lazy loading.

**Data fetch** (one-off, committed via Git LFS — see parent `stellata-bk5` for the exact curl URL and Git LFS commands):
- `data/exoplanets_nasa.csv` from the TAP API
- Fields: `pl_name, hostname, hip_name, hd_name, ra, dec, sy_dist, pl_rade, pl_radj, pl_orbsmax, pl_orbper, pl_eccen, pl_bmasse, sy_pnum, st_teff, st_rad, st_lum`

**Parser:** new `parseExoplanetData(csvPath)` alongside the GCVS parsers. Returns `Map<hostname, ExoplanetSystem>` with planets sorted by `semi_major_axis_au` ascending (nulls last). Schema in parent `stellata-bk5`. Don't filter null-radius planets — renderer handles that.

**Cross-match — HIP + HD + Gliese.** Build symmetric reverse maps (`exoByHip`, `exoByHd`, `exoByGl`) after the existing post-sort `hipToIndex` map. Resolve hostname → ATHYG catalog index; record on each matched star a 'has-exoplanets' bit + an offset into the lazy json.

**Outputs:**
- `catalog.bin` gains a flag bit per star (likely fits in an existing reserved bit; check the binary v4 layout)
- `public/exoplanets.json` keyed by catalog index → `ExoplanetSystem`. Lazy-loaded by the client on first focus of a star with the flag set.

**Files touched:** `scripts/build-catalog.ts`, `scripts/catalog-pure.ts` (new parser there per existing pattern), tests in `scripts/catalog-pure.test.ts`.

title: Search index integration + RNe illuminator cross-match to ATHYG
description:
**Goal:** named nebulae appear in the search bar like stars and clouds; reflection nebulae's illuminating stars are resolved to ATHYG indices for colour-biasing in `stellata-9hp.3`.

**Search index:**
- Extend `search-index.json` to include nebula entries with `kind: 'nebula'` discriminator (existing pattern: stars use `kind: 'star'`, clouds use `kind: 'cloud'`)
- Common-name + type-prefix entries (e.g. 'Orion Nebula', 'Ring Nebula', 'M42')
- Focus search box dispatches to `flyToNebula` (analogous to `flyToCloud` from the cloud overlay)
- `stellata.ts` gains `flyToNebula(nebulaIdx)` mirroring `flyToCloud`

**RNe illuminator cross-match:**
- Magakian RNe entries name an illuminating star (HD/HIP). Resolve at build time via the same hipToIndex / hd → index maps used by `build-catalog.ts`
- Emit `illuminator_idx: number | null` per RNe entry in `nebulae.json`
- Renderer (in `stellata-9hp.3`) uses the illuminator's colour to bias the bluish reflection tint

**Depends on:** `stellata-9hp.1` for the nebulae data file.

title: Type-specific visual treatments (H II, PNe, RNe, SNR)
description:
**Goal:** distinguishable visuals per nebula type so the type encoding reads at a glance. All visuals procedural — **no photographs**, matching the rest of the app's aesthetic.

**Per-type treatment** (full notes in parent `stellata-9hp`):
- **H II regions** — warm reddish-pink, soft sphere with density falloff
- **Planetary nebulae** — greenish-teal, often shell-like; small central point + thin shell
- **Reflection nebulae** — bluish, irregular blob biased toward the illuminating star's colour (look up via ATHYG cross-ref from `stellata-9hp.4`)
- **Supernova remnants** — filamentary; pink → blue gradient by age

**Approach:** type-specific density profile + colour mapping passed as uniforms to the raymarch shader from `stellata-9hp.2`. Procedural shape variation per type is essential — without it, every nebula renders as a blob and the type encoding is lost.

**Depends on:** `stellata-9hp.2` (raymarch shader) and `stellata-9hp.4` (RNe illuminator cross-match for the bluish-toward-illuminator bias).

title: Per-nebula raymarching shader (small AABB volumetrics)
description:
**Goal:** the volumetric rendering primitive for nebulae. Each nebula occupies a small AABB (typically 1–50 pc), so per-nebula raymarching with 16–32 samples is dense in voxel-space and produces smooth volumetrics without the aliasing that killed the all-sky fog approach.

**Reference:** recover the fog shader pattern from git history at commit `5ad5444` — clip-space fullscreen triangle, ray-AABB slab intersection, fixed-step march with log-encoded density decode. See `memory/feedback_raymarch_for_nebulae.md` for context on why the all-sky version failed and per-nebula AABBs work.

**Implementation:**
- New shader pair `src/client/shaders/nebula.{vert,frag}.glsl`
- Per-nebula uniform set: AABB centre + extents, density profile parameters, type-coded colour (set by `stellata-9hp.3`)
- Render-order placement coordinated with stars / clouds / Milky Way

**Out of scope:** type-specific procedural shape variation lives in `stellata-9hp.3`. This issue delivers the raymarch primitive with a single neutral density profile.

**Depends on:** `stellata-9hp.1` (data) for AABBs to render against.

title: Evaluate perf + first-load tradeoffs: reduced_m12 (~2M stars) vs reduced_m11 (~800k)
description:
**Goal:** decide which ATHYG reduced subset the catalog upgrade in `stellata-cns` should target. The parent issue currently specifies `reduced_m12` (~2M stars, ~80 MB binary, ~20 MB gzipped). `reduced_m11` (~800k stars) is the smaller alternative.

**Tradeoffs to measure:**
- **First-load size**: m12 ~20 MB gzipped vs m11 estimated ~8 MB. Significant for slow connections and mobile.
- **Render perf**: 2M instanced quads at 60 fps on integrated GPUs / mobile chips vs ~800k. Three.js instance-attribute memory grows linearly. Two-pass core/glow split helps overdraw, but instance count is the dominant cost.
- **Scientific completeness**: m12 reaches further into the kpc range; m11 truncates at brighter apparent mag and leaves more gaps at intermediate distances (50 pc – kpc).
- **Build pipeline cost**: m12 CSV is ~400 MB (LFS); m11 is smaller. Build time + CI implications.

**Method:**
- Pull both ATHYG subsets and run `scripts/build-catalog.ts` against each
- Measure: binary size, gzipped wire size, instance-attribute memory, FPS on a representative low-end target (integrated GPU, ideally a mid-tier phone)
- Compare scientific feel: are intermediate-distance gaps in m11 visually obvious during travel?

**Output:** a recommendation in this issue's notes for which subset `stellata-cns` should target. Update `cns` accordingly when decided.

**Watch out for:**
- m11 ⊂ m12 ⊂ `reduced_m13.5` etc. — all are subsets of full ATHYG; differences are purely the apparent-mag cutoff.
- Mobile perf may be the deciding factor — if m12 doesn't run on phones, the answer is m11 regardless of completeness.
notes: 2026-05-02 — note: this decision sets the **model's magnitude floor**, not just the star catalog cutoff. Whichever cutoff (m11 or m12) wins, it becomes the dimnest threshold for all object categories — variable stars, nebulae, projected distant DSOs (`stellata-38m` and the new projected-DSOs task), and any future object types. Factor this into the decision: m11 (~mag 11 floor) gives a cleaner, brighter sky; m12 (~mag 12 floor) gives a deeper sky but more visual density and more catalog work for every object type that has to respect the floor.

title: Dataset boundary overlay: visualise where high-fidelity data ends (Edenhofer dust volume, etc.)
description:
**Frame:** part of the artificial-visualisation cluster — like the dust particle layer and MC silhouettes, this is an opt-in overlay that surfaces something you can't directly see in the rendered scene. Specifically *not* a "you're outside the realistic model" status chip: that framing implies degraded mode and breaks immersion. Going farther from the high-fidelity volume is just part of the experience; the underlying dataset boundary becomes visible only if the user opts in.

**What to visualise (initial scope):**
- The **Edenhofer dust voxel cube** (~1.25 kpc AABB at world origin) — the high-fidelity 3D dust volume that drives per-star extinction inside it. Outside, an analytical disc/scale-height profile takes over (see `stellata-74e`). Render as a faint translucent sphere or box mesh, off by default.

**Future extensions (not blockers for this issue):**
- ATHYG catalog completeness boundary (e.g. apparent-magnitude horizon as a faint shell)
- Zucker cloud coverage region
- Per-star dust extinction validity envelope
- Other dataset envelopes added later

**Implementation sketch:**
- Faint translucent mesh at world origin (size = Edenhofer AABB). Premultiplied alpha; very low opacity. Sits in the same toggle group as the rest of the artificial-viz overlays.
- No status chip, no banner, no "degraded" framing in the UI.

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — boundary mesh + group registration
- `src/client/controls.ts` — toggle in the artificial-viz panel section (once that section's UX is designed under `stellata-io3`)
- `src/client/url-state.ts` — toggle persistence
- `src/client/index.html`, `src/client/styles.css` — minimal

**Out of scope:**
- Any text indication that the user is in / out of a high-fidelity region — explicitly rejected during scoping.
- Changes to extinction physics — that's `stellata-74e`'s scope.

**Estimate:** ~2 hours once the artificial-viz toggle group exists.

**History:** extracted from `stellata-74e` Part 2 ("realism indicator + boundary mesh") on 2026-05-02. The original framing as a status chip was rejected; reframed as an artificial visualisation overlay alongside dust-particle viz and MC silhouettes.

title: Re-purpose shelved warm-glow MC overlay as part of artificial-viz toggle group
description:
**Goal:** the currently-shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` warm-glow MC overlay (decorative, not physical) is a candidate inhabitant of the artificial-visualisation layer. Decide its role *after* Epic A (`stellata-c7u`) lands physical MC rendering.

**Current state:** `FilterState.showMolecularClouds = false`, panel toggle removed, URL flag bit 2 reserved. Render path, shaders, and data are preserved.

**Question this issue captures:**
- After Epic A's physical MC rendering ships, do we *also* want the legacy artificial overlay as a separate user-toggleable layer? (Cases for: explicit "show me the cloud locations" mode for users who want to find clouds, similar in spirit to the existing molecular-cloud focus / vector / warp UX. Cases against: A.6's subtle stylised presence may already cover this need.)
- If yes: re-frame the warm-glow renderer as the artificial-viz cloud layer, distinct from Epic A's physical pass. Toggle lives in the artificial-viz group from `stellata-io3`.
- If no: delete the shelved code path entirely.

**Decision deferred** until Epic A's A.6 lands, so the team can evaluate whether subtle stylised presence is sufficient. This issue is a placeholder for that decision.

**Depends on Epic A's A.6 (`stellata-c7u.6`) for the assessment to make sense — relates_to dependency rather than blocks since neither outcome is required for the epic to ship.**

title: Artificial visualisation layer: structures invisible/near-invisible to the naked eye
description:
**Tracking epic — no decisions yet, just a grouping.**

**Frame:** some structures in the catalogue are essentially invisible (interstellar dust outside dense clouds), nearly invisible (most molecular clouds, against background stars), or only partially visible (faint nebulae, distant SF regions). Showing them at all is a *deliberate non-physical choice* — a visualisation, not a recreation. They belong together as a UX cluster: there should be a clear conceptual line between "what you'd actually see" and "what we're overlaying to help you see structure."

**Possible UX shape (deferred):**
- A single "artificial overlay" toggle group in the settings panel — e.g. dust visualisation, MC silhouettes, faint nebulae overlays — clearly labelled as visualisation, not as physical truth
- Each layer can be independently toggled within the group
- Off by default? On by default? — design decision later
- Possibly a single master switch with detail toggles underneath, or a mode-style switch (physical / visualisation / chart-mode)

**What this epic does NOT do today:** make any decisions. It tracks the related issues so a future design pass can treat them as one coherent UX cluster.

**Children (work issues that fit this frame):**
- `stellata-zq3` — Revisit the dust visualisation layer (the always-artificial dust particle splat layer)
- (filed under this epic: a child to evaluate re-purposing the currently-shelved warm-glow MC silhouette as part of the artificial-viz toggle group, separate from Epic A's *physical* MC rendering)

**Related but not (yet) reparented:**
- `stellata-9hp` — Nebulae catalogs (PN, RNe, H II, SNR). Many catalogue nebulae are faint enough to be effectively invisible without aperture; conceptually fits this frame, but it's a self-contained feature with its own scope. Revisit grouping if a unified "artificial overlay" UX emerges.
- `stellata-c7u` (Physical MC rendering epic) — the *physical* counterpart to the artificial MC overlay child below. Both can coexist; they answer different questions ("what would I see?" vs "where is the structure?").

**No hard dependencies; epic is purely organisational.**

title: A.7 Inside-the-cloud immersion + ambient response tuning
description:
**Goal:** confirm that flying into a cloud reads as physics. The expected experience (per scoping discussion):
- Stars whose line of sight traverses cloud material in front of the camera dim and redden proportional to remaining-depth.
- Peripheral stars (off-axis, sightline doesn't traverse cloud) are unaffected.
- Ambient gets very dark — there's almost no scattered light inside an MC in the visible.
- Where embedded young stars carve cavities (A.5), local density drops and visibility recovers near them.

**This phase is mostly tuning + verification once A.2–A.6 have landed:**
- Verify per-star extinction integration handles the camera-inside-cloud case correctly (the raymarch starts inside the medium; integration must work from camera→star regardless of which side of the envelope the camera is on).
- Tune ambient / fog so darkness is conveyed without losing the sky entirely (some fraction of distant starfield should remain visible — the sky outside the cloud should not be pitch-black if the cloud is finite).
- Test all known approachable clouds: Taurus, Orion A, Orion B, Perseus, Ophiuchus, Chamaeleon, Lupus, Cepheus, Pipe, Corona Australis. Record any cloud-specific calibration tweaks needed.

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/stellata.ts` — ambient / fog adjustments per camera position relative to cloud
- `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` — verify camera-inside-medium raymarch correctness
- Possibly `docs/molecular-clouds.md` for the cloud-by-cloud notes

**Visual gate:** flying into Taurus produces a dimming/reddening experience that feels physical — not a hard fog wall, not unchanged starfield.

**Depends on A.2–A.6.**

title: A.6 Subtle stylised 'presence' pass for empty-sky cloud silhouette
description:
**Goal:** physical extinction is the dominant signal, but on directions where there are no bright background stars the cloud would be invisible. A *very* subtle stylised pass conveys the cloud's shape without competing with the physical extinction signal.

**Constraints:**
- Must read as decorative, not as the cloud's body. Users should never mistake the stylised hint for the actual luminous emission of the cloud (MCs barely emit in visible).
- Easy to under-do; easy to over-do. Tune so the stylisation is invisible against starfields and only emerges over starless directions.
- Should respect mode: chart mode already has its own isobar contour treatment (`uChartIsobar` path in `cloud.frag.glsl`); coordinate so the stylised presence is dark-mode-only or differs between modes.

**Possible approaches (decision in A.1):**
- Re-purpose the existing shelved `cloud.frag.glsl` warm-glow as the stylised pass, but with opacity ~0.05–0.1 instead of decorative levels, possibly modulated by absence of background stars
- Faint fresnel-style edge glow keyed to silhouette direction
- Substructure noise (from A.4) reading through as faint texture rather than smooth shading

**Files likely touched:**
- `src/client/shaders/cloud.frag.glsl` — possibly retained, possibly rewritten
- `src/client/molecular-clouds.ts` — re-enables the layer (`FilterState.showMolecularClouds` default flip)
- `src/client/controls.ts` — re-add the panel toggle if we want one (decision in A.1)
- `src/client/url-state.ts` — URL flag bit 2 was reserved; re-encode or leave reserved per A.1 decision

**Visual gate:** flying past Taurus against a starfield reads as physical (stars dim and redden behind it). Flying past Taurus against a star-poor direction reads as a *very faint* hint of cloud shape, never as a luminous blob.

**Depends on A.3, A.4 ideally landed first so the physical signal exists before the decorative pass is layered on top.**

title: A.5 Embedded young-star cavities + cloud-type variations (HII, reflection nebulosity)
description:
**Goal:** model the cloud-shaping effect of embedded young stars. O/B stars carve density cavities via stellar winds + ionising radiation (Strömgren spheres, HII regions), and hot enough hosts produce visible emission. This is what gives an active SF region (Orion A) a different inside-the-cloud experience from a quiescent dark cloud (Taurus dark cores).

**Cross-match (build time):**
- Identify O/B stars and known YSOs whose 3D positions fall inside any Zucker cloud bbox
- Use ATHYG spectral types for O/B identification
- For each cloud, list embedded hot stars with: position, spectral type, luminosity proxy
- Output: per-cloud cavity descriptor in the dust manifest or cloud JSON

**Density modulation:**
- For each embedded hot star, reduce local density inside a radius scaled by stellar luminosity (Strömgren-sphere-style; constants from A.1)
- Soft falloff at the cavity edge — `density × (1 − cavity_strength × falloff(r/r_cav))`

**Cloud-type variation:**
- Quiescent dark cloud (no/few hot embeddings): high peak density, sharp envelope
- Active SF region (multiple hot embeddings): cavities, lower mean density, optionally an emission overlay
- HII region: when embedded star is hot enough (≥ early B), add a faint emission/scattering term — a red/pink tinge near the star, fading with distance. Threshold from A.1.

**Reflection nebulosity (optional within scope):** B-type stars in dusty regions backscatter blue light. If included, decide whether it's worth modelling here or deferred to the the nebulae catalog work Nebulae epic (`stellata-9hp`) which already covers reflection nebulae as discrete blob objects. Likely defer.

**Visual gate:** Orion A (multiple O stars) should render visibly differently from Taurus dark-cloud cores (quiescent) — Orion's density should feel "hollowed", and the area around hot embedded stars should be perceptibly less dim.

**Depends on A.2 (data pipeline) and A.4 (substructure landscape into which cavities are carved).**

title: A.4 Procedural substructure: 3D noise inside cloud envelope for filamentary density
description:
**Goal:** real MCs are filamentary and clumpy with a log-normal/power-law density PDF, not smooth ellipsoids. Substructure makes the cloud feel real — both visually (where the subtle stylised pass A.6 reads filaments) and physically (extinction varies along sightlines through the cloud, not just by depth).

**Approach:** procedural 3D noise (fBm and/or Worley/Voronoi) shaped by the ellipsoid envelope from Zucker. Octaves and amplitudes calibrated in A.1. The noise modulates density inside the envelope; outside the envelope density goes to zero smoothly.

**Where the noise is sampled — design decision in A.1:**
- (a) Bake into the dust voxel grid in `build-dust.py` — single source of truth, but voxel resolution caps detail.
- (b) Evaluate per-fragment in `star.vert.glsl` raymarch — finer detail, but more shader cost per star and harder to integrate.
- (c) Hybrid: voxel-scale noise baked in, sub-voxel detail evaluated in shader for the close-camera case.

**Implementation depends on (a/b/c):**
- (a) → `scripts/build-dust.py`
- (b) → `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl` plus a noise function include
- (c) → both

**Visual gate:** a sightline grazing the edge of Taurus should show patchy/filamentary extinction (not a smooth ellipsoid darkening), and the subtle stylised presence pass (A.6) should read structured, not blob-like.

**Depends on A.2.**

title: A.3 Wavelength-selective extinction: redden background stars passing through dust
description:
**Goal:** the per-star extinction layer in `star.vert.glsl` should redden background stars, not just dim them. This is the dominant *visible* signature of an MC against rich star fields and is what makes a dust lane look like a dust lane.

**Investigate first:** does the current per-star extinction path apply a single grey magnitude penalty, or does it already separate B/R? Confirm during research and adjust scope accordingly. (A.1 should answer this; record findings here.)

**Model:** standard interstellar reddening with RV = A_V / E(B−V) ≈ 3.1 (Cardelli-Clayton-Mathis 1989). Compute E(B−V) per star from the integrated optical depth, then apply A_B = A_V × (RV+1)/RV and A_R proportionally. Use existing per-star B and V (or B-V) channels in the catalog binary.

**Implementation:**
- Touch: `src/client/shaders/star.vert.glsl`, possibly `star.frag.glsl`
- Add a reddening factor in the colour pipeline so the displayed star colour shifts toward red proportional to extinction depth, on top of the existing dimming
- Verify chart-mode and observer mode both pick up the reddening (or one of them is intentionally exempt — decide in A.1)

**Visual gate:** an O/B star (intrinsically blue) sitting behind ~10 mag of MC dust should render distinctly red, not just dim blue.

**Depends on A.2** for the calibrated dust density. Doesn't depend on A.4 (substructure) or A.5 (cavities) — those refine the density field A.3 reads from.

title: A.2 Build pipeline: bake Zucker MC density into Edenhofer dust voxel volume
description:
**Goal:** make the per-star extinction raymarch in `star.vert.glsl` the single source of truth for absorption, with Zucker MCs supplying higher-fidelity local density inside the Edenhofer volume. No double-counting between the two layers.

**Touches:**
- `scripts/build-dust.py` — at the end of the existing dust-volume bake, overlay Zucker MC contributions per voxel. Each cloud's density profile (centroid, semi-axes, density model from A.1) replaces or augments the underlying Edenhofer value where they overlap.
- `data/dust/manifest.json` — bump version; record that Zucker has been merged in.
- `scripts/build-clouds.py` — likely needs to emit the per-cloud parameters consumed by `build-dust.py` (peak density, profile shape, etc.) in addition to today's bbox/centroid.

**Decision points (resolved in A.1, applied here):**
- Replace vs additive blend in overlap voxels
- Whether procedural substructure (A.4) is baked into the voxel field at build time, or applied per-fragment in the shader at runtime, or both
- Calibration: ensure that flying through Taurus produces reasonable Av (~5–15+ mag) rather than under- or over-extinction

**Sanity gate:** `scripts/verify-catalog.ts`-equivalent check for the dust manifest — pick a cloud (e.g. Taurus), compute a sightline through its centre, confirm Av matches the literature value within a factor of ~2.

**Out of scope:** the shader-side reddening (A.3) and substructure noise (A.4) are downstream. This phase produces the data they consume.

title: Mobile Safari chrome polish + Add-to-Home-Screen PWA path
description:
Goal: a deliberately-mobile-aware chrome layout in iOS Safari, plus a polished Add-to-Home-Screen (PWA standalone) path for users who want full-bleed.

WHAT WAS LEARNED (from PR #6, abandoned):
- Browser iOS Safari does NOT let arbitrary content paint behind the URL bar while the URL bar is at full size, regardless of `viewport-fit=cover` + `100lvh` + safe-area-inset. The chrome stays opaque.
- The 'modern site' behaviour the user remembers (URL bar shrinks; canvas extends behind) only kicks in for *scrollable* pages — iOS minimises the URL bar after a user scroll. Stellata is fixed-viewport (overflow: hidden) so there's no scroll, so the URL bar never minimises, so full-bleed never engages.
- Workarounds (artificially-scrollable body, programmatic scroll-to-1px) are fragile across iOS versions and fight our gesture-capturing canvas.
- Standalone PWA (Add to Home Screen) has none of these limits — no URL bar at all, full-bleed by default.
- We did achieve correct sizing of the GL buffer relative to the layout viewport via documentElement.clientWidth/clientHeight (vs window.innerWidth/innerHeight). That code is in PR #6's history if we want to recover it, but the user opted to revert and start fresh under a re-scoped issue.

NEW SCOPE:
1. Polish what we can in browser Safari without chasing impossible full-bleed:
   - Confirm + fix scale-bar / settings panel positioning so they sit cleanly above the URL bar (use `100vh - 100dvh` / safe-area-inset-bottom thoughtfully, not sum).
   - Add the meta tags that improve the experience: `viewport-fit=cover`, `apple-mobile-web-app-capable`, `apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style=black-translucent`, `theme-color`.
   - Avoid the `100% / inner*` buffer-vs-CSS stretch: size the GL buffer to the layout viewport (documentElement.clientWidth/clientHeight) so stars don't squash on iOS.
2. Make Add-to-Home-Screen produce a clean standalone experience:
   - Manifest.json with name, short_name, theme_color, background_color, icons (192/512 PNG), display: standalone.
   - Apple touch icon link in <head>.
   - First-launch behaviour in standalone mode (no URL bar; full-bleed by default).

DEPENDS ON 47c.3 (design-decision) — the gating-vs-recommend question changes how aggressive this work is. If we hard-block phone viewports, this issue shrinks to 'just AtHS polish'. If we soft-warn, this is the scaffolding for a deliberately mobile-aware chrome layout.

OUT OF SCOPE: touch gestures (split into 47c.4); any layout work for the settings panel itself (covered by the existing panel-rework design issue 1yu).
notes:
Implementation landed on claude/add-mobile-and-ux-issues:
- index.html: viewport-fit=cover, theme-color, apple-mobile-web-app-capable, status-bar-style=black-translucent
- styles.css: html/body height -> 100dvh; #scene + .overlay sized to 100dvw/100dvh so the CSS rect tracks the iOS visual viewport (matching the GL buffer that's already sized to window.innerHeight); modal max-height + kb-modal margin migrated to dvh; the four edge-anchored chrome containers (.ui-top, .ui-top-left, .ui-bottom, .warp-btn) wrapped with max(<n>px, env(safe-area-inset-*)) / calc(<n>px + env(safe-area-inset-*)) so they clear the notch / Dynamic Island / home indicator without changing desktop spacing.
- Three.js resize path unchanged: window.resize already fires on iOS URL-bar transitions and feeds window.innerWidth/innerHeight into renderer.setSize / camera.aspect.
